John Fetterman on Depression, Vance vs. Rubio, and Why He Won’t Toe the Party Line

Citizen McCain with Meghan McCain(30.2K) ·69:46 ·3.4K views · 2026-04-23
John Fetterman (US Senator) Meghan McCain (Host, Citizen McCain)
Senator John Fetterman discusses his recovery from depression and his commitment to bipartisanship, while host Meghan McCain expresses deep alarm over Virginia’s recent redistricting, which she argues effectively disenfranchises Republican voters and...
TL;DR Senator John Fetterman discusses his recovery from depression and his commitment to bipartisanship, while host Meghan McCain expresses deep alarm over Virginia’s recent redistricting, which she argues effectively disenfranchises Republican voters and signals a dangerous...

TL;DR

Senator John Fetterman discusses his recovery from depression and his commitment to bipartisanship, while host Meghan McCain expresses deep alarm over Virginia’s recent redistricting, which she argues effectively disenfranchises Republican voters and signals a dangerous national trend toward extreme gerrymandering.

Virginia Redistricting and the "Lobster" Map

  • Congressional Shift: Meghan McCain highlights a special election and redistricting in Virginia that shifted the state's 11-member delegation from a 6-5 Democratic edge to a projected 10-1 edge.
      • Disproportionate Representation: McCain argues that despite Virginia being a "purple" state with a near 50-50 split, Democrats will now control 90% of the districts.
      • The "Lobster" District: The new map concentrates Republican voters into a single, oddly shaped district, leaving the rest of the state to be represented by Northern Virginia’s bureaucratic interests.
  • Political Fallout: McCain expresses heartbreak over the state's trajectory, comparing it to California and New York. She explicitly mentions considering a move to a red state (Texas or Arizona) because she feels her "voice does not matter" in Virginia.
  • Bipartisan Criticism: Both McCain and guest Senator Fetterman condemn gerrymandering regardless of party. Fetterman notes that while Virginia is a Democratic "win," it is an "American loss" because it stacks the deck against true democracy.

Mental Health and Suicide Prevention

  • Fetterman’s Personal Struggle: Senator Fetterman speaks candidly about his battle with clinical depression, describing the "lethal" mindset where the brain convinces an individual that the world is better off without them.
      • The "Emergency Brake": Fetterman identifies his children as his primary reason for staying "in the game" and surviving his darkest period.
      • Advice to Others: He urges those struggling with suicidal ideation to promise themselves they won't "take themselves out," noting that survival often leads to a "spontaneously curative" realization of wanting to live.
  • "Stop the Scroll" Initiative: Fetterman is partnering with Republican Senator Katie Britt to address the negative impact of social media on youth mental health, comparing the "best friend" status of social media in a child's life to a stranger spending hours alone with them.
  • Alternative Treatments: Fetterman supports the FDA fast-tracking research into psychedelics (psilocybin and ibogaine) for veterans suffering from PTSD, citing his previous town halls where veterans begged for legal, safe access to these substances over standard VA pharmaceuticals.

Bipartisanship and Party Orthodoxy

  • Zionism and the Democratic Party: Fetterman identifies as a proud Zionist and expresses concern that support for Israel is becoming a "political liability" in his party.
      • The Overton Window: He warns that the "younger generation is gone" regarding support for Israel, noting that extreme anti-Israel views are being normalized by figures like Hasan Piker.
      • The 20%: Fetterman acknowledges that his pro-Israel stance puts him in the minority (20%) of his party, but he refuses to "turn his back" on the Jewish community.
  • Respect for the Opposition: Fetterman praises Senator Marco Rubio, stating Rubio refuses to "pander to the ugliest side" of the GOP. He also expresses a desire to emulate John McCain’s "independent voice" by putting country over party.
  • Rejection of Political Performance: Fetterman dismisses the trend of using "F*** Trump" as a campaign slogan, stating he will not engage in degrading the tone of political conversation.

Notable Quotes

"The second that they crossed the rail on the bridge, they scream out like 'I want to live, I want to live.' Uh and if they survived that jump, that’s spontaneously curative... I’m a testament right now and it got worse for me after I won." — Sen. John Fetterman
"Gerrymandering is bad, it's toxic, it's going to hurt America. And this is the kind of stuff, this is the kind of ways that civil wars start... You want to disenfranchise people? Make sure that no matter what, their voices will never be heard." — Meghan McCain
"I don’t just represent 13 million Democrats, I represent all 13 million Pennsylvanians... that’s my mandate. Why wouldn't you want to work with the other side? That doesn’t mean you agree on everything. That’s the way this democracy was designed." — Sen. John Fetterman
"The spirit of the [Democratic] party lives in the elections right now and in the primaries. Where is the single primary where there is a proud Israel supporter that’s winning? ... In every single primary, it's getting more and more extreme anti-Israel, openly hostile." — Sen. John Fetterman

And if all of you would just stop being so psychotic to me on social media, I would share that picture, but I'm too freaked out to share anything anymore. I don't blame you. I don't blame you. Anyway, for people that haven't been paying attention, there was a special election yesterday in Virginia where Virginia voters narrowly approved a complete redrawing of congressional districts that offset pushes by Trump to increase Republican majority in the House of Representatives. This shift is going to make Old Dominion's 11-member delegation from a six to five Democratic edge - so six Democrats, five Republicans - to ten Democrats and one Republican in a state that is 50, literally almost 50-50 percent purple. Like 50% Republicans, 50% Democrats. Now in Congress, we will be represented in a 90% to 5% representation Democrat to Republican. So all the congressmen of the... I don't know if I'm... I was just trying to figure out how I'd explain this to someone who didn't know what gerrymandering was. In our state, despite the fact that our population is half and half in the state of Virginia, where I'm currently a resident, of Republicans and Democrats, now our congressional representatives will be 90% Democrats representing us and 10% Republicans. Miranda, if you don't mind, could we please show the map of the proposed map number five? And if you see really quickly, it'll come up in a second, sorry. Thank you very much. Basic... Northern Virginia which is the area which I live in. See right here? Northern Virginia, which is ostensibly Washington D.C. When people ask me where I live sometimes and they don't know, I'll just say D.C. because I'm in the Beltway just like all the other like bureaucratic political analyst assholes. But see this? See there? They're calling it the Lobster. That's the rest of the state that are all Republican voters and we're all going to be represented by the tiny little part. And there's many reasons why this is problematic. And I would just like to say before anyone says "It happened in Texas," it did happen in Texas. It wasn't this egregious because Texas is a majority Republican voting state. I'm against gerrymandering across the board, on the left and the right. I think it should be illegal. I don't even understand how this is democratic. I think this is the type of thing that starts civil wars. The baseline of our country is no taxation without representation. My representation should be what I vote for. Granted, the district that I live in is like entirely blue and it's like a joke how blue it is. I'm probably one of seven people voting Republican in it, but I still deserve to have the right to vote. And the rest of the state of Virginia should, if they are Republicans and are a Republican area, should be represented by Republicans. I am so upset. I think that this means that poor people, disenfranchised people, minorities, a bunch of people will not have their, their views represented in Congress. And I think this sets an extremely dangerous precedent going forward, again on the left and the right. And I would also like to give a special thank you to Republicans and the RNC and everybody who completely dropped the ball on this and let this happen because we clearly didn't care. I mean, Governor Youngkin was going out... actually, can we play the clip of Governor Youngkin, number three please? Because I want to... again, I'm trying to explain this, but it's a little wonky. But I'm going to tell you why I'm so upset. Number three please. Martha, it's great to be with you. And listen, today is a critical election day in Virginia and for America. And we've been pounding the pavement for the last many weeks. And what we're hearing and seeing is that urban voters are being outweighed by rural voters because rural voters are mad. They see that the this, this illegal power grab is trying to disenfranchise millions and millions of voters. We're also hearing from independent voters that they feel that they have been lied to by Abigail Spanberger who last summer said she had no interest in, in any kind of gerrymandering. And on top of that, most Virginians stand for fairness. And this is blatantly unfair. Even the Washington Post said it was the most brazenly dishonest campaign by the "Yes" campaign and therefore they even the Washington Post has said that they believe "No" should prevail. Okay, so I came to the state of Virginia... people often ask me how I ended up here. I was living in New York City for over 15 years, I went to college there and then I stayed New York City not because I was particularly obsessed with it but because all my work was there. I came to Virginia sort of as a political refugee during COVID. And during COVID, I was so grateful to live in this state under Governor Youngkin because he stopped so much of the chaos that was happening in other cities here in Virginia. He stopped the, the rioting, the people tearing down statues, the crazy mandates, the... the... he gave parents a lot more power when it came to our say when it came to our schools and and just school overreach in general when it came to keeping kids home. There was so much of Governor Youngkin that was... I was so happy and grateful I lived in the state of Virginia. And I was like, thank God I'm still in Virginia. I pro... I would have preferred if I'd somehow landed in Arizona because it was really free then. But I've, I've been really proud to be here for the past almost five years. I like living in Virginia to a point because there's a lot of beautiful things about living in a founding state. There's a lot of history. I live very close to George Washington's house. I like fully... I always say I really like feeling all four seasons completely here. There's just something beautiful about the history. I like living close to D.C. I'm a political animal. Last night when this came in, my husband and I had a very long conversation about if we can still continue living here. And I'm going to tell you why for two reasons. Number one, this... I like living in a purple state. I don't mind it. I'd prefer a red state, but I don't mind a purple state because I think we can, we can find bipartisanship and fairness. I cannot live in a fully blue state. It, it's a hard line for me. I've got young kids. I have seen through my experience in COVID what happens when Democrats have ultimate control. And I, I always feel like political analysts sometimes are like storm chasers and we see the tornado coming before everybody else and I'm warning everybody. There's a big goddamn tornado coming. We are going to be like California, we're going to be like New York City. I cannot live in these places anymore. These policies directly impact all our life. I am heartbroken that I'm living in a state where the governor and the attorney general of this state - the attorney general has said was caught saying that he didn't mind if bullets were in Republicans' heads and their children's heads - and that it still got voted for in the state of Virginia. I'm heartbroken that anybody thinks this is good for democracy. Democrats who are always railing about how Trump is going to end democracy and our democratic process, you are taking away the, the very rights our founding fathers gave us, which is what is so beautiful about being an American is our voices really matter. And right now my voice does not matter in this state at all. It doesn't matter what I do, how I vote. The swing aspect of it is completely blown up in the air unless it comes to the general election. This is something that is worth noting that this will go to the likely the Supreme Court to see if this is even, even justifiable. Gerrymandering is bad, it's toxic, it's going to hurt America. And this is the kind of stuff, this is the kind of ways that civil wars start. And I know that sounds probably a little histrionic and a little excuse me over the top, but I, I don't know that... you want to disenfranchise people? But my... the value of where I live and the house I live in, I have been told by different real estate agents that it doesn't really matter what happens one way or the other, it's going to be safe because people will always need to live here because they work on the Capitol Hill, they work in the White House. The people who are going to make the regulations in this country - or I'm sorry, in this state - that are living in Northern Virginia, in places like Arlington, those people don't understand the everyday struggles of the people living further out in the state. The farmers, the people living on minimum wage, the people who are not wealthy bureaucrats living off the government. I don't understand how Fairfax County is going to be the only voice that matters in this state. And it's, it's a really bad precedent and my fear is Republicans are going to get angry, Democrats are going to get angry, and this is going to spread like a contagion to every state in the country so at a certain point things will be gerrymandered within an inch of its life so nobody's vote matters. And that ladies and gentlemen is how you get that movie Civil War with Kirsten Dunst happening. And I'm telling you, this is bad and it's dangerous and I can't believe I'm living in a state where it happens and I I want to move, I want to move to a state where my voice will matter. No offense, like I just I really would rather not move to Florida. Everybody keeps saying that, but at a certain point we just can't keep isolating ourselves. I went off on a tangent. Well, it just won't matter and I really think that is the type of thing that will make people just lose their minds understandably. And I don't understand how again on the left and the right... I was very surprised to see like Gavin Newsom celebrating this. I was surprised to see President Obama endorsing this because he'd been anti-gerrymandering for his entire presidency. I don't know, I've just I've been really disappointed in in so many things regarding this. And I think last night watching the polling come in, I was watching Steve Kornacki, who is still like one of my favorite people to watch on election nights. The best. Yeah, with his like sleeves rolled up. I, I don't know what the answer is. What do you think the answer is Ray? I think I'm a big fan of independent redistricting commissions. Now of course nothing's ever independent, there're going to be politics in there. But I really think that that's the way. And I believe there are nine states in the country that already have those commissions or a total of 16 that have kind of like a commission/quasi-commission, so it's been done. It tends to be successful. I've written about that quite a bit in the past. Politicians hate it. The Republicans here in Ohio hate it, Democrats in New York hate it because it's all about them keeping power. So egregious here in Ohio that the oh I forget what... the Senate president, who by the way has gerrymandered here in Ohio to the point where even though it's like 55-45, Ohio now has a veto-proof majority in both chambers. So and he came out and said, "Look," and I'm glad he said it because you love this honesty, he said, "We can do whatever we want now. It doesn't matter." I can find the quote for anybody that doubts that's the quote, I can find it and send it to them. I believe you, Ray. Democrats will now control in Virginia 90% of districts in a state that they only won by 5%. And it's... I want everyone to just think of that, like. Yes, a great data point right there. All right, without further ado, we're very, very, very excited to have here Senator John Fetterman who needs no introduction but he is the Senator from the great state of Pennsylvania, author of the book Unfettered and you can find him at @SenatorFettermanPA and John Fetterman on social media. Thank you so much for coming on Senator Fetterman, we are so excited to have you today. Oh it's great to be here. Hi, hello. Hello. Really quickly, I don't know if you know this, but I actually had the privilege and pleasure to go to your hometown of Braddock and interview your lovely wife Gisele last year. Did you know I did that? Did I know that? Yeah, sure, of course. Yeah, I mean she... yeah. She said just... I mean I'm not surprised, she said just great things and had a a great opportunity to just get to sit down with you, yeah absolutely. Your hometown is lovely. It's really beautiful, I really enjoyed my time there. I found it like very quaint and gorgeous. I just want to ask you, Gisele I just found to be so smart and warm and lovely and very different than many Senators' wives that I have gotten a chance to meet. Can you tell me a little bit about your love story when you first met her and how you guys keep your marriage thriving and happy in such an intense world and with you being a famous sitting Senator? Oh there... well there's a lot to to unpack for that. No .. she saw an article that that featured me and and our town and then she decided to to visit and then that really was that start of that conversation. That was back in 2007. So a lot's a lot's changed since then and now in June, that would be 18 years and then .. so yeah. I mean things... a lot's changed, our nation's changed a lot, you know our lives have changed a lot. You know, I'd never been married and then 2007 now I'm you know married for 18 years and I have three kids and and now so so a lot's... so politics and how divisive things are hasn't made it easier for political marriages in in that sense. So that's .. yeah. You're... Yeah, I you know my my throwaway line is I I don't give people advice except on fashion, otherwise on on everything else. That's totally fine. You know, you're saying I got to meet your youngest son. You're raising three kids. What advice do you give to them like every day because again it's it's not easy having a famous dad, I know a few things about that. It's not easy when they're sitting in the Senate. How do you... do you have conversations with them about just like the divided world we're in? And do you have any parenting advice for not only people with famous parents but people raising teenagers? I met your youngest son and he was he was really sweet and very polite. Yeah no, I'm incredibly I'm incredibly proud of of the kids. And I've I've always told them stay off social media. You know like one of my... one of the things I might have regret as a parent is it's like allowing them to even... You know, I try to have like we all have parents have a choice is like make it the forbidden fruit or do you just allow them to just experience it and they might discover the right the right conclusion. So I've had two really made the right conclusion and then I had, you know, one where you know I don't think their lives were were harmed or made less happy or or productive so so that I I wonder if I made the right things by allowing them to experience that that thing. So so my advice has been pretty consistent. And thankfully now she's emerged from that place where that was becoming a really negative impact on her life. and now I'm I'm partnering up with Katie Britt, a Republican you know from from... I love her, yeah. Yes, to start the "Stop the Scroll" for American parents and now if you had a friend that's spending hours and hours and hours with your child, you want to know about about the kid and you want to know about his parents. You want to know where they live and you want to know their circumstances because that that best friend is social media and I mean kids are spending way way too much time in that and that's it's really entirely appropriate I think we have a responsibility as a Senator to provide as a parent and understand exactly what's at and social media it is shaping society with without a doubt. And we need to make it most safe and more responsible to make it sure because kids are going to be a part of it and find a way to do it. You know you brought up Katie Britt, I know you have friends on both sides of the aisle. I really like Senator Katie Britt, I think she's really smart and you know just total rockstar. When you do things like presenting bills in a bipartisan way with people on the other side of the aisle, you know there's pushback. We're living in such a divided time of great partisanship. What why have you decided to sort of buck the trends of both parties to be a more bipartisan, you know, Senator? You obviously have some beliefs that are more, you, know, swayed on the other side. Has that been difficult? I find it really refreshing, but has that been difficult for you? Yeah, I mean it's just becoming I mean I it's a great quote that I'll butcher it but they said if if one person from one side and one person on the other side won't agree to work together to make a third person's lives better, then you're doing it wrong. And and so why wouldn't you want to work with the other side? That doesn't mean you agree on everything. That's the way this democracy was designed. It's here in the Senate. One time the party's the majority, one time what one party is the majority, vice vice-versa. That thing is going to change. And now unfortunately if you just represent a very very blue or or red state, that that conversation is just to one side. Now in Pennsylvania, as a very the most purple state, that's that's my mandate. You know I don't just represent 13 million Democrats, I represent 13 all Pennsylvanians, 13 million that are our Republicans and Democrats and independents and everything in between of all of that and that's I think that's an honor. But I also think that's the most difficult kind of a job in in politics to find a way forward as things become more toxic to just agree with the other side and now that's that's where I'm at. You know you've been very open about your mental health and you do a lot with mental health awareness, even what you were just talking about with "Stop the Scroll" was about mental health. There was a time when it was like sort of taboo to talk about mental health in politics. I can't remember which vice presidential candidate, but there was a candidate for president who it was found out that he had seen a therapist and it basically like torpedoed his entire campaign, I apologize I can't remember who it is off the top of my head, it might have been Eagleton. How... what made you decide to be so open about your struggles with depression and just you know your mental health in general because it is something that maybe political analysts would have told you not necessarily to be so open about? Oh, I mean yeah. I'm not sure if it's still a good a political win to talk about MH and and ultimately the the ultimate S-word, suicide. But like that's what's appropriate if if you're in that space and I decided to do it because it's such an important conversation and there's an epidemic here in our nation. 50,000 Americans last year took their lives. And that's the ultimate tragedy. And for for me, I was I got lucky. You know I got to overcome depression. And before I ever really experienced true true depression, I mean this this "Oh I can't get out of bed," you just think that's an expression. That's not true, that's really the experience where you just can't get out of bed. And and then when you get into this mode where where your mind convinces you that you are the problem and you know the best solution is to take yourself out. And you start to think oh maybe that's more and more reasonable. And now these other people in your life would be convinced they'd better without you too. That's the that's how dangerous it is. That's lethal and that leaves people behind and they can't understand why they've done that. So I've had that conversation and I beg people, I beg people. I don't know what resources you have, I don't know what your path is or that put you in this dark dark state. But if you stay in that game and you promise yourself that you don't take yourself out, I keep it as simple as it possible, but the way you stay in it that puts you back on the path. Every single person that's survived a serious suicide attempt, they immediately just my god what have I done. You know the second that they crossed the rail on the bridge, they scream out like "I want to live, I want to live." and if they survived that jump, that's spontaneously curative and they realize like I want to live, I want to live. Something that you can't take back. So and I find myself I never I never took I never took any actions, but I seriously seriously considered it and every single day it's affirming why I thank God, thank God I didn't and I got lucky. And I was able to realize hey and for me that emergency brake was my kids. You know my kids. Actually they I just discovered that they really wanted me to stay around and they love dad. And so that was emergency brake. Some people may not have emergency brake, have your own emergency brake. And that's says "I won't take myself out, I'm going to promise myself I'm not going to do that." There there is a way out. And it's not a tomorrow, it's not next week necessarily. Stay in it and now I'm a testament right now and it got worse for me after I won, after I won. And that's part of the paradox. It doesn't mean you have to have a win or things seem good on surface. There's there's a lot of other issues. So as a if a US Senator that just won, yeah. It's an important conversation. No, I I really appreciate that and I thought it was so brave for you to be so vulnerable with so much of that. We've all been in dark times of different variations. When you talked about your your experience with suicide, I my brother had a very good friend that he was in the Marines with and I we all spent 2013 New Year's together and then a few days later he put a gun in his mouth and blew his head off. And he was a veteran who was dealing very seriously with P... I did not know the degree and neither did anyone else. Very seriously with PTSD when he came back from Iraq. I don't know if you saw this, but Joe Rogan went to the White House a few days ago on Sunday to talk about mental health awareness and also the use of psychedelics. My friend Dakota Meyer who is a Congressional Medal of Honor winner is a proponent of these psychedelics. He said it really helped him with his PTSD that he had very severely when he returned from Iraq and Afghanistan. I can show the clip if you want of Joe Rogan, but do you have any feeling about the fast tracking of the FDA of these studies like Ibogaine and these psychedelics that President Trump and Joe Rogan are saying are helpful? Yeah, go ahead, sorry. Oh yeah, that that that's fantastic. Absolutely. And I've actually been been pushing that years years ago. Back when I was Lieutenant Governor, you know I was I decided to do town halls all across every single 67 counties in Pennsylvania. No matter how small. Pennsylvania has a gigantic counties like Philadelphia with with millions people and our smallest county has 5,000 people. But I went to every single one of those counties talking about legalizing marijuana. And consistently in every single meeting was veterans coming and they were begging it's like can we just legalize this. You know for me, you know I I need legal safe and I don't want to be turned into a criminal just to using those kind of I can get countless pills from the VA but the one thing that I need what I really want is is as I use cannabis. And also part of psychedelics, mushrooms. Pennsylvania is the single biggest mushroom producer in the entire world. And I thought what an amazing opportunity, you know like why can't we just make this more part of the research and part of the solution. I have had veterans sitting in these town halls saying "This is what I need, this is the one thing, you know, it doesn't make me better but it allows me to be okay enough for that." So why can't we just do that? And so when I saw that announcement, that's fantastic 100%. That's been a long conversation. I haven't I haven't used either one as but I don't ever judge anyone whatever helps knock the edge off and allows them to make him you know I support anything whatever path is to making feel better. So that's been important. And I was originally called a a pothead or a weirdo having that conversation. And then in the White House and in the Oval Office, that happened what I've been talking now gee seven years ago here. Like it's an important conversation and this is a great development 100%. Yeah I know quite a few veterans who have done this. And I know people who when I was struggling with my grief after my dad died of brain cancer, a lot of people suggested this to me. And I'm sort of like a goody two-shoes, I don't even drink, so I was like "this is too much for me, I don't know." But it's interesting that it's been... the people I know who've done it have done it in other countries, like one friend did it in Costa Rica. So I too am happy to see to see this, you know, develop. When it comes to, I know there's a huge veteran population in the state of Pennsylvania. The story I just told you, you know there's still a huge suicide rate I believe it's like 1,000 a day. How... I still one of the things that I'm I'm worried about, I'm not not a supporter of the war in Iran - like I believe what President Trump is doing is is understandable and I hate Iran - but I do worry about talking to people in the military because I don't think we've gone done a good job when they return, particularly Iraq and Afghanistan, returned home, taking care of them. What do you think can be done from your perspective and in your position to to quell those anxieties and to help the men and women in our armed forces that I think have been really left behind? Well, I mean first I mean I think whatever the statistics it's 18, 19 every day veterans take their lives. Every every day. And that's heart... absolutely heartbreaking. I can't imagine anything more tragic. So I've been an advocate to to talk about this. It's an ugly conversation because what's really behind it, I mean how how awful that is. You know you've cited friends that they've taken their lives. I've had friends, the one had a stroke and then the other one had a heart attack and they both survived that. And they both had young children and then they took their life. And that just was devastating because their children can't possibly understand that. Supporting our veterans, that's part of that. And I was thrilled thrilled... people don't realize how that really started. That was made possible partisan bipartisan. Rick Perry in in Texas, you know as a Republican the former Governor in Texas, he just had met enough veterans to say "Let's let's normalize this conversation about psychedelics." And I had in my home actually the the Texas representative was a Democrat, they were part of that bill for there too. So supporting veterans, that's very very necessary. I'm proud to say you you got a chance to be in my home. A block away from my home, there's there's veterans' housing there to make more and more of those kind of resources of available. So my support about the war in Iran comes about standing firmly with with Israel. And what's really necessary Iran, right. Iran, I think every single president since '79 has had to confront Iran. This is the fine opportunity to address Iran to create maybe more enduring kinds of peace in that region. Can I ask you about your support of Israel? Sometimes Senator I feel like the last Zionist on the planet. Like I feel my social media... I know you don't look at yours, sometimes I look at mine... it is just a barrage of antisemitism and people calling me a genocide enabler and that I like want babies to die and all the disgusting things that people say. I've been a supporter of Israel, I am a supporter of Israel, I will be a supporter of Israel, you are as well. But it's lonely out here. I mean every day that the data is showing that people not only on the left on your side but on the right as well the support of Israel is is going down almost monthly. Why do you think that's happening and what can be done to help convince people of of our perspective in a in a more effective way? Well, that's what history demonstrates that whenever whenever the Jewish community becomes a a political liability or or thing, you'll turn their back on that and then you seem to you excuse the kind of violence and that ends badly. I mean that's what history that's the lesson from history and that's why I'm never going to be on the wrong side. So I'll never turn my back to the the Jewish community and I'm proud to stand with Israel. I mean today's Independence Day, you know when the day when we're filming this. That's a testament and how necessary what a miracle Israel is what's happened that was the necessity to to found Israel first. And it's it gets more and more unpopular because there's a lot of organized kinds of organizing. Social media, that's another part of it too. I mean the entire young younger generation's gone. They're gone. Gone. Yeah, 80% of Democrats have a negative view on Israel. You know that that puts me in the 20% as a Democrat and 4% of Democrats support military against Iran. And and I'm part of that too. I'm not dumb, I know how to read you know polls and I know how toxic it is as a Democrat to support Israel. And and now they are they are moving the Overton window right now, it's deliberate, they're moving that Overton window. Now it's going to make like J-Street that made it okay as a Democrat to vote to not support supporting Iron Dome. And now you're making it more and more normalized. Now right now today you know I saw they the New York Times put Hasan Piker into a a sewing circle, you know at the New York Times, you know conversation about Netflix and just like a regular kind of chit-chat. A guy that is absolutely loves Hamas. A guy that that describes about the rape hoax and all this. And America deserved 9/11 saying some of the most extreme things. And it's normalizing people with these extreme kinds of views. That's that's where our Democratic Party's going. Now same on the New York Times today you know you have talk about praising you know Plattner, the guy with a Nazi tattoo. I know, I know. Yeah and a guy that you know what what kind of a ghoul looks online and celebrates the the beating and torture and death of Israeli soldiers. It's like well that's going to be the Democratic nominee in Maine. And then let's look what happens in in Maine too. a guy that described that Israel is as evil as Hamas is compares them to as as the same. So that's where the Democratic Party it's not just where it's going, it's actually where it is for. What happens in 2028 at the Democrat Convention or Republican by the way, because again this is a problem on my side as well? You know, whoever... Yeah but can I just Meghan the problem though you know you know who now like the Democratic Party we love mascots. You know now like MTG, now so now we love MTG. I know, I know. And now we're celebrating you know Tucker Carlson, you know like now we're citing Tucker Carlson. You know, Alex Jones is back here. You know, you know these people are being you know being embraced by the Democrats because now because now we're criticizing Israel and we're criticizing you know Trump. So that's part of where our party's going. And thankfully at least within the Republican side, it seems to be localized in in for those kinds of people and those personalities like in Candace Owens. But now we we love them now we we can't get enough about them on the Democratic side. But I'm sorry to interrupt, but I wanted to get that off. Interrupt any time you want. No you're right. And the thing that's so scary and sad for me is it's the unification like they're bonding over how much they hate Jewish people and how much they hate Israel. And like nothing else matters. Tucker Carlson is, you know, one of the long-time right-wing analysts for my entire lifetime who has very extreme views on many things that most Democrats would think are extreme but because he hates Israel so much, all of a sudden he's being embraced by the hard left. I'm really worried about this in general and like I said you keep seeing so many politicians who are like, "I'm not taking money from AIPAC and I you know I won't support Israel." I don't know if the Democrats are capable of nominating anyone who support who even like in a in a mild way has some kind of just doesn't hate Israel. Do you think there's a pathway for anyone to be the next presidential candidate for Democrats that is not just a someone who has just absolute revulsion about Israel? Because I can't see it and I I don't know what happens anymore because the majority of independents and center-right/center-left people still are supportive of Israel. I I don't I don't think so honestly. The spirit the spirit of the party lives in in the elections right now and in the primaries. Where is the single primary, whether it's in the House or in the Senate where there is a proud Israel supporter that's that's winning? You know in Michigan, they actually booed they booed one off the stage. You know, even has a reasonable view on you know she she was booed off the stage in the middle the Michigan Democratic convention there. So in every single primary, it's getting more and more extreme anti-anti-Israel, openly hostile, aggressively aggressively anti-Israel. So that's only going to accelerate honestly at this point. Do you enjoy being a Senator? Well, it's it's an absolute honor. I mean you know Pennsylvania picked me to be the voice and so I I I think they they elected me to be to play play it straight. And you know some things I agree, some things I don't, but but that's it's easy it's easy if I represented it like a state like Connecticut, I can just yell yell yell yell and I I know now people you know Democrats are literally they have campaign campaign campaign commercials saying "F*** Trump." You know like honestly like again and again and again. That was the winning that that won in in Illinois. I mean that that's that's what pays the bills. And I'm not going to engage in that. I'm not going to continue to degrade the the tone of the conversation. That doesn't mean I agree with things that are happening on the other side. Absolutely not. But but what it does mean we have to find a way forward, a better way to do it and and that's a responsibility. So yeah, I mean it's an honor and and... so. Do I like being the job? I really want to answer that. It's like I I do I do enjoy the job. It is an honor. but but overall right how dysfunctional it's becoming and how frustrating well our DHS is still shut down. And and now we're here so but I'm grateful to have this conversation absolutely. I just have one more serious question and then can we have a little fun because there's we should have fun. Do you do you have do you like Team Vance or Team Rubio if you had to choose? It you mean on the Republican side? Yeah, because there's a showdown about who Republicans are going to probably nominate vice president Vance or Secretary Rubio. Oh well I mean I I think I think overall that that I've been I've appreciated Secretary Rubio without without a doubt. And Rubio has never been willing to kind of pander to the to the ugliest side of of the Republican side just the way I refuse to pander to entertain some of the very ugly parts of of my party as well too. We're all... I think I think it's funny because it absolutely true I've seen that on you know he he's sitting on the couch and then they keep they keep putting him in different part whatever. I mean I think I think people realize that he's the like the go-to guy when when important things need to happen, you know they can kind of turn him to handle it in in the appropriate way when things really really really matter like that. We were very curious, there's a rumor on the internet you have a Nine Inch Nails tattoo. Is that true? And have you gotten any new tattoos in the past few years or since becoming Senator? I haven't gotten any new ink for a long time. And you know like but I promise you there are no Nazi ones 100%. You know and you know it's like so but maybe maybe if I did have a Nazi tattoo, the New York Times would explain why that's perfectly normal. And just what's the big what's the big what's the big deal. So I mean actually if you I know if you see my wife she can ask about she can yeah she can ask about her her new ink. but for for me that's .. I mean I I was listening about redistricting you know too and you know that that I think yes it's a Democratic win what happened in Virginia but but overall we all lose, we all lose on this. The second that we we gerrymandered people out of relevance our democracy is damaged there. I understand the logic to do that what happened in Virginia what started in Texas. But you know after after this America loses and Pennsylvania is the ultimate example of that. After after the Democrats got gerrymandered out of it, you know Pennsylvania went 13 Republicans to five Democrats. And then our Supreme Court actually said "No that's that's that's not democracy," and they put very very fair districts. And we immediately the next election in 2018 that went to nine-nine, nine-nine. That represents a very you know purple purple state. So I I think it's sad, Texas sad, Virginia sad, California sad, all these other states that I mean that that's always wrong and that means that everyone loses and that's not the true voice of their states, that's not the true voice. I only want the true voice. And if that true voice says they want Fetterman out, I I revere democracy. I you know I don't ever want us to to stack the deck you know because that's not real democracy. You know democracy made me the voice if if the democracy says John's the wrong Democrat or for whatever reason, I'll respect that too. But what it's described as a win, a win. That that that's sad because America and democracy loses the second that we start stacking the deck in those ways. I completely agree. Are you going to the White House Correspondents' Dinner this weekend? Well, you can ask you can ask Gisele if she wants to or not. You're like I might be, it depends on whatever my wife tells me to do, that's what I'll be doing. Yeah exactly whatever the boss says, but but what I will say if I do go I'd be the worst dressed you know there for sure. Well, last question we had for you actually came from Twitter. Someone wants to know who is your favorite hoodie manufacturer? I don't even know if there's a method to your hoodies or how you get dressed, but do you have a favorite? I really don't think much about it. It's like if it fits, you know if there's a great you know that has great 4XLT like okay I want you more like I'm not an off-the-rack kind of a guy and so that's there's no statement, there's no more insights into how I dress. It's just become more, you know, comfort and what works, and then that's—that's why I'm saying my only advice for fashion is, you know, even to my own kids: please don't—don't dress like your dad, for sure. Well, I think that it's good to be comfortable and I think that some of the fanciest dressers are the biggest assholes, so I'm all for you wearing a hoodie any time you want. Senator Fetterman, I know you're incredibly busy and I just want to thank you so much for taking time today. It's a pleasure meeting you and, like I said, your wife is just—you can tell so much about a man by the woman he chooses and she's just really a spectacular woman and we had such a nice time getting to know each other in person. And I just want to thank you and wish you best of luck and please come back any time. Well, thank you for this conversation. It is a pleasure and also thank you giving a chance how much I've always admired your—your father. Thank you. So in a small, small way I try to be the same kind of independent voice that's always—always going to put the country over party. Thank you for having me on. I love it. Thank you. See you soon. All right, we are going to switch gears just really quick. I have an ad, right? Just really quick, Miranda, 'cause we got to keep the lights on here in my tiny little baby studio. Thank you so much to Senator Fetterman. You guys didn't have any questions. Okay. Support for the show comes from VCX, the public ticker for private tech. For generations, American companies have moved the world forward through their ingenuity and determination. And for generations, everyday Americans could be a part of that journey through perhaps the greatest innovation of all: the US stock market. It didn't matter whether you were a factory worker in Detroit or a farmer in Omaha, anyone could own a piece of the great American companies. But now that's changed. Today our most innovative companies are staying private rather than going public. The result is that everyday Americans are excluded from investing and getting left further behind while a select few reap all the benefits. That's until now. Introducing VCX, the public ticker for private tech. Now available wherever you buy stocks. VCX by Fundrise gives everyone the opportunity to invest in the next generation of innovation, including the companies leading the AI revolution, space exploration, defense tech and more. Visit getvcx.com for more information. That's getvcx.com. Carefully consider the investment material before investing, including objectives, risk, charges and expenses. This and other information can be found in the fund's prospectus at getvcx.com. This is a paid sponsorship. Thank you very much, thank you very much for our wonderful sponsors. Miranda, do we want try and bring Ray back in, by the way, just to end our conversation on gerrymandering if he's still here? I don't know if he's here, but I think—I mean, I think we did it. Never mind, he popped off. Is there anything else you wanted to say about Virginia, Miranda, before we—before we say goodbye? I'm sad for the people of Virginia who are sad today. I hope that this doesn't come back to bite y'all in the ass because, you know, Virginia's very important. It's an—it's an important state in our union. And you know, the only people it's going to hurt are people like y'all who are just everyday folks. So we'll have to wait and see. But you know, FAFO and—and talk about a referendum, they're going to see what happens next time around. What was really fun about not being able to participate in the Fetterman interview was A) getting to watch you... no, I'm—I'm so not trying to say that in a—in a nasty way. I got to be in the chat and that was so much fun, is engaging with everybody and communicating about what he was talking about. And you know, the big feeling was everything he had to say was so interesting and fascinating, but what really resonated with the audience was his very open and honest communication about mental health. His—his struggles, and everybody in the chat including myself, we were talking about our own struggles with mental health and depression and anxiety. Especially in these times that we're living in now with financial uncertainty and, you know, turmoil abroad and just the political strife and it's never been more divisive... I mean possibly it has but in—at least in our lifetimes, it's never felt more divisive. It was very, very refreshing to hear a politician, but a man—an adult man—speak so openly about their feelings and in a way that was very sincere and heartwarming and so I just—I really just truly appreciate him coming on. I don't want to say that he's brave because I mean obviously it's brave, but also this is a conversation that needs to be normalized. I think that keeping it in the shadows... I had a conversation with someone in our YouTube stream about, you know, they were talking about Robin Williams and Anthony Bourdain taking their lives and how selfish it was. And I was like, you just don't get it. It's like, you know, if you've never been depressed you don't understand that that's how much you hate yourself. It's like you are in a hole so deep that you think that the people that depend on you—your loved ones, your friends, your family, your children—are better off without you. And that's how bad it is. So it's like we need this—this conversation needs to be had way more frequently and way, way more into the nitty-gritty. But I mean he's—he's a delight. What a lovely man. Gosh, I wish we could have him on all the time because I want to hear him talk. He—he should have a podcast. I mean he's... but he's got a real job. He has a real job. I agree and I think I remember when he first started talking about the fact that he had thought—considered—he—I think he had suicidal ideations and being in that deep dark place. I—like I said, I believe it's Eagleton—there is someone who ran for president and they found out that they were going to see a therapist and it just nuked everything. Like, people didn't trust him, they thought he was like basically mentally ill and a crazy person. Bury the Sopranos. And to go, yeah, to go from that to Senator Fetterman is obviously a big—a big change. I applaud him as well. I think he is an iconoclast and I think he is an original. The thing I worry about, like I saw he was talking about Hasan Piker, I've seen—I've seen other very left-wing insane analysts talking about how John Fetterman's like his biggest disappointment. And all of these—the thing I worry about is just, you know, there are no—there's like no space in our party for anyone in the middle. And we do it to ourselves on the right too, so I hope he's in office for an extremely long time but I worry about what the Democratic apparatus is going to do to him. And like I said we brought up the '28 convention. Is there going to be a place for him to speak and be heard at the '28 convention? Is he going to be a figure that will be embraced? Like, you know, we have our demons on the right but the left have I think like, you know, arguably more challenging ones. And just becoming a Senator just like—just like becoming a celebrity, running for president or becoming the president, that should come with a personal therapist. Like that is a level of life and achievement and power and access that is so limited and unfathomable that the human brain can't even comprehend it. It's like, you know, you should have someone that can keep you grounded and in—in reality and—and that you can discuss these complex feelings about that with openly. I—the fact that being so self-aware that you have are like "I need to talk to someone about this," having an advisor is okay but seeing a counselor is weak? Bullshit. I think that is so stupid. It's just so stupid. Anyway, I—I agree. But these are tough conversations though. But I'm glad they're being had and like I said I don't know a lot about these psychedelics but a lot of veterans I know really stand by it and say that it has really helped them a lot. Again, Dakota Meyer being at the top of the list, the Congressional Medal of Honor winner, our friend. So. I have some anecdotal evidence on—I have an anecdotal story if you want. If you're trying to get out of here we can get out of here. But I mean it's helped a family member of mine get their spark back. Someone who was anorexic in my family—not obviously not me, ugh, problem problems to have. But not me. Someone who was anorexic, someone who could not get out of bed, they were so depressed and their anti-depressants stopped working. And so I got them some medicinal shrooms, psilocybin, legal in California, and totally changed their life. They were able to get out of bed, got them their happy, they brought their joy back and brought their appetite back and they're able to enjoy their life again. And you know, they do it for a couple of months at a time and re—kind of resets their brain and then they stop taking them. And when they start—they feel themselves go back into that dark place, they start taking the psilocybin again. So you know there's something to be said and it's natural. This is a natural plant. You're not, you know, shoving yourself full of chemicals, not that there's anything wrong with that either, but you know for people who are uncomfortable with, you know, taking some kind of medication or pharmaceutical, this is an alternative that maybe it is worth looking into. I agree. And like I said, I'm not—I don't know, I really like John Fetterman and I feel so—I felt weird like trashing Democrats at the top of the show because there's obviously that's not all—we're not all a monolith. But I was really happy to have him on and I'm sorry if my energy is a little, you know, sort of saccharine right now. I just—I'm really, really, really sad about what's happening in Virginia and I'm really disappointed. And there's, like I said, the choices that are made are going to have real-life ramifications very quickly and very directly at my everyday life and anyone else living here. And again, if you're a Democrat who wants to pay more in taxes and have no crime bills... And like, you know, there are certain Democrat policies I just will never understand as long as I live. I will never understand. Like even I was listening to Spencer Pratt on Joe Rogan and it's like, I just don't understand why it is illegal to be homeless, it is illegal to do drugs on the street and there's no policies getting the homeless people off the street and doing drugs. And then the homeless people are testing fentanyl on dogs and killing them on the street and that's still not enough to get people in. And I'm sorry to like shit on California, I know you love California. I do love California but I mean I'm not going to—I'm not going to say that California like other every state doesn't... there are—there are problems. I was shocked to see that fentanyl and dogs being ostensibly drug testing kit meals or whatever. That was horrifying to me. I had no idea that that was happening. Skid Row, when you—Skid Row is tiny. It's not this like big section of Los Angeles, it's a—like a very, very small section of downtown LA where it is very far from where I live. Like LA is humongous. So that's far from where I live, I don't—I don't experience downtown LA. So to hear that that was happening... I have a soft spot for animals, I think most people do, animals should not be—humans, nobody should be treated that way. It's horrific. And to see that there are volunteers bravely going in—and I see Elena picking up her little pet, her little sweet baby—to see that there are volunteers going in there to help those people. You know, people on Skid Row. We've seen Soft White Underbelly the YouTube show though, yeah, it's intense. Those people are wildcards; you never know what you're going to get when you go down there. So I applaud those people and I want to look up their charity and maybe, you know, fork over some cash for them to—to help safeguard that because that's a—that's a worthy cause. But that would never happen in Florida and I'm—the thing I'm worried about is despite the fact that I'm living in a purple state that's 50% Republican and 50% Democrat... It's funny you said that because literally as you said that someone commented "Homeless people doing that in Florida too." It's just interest—literally the exact same time you said that. I mean, there—it's not in the same degree. I mean I'm sure there's home- I mean homeless people aren't absolved everywhere but they don't have the same kind of level of problem as in California. Regardless, red states do not—we are—we are the party of Mayor Giuliani. There's just a zero tolerance for some of these things if a Republican governor or Republican you know Senator, whatever, is running things. And again, Democrats can have an existential conversation at another time where they are more okay with watching—that's just one example. Again, there's lots of different policies that I'm very fearful of and COVID was really an extreme time for me in this state when I just saw how extreme Democrats can get. And what they will allow and what they will do and the fact that like this CVS that I worked by—that poor CVS was bashed in and bashed in and bashed in and looted and looted. And I just was like those people working there and the people who own that—I don't know how CVS works but like the manager—like it's just every day. And it was in the—you know, people were saying it's fine because nothing else worked because of, you know, racism, so we have to resort to violence. And any time you have to resort to violence, that's what terrorists do. And I just know how extreme it can get here. I lived through it and I'm very fearful it will start happening again in another incarnation of itself. And I'm really heartbroken that Democrats that are my literally my neighbors that I go to school—my go to my kids' school—they are comfortable and okay with an attorney general who wants to put bullets in my and my kids' heads. And so like again I'm not exactly sure how that man is not in jail, it's just yeah. I don't know exactly where Ben and I are landing right this second, but we are like for the first time... like I'm talking to a real estate agent later today in another state. Wow. I wish you didn't hate California so much because the town that I live in is purple. I live—I happen to live in like the only purple city in LA County. So it's very, very, very mixed and diverse and a lot of varying opinions where I happen to be. I'm not moving to California. By the way, we're looking at two states. One of them is obviously Arizona. But the problem with Arizona is it's like so blue, like it's still like it's... you know, we have two blue Senators and until Arizona stops nominating nut- nutball lunatic assholes like Kari Lake it's going to continue being blue. I don't think Ruben... I don't think Ruben Gallego's long for this world of politics so you know I'll let you know where we all land. I'll let you know but I got—like I said—I almost cried last night. I was so upset and like I just—it is what it is. But one thing—one thing that'll happen is you do these policies, you have these taxes, people will leave. They will leave. Look what's happening in California, they're all moving to Texas and Florida. I just think like—I think I ostensibly live in California now, which is fucking crazy because I've tried to safeguard my life so much in so many different ways not to. And the voters have spoken. Better go somewhere that's not like a six-hour flight away for me because our girls are just dying to play. They had—just you probably can surmise the two places we're thinking about because it's where all the Republicans move to. I already know where you're going: Texas or Arizona. Texas or Arizona, yeah. So at the moment I can't move... oh can we please, I'm begging you, can we please Baggin' Darling please? I don't have it, Ashley... We don't have it, we'll do it Monday. Sorry. It's fine. It's fine. We'll do it on Monday, it's no big deal. Thank you guys so much, we went a little long today. Thank you to Senator Fetterman, thank you Miranda, thank you everyone and we'll see you again Monday. Honestly don't know who our guest is Monday, I apologize. But you know what? You just had a solid week of incredible guests, so what more could you want? You might just get Meghan and me yapping, so. Thank you. All right, I like that. See you guys on Monday. Thank you guys. Bye!

The billionaires' utopia blueprint | Throughline

NPR Podcasts(118.0K) ·54:11 ·526 views · 2026-04-23
Elon Musk (Founder, SpaceX and Tesla) Peter Thiel (Co-founder, PayPal and Palantir)
Modern tech billionaires are attempting to bypass traditional nation-state governance by creating "startup nations" and private jurisdictions, evolving from historical precedents like Svalbard to modern experiments like Próspera.
TL;DR Modern tech billionaires are attempting to bypass traditional nation-state governance by creating "startup nations" and private jurisdictions, evolving from historical precedents like Svalbard to modern experiments like Próspera. This movement prioritizes "exit" from...

TL;DR

Modern tech billionaires are attempting to bypass traditional nation-state governance by creating "startup nations" and private jurisdictions, evolving from historical precedents like Svalbard to modern experiments like Próspera. This movement prioritizes "exit" from existing regulations in favor of corporate optimization, often leading to significant conflicts with national sovereignty.

The Historical Precedent: Svalbard and "Terra Nullius"

  • The Polar Emperor: In 1901, American businessman John Munro Longyear established a private coal-mining settlement on Svalbard, treating the uninhabited Arctic archipelago as a "terra nullius" or legal no-man’s land.
      • Corporate Governance: Longyear created a company town with its own shops and dorms, essentially acting as a "polar emperor" until labor disputes and property conflicts necessitated state intervention.
      • The Guano Act: Longyear attempted to use the 1856 US Guano Act—which allowed the US to claim unoccupied islands containing bird droppings—to protect his property rights, though the government declined.
  • Open Borders Legacy: The 1919 Treaty of Versailles eventually granted sovereignty to Norway but mandated that the borders remain open for international business, a unique status that persists today.
  • Modern Fragility: Author Atossa Araxia Abrahamian suggests the current nation-state model is a "fragile blip," noting that Norway is now moving to reassert tighter control over Svalbard as the Arctic becomes a contested site for resources and satellite data.

Seasteading: "Homesteading the High Seas"

  • The Libertarian Vision: In the late 1990s, engineer Wayne Gramlich and Patri Friedman (grandson of Milton Friedman) collaborated to design permanent dwellings at sea, free from the laws of existing nations.
      • The "Exit" Strategy: The project was fueled by a desire for competition in the "market" of governance, seeking to let "a thousand nations bloom" on the ocean.
      • Peter Thiel’s Influence: Thiel provided $500,000 in seed funding for the Seasteading Institute in 2008, viewing technology as a way to "unilaterally change the world" without political interference.
  • Engineering vs. Reality: While the dream of "Apple Nation"—a country as intuitive as an iPad—captured Silicon Valley’s imagination, the movement struggled with the harsh physical realities of the ocean.
      • Current seasteading efforts have scaled down from massive floating cities to single-unit, self-sufficient eco-homes in places like Panama and Mississippi.

The Rise of the "Network State"

  • Startup Nations: Former Coinbase CTO Balaji Srinivasan popularized the concept of "The Network State," where digitally connected communities build power online before manifesting as physical "startup nations" on leased or purchased land.
      • "Tech Zionism": This philosophy focuses on choosing one’s neighbors and laws, often categorizing outsiders as "the blues" (traditionalists) and insiders as "the grays" (tech-aligned).
  • Private Fiefdoms: Tech elites are moving from maritime fantasies to literal land-based projects, including:
      • California Forever: A billionaire-backed plan to build a new city on 50,000 acres of California farmland.
      • Starbase: Elon Musk’s private city in Texas, designed as a waypoint for Mars colonization.
      • Freedom Cities: A proposal to build privately funded, deregulated cities on federal land.

Case Study: Próspera and the Sovereignty Conflict

  • The Honduran Experiment: Próspera is a private "charter city" on the island of Roatán, operating with its own laws, low taxes, and a corporate board of directors rather than a traditional government.
      • Investment: The project is backed by high-profile investors including Peter Thiel, Marc Andreessen, and Sam Altman.
      • Legal Autonomy: Próspera operates without oversight from the Honduran government, offering a "menu" of regulations for businesses and using cryptocurrency as its preferred tender.
  • Sovereignty Backlash: After a shift in Honduran leadership, the government repealed the law allowing charter cities, declaring Próspera an illegal settlement.
      • The $10.7 Billion Lawsuit: In response, Próspera is suing Honduras in an international tribunal for a sum equal to roughly 25% of the country’s annual GDP.
      • The Colonial Irony: Critics, including journalist Jacob Silverman, argue these projects are less about "utopia" and more about "tech colonialism," leveraging the wealth of the developed world to coerce smaller nations.

Notable Quotes

"I can perceive nothing but a certain conspiracy of rich men procuring their own commodities under the name and title of the nation." — Raphael Hythloday (Character in Thomas More's *Utopia*)
"The nation state model... is both very new and very fragile and might just be a blip... If the rules are being rewritten, who gets to rewrite them?" — Atossa Araxia Abrahamian, Author of *The Hidden Globe*
"I don’t think that Elon Musk is saying we’re going to create this perfect society... The focus really is on how can we make business work better, how can we cut through red tape. That’s not utopian unless you live in a society of corporations." — Jacob Silverman, Tech Journalist
"Starting new countries is actually possible, preferable, and profitable." — Throughline Narration regarding the "Network State" philosophy

There is never any shortage of horrible creatures who prey on human beings, snatch away their food, or devour whole populations. But examples of wise social planning are not so easy to find. It’s the year 1516. We’re inside the pages of a book called Utopia, breathing the fictional air of Antwerp, Belgium. The Utopians fail to understand why anyone should be so fascinated by the dull gleam of a tiny bit of stone when he has all the stars in the sky to look at. An old sun-burned, long-bearded traveler named Raphael Hythloday has just returned to Europe after spending five years on an island called Utopia. And he’s seeing the world with new eyes, ranting to anyone who will listen. "I can perceive nothing but a certain conspiracy of rich men procuring their own commodities under the name and title of the nation." He rants about corrupt leaders, absurd laws, and the enclosure system, in which so-called landlords fence off lands belonging to villagers, turning them into their personal fiefdoms, all for the sake of profit. What a contrast to the island of Utopia, he reminisces, where every man has a right to everything. Gold is used for chamber pots. Private property isn’t a thing. Everyone wears the exact same colorless clothes and works six-hour days. Everyone has his eye upon you, and all movement is perfectly regulated. If any man goes out of the city to which he belongs without leave, he is taken for a fugitive and severely punished. And if he does this often, he is condemned to slavery. The author of this book, Thomas More, invented the word Utopia. It’s a Greek pun combining outopos, "no place," and eutopos, "good place." It asks in a tongue-in-cheek way: is a perfect society possible, or is the fantasy just a mirror held up to reality, and a chance to change it? I mean, I hate to sound like a utopian tech bro here, but if you'll excuse me for three seconds... You know, these are things that are going to save lives. We can make the world amazing, more profound than fire or electricity or anything that we have done in the past. Over the last few decades, the tech sphere has thrived on the urge to optimize everything, including utopia. If we can just solve for x here and invent for y there, we can build the perfect society. Perfect for who? That’s a different story. You would prefer the human race to endure, right? You're hesitant. Yeah, well. From private cities—California Forever is building the next great American city—to interstellar colonies... You could absolutely colonize the whole galaxy. Tech billionaires like Elon Musk and Peter Thiel have backed experiments designed to operate beyond the borders and laws most of us live by. Starting new countries is actually possible, preferable, and profitable. So we were curious: Has anyone tried it? Has this fantasy of exit, of opting out of the rules and building a new world, been put to the test? I’m Rand Abdel-Fattah. On this episode of Throughline from NPR, we’ll take you from a forgotten Arctic archipelago... the only place in the world with open borders... To floating cities in the ocean... out in the middle of nowhere, on your own... to private startup nations that might be coming to some land near you. Is this going to be a little private fiefdom run by these venture capitalists? What do they really want? Hi, this is Emil Hartz from Denver, and you’re listening to Throughline from NPR.
