Just go to linkedin.com/nprpod. Terms and conditions apply. This message comes from Sony Pictures Classics with I Swear, starring Robert Aramayo. The extraordinary true story of John Davidson and his journey toward acceptance while living with a misunderstood condition in 1980s Britain. In theaters April 24th. Part 1: A Weird World. We’re told all the time that you have a certain number of countries in the world, that they all have borders surrounding them, and that’s kind of the architecture of the political world and of the geographic world. But it turns out there’s a lot more to it than that. This is Atossa Araxia Abrahamian. I’m the author of two books: The Cosmopolites, which is about the global market for citizenship, and The Hidden Globe, how wealth hacks the world. Atossa has spent the last couple of decades traveling and studying the world with a skeptical eye, observing its hidden architecture, which she describes as a jumble of weird jurisdictions. Lots of people will find themselves in a weird jurisdiction at some point in their life. You might be at a border checkpoint, you might be working in a factory that’s in a special economic zone, you might be on a ship that’s flying a flag that you don’t totally recognize. Or you might find yourself sailing by a small, very frozen archipelago called Svalbard. Svalbard is a northern territory of Norway in the Arctic Circle. Okay, let’s be real. You probably won’t find yourself there anytime soon. And honestly, I hadn’t even heard of Svalbard before talking to Atossa. But you’ve likely heard of Svalbard’s neighbor, Greenland, which has been a hot topic lately. President Donald Trump reasserted in the new year that the United States wants Greenland. "We are going to do something on Greenland whether they like it or not." Like Greenland, Svalbard is involved in the race for the Arctic. Being near the North Pole makes it an ideal place to track missiles flying across the planet and download data from satellites. New shipping routes are buried under the ice that climate change is rapidly melting. And buried in the ocean floor are a bunch of mineral resources: copper, zinc, cobalt, lithium, and rare earth elements used in all kinds of technology. But there’s also something that makes Svalbard weird. Svalbard is the only place in the world with open borders. Open borders. Svalbard is part of the Kingdom of Norway, but everyone from Indian climate scientists to Russian coal miners to Thai hikers are welcome. No visa required. Some might call that a fantasy, others a nightmare. But definitely weird. And the story of how Svalbard ended up that way gives us a window into how the world of nations and passports came to be. About 8 AM, land came in sight. Steep, rocky crags and the peaks covered or streaked with snow. It was a grandly desolate, sublime, weird landscape, utterly barren and unlike anything I had ever seen. The sun seemed to be boring holes through the clouds. In 1901, an American businessman named John Munro Longyear stumbled across Svalbard while on a tourist cruise with his family. Longyear had built a huge timber and mining business in northern Michigan. This was a man who legend has it could smell coal. He’d go somewhere and he could just smell the coal; he knew where it was. Call it a sniffed sense. Sorry, I had to. I went to look at his archives in Marquette, Michigan, very far north. And I was immediately struck by how similarly Marquette in the winters smelled like Svalbard. Quick context: Svalbard, being so cold and so far north, was uninhabited pretty much until the Europeans discovered it in the late 16th century. And by the time Longyear came along a few hundred years later, Svalbard still had no permanent population. It was terra nullius, a legal no man’s land. A rare thing to find by this time because of industrialization and colonialism. People knew there was coal there, but previous efforts to get it had been abandoned. Longyear, though, was up for the challenge. The enterprise of developing a new and practically unknown coal field within 800 miles of the North Pole was an interesting and satisfactory experiment. He sets up a settlement, names it Longyearbyen after himself, and starts the Arctic Coal Company. People have said that he thought of himself as a polar emperor, which gives you a glimpse into his mindset and the kind of animating philosophy behind these things. For a few years, he could live in this fantasy, slowly building a new little world on his terms. He’s creating a company town. There’s a shop; the laborers can only shop at the shop, they can only sleep at the dorms. Good luck finding another housing out there. But it turns out building a new society was hard. There were many difficult and unusual problems. There were two kinds of conflicts that took place. One was between the management and the workers. John Munro Longyear and the local guys that he hired didn't get along. They didn’t like the food; he didn’t like how lazy they were. A classic tale. Hundreds of laboring men speaking a foreign language were not always amenable to discipline. Strikes were instituted by disaffected socialistic leaders. And the other conflict was between him and other people like him, other people who were trying to start businesses and mine coal. And these were essentially disputes about property: who owns what, who can go where, who planted the stake first. It got a little messy. But Svalbard had no courts, no police, no property law. There was no authority to really rule on these things. It seemed only a matter of time before some nation or empire would claim sovereignty over Svalbard and threaten his business. Longyear wanted to get ahead of that. So he reached out to his country of origin, the United States, and lobbied the government to get involved to protect his property rights in Svalbard. He did so under an older law called the US Guano Act that allowed the US to claim unoccupied islands in the Pacific that contained large amounts of guano—bird shit, bird droppings. No joke, this is a real thing. This act from 1856 says that if an American citizen finds enough guano on an island not yet claimed by another country or empire, the US president could choose to use military force to claim sovereignty there. Why guano? Because it’s a great fertilizer, necessary for maintaining food production at a time when synthetic fertilizers didn’t yet exist. And it was also used to produce an ingredient for explosives. It was considered so valuable, it got the nickname "white gold." US citizens invoked the Guano Act to claim over a hundred islands around the globe. And John Munro Longyear tried his luck with it in Svalbard. He argued that it should be expanded to include not just guano, but also coal and other minerals. He had a lobbyist, he had a guy on K Street, and he was even showing up to hotel lobbies to try to talk delegates into taking his side. The US government ultimately decided not to intervene. And then, in 1914, reality came knocking. The war made it quite difficult to export coal. When World War I broke out, his company’s shipping and trade ground to a halt. The clock was ticking, right? And in 1916, Longyear sold his company’s assets to a Norwegian coal mining company. In order to promote international cooperation and to achieve international peace and security... agree to this covenant of the League of Nations. On June 28, 1919, in France’s Hall of Mirrors, the Treaty of Versailles was signed, formally ending World War I. The treaty accelerated a shift that was already underway, moving the world from the age of empires to the age of nation states. And it established the League of Nations, an international organization designed to maintain world peace through diplomacy. The powers were meeting to kind of divvy up what was left of the world. This included convening a conference on "passport and customs formalities" to create a uniform 32-page booklet—a passport—that would be required to travel across borders. There wasn’t really a place for a place with no ruler. And so Svalbard was formally kind of bestowed upon Norway by the international community. Why? Because Norway had been a good ally during the war and it had the biggest presence on Svalbard, including a company that until a few years earlier had been owned by John Munro Longyear. The treaty also carved out an exception for other corporate interests in Svalbard, keeping its borders open for business. It is a pleasure to know that Svalbard, though now under the flag of Norway, is forever dedicated to the arts of peace. Svalbard wasn’t a utopia, but over time it did come to represent a place of global cooperation. 75 boxes of seeds were carried down a red carpet today on a Norwegian island in the Arctic Ocean, headed for cold storage. Really cold storage. Since 2008, Svalbard has even housed a large post-apocalyptic seed vault meant to safeguard the planet’s food crops if the worst ever happens. Some call it a doomsday vault, others a Noah’s Ark for global agriculture. This is the most valuable natural resource in the world. So in other words, as long as we intend to be on Earth ourselves, we’re going to need this diversity. Tonight, we’re heading north, way north, to the Arctic Circle. The Arctic is fast becoming one of the most contested regions in the world. And that means Beijing, Moscow, Washington, and the European continent are in a race for influence. Recently, with the race for the Arctic heating up, Norway has begun pushing more firmly to assert its sovereignty. They are cracking down on land sales to foreigners, stripping away foreigners’ voting rights, limiting scientific research, and claiming hundreds of miles of Svalbard seas. Maybe they’re seeing the writing on the wall, that the world order might be shifting again. The nation state model, I think if we take a thousand-foot view from it, is both very new and very fragile and might just be a blip. The question is, if the rules are being rewritten, who gets to rewrite them? Svalbard is a story about men who want to start something new in a place that they consider almost a blank slate. I think there’s a lot of parallels with somebody like Elon Musk who wants to explore space. And this kind of awareness that none of the rules are all that fixed. If you just try hard enough, maybe the rules will bend to your own desires. Coming up... it was like a message in a bottle, you pop the cork and you throw it out into the sea of the internet and see what happens. We take to the high seas. Hi, my name is Tim Berry, I’m calling from Charleston, South Carolina, and you’re listening to Throughline from NPR. This message comes from BetterHelp. As a dad, BetterHelp President Fernando Madeira relates to needing flexibility when it comes to scheduling therapy. "I have kids under eighteen, so like, time is very limited. That’s why at BetterHelp, our therapists try to have sessions sometimes at night depending on the therapist, or during the weekend." This message comes from Capella University. That spark you feel, that’s your drive for more. Capella University’s FlexPath learning format lets you earn your degree at your pace. Learn more at capella.edu. Part 2: Let a Thousand Nations Bloom. What makes a man to wander? What makes a man to roam? What makes a man leave bed and board and turn his back on home? These are the opening lines of the 1956 movie starring John Wayne called The Searchers. The music feels almost wistful, reveling in the adventurous spirit that pushed so many to head out into the frontier. The movie follows Wayne’s character, a Civil War vet who fought on the side of the Confederacy, after he returns home to Texas. And like any good western, there are long panoramic shots of the vast landscape. It’s set just a few years after the 1862 Homestead Act passed, when anyone moving out West could claim land if they were willing to settle on and farm it. What was it like when people were colonizing the West, setting out on their own to build a ranch and stuff like that? This is Wayne Gramlich. He’s a retired computer engineer. And he remembers watching The Searchers back in the 1990s, a time when he was finding himself spending more and more hours on the newly minted World Wide Web. He stumbled across a fascinating trend: stories of people who, in the 60s and 70s, attempted to build micronations at sea. All of which pretty much failed. "Well, this is weird, but okay." He found himself imagining what those attempts might have been like and considering how he would try to build a new nation in the middle of the sea. I was just sort of noodling around in the back of my brain. Scenes from The Searchers flashed through his mind. He thought maybe it would feel a lot like the Wild West, only wetter, with one big difference. "I don’t want to be political, but boy, we really treated the Native Americans really poorly." So I thought it was a positive endeavor largely because, unlike the colonization of the West where there were previous occupants, in the ocean the only previous occupants are the fish. So on a whim, he sat down at his computer and started typing. In the paper, Wayne brainstormed all kinds of engineering hacks to different problems he foresaw coming up. How do you make the structures safer? Maybe we recycle these 2-liter bottles. Hoping to avoid the pitfalls that had sunk those previous micronation attempts, like how to survive the elements. You can extract energy from waves... huge waves, relentless sun... Just the difference in temperature of the surface water. Wayne called the paper "Seasteading: Homesteading the High Seas." To capture some of the romance of, you know, manifest destiny. It reads like an instruction manual, very much seen through an engineer’s eye. He didn’t talk much about the more dicey political stuff, like how you’d deal with pirates or how you’d get the nation recognized by the UN. He figured it was just a thought experiment. Back then, I was just publishing everything I did on the internet, like, why not? In 1998, he uploads his paper to the web. And for a while, nothing much happens. Three years later, in 2001... "You've got mail." I got contacted by this guy named Patri Friedman. That was the beginning of the next phase of the story. I remember he said that he just eventually just joined the family business. Journalist Atossa Araxia Abrahamian has interviewed Patri Friedman, the grandson of free-market pioneer Milton Friedman, a number of times. Atossa says Patri came to seasteading from an economics angle. It was a way to create more nation states in the world, to create competition and have better ideas and kind of evolve from our land-bound system of governance. And we had a few back and forths and then I think I asked him, like, by the way, where are you? He says, well, I currently live in Sunnyvale, California. So, well, guess what, so do I. I said let’s get together for lunch. Can you take me to that first meeting? Because it’s almost like an intellectual blind date, right? Oh, it absolutely is an intellectual blind date. And they hit it off. Wayne learned that Patri had studied mathematics and computer science. And they were both excited about seasteading. They started meeting up periodically, revising that instruction manual Wayne had drafted, getting to know each other along the way. Occasionally he would tell me stories of grandma and grandpa talking about economics. This is a family who’s very much into the libertarian movement. And I’m not really into the libertarian movement. The libertarian movement generally believes in individual liberty above all else. And Patri saw something very libertarian in the seasteading idea. Experimentation was something that they talked about a lot. And Patri really wanted to make that experiment a reality. From that first email, he’d said to Wayne: I would really like to build one of these things. I’m going, like, oh, that’s a lot of work. And it would require a lot of money. Money they didn’t have. Then the solution fell into their laps. It was 2007. Patri was interviewing for jobs at different companies in Silicon Valley. He did apply for a job at Founders Fund, which is Peter Thiel’s fund. Peter Thiel, the don of what’s become known as the "PayPal Mafia." The companies that have defined our era all share one link. Their founders trace their origins back to PayPal. Reporting about the PayPal Mafia can sound like a who’s who of every major tech company of the past 30 years. Peter Thiel started a hedge fund; they were like the earliest angel investor into Facebook. In the 2000s, Thiel was pushing the idea that technology was an alternative to politics that could, quote, "unilaterally change the world." His biographer described him as "secretly the most important person in Silicon Valley." Whether we’re going to have a much better future or not, I think it gets driven by the rate of technological progress. We reached out to Peter Thiel for this episode, but got no response. Before he leaves, Patri casually brings up seasteading. And the idea eventually makes its way to Peter Thiel. That is just nirvana to the libertarian movement, a place where you can set up a libertarian society via seasteads. And so Peter said, well, what if I give you half a million to promote the idea and push it forward? So on April 15, 2008, they co-founded the Seasteading Institute, with funding from Peter Thiel. Patri liked to say, "let a thousand nations bloom." And their logo seemed to reference the libertarian classic Ayn Rand’s Atlas Shrugged: a man holding up the seastead above his head. The science fiction things are very interesting because so few people are doing them. There is something about that that is contrarian, fundamental, it’s not being done enough of. So anyhow, money was allocated. Peter Thiel gave them two marching orders: One, push the engineering forward. And two, market it a little. "So our love for the oceans has brought us together today to embark on a short journey into the unknown." Patri took the lead, giving TED Talks, doing the press junket, spreading the gospel of seasteading. "We’ve run out of frontier. All land is claimed. And our revolutions have become increasingly superficial." I’m going to read you a quote from a story I did more than 10 years ago, where Friedman said, "What if Apple’s genius designers build a city that’s as fun to use as an iPad? Apple Nation, the country that knows what you want even better than you do." And I think that really sums up both the moment and the sort of optimism around seasteading at the time. While saving humanity, we can also save the oceans. The seasteading pitch was pointing out some real problems with existing governments: things like corruption, increasing federal power, and slow-moving bureaucracy that were making it harder to respond to real social problems. And a lot of people responded positively. It was time to give it a go. And then the question was whether or not we were going to do what we call the SFS, single family seastead, or a larger one. They debated that question at the offices of one of Thiel’s biggest companies, where they had a small space, a data analytics firm called Palantir. They didn’t give us a room; they gave us a nook. Palantir might sound familiar for two reasons. The company has recently attracted a lot of controversy for its close, secretive work with government intelligence, defense, and immigration agencies. And it’s the name of the seeing stones in Lord of the Rings. Originally created by the elves, it was meant to be used for good purposes. It is a potentially very dangerous technology; it’s very powerful. Anyway, back in the mid-2000s, when Wayne was working on seasteading in a nook of their offices, Palantir wasn’t on most people’s radars yet. Wayne remembers one crucial meeting where he and Patri met with Joe Lonsdale. Joe Lonsdale was the co-founder of Palantir and Peter Thiel’s business partner. They were deciding how ambitious their first seasteading attempt should be. I voted small and Joe and Patri voted large, and that’s the way we went. But just as they started to put the wheels in motion to actually try to build something, life intervened. "Let’s talk about the speed with which we are watching this market deteriorate. The stock market is now down 21%... 43%." Wayne had been living off some internet stocks that were doing great until the 2008 recession hit. "I had to go find another job. Which meant less time for seasteading. So I was still on the board, but I had said I can’t spend nearly as much time on it." I asked him if the co-opting of seasteading by libertarians played any role in his decision to step away. "You’re pulling me into the politics." Which he told me he wanted to avoid discussing. He said he’d always been most interested in the engineering. It was never really my intent to get involved in the politics and so, sorry, you’re just not going to find a very political answer out of me. My general view is, sometimes you’re walking along the road and you just need to pick up a stone and you just need to move it a little further down the road and the next person picks it up and moves it a little further. Patri Friedman continued full force with seasteading for a few more years after Wayne left. The closest he got to building a seastead was Ephemerisle, also known as Burning Man on the water. It’s billed as a floating celebration of community, learning, art, and seasteading. In 2011, Patri stepped down from the Seasteading Institute, as did Peter Thiel. Since then, there have been attempts to build a seastead island. One project involved an agreement with French Polynesia. But public concern over "tech colonialism" led the local government to cut ties with the Institute. These days, most projects are focused on building single-unit, self-sufficient floating homes. Seastead projects are underway worldwide, including in Panama, South Korea, and even right here in the US, in Florida and Mississippi. I view large seasteading as a complete failure, but the small stuff is happening. And it helped bring a fringe libertarian dream of exit more into the mainstream. Though Wayne says: "If you’re going to do this libertarian stuff, you might want to see if you can just find someone who will loan you some dirt to do it on." Part 3: The Cities of Tomorrow. Ladies and gentlemen, Ramtin would like to welcome you to Honduras. Local time is PM. Please remain seated. In June 2023, Dan Girma, a producer on NPR’s Embedded podcast, took a trip to the Honduran island of Roatán. Mangrove trees line tons of the coastline and you have pretty beaches on either side. But Dan wasn’t there for that. He drove to the northern side of the hills, to a place isolated from the rest of the island. A place called Próspera. There is a big sign, a big Próspera sign, once you get to that part. Then it kind of just cascades right into the sea. Próspera is kind of an experiment in what the future of cities could look like. Almost everything in Próspera happens without any oversight from the Honduran government. It offers companies operating there a menu of laws and regulations. There’s no FDA, no HHS, taxes are low, and crypto is a preferred currency. Over $150 million have been invested in Próspera by venture funds affiliated with tech titans like Peter Thiel, Marc Andreessen, OpenAI’s Sam Altman, and former Coinbase CTO Balaji Srinivasan. "Google was started within a garage within our lifetime... Bitcoin was started from a white paper within our lifetime... could we start new countries?" Próspera is a real-world case study for a growing movement to create so-called startup nations. The spiritual guide of this business movement is a book by Balaji Srinivasan. It’s called The Network State: How to Start a New Country. It outlines a vision of digitally connected, exclusive communities that design states online first. The idea is somehow they’ll find land, push out the people that they don’t want, who they call "the blues," and keep the people that they do want, who he calls "the grays," then lobby existing governments for sovereignty. Srinivasan calls this approach "Tech Zionism." A reference to the movement that led to the creation of the State of Israel. To me, Tech Zionism only really says one thing, which is that we only want to live with other Tech Zionists. "Well, who are the 'Tech Palestinians' in this situation?" I don’t think that’s in the book. I think for a long time tech considered itself sort of searching for new frontiers and in recent years, they’re starting to look for literal frontiers. By the way, this is journalist Jacob Silverman. He writes a lot about the tech industry, focusing on the intersection of tech and politics. Rather than reform or change existing institutions, a lot of tech elites want to create their own alternatives. There are all kinds of network state-type projects being imagined right now, abroad and within our own borders. Exiting the system is no longer a fringe or weird idea. Starbase, Elon Musk’s city in Texas, was created to build a path to Mars. The billionaire-backed California Forever project is planning a new city on 50,000 acres of farmland on the edge of Silicon Valley. And President Trump has proposed building so-called Freedom Cities. "We should hold a contest to charter up to 10 new cities built on federal land but privately funded and free from traditional regulations, environmental laws, and labor unions." These projects are expensive and most are still in the digital design phase. Which brings us back to Próspera, a place that does exist. There was a very long back and forth with the Próspera people about coming in as a journalist. It took Dan Girma almost a year to get permission to visit. Their version of a government is like a board of directors. And in the meantime, he was digging into how this place ended up in Honduras. What I ended up finding out over time was that Próspera was born under geopolitical turmoil. "The coup has left Honduras deeply polarized." This is around 2008, 2009. Honduras had just undergone a coup. "It’s an example for all the other countries. Don’t let yourself be overrun by tyrants." Honduras was trying to find a way to be let back into the global community, trying to find new ways to develop its economy. "Are there some rules we can develop for changing rules?" While that’s happening, there’s this idea of something called a charter city. A Nobel Prize-winning American economist named Paul Romer came up with the idea. The idea was to have a more successful country lease an empty tract of land. The Honduran government learned about this man and his ideas and they got interested in what he was proposing. Romer was eventually sidelined, and Honduran lawmakers opted for a slightly different proposal. Instead of another country administering the land, a private corporation would. It was an attractive idea for Honduras, which had long been open to private investment. This goes back all the way to the idea of banana republics. Honduras is the first nation to be labeled one of those and it’s always been a very capitalism-minded country. But plenty of people objected. Would it basically act as a state within their state? Still, in 2013, a law green-lighting charter cities was passed. The Supreme Court of Honduras deemed it unconstitutional. But afterwards, the Honduran Congress ousted four members of the Honduran Supreme Court. On top of this, there was a lack of transparency which didn’t sit well with Paul Romer. The tech billionaire backers funding Próspera were pushing the project in a direction he wasn’t comfortable with, becoming a "libertarian fantasy." But the project continued without him. In 2017, Honduras Prospera Inc. purchased its first plot of land: 58 acres that bordered a small local fishing village whose residents say they were not properly consulted. Over the years, Próspera has come to own more than a thousand acres. Some local landowners protested. Not all residents have formal property titles and they fear their land claims are being undermined. Amid that, construction got underway on new housing and research facilities. Próspera has its own labor systems, which aren’t clearly spelled out. When one worker died in an accident on the job, the details were not made public. And that’s by design. The point of a place like Próspera is that there isn’t really a public to answer to. It creates a particularly core irony when we think about Próspera as a libertarian hope. In other words, it’s not pure exit in the classic libertarian mold. It’s using the system’s power to exit while shaping the system for others. Critics of Próspera say it echoes colonial dynamics familiar to Roatán. In April 2022, after a new leftist government took power in Honduras, the Charter City Law was repealed. And then the Honduran government goes a step further and declares that the previous law is null and void. Próspera then sued the Honduran government in an international tribunal, seeking a massive amount of money: $10.7 billion. For context, that’s about a quarter of Honduras’ annual GDP. The tech investors backing Próspera are collectively worth much more than that. And they have the backing of the country with the most powerful military on earth. How is this not coercion? The case is still ongoing, the future of Próspera hanging in the balance. Based on what Dan saw when he finally got to visit last year, development seems to have slowed down. A lot of it is virtual. There were some residential buildings, a Montessori school, and a few research facilities working on out-of-the-box medical experiments like gene therapy meant to cure aging. But not many people. I’ve spoken to some people who are working on charter cities outside of Honduras. When I speak to them about Próspera, they describe it as a learning experience for this movement, not so much the model they want to replicate. I think Próspera best reflects some naked truths about the power that the developed world has right now and the tools at its disposal to maintain its power. It is kind of the ideological groundwork for these efforts. The Utopians fail to understand why anyone should be so fascinated by the dull gleam of a tiny bit of stone when he has all the stars in the sky to look at. When Thomas More wrote Utopia, designing a perfect society was an allegory. Today, technology is making attempts to reshape reality and create versions of so-called network states more possible than ever. The impact of this kind of thinking can be seen all around us. The people designing these cities might not care if their choices lead to utopia or dystopia for the rest of us, because the cities aren’t necessarily for us. I don’t think Elon Musk is saying we’re going to create this perfect society on the Moon. The focus really is on how can we make business work better, how can we cut through red tape. That’s not utopian unless you live in a society of corporations. That’s it for this week’s show. I’m Rand Abdel-Fattah and you’ve been listening to Throughline from NPR. Next week on the show, how reality became something you could edit and sell. Throughline was created by me and Ramtin Arablouei. This episode was produced by me and Julie Caine, Casey Miner, Cristina Kim, Devon Katayama, Irene Noguchi, Keana Moghadam, Thomas Lu, Sarah Wyman. Fact-checking for this episode was done by Kevin Vocal. Special thanks to Holly Baxter, senior staff writer at The Independent, and Rachel Corbett. Thanks also to Tom Nickelson, Johannes Dürgi, Dylan Kurtz, Rebecca Farrar, Liana Simstrom, Julia Redpath, Beth Donovan, Yolanda Sangweni, and Tommy Evans. This episode was mixed by Jimmy Keeley. Music for this episode was composed by Ramtin and his band Drop Electric, which includes Navid Marvi, Sho Fujiwara, Anya Mizani. Finally, if you have an idea or like something you heard on the show, please write us at throughline@npr.org. And if you’re open to us giving you a call back, leave your number too. We might feature your idea in an upcoming episode. Also, make sure to follow us on Apple, Spotify, or the NPR app. That way, you’ll never miss an episode. Thanks for listening. This message comes from MIDI Health. Co-founders Dr. Kathleen Jordan and CEO Joanna Strober discuss why they started a virtual care platform for women in perimenopause and menopause. "The symptoms and experiences that women have in midlife, I think were underappreciated or possibly even trivialized. Too few clinicians are trained in it. We’ve tried to create a new type of care system." MIDI Health, committed to helping women in midlife with perimenopause and menopause care, accessible via telehealth visits at joinmidi.com. This message comes from Capella University. That spark you feel, that’s your drive for more. Capella University’s FlexPath learning format lets you earn your degree at your pace, without putting life on pause. Learn more at capella.edu. This message comes from Jerry. Are you tired of your car insurance rate going up even with a clean driving record? That’s why there’s Jerry, your proactive insurance assistant. Jerry compares rates side-by-side from over 50 top insurers and helps you switch with ease. Jerry even tracks market rates and alerts you when it’s best to shop. No spam calls, no hidden fees. Drivers who save with Jerry could save over $1,300 a year. Switch with confidence. Download the Jerry app or visit jerry.ai/npr today.

BREAKING: Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick Testifies Before The House Appropriations Committee

Forbes Breaking News(5.5M) ·82:05 ·5.8K views · 2026-04-23
Howard Lutnick (Commerce Secretary)
Secretary Howard Lutnick defended a $9.2 billion FY 2027 budget request that proposes a 16.5% spending cut, largely through the elimination of domestic grant programs (EDA, MBDA, MEP), while seeking historic funding increases for trade enforcement and export controls to counter China.
TL;DR Secretary Howard Lutnick defended a $9.2 billion FY 2027 budget request that proposes a 16.5% spending cut, largely through the elimination of domestic grant programs (EDA, MBDA, MEP), while seeking historic funding increases for trade enforcement and export controls to...

TL;DR

Secretary Howard Lutnick defended a $9.2 billion FY 2027 budget request that proposes a 16.5% spending cut, largely through the elimination of domestic grant programs (EDA, MBDA, MEP), while seeking historic funding increases for trade enforcement and export controls to counter China. The hearing featured intense disputes over the efficacy of "America First" tariffs, the administration's handling of the National Weather Service, and Lutnick’s alleged personal ties to Jeffrey Epstein.

FY 2027 Budget Strategy and Domestic Program Eliminations

  • Overall Funding Reduction: The department is requesting $9.2 billion, a $1.8 billion (16.5%) decrease from the current level.
  • Proposed Agency Eliminations:
      • Economic Development Administration (EDA): The budget seeks to zero out EDA grants and claw back $63 million in previously approved funding.
      • Minority Business Development Agency (MBDA): Lutnick proposed eliminating the agency, citing a "brutal" Inspector General report claiming 92% of awards were improperly made.
      • Manufacturing Extension Partnership (MEP): The administration seeks to defund this program, which supports small and medium-sized manufacturers, claiming the execution is "broken."
  • Budget Increases:
      • Bureau of Industry and Security (BIS): A $215 million increase to double the size of the bureau and hire hundreds of law enforcement agents.
      • NOAA Shipbuilding: A $135 million increase for shipbuilding and unmanned systems.
      • Trade Enforcement: A $10 million increase for the International Trade Administration (ITA).

Trade Policy and Economic Impact

  • Tariff Performance: Secretary Lutnick touted a 16-year low in the trade deficit for 2025 and a 6% increase in exports, claiming trillions in capital investment are "pouring into America."
  • Manufacturing Jobs Debate:
      • The Administration’s View: Lutnick highlighted massive semiconductor investments (Micron: $200B, TSMC: $165B) and predicted a manufacturing "golden age" once factories currently under construction are completed.
      • The Opposition's View: Rep. DeLauro cited Bureau of Labor Statistics data showing the U.S. lost over 88,000 manufacturing jobs (100 per day) between February 2025 and February 2026, alongside a $1,700 annual increase in food costs for families due to tariffs.
  • Global Trade Deals: Lutnick noted recent deals with Korea, Taiwan, Japan ($550B), and a major investment deal with the European Union.

Infrastructure, Technology, and Energy

  • BEAD Program Savings: Secretary Lutnick claimed the administration saved $21 billion (50% of the original $42B budget) for the Broadband Equity Access and Deployment (BEAD) program by abandoning a "fiber-only" model in favor of technology neutrality (satellite and wireless).
  • Export Controls: The department is using expanded authority at BIS to prevent sensitive chips and software from reaching China and other adversaries. Lutnick emphasized that BIS law enforcement agents "pay for themselves" through fines that generate 3x to 10x their cost in revenue.
  • Modernization of NOAA: Lutnick announced the transition of NOAA’s IT systems from legacy hardwired technology to a modern distributed cloud to improve weather forecasting accuracy.
  • Digital Fraud: The department expressed interest in exploring stronger authentication standards for foreign digital traffic to combat AI-driven phishing and "pig butchering" scams.

Oversight, Ethics, and Mismanagement Allegations

  • Jeffrey Epstein Ties: Ranking Member Meng and Rep. Dean questioned Lutnick regarding the "Epstein files," which allegedly show a 2012 visit to Epstein's private island and a 2018 business deal (Adfin), contradicting Lutnick’s previous claim of cutting ties in 2005. Lutnick declined to answer specifically, stating he would address these questions in a separate session with the House Oversight Committee.
  • Conflicts of Interest: Rep. Meng noted a *New York Times* investigation into the intersection of the Commerce Department’s activities and the financial interests of Lutnick’s own family.
  • National Weather Service (NWS): Critics alleged the department fired frontline meteorologists despite previous testimony to the contrary, and pointed to a failure to backfill essential positions.
  • Responsiveness Issues: Committee members criticized the department for late spend plans and a 10-month delay in providing answers to written questions for the record (QFRs).
"These [BIS] agents are massively economically and intellectually important to the United States... These agents bring between three and 10 times their revenue annually in fines." — Secretary Howard Lutnick
"The President announced he was imposing a 10% tariff on almost every country in the world, including a group of volcanic islands that are populated not by people but by penguins. This would be amusing if not for the fact that this... increased the cost of food for American families by $1,700." — Rep. Rosa DeLauro
"We have executed what you wanted to do exactly right for $21 billion, and we have $21 billion remaining... It was crazy town [before]. You had some people who had a $150,000 fiber run to a house that was worth $100,000." — Secretary Howard Lutnick
"The Secretary's dishonesty causes me to question the truthfulness of other statements he has made to Congress on the record. The American people deserve honesty from those with the privilege of holding public office." — Rep. Grace Meng
This verbatim transcript covers the proceedings of the House Appropriations Committee hearing on April 23, 2026. The subcommittee will be in order. Without objection, the chair is authorized to declare a recess at any time. I would like to welcome everyone to this hearing. I also want to welcome a new member to the Appropriations Committee and to CJS, Representative Jefferson Shreve. Welcome, sir. Thank you, Mr. Chairman. I know he will be a strong voice for Indiana on the committee. First one in the last—is it 40 years? 30. Yeah. And we look forward to working together. Likewise. Thank you to our subcommittee members and to Secretary Lutnick for joining us to discuss the Department of Commerce fiscal year 2027 budget request. The request for the Department of Commerce totals $9.2 billion, a $1.8 billion or 16.5% decrease from the current level. The request reiterates and builds on the budget the President submitted to Congress last year. He seeks to reinforce and expand on efforts to counter the strongarm trade tactics of China and refocuses the department's trade activities on investments that create jobs in America and protect national security. The Department of Commerce has a wide-ranging portfolio. It must level an international trade landscape tilted against America, foster economic growth, conduct weather research and response, and produce statistics about our nation and economy. These responsibilities are bound together by the common goal of ensuring American prosperity. Its investments and resources are invaluable to rural communities like my district in Eastern Kentucky. This ranges from NOAA's weather forecasters, who provide severe weather warnings when seconds count, to EDA's essential work to grow economic opportunity and the BEAD program's expansion of high-speed, reliable broadband to rural areas, opening opportunities and improving lives. The department's vital mission requires strong leadership and strong oversight. I would like to note that the committee only just this week received the department's complete fiscal year 2026 spend plans and detailed budget request documents for fiscal year 2027. Additionally, the department must ensure response to written questions from subcommittee members. Secretary Lutnick, these areas of communication and responsiveness should be a priority of yours. With that said, I look forward to hearing how this budget request furthers our shared goals. Let me recognize now the ranking member of the subcommittee, Ms. Meng. Thank you, Mr. Chairman, and I want to join you in welcoming Secretary Lutnick back to this subcommittee and also welcome our new member on the subcommittee, Mr. Shreve. At a time when American families are struggling with a high cost of gas, groceries, housing, and other essentials, the Trump administration is proposing a commerce budget that would make things worse. The fiscal year 2027 budget request would hurt American jobs, small businesses, and economically distressed communities by completely eliminating Economic Development Administration grants and gutting the ITA's efforts to expand US exports. The administration is once again proposing to zero out the Manufacturing Extension Partnership program, which helps small and medium-sized US manufacturers create and preserve US manufacturing jobs. It also is again proposing to defund the Minority Business Development Agency after attempting to illegally shut it down. And the administration is proposing to rescind all of the remaining funding for the Digital Equity Program, which Congress appropriated on a bipartisan basis to help veterans, seniors, rural communities, and people with disabilities afford and make full use of high-speed internet. The American economy and hardworking Americans are getting squeezed. Yet, at the same time, Secretary Lutnick's own family has greatly benefited from many of the deals that have been arranged by the Commerce Department over the past year. According to a New York Times investigation in November, never in modern US history has the office of the Secretary of Commerce intersected so broadly and deeply with the financial interests of the Commerce Secretary's own family. Furthermore, the Commerce Department has been plagued by mismanagement over the past year, beginning with the damage done by Elon Musk's DOGE. Last year, Secretary Lutnick, you told this subcommittee that no Weather Service meteorologists were fired, when in fact many meteorologists were fired. We learned in a hearing before this subcommittee last month that the department and the National Weather Service still have not backfilled all of the National Weather Service's frontline staff positions that were so needlessly lost last year. The department's responsiveness, or lack thereof, to this committee has also been concerning. More than 10 months after our subcommittee submitted questions for the record following the Secretary's last appearance, we still have received no answers. The department also failed to submit its fiscal year 2026 spend plan in accordance with the legal deadline, as all of the spend plans were four or more weeks late. We only this week received the spend plans for the department's largest bureau, NOAA, and for the Minority Business Development Agency, which were legally due to our Appropriations Committee a month and a half ago. The department's fiscal year 2027 budget justification documents were also late in being publicly released. All of these delays are completely unacceptable and obstruct our committee's ability to conduct oversight and to make informed decisions about the federal budget on behalf of the American people. Finally, I was disturbed to learn from disclosures in the Epstein files that Secretary Lutnick had personal and business interactions with Jeffrey Epstein for years after Epstein was registered as a sex offender. Secretary Lutnick previously claimed that he cut ties with Mr. Epstein in 2005, but the Epstein files show the Secretary took a trip to Epstein's private island in 2012, four years after Epstein's conviction for solicitation of prostitution from a minor. The Secretary's dishonesty causes me to question the truthfulness of other statements he has made to Congress on the record. The American people deserve honesty from those with the privilege of holding public office. And I will say once again that the survivors of Jeffrey Epstein's horrific crimes deserve truth, accountability, and justice. I look forward to the Secretary's testimony and to answers to the many questions that my colleagues and I have. Thank you, and I yield back. Thank you, Ms. Meng. I'd like now to recognize the ranking member of the full committee, Ms. DeLauro. Thank you very much, Mr. Chairman, and Ranking Member Meng, for convening this hearing today. And thank you, Secretary Lutnick, for being here to listen to your testimony and have a good back and forth and give and take on questioning. From where I'm sitting, it is difficult to view the Trump administration's trade policy as anything short of disastrous. The President ignited a global economic crisis by imposing significant taxes on American consumers without any thought to whom they were taxing or why they were imposing these taxes in the first place. In case my colleagues have forgotten, in April of last year, the President announced he was imposing a 10% tariff on almost every country in the world, including a group of volcanic islands that are populated not by people but by penguins. This would be amusing if not for the fact that this lazy and haphazard application of tariffs has had such a harmful effect on American consumers. Data from the Yale Budget Lab found that the President's trade war increased the cost of food for American families by $1,700 throughout 2025. Minor changes in our trade deficit are cold comfort to anyone who has to pay attention to the price of groceries, although I understand there aren't many people in the President's cabinet who fall into this category. If the purpose of implementing these tariffs and price hikes on consumers was to promote US job growth, it failed. The US economy created less than 600,000 jobs in 2025. In 2024, before the President took office and put these tariffs in place, that number was 2 million. When all was said and done at the end of 2025, unemployment was up and the economy had the worst year for job growth since 2020, the last year of President Trump's prior term. Earlier this year, we learned that the tariffs the President chose to charge American consumers were not only ill-advised but also illegal, according to the Supreme Court. And now, after charging higher prices because of these illegal tariffs, American companies are trying to get their money back from the government. American consumers, on the other hand—working families who are struggling to keep up with the rising cost of living—they get no relief. I've introduced legislation, the Tariff Relief for Consumers Act, to ensure that refund dollars provide relief for consumers and small businesses and do not simply line the pockets of large corporations and other importers. The American people are fed up with this administration's gross mismanagement of the economy. A poll released earlier this week, an Associated Press poll, found that 70% of Americans disapprove of the President's handling of the economy, while only 30% approve. Mr. Secretary, I appreciate that you are here today to tout what you believe the administration's accomplishments on trade and the economy are. But seven in 10 Americans are not buying what this administration is selling. Given the increasingly dire economic outlook, I would have thought that the Trump administration would look to shore up its domestic economic programs to find ways to pull us out of this hole that they have been digging. Unfortunately, the President's budget request does just the opposite. The administration is proposing to eliminate all Economic Development Administration grants and claw back another $63 million in grant funding that was already approved in this body on a bipartisan basis. The EDA helps create jobs, especially in rural areas. From 2018 to 2024, the EDA created or retained more than 727,000 jobs and spurred more than $80 billion in private investment. I know I do not need to sell you on the Economic Development Administration, Mr. Secretary, given that during your confirmation hearing you expressed support for the program and said that you intended to maintain it. The President's budget also proposes eliminating the Manufacturing Extension Partnership program, which is designed to help small and medium-sized American manufacturers grow, creating more jobs and stimulating local economies. I thought the whole point of the trade war was to support American manufacturing. It has obviously failed in that respect, so why eliminate a long-standing program that actually helps solve the problem? It does not make any sense. You've eliminated the Minority Business Development Agency, and again in your confirmation testimony, you assured us you did not support dismantling this program. Currently, there is a permanent injunction blocking the implementation, but still the administration proposes the elimination of the Minority Business Development Agency. You cut funds for the International Trade Administration. The administration proposes clawing back a total of more than $2.3 billion from funding under the Commerce Department. In addition to the EDA, the administration is proposing to rescind funding that was passed—I want to repeat, we passed as an Appropriations Committee: Democrats, Republicans, House and Senate. We appropriated this money. It was part of the Bipartisan Infrastructure Law, as well as clawing back money and rescinding funding for operations, research, and facilities at NOAA. Lastly, the President's request proposes cutting the budget for the Office of the Inspector General at the Commerce Department by $10 million, more than a fifth of their current funding. We have seen how officials in this administration have exploited the public trust for private gain. It is deeply concerning to see that the internal oversight office charged with preventing waste, fraud, and abuse is being defunded under this budget. The American people should have faith that government officials are working in their interest and are not just out to make a quick buck for themselves. I look forward to hearing your testimony and again, the answers to our questions. Thank you very much for being here, Mr. Secretary. I thank the chairman and I yield back. Thank you, Ms. DeLauro. I would now like to recognize our witness, Secretary Lutnick, for an opening statement. Without objection, your written statement will be entered into the record. Now, Mr. Secretary, we're delighted to have you with us. This is your official congressional oversight subcommittee. We want to be helpful to you in your duties, and we want to know some answers that we all have. So, Mr. Secretary, thank you for being here. The floor is yours. Thank you, Mr. Chairman. And thank you for having me here today. Thank you, Representative Meng and the members of the subcommittee. I appreciate you having me here today and welcome. Over the past 15 months, President Trump has proven that the American economy is the strongest and most resilient in the world. When businesses look for the place to expand manufacturing, they drive innovation and they hire more workers, and they are decisively choosing the United States. Micron, for example: $200 billion investment in memory semiconductor manufacturing in Idaho, upstate New York, and Virginia. TSMC's $165 billion advanced manufacturing investment in America's semiconductor logic sector. Texas Instruments: $60 billion investment for seven US semiconductor fabs across three manufacturing mega-sites in Texas and Utah. These are just a few examples of the Trump administration's commitment to driving semiconductor investment and bringing jobs back to the United States. I could spend our entire time together listing the record-setting commitments company after company and industry after industry have made to build in America. Trillions of dollars of capital investment are pouring into America, led by President Trump and the United States Department of Commerce. I'm proud of the leadership role the Department of Commerce has played in providing the support and encouragement these businesses need to revive American manufacturing, and we have only just begun. Over the past few months alone, the Department of Commerce has announced multiple $15 billion-plus record-setting critical energy investments across this country. From Pennsylvania and Georgia to Texas and Tennessee, they will accelerate economic growth, ensure US energy dominance, bolster critical supply chains, and strengthen US national and economic security. In addition to the massive historic trade agreements with Korea and Taiwan, the impressive $550 billion trade deal with Japan has announced six projects in the past six months. These include $40 billion for small modular reactors in Tennessee and Alabama, a $33 billion 9.2 gigawatt natural gas fired facility in Ohio, a $17 billion natural gas fired power generation hub in southwest Pennsylvania, and $16 billion for natural gas in East Texas. That goes on top of $2 billion for a deepwater crude oil export terminal in the Gulf of America and $600 million for a critical high-pressure, high-temperature synthetic diamond grit facility in Georgia. Just last month, Secretary Wright and I were in southern Ohio to break ground on that 9.2 gigawatt natural gas facility. This project will develop the largest natural gas facility in history. It'll lower electricity costs across the region, create thousands and thousands of American jobs, strengthen our national security, and help the US meet the energy needs of our future. Beyond these historic investments, the Department of Commerce continues to work around the clock to implement President Trump's America First trade policy. Under Section 232, the Commerce Department is taking action on steel, aluminum, copper, pharmaceuticals, autos, semiconductors, and investigating other sectors to protect, re-shore, and revive manufacturing. These actions have led to multi-billion dollar commitments to re-shore steel and aluminum manufacturing and restore American excellence in critical industries. In the first year of his second term, President Trump has dramatically reduced the trade deficit, lowering imports and increasing exports. Exports went to $3.4 trillion, which is a 6% increase from 2024, and in 2025, the trade deficit was the lowest we've seen in 16 years. I'm proud of the role I played in negotiating our most important trade agreements, including the largest deal ever done, which was our trade deal and investment deal with the European Union. The BIS is working hand in hand with the Department of Health and Human Services, and we've arranged historic agreements to lower drug prices for American consumers, negotiating most favored nation deals and ensuring Americans pay the lowest prices for innovative medicines. As a result of these deals, additionally, we have secured over $400 billion in commitments to re-shore pharmaceuticals and manufacturing in America. The budget includes a $215 million increase to BIS above the 2026 enacted level, which supports the hiring of hundreds of new special agent law enforcement officials to address the theft of American technological leadership by bad actors. These officers are critical to protecting and defending America's innovation. In ITA, we are driving record foreign direct investment, cracking down on harmful foreign trade practices, and supporting our 1.3 million American jobs with export and investment deals. In 2025, ITA helped Boeing, Wabtec, and other major American companies secure a record $244 billion in contracts with foreign public buyers. This exceeds the $17 billion that the Biden administration did in its first year. The budget includes a $10 million increase to the 2026 enacted level for trade enforcement in ITA and $100 million for the United States Investment Accelerator. The budget slashes unnecessary wasteful spending at NIST and other groups and eliminates the Manufacturing Extension Partnership, which I'm sure we will talk about. At the USPTO, we are rapidly modernizing and leveraging technology to cut classification prep dramatically. We want to get rid of the massive backlog that we had and do a much better job. And finally, we have launched a new era of modernization of NOAA, where we are in the process of transferring the agency's IT system from a decades-old hardwired technology to the modern distributed cloud. This update will improve our forecasting capabilities, transform the way our forecasters do their jobs, and will make Americans safer. The budget also includes a $135 million increase to the 2026 enacted level for NOAA shipbuilding and our improvement of unmanned systems programs. For far too long, our nation was mired in status quo, leaving American consumers and producers alike with unfair, poorly implemented trade deals, outsourced jobs, and poultry investments that failed to meet our country's potential. President Trump is committed to ushering in a golden age of domestic commerce and bringing jobs back to the United States, and that's exactly what he's done in his first 15 months of this administration. I look forward to working with you as the department continues to spur economic growth and secure unprecedented prosperity for the American people. Thank you, Mr. Chairman, for having me here today. Thank you, Mr. Secretary. We will now proceed under the five-minute rule for questions. I will begin by recognizing myself. Mr. Secretary, the request for the Bureau of Industry and Security, which is instrumental to the nation's export control system, seeks to nearly double the size of the bureau's annual appropriation. How would this historic investment help counter China? Thank you, Mr. Chairman. BIS is the frontline and our key defense from China trying to get our best chips and our best technology, which we have export controls on and we don't allow them to get. But if we don't have the law enforcement officials and we can't stop them, then they'll find their way around our rules. And so we want to significantly grow our law enforcement special agents at BIS. And their returns are remarkable. I know on the budget we talk about expense, but these agents bring between three and 10 times their revenue annually in fines. Not only do they stop these companies, but then we fine these companies and make way, way more revenue for the United States of America. But that budget is two sides, right? The revenue part is not part of my budget, just the expense. But I want you to know these agents are massively economically and intellectually important to the United States. On this topic, how is the department using its export control authority to prevent sensitive US technologies from ending up in the hands of our adversaries? We use at BIS our export control to make sure that countries of concern and countries that are our adversaries cannot get our best technology. We don't want them to get our best chips. We don't want them to get our best software. We want to make sure that we, who are the innovator in the world, remain far in the lead. And so this group is the frontline that holds those back, and we continuously discuss across this administration each and every product, work together as an administration to make sure that those countries are not getting our best technology and innovations. How is BIS partnering with other law enforcement agencies? We work extremely well with Customs and Border Protection, right? That is a key place. DHS is very close with us. The FBI is very close with us. The DOJ—we just had a huge criminal action where a large company was trying to ship billions of dollars of our best chips to China, and they were arrested together with DOJ. So we work very closely with law enforcement. These are the intellectual law enforcement agents of the US government. They're in BIS. They have badges and guns, but they work closely with other law enforcement to make sure those who are trying to steal our best can't get away with it. Let me talk with you briefly about the important work of EDA in rural communities like mine. The assistance to coal communities supports areas negatively impacted by changes in the coal economy. I've seen the devastation that a local economic downturn can cause to a small community and the positive impact that economic development programs have in the aftermath to support recovery and foster economic growth. Sir? I think the coal program can and is very important. And we can help these communities. You know, the Trump administration just announced a deal in Pennsylvania to open two very large coal mines. We are very pro-clean, beautiful coal, as you know. The EDA has significant appropriations. And I will make sure to get that money out the door efficiently, effectively, and with deep consideration. If you appropriate it, I will make sure and our department will make sure we do it as smart and efficiently as possible. Prior administrations sat on these. That is not this department. This department will have the appropriated money that you have given us for EDA smartly, efficiently, and effectively out the door by the end of this year. NTIA under your leadership was able to refocus the Broadband Equity Access and Deployment program on maximizing taxpayer value. Kentucky was allocated $1.1 billion under the program and had plans approved for about $400 million to expand broadband access. When will NTIA provide Kentucky guidance on eligible uses for the remaining $700 million? For the BEAD program, Congress had allocated $42 billion when I walked in the door. And you had a set of rules on who to cover for each state and their teams were amazing. The working with the states was amazing, and working with Kentucky was very impressive. Your team did a great job. But they were only going to give it to fiber and spend all $42 billion in a one-size-fits-all model, which made no sense. You had some people who had a $150,000 fiber run to a house that was worth $100,000. It was crazy town. So we re-bid it without any technological limitations. If it's satellite, if it's fixed wireless, if it's fiber—whatever is best. And we executed the plan for $21 billion exactly according to the statute. Half the money! So we now have a high-class problem. We have executed what you wanted to do exactly right for $21 billion, and we have $21 billion remaining. So here's what we did. We went on a listening tour. We had over a thousand people give us hundreds and hundreds of comments. We're sorting through those hundreds of comments, and we're going to come out with a plan for the high-class problem of having absolutely executed the model that you gave us to go out and now do new and exciting things with that money in the model of the statute that you've given us. Thank you, sir. Ms. Meng. Thank you, Mr. Chairman. And first, before I begin my question, I just want to say that it's great to hear that you, Mr. Secretary, and the Trump administration are continuing the great investments that were made into our domestic chips manufacturing started by the Biden-Harris administration. Whether it's TSMC, Micron, or Texas Instruments, billions were invested during the Biden administration. And I was grateful as a New Yorker to see the investments in Micron in my home and your home state of New York. And we were so thrilled to welcome President Joe Biden to announce them during his administration. But I do want to start, Mr. Secretary—I mentioned in my opening statement that we had learned in recent months that contrary to your earlier statement in which you suggested that you had cut off all contact with Epstein in 2005, you and your family had in fact had lunch with Mr. Epstein on a boat during a trip to his private island in 2012 and exchanged business emails with him as recently as 2018. This was years after his 2008 conviction for soliciting prostitution from a minor. I just wanted to ask, why did you publicly claim following your nomination that you were never in a room with him after 2005? So I have voluntarily agreed in less than two weeks to sit with your colleagues in the House and answer any and all questions on this topic. I have nothing to hide and I am glad to answer their questions. Whatever questions you have on this topic, I will be glad to answer. It's in less than two weeks, and we've set aside the time to do that, and I will answer all the questions. So whatever questions you have, if you would give them to your colleagues, I will answer them. But today, I am here to testify about the budget. I've set aside voluntarily the time to answer those questions in less than two weeks, so today I am here to testify about the budget. No, I appreciate that, and we'll definitely make sure that that message and offer is emphasized. When Senator Van Hollen in February asked if you would be willing to share any records that you have, you told him that you would surely talk about that and you had nothing to hide. What, if anything, have you done since that hearing to ensure that you are sharing all of your records? I've set aside in less than two weeks the time that your House colleagues want to have me answer these questions. I am here to testify about the budget, but I have set aside voluntarily the time to answer these questions. And as you correctly have said, I have nothing to hide. Okay. And I just—you know, why I'm asking is because our American public deserves honesty and accountability from those of us who have the privilege of holding public office. This is a budget hearing, but it's also about our American taxpayers' money. The survivors of Epstein and his associates' horrific crimes deserve so much better from this government. I do want to use the rest of my time to ask about the Gold Card Visa program. President Trump directed you in coordination with the Secretary of State and Homeland to establish a Gold Card Visa program. As you know, people who are able to pay at least $1 million into Commerce's gift account are eligible for permanent legal residence and potentially citizenship. I wanted to ask how many of these applications have been approved and how many gifts have been received by Commerce? The process was recently resolved with DHS, who runs the program, and they do a $15,000, the most serious vetting and analysis of any potential applicant in the history of government. Usually it was $600. These pay $15,000 for an extraordinary vet. So they have approved recently one person, and there are hundreds in the queue that they are going through the process. But this is a new program and they've just set it up and they wanted to make sure they did it perfectly. And so we've worked through that, but it's a DHS program done with a rigorous, rigorous vetting. Okay. And do you know how the department would intend to spend the billions of dollars in the gift account? That will be determined by the administration, and its terms are for the betterment of the United States of America. It needs to be for commerce and the betterment of the United States of America. Thank you, Mr. Chairman. I yield back. Thank you, Ms. Meng. Mr. Alford. Well, thank you, Mr. Chairman, thank you, Ranking Member Meng and our ranking member of our full committee. Good to see you. And thank you, Secretary, for being here today. You know, we depend on the Department of Commerce to play a central role in supporting American competitiveness through weather forecasting, standard development, trade enforcement, and economic development in communities all over our great nation. The fiscal year 2027 proposal reflects the administration's effort to streamline operations, to prioritize core missions, and to make sure that taxpayer resources are focused where they can have the greatest impact. With proposed budget adjustments across NOAA, NIST, EDA, and ITA, I look forward to hearing how the department will continue delivering strong, reliable services while advancing productivity and modernization. I want to talk a little bit about weather. I think last time you were here, we had a very robust conversation about NOAA. I'm glad to say that our operation in Pleasant Hill, Missouri, is still operating with humans, and thank you for that. We have this tornado season in Missouri and we had an EF1 hit not too far from my home last week in Belton, Missouri. It did minimal damage. The next few days could become an outbreak for tornadoes in the Kansas City area—Sunday and Monday is not looking good. The FY 2027 budget proposal includes $70 million in procurement funding for the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration, including investments in next-generation weather satellites. How will these planned upgrades translate into benefits for my constituents? The National Weather Service had been sort of—it had walls around itself, right? We had satellites; we used the data we had from satellites. We'd launch balloons; we used the data that we captured from balloons. We had our own tracking aircraft. We are now going to buy data; that $70 million allows us to buy data available on commercial satellites. So we take in more data. We give up none, but add data, right? We will have unmanned aircraft that can go into the hurricane and stay there without the risk of loss of life. Think of how much better that is. They can live inside the hurricane. And you know how horrifically scary tornadoes are, and the ability to warn early on tornadoes is absolutely difficult. But I, you know, the people at the National Weather Service have been the most impressive. They are the most dedicated, most loyal, and they work their tails off. And you see when it becomes risky, you've never seen people that pour in to try to help. And that's why we need to have an automated model so that the people in the next town, right, those forecasters, can get the same data and assist. We can have the whole set of meteorologists and hydrologists help. And that's what I'm going to leave this department with: the most modern National Weather Service ever that will help protect and defend all of us because we rely on it, and it's really important to you and really important to me. I appreciate that, Mr. Secretary. I'm also proud to support the University of Missouri's research reactor, MURR, the most powerful university-based reactor in the country. It is a critical national supplier, as you know, of life-saving medical isotopes that treat cancer patients each and every year. We're trying to raise the money now for a second reactor. It is a game changer in medicine. How will Commerce continue to support university-linked research facilities such as reactor operations? Our university system is one of the great assets of our country, and the tech hubs and our biology work, medical work—these are extraordinary opportunities. And we are focused on making sure that the universities work together in consortia with business to create the best outcomes. We are very, very focused on outcomes. And that's what matters the most to Commerce is that we want to make sure if you appropriate money that we're going to get the benefit of the bargain for the American people and be great stewards of the money you give us. Mr. Secretary, thank you for your service and with that, I yield back, Chair. Thank you, sir. Ms. DeLauro. Mr. Secretary, during the campaign, the President made a lot of promises about trade policy. And I quote: "he would put more money into our pockets and create millions and millions of new manufacturing jobs." I mentioned earlier in my opening remarks, I think hardworking families are not really feeling this reality. The President's tariffs have exacerbated the cost of living crisis that people are experiencing today. I know the President says it's not an affordability crisis, that that's a hoax, but talk to working Americans every day. They are living paycheck to paycheck; it's tough. And American families now have paid more than $1,700 each in tariff costs last year. And so things are now worse because of the President's war of choice with Iran, and we see the price of gasoline going up. The President made promises also about manufacturing, and the US trade deficit in manufactured goods was $62 billion higher in 2025 compared to 2024. Not exactly the manufacturing renaissance that was promised. So let me ask you, Secretary—and I asked Ambassador Greer the same question last week and let me ask this of you: do you know how many manufacturing jobs the United States lost per day last year, the first year of President Trump's second term? I do not know that statistic one way or the other. Okay, well, and let me just say this is according to the Bureau of Labor Statistics data. US manufacturing lost more than 100 jobs per day from February 2025 to February 2026. That's more than 88,000 jobs in total. It's not a trade policy that delivers for working people. I don't—do we not calculate the number of jobs lost? And what do we think about that? You know, this is really pretty extraordinary when we were supposed to be producing all of these manufacturing jobs. I will just say that the other thing with the Bureau of Labor Statistics, according to them, on net there was an increase of over 500,000 manufacturing jobs under the Biden administration. And I don't really want to talk about the Biden administration, but you all have been here for well over a year and we now need to measure your performance against standards that you set. There is a decline in manufacturing jobs that we have seen in the first year. So let me ask you what your response is to that decline in manufacturing jobs. The tariff and trade policy of President Trump and this administration is to bring manufacturing back to America. But as Representative Meng points out, when we were up in Micron, we broke ground on a hundred billion dollar plant. That's going to have 40,000 construction jobs. TSMC in Arizona, again, 40,000 construction jobs. I broke ground in Ohio, 25,000 construction jobs. So the manufacturing is going to start in two or three years when these factories come online, but construction—bringing this back, 400 billion in pharmaceuticals, 200 billion in autos—these are huge, historic numbers. Let me reclaim my time for a moment, Mr. Secretary, because I know, but we—you can't even tell us how many jobs we've lost, so I don't know about what we're seeing in the future. But your budget suggests that this committee—and in your budget request, you abandon the programs that do the work of trying to promote American manufacturing. Just look at it: you zero out the Manufacturing Extension Partnership, which provides small businesses with tools, training, and resources to grow, innovate, and remain competitive. You also eliminate the Economic Development Administration grants supporting workforce development, innovation, and entrepreneurship. And let me also say it's worth noting that in your confirmation hearing, you opposed cuts to EDA, and it's now going to be eliminated. You gut the International Trade Administration, the Global Markets program. How do you propose that small businesses survive and thrive if you're not going to give them the tools that they need to be able to perform, to support development, to support workforce development? It's just incomprehensible. You mentioned these very large projects that you're doing, but you're cutting all of these efforts off to deal with small businesses and their ability to perform—to succeed, and to grow manufacturing. Comment? What's the matter? Ms. DeLauro, thank you. The time—I just wanted to ask whether you wanted me to respond. Why don't you just try to answer? The chairman will—why don't you try to answer the question, Mr. Secretary? The time of the gentlelady has expired. Mr. Clyde. No answers to the questions. Thank you very much, Mr. Chairman. Secretary Lutnick, thank you for appearing before our committee today. I want to thank you for the Bureau of Industry and Security's decision to rescind the Biden-era interim final rule on firearm export controls. That rule placed an undue burden on American firearm manufacturers and shut them out of overseas markets. Rescinding it was the right call. And so I appreciate the administration's action on this and want to say thank you. And also, the example that you gave of the BEAD, the Broadband Equity Access and Deployment program, savings of $21 billion. Did I get that right, 21 billion dollars? 21 billion. 21 billion dollars. That is incredible and that is a great testament to someone who comes in and says, you know, I think we need some competition in this and we need to be able to look at it and say one size doesn't fit all. All right, and so I think this highlights the President's constitutional authority of impoundment. Here you have done something with 50% of the money and you've got 50% of the money saved, and therefore I think that should be able to either go back to the Treasury or simply be unspent, because in the end it ends up being borrowed money. So with that, I thank you. Now, I want to talk a little bit about the BEAD program. Blue Ridge Mountain EMC in my district is a member-owned electric cooperative that was excited to receive a $3.8 million BEAD award towards their $13.1 million project to connect 3,700 hard-to-reach households in northeast Georgia with broadband. But on January 12th of this year, the National Telecommunications and Information Administration quietly added new language to the BEAD general terms and conditions requiring cooperatives to comply with FCC pole attachment rules across their entire service footprint—not just the BEAD-funded areas, but for the entire service footprint.
