And I mean that's certainly what happened when I first went to HVF, which would become SciFi VC, and started working with the Levchins. On that note, thanks so much for jumping on and sharing your wisdom in your career and looking forward to doing this again soon. Sounds great. Thanks so much for having me. Thanks for watching. If you enjoyed this episode, please press subscribe above so that you don't miss future episodes with the world's top investors.

The rise of industrial policy - why governments are back in the business of business

World Economic Forum(1.1M) ·54:33 ·1.0K views · 2026-04-23
Erik Peterson (Managing Director, Global Business Policy Council, Kearney) Lizhi Liu (Assistant Professor, McDonough School of Business, Georgetown University)
> "We're moving from a system that's optimized for efficiency to one that's optimized for security.
TL;DR: Industrial policy has returned as a central tool of geostrategy, shifting the global economic focus from market-driven efficiency to national security, self-reliance, and the control of strategic "choke points." Defining the New Era: Lizhi Liu defines industrial policy...

TL;DR: Industrial policy has returned as a central tool of geostrategy, shifting the global economic focus from market-driven efficiency to national security, self-reliance, and the control of strategic "choke points."

The Resurgence of State Intervention

  • Defining the New Era: Lizhi Liu defines industrial policy through two features: it is selective (targeting specific firms or sectors) and long-term (aiming for structural transformation rather than short-term market fixes).
  • From Efficiency to Security: The global narrative has shifted from seeking "absolute gains" (where trade benefits everyone) to "relative gains" (ensuring one’s country is better positioned than rivals).
      • Optimization Shift: The global system is moving away from being optimized for efficiency toward being optimized for security and control over critical nodes.
  • The "Islandization" Trend: Erik Peterson notes a transition from globalization to "islandization," characterized by the parochialization of economic policies and a renunciation of the theory of comparative advantage.
  • Skyrocketing Intervention: Government intervention has reached historic levels. In 2023 and 2024, the world saw roughly 1,400 significant government interventions annually, a massive increase over the previous decade.

Strategic Tools and Economic "Choke Points"

  • Policy Levers: Governments are utilizing various tools to influence markets:
      • Subsidies: Predominantly used by advanced, resource-rich economies to foster specific sectors.
      • Trade Barriers: Developing nations rely more on tariffs and export controls.
      • Localization Requirements: Mandating that foreign investors use domestic components or go through intensive screening.
  • Identifying Choke Points: Geopolitical power now hinges on controlling "critical nodes," which include:
      • Geography: Physical bottlenecks like the Strait of Hormuz.
      • Technology: Intellectual property, semiconductors, and AI.
      • Resources: Access to water, fertilizers, and critical minerals.
  • The VC State: In China, the government has evolved from simply "picking winners" to acting as a Venture Capitalist through "government guidance funds" that invest directly in startups and strategic sectors.

The China Factor and Self-Reliance

  • Decentralized Implementation: While politically centralized, 80% of China’s industrial policy is actually implemented at the local government level, with the central government setting broad priorities.
  • Unintended Consequences of Pressure: Lizhi Liu argues that Western export controls have unintentionally strengthened China’s resolve.
      • Incentivizing Domestic Markets: By cutting off access to foreign chips, the U.S. created a guaranteed domestic market for Chinese chipmakers, as Chinese firms no longer have the option to buy foreign alternatives.
  • The Impact of Shocks: Supply chain disruptions during COVID-19 forced Chinese companies like BYD to switch to domestic industrial robot suppliers when foreign makers couldn't deliver, accelerating technology diffusion and domestic market share.

Future Outlook for Global Trade

  • Erosion of Rules-Based Order: Both experts agree the world is moving from a "rules-based" system (governed by the WTO) to a "power-based" system.
  • "Geo-distance" vs. Physical Distance: Diplomatic alignment ("geo-distance") is becoming more important for trade than actual physical distance. Trade is increasingly being reorganized into blocks of "friendly" nations.
  • Neo-Mercantilism Risks: There is a growing concern that if government intervention competition gets out of control, it will lead to a fragmented global system with significant debt overhang and high consumer costs.

Strategic Advice for Executive Leadership

  • Avoid "Wait and See": Erik Peterson warns that companies waiting for the smoke to clear are "asking for trouble." Firms must use strategic foresight to plan for a world of unstable geopolitics.
  • Look Closely at Policy Details: Lizhi Liu advises distinguishing between "performative" policy (announced to please leaders or voters) and "substantive" policy (backed by real enforcement and funding).
  • Look Upstream: Rather than competing in crowded, policy-driven sectors where the failure rate is high, businesses should focus on supplying the inputs, minerals, and infrastructure those strategic sectors rely on.
"We're moving from a system that's optimized for efficiency to one that's optimized for security." — Erik Peterson
"In the past [globalization] is really about absolute gains for each of the countries. Trade makes everyone better. But now it is about relative gains, whether you're better positioned than others." — Lizhi Liu
"External pressure is unlikely to curb China's industrial policy. If anything, export controls and technology restrictions have strengthened Beijing's resolve to support strategic industries and accelerate self-reliance." — Lizhi Liu
"Government is now back in the business of business... putting their fingers on the scales." — Erik Peterson
We had 30 years of remarkable growth where we saw this political and economic liberalization. Governments kind of retracted themselves from the picture. Government is now back in the business of business. Welcome to Radio Davos, the podcast from the World Economic Forum that looks at the biggest challenges and how we might solve them. This week we're looking at the rise of industrial policy where governments are increasingly involved in the business of business. Industrial policy is going to be a massively important issue in the years ahead. And the bottom line here is that the level of government intervention has skyrocketed. Governments have often imposed policies aimed at spurring or protecting industries in their own countries. After a period of economic liberalism and globalization, industrial policy is now surging back. Two experts tell us why and what's different now. The narrative about globalization has changed over the years. In the past, it was really about absolute gains for each of the countries. Trade makes everyone better. But now it is about relative gains, whether you're better positioned than others. We hear how industrial policy is being wielded as a tool of geostrategy. It's a very interesting strategic game that is being played on the global stage right now. We're moving from a system that's optimized for efficiency to one that's optimized for security. I'm Robin Pomeroy at the World Economic Forum and with this look at the new era of industrial policy. Industrial policy always has mixed results. People like us try to figure out what works and what doesn't work. This is Radio Davos. Industrial policy is not merely resurging, it is accelerating at a historic pace as economies face mounting pressures from supply chain disruptions to geopolitical uncertainty and the climate transition. Traditional market mechanisms and policy tools alone are no longer sufficient. I'm reading from an article co-authored by Erik Peterson, a managing director at the consulting firm Kearney. He's one of the two experts I spoke to for a conversation about industrial policy to find out why governments are getting more, not less, involved in, as he calls it, the business of business. It might sound like classic policy wonk stuff, but this resurgence of industrial policy has huge implications for our economies and for the way the whole world works. Our other expert is Lizhi Liu, Assistant Professor at the McDonough School of Business, Georgetown University, who has a particular interest in how China fits into all of this. They both cite research and books for further reading, so once you've listened to this, I hope it will have whetted your appetite to learn more and you can find links to all that interesting stuff in the show notes. Here's my conversation with Liz and Erik. Hi, my name is Lizhi Liu. I'm Assistant Professor of International Business at the McDonough School of Business at Georgetown University. So I do research on the intersection of politics, technology, and also business, with some focus on China as well. Erik Peterson, 16-year partner at Kearney, where I have the privilege of heading the Global Business Policy Council, a 35-year-old think tank. Welcome both of you to Radio Davos. We're going to talk about industrial policy and it's one of those phrases that gets bandied about; I think we've heard more of that phrase in the last few years. So first I'm going to get you to define what we mean by it, what industrial policy is today, maybe how it was different from before, and where we see things going from now. So I'm going to start with either the easiest or the hardest question: what is industrial policy? Which one of you wants to start? I can start. So industrial policy isn't just any government intervention. I think sometimes there's some misunderstanding around that. There are actually two defining features of industrial policy. First, it has to be targeted or involves some selection. These are the selective government interventions that really support specific firms, sectors or regions over others. So the selection is very important. Some of the broad economy-wide policies like general education spending wouldn't count. And second, industrial policy usually has a long-term view and it's structural. It aims to transform the structure of the national economy to pursue some broader public goals. In other words, it doesn't focus on fixing some of the short-term market failures. So these are the two features of industrial policy. I'm in total agreement with that. And I also believe that in answering your question, Robin, it's the most difficult question. Because although it's not a new phenomenon by any means, industrial policy after all has been around for centuries, it's manifested itself in very different ways. And I think that we're going through a period now where we're seeing a significant reformulation and intensification of industrial policies. Of course it's been around forever. Governments have policies about their economies, and they can choose how, how much, and how and where to get involved in industry in terms of supporting their industries. And there are various policy levers for doing that. And it strikes me there was a period of kind of globalization where industrial policy kind of was out of fashion. This idea of free trade, globalized trade with as little as possible government intervention. The world seemed to have agreed, in some economists' eyes anyway, that that was the way forward. It's almost the end of history, end of economic history. Am I reading things right? Is that did we go through that period at some point? Yes, indeed, I think recently we've seen some resurgence of industrial policy, and it's actually helpful to look at some of the data. I highly recommend one of the datasets called the New Industrial Policy Observatory that was put in together by my GFC colleagues, Professor Simon Evenett and his co-authors. So basically the dataset has very detailed tracks of industrial policies globally since after the global financial crisis since 2009. And that actually shows how industrial policy has been shifting, has been resurging, and also the motivations underlying that have been changing. Just to give you some examples. So basically we see that between 2009 and before 2020, the whole period already sees that industrial policy has been on the rise. But the major motivation for industrial policy during that period was one, promoting sectoral competitiveness. In other words, pick on and promote certain strategic sectors to boost national competitiveness. And the second is to achieve climate goals. So a lot of expenditure on environmental sectors and etc. But around 2020 we see actually a clear structural break. In fact industrial policies, especially in the advanced economies that Robin just mentioned like used to refuse industrial policy for a while, now doubling down on industrial policy and also tie that to I would say geopolitics and national security concerns and also supply chain resilience. So we do see that there is more sort of a motivation around industrial policy and the whole phenomenon has been rising. Could you help me out by defining some of the types of policies that we consider as industrial policy? Would you include trade tariffs in there? What else is in there? Yeah, so industrial policies, I think the most commonly used industrial policy is actually subsidies. So right now across both advanced and developing economies, people really use subsidies, try to foster certain sectors to grow. However, there's actually some difference between advanced economies and developing countries. In advanced economies because they're more resource-rich, so they actually use more of this kind of a subsidies, but for developing countries usually they rely more on trade barriers, something like you said tariffs and others. But beyond subsidies, there are other measures. For example, trade measures you have just mentioned, export controls, tariffs, or government procurement can be another type of industrial policy. For example, I just buy from domestic companies. And also there are entry barriers, for example if you're a foreign investor investing in my country, you cannot invest in some areas I try to protect. For example, sensitive areas like telecommunication and others. And also you have to go through certain screening. And also there will be usually some sort of localization requirements. That's very important. Which is if you invest in my area as a foreign investor, you have to make sure like certain part of the components that you use in the production come from domestically. So these are the normal types of industrial policy tools. Robin, if we zoom back, I think, starting with that period that you mentioned, the end of history, which clearly is an allusion to the seminal work that was written by Francis Fukuyama and the important books that he's written after that. What we saw after the collapse of the Soviet Union, and this of course is ancient history, was a remarkable renunciation of the kind of economic statism that existed before then. And the logic was that industrial policy at that time didn't work. It was a slippery slope to economic autarky. And it in effect was a renunciation of the whole theory of comparative advantage in trade and the massive movement cross-border movement of goods and services and everything else at the time. So what happened was that we had 30 years of remarkable growth where we saw this political and economic liberalization such that governments kind of retracted themselves from the picture. Well, I think that Liz is absolutely right, especially at the end of the century and in the last 20 years, government is now back in the business of business. And they're putting both developed and developing country governments are putting their fingers on the scales. I think an easy way to kind of look at things is to ask two three-word questions. Policy for what? And policy for how long? And right now what we're seeing is a movement of kind of traditional areas where governments were very protective of their systems, national security, energy, other areas like that. And we're seeing the kind of migration that Liz has mentioned into competitiveness and now we're even seeing it kind of move into protectionism in some areas. And so the key question is how long how long will this go on? And how wide will the scope of government intervention be? And the bottom line here is that the level of government intervention has skyrocketed. If you look at the numbers, I won't throw too many numbers at you, but this year alone we now stand at where we were total last year which was about about 1,400 government interventions. And this is really off the charts compared to those earlier years that you were talking about. So this is a sea change and you know, before we started Liz and I were agreeing that probably industrial policy is going to be a massively important issue in the years ahead. Before we look at kind of the present and the future, let's have a tiny bit more history. Erik, you mentioned the collapse of the Soviet Union. So what happened then? You know, what's happened more recently that's brought industrial policy back? I think maybe you've already touched on a couple of things. The financial crisis of 2008 has been mentioned, and also you've mentioned China and China joining the World Trade Organization. China, you know, is a centralized economy. I don't suppose China ever turned its back on industrial policy in the way Western governments at least say they did. Could one of you, maybe Erik again, could you walk us through what what were those historical turning points that brought us to where we are today? Well, I think there are a lot of these historical turning points, Robin. And you mentioned a few of them. What I see is kind of two parallel movements. The first is that as we see the gradual erosion of the rules-based system, and that we saw the kind of development of new trade and other tensions. We saw the attitude of kind of every economy for itself a bit. We presented a report away back when in 2015 entitled, awkwardly, "From Globalization to Islandization." And this report that we did in our think tank highlighted concerns that we had that we were seeing this kind of move toward a period of parochialization of economic policies and that when did that happen and why? Because it's tempting to look from today and say Trump 2.0 kind of ripping up the rulebook, pulling out of international treaties on various things, very skeptical about anything that smells in some way of globalism or global rulebooks. But from what you're saying, no, it actually predates that by quite some time. Yeah, it does. And here in the US, I think it's an equal opportunity phenomenon when it comes to politics. I mean we've seen exercises of industrial policy on both sides of the political aisle. So while we're getting a lot of significant volatility, to be sure, from the current administration, I think we can't we can't point fingers merely at what's happening now. This started a long time ago. Wouldn't you agree, Liz? Yeah, I fully agree with what Erik just said. I want to add a little bit about that. I think at the beginning, especially in the 80s and 90s, you know, a lot of Western economies, they had this like neoliberal market-oriented philosophy and ideology. Basically they felt that governments are really bad at picking winners. So industrial policy usually generates more government failure even though it is supposed to fix market failure. So government failure was the problem at the time. But I think a lot of things have changed. I think the one fundamental change as Erik and I spoke, you know, before the podcast, is the narrative about globalization has changed over the years. In the past, it was really about absolute gains for each of the countries. Trade makes everyone better. But now it is about relative gains, whether you're better positioned than others, right? And also we're moving from a system that's optimized for efficiency to one that's optimized for security and control over choke points. So I think that is a very important change that also justifies certain wastes or inefficiencies under industrial policy. And industrial policy has become increasingly more politically popular. However, I think the risk is that it will also generate a fragmented global system that probably will carry a lot of costs down the road. Totally agree. And Robin, I think a lot of this too is the broader social attitudes. I think that with globalization we saw a lot of populations that were very concerned that they were losing their jobs and opportunities and livelihoods as a result of what was happening in countries far beyond what they could see day to day and they were feeling very intimidated. And I think that there's evidence of this in a number of the advanced economies now starting with the US. And in the end, it's kind of a tragic thing because these last 30 years have brought prosperity to a lot of people across the world. And if we allow the kind of fragmentation that Liz was just talking about to occur, I think everybody's going to pay a price in one way or another. And that that's a disappointment in my view. You both wrote great articles for the World Economic Forum's website, published around the annual meeting time. I'm going to quote one from each of you. And I think this was you, Erik, from yours: "the key to geopolitical power hinges in part on control over economic choke points or critical nodes in the global system." So you've both mentioned the importance of geopolitics and national security as being kind of motivations for countries deploying forms of industrial policy. And here you've used the word choke points and critical nodes. Now, this was published in January, Erik, and as we speak now, I'm not sure when this episode will go out, but the Strait of Hormuz is blocked. That might be a geography that many normal people had not heard of until fairly recently. And now they realize if that's blocked, so much of the energy and other critical things are blocked and it has a knock-on effect all over the world. I mean, I don't know if the blockade, in a wartime environment, a blockade of a waterway like that would count as classic industrial policy. I think it's probably something a little bit different. But it certainly does count as an economic choke point. So Erik, when you were writing that, what kind of choke points or critical nodes were you thinking of and what do you think we should be looking out for? Yeah, it's a great point. And I think that we have a very, very significant example happening right now, as you suggest, in the Strait of Hormuz. But it's happening elsewhere. And in the end, if we look at the shadow movement of oil on the oceans now, there are a number of kind of choke points that suggest geographical importance, but there are other kinds of choke points as well. I'm sure that Liz is going to address herself to this, but in technology there are choke points with respect to availability of resources and critical materials and minerals. And these nodes or choke points are really vital. I would recommend to your listeners that if they really want to learn a lot about this, there's a seminal book that came out of a colleague of Liz's at Georgetown by the name of Ed Fishman, entitled "Choke Points." It's a great book. And then she has other colleagues who are doing some extraordinary work in this area at Georgetown as well. But I think that generally speaking we have to be looking at a widening scope in the future. Choke points won't just be geographical. They'll be in a number of other areas and other dimensions as well. Could you give us some examples of that? I mean the ones that think come to my mind are critical physical things that we need to make things, like certain minerals or certain high-tech technology products, chips. Are those the kinds of things you're talking about? Absolutely. Or the movement of fertilizer through the Gulf, or what about water movement, or availability or access to water now? We're all watching the whole desalination dimension of the conflict in the Gulf right now. But a lot of it involves resources, a lot of it involves ideas and technologies and intellectual innovations. I think that these choke points are going to take many forms. Yeah, I actually want to add a little bit on that because I think Erik's points are great and very insightful. One thing that countries really care about choke points is that theoretically this kind of a reliance of other countries relying on your choke points can be used for you as economic statecraft. You can use those choke points to pressure other countries. However, the issue is that when you overuse it, the other countries will have incentives to build alternatives, right? As we see that, for example, China is building its domestic chip industry with mixed results. And also other countries are building rare earth mining capacity and manufacturing refining capacities outside of China because both US and China kind of pressed other countries on these choke points. So I think it's a very interesting strategic game that is played on the global stage right now. Let's turn our attention a bit to China then, because I wonder how much of this resurgence in industrial policy, and I'm sure this is only part of it, but how much of it is to do with the West and maybe other parts of the world seeing China, this massive success story of China over the last 20 years or so, and feeling threatened by it? Erik, you mentioned there's public opinion that we've lost manufacturing jobs to Asia, to China and elsewhere. And that from a political standpoint that has maybe brought back both on both sides of the political aisle this kind of appetite for industrial policy for reshoring some of those jobs. So let's look at China. And I'm looking at a blog you wrote or you co-wrote, Liz, also published in January. And you said that "Made in China 2025," which was an initiative of the Chinese government from May 2015, marked a defining comprehensive shift and the beginning of a new era of Chinese industrial policy. In what way was it new? Because like I said before, viewed from the outside, China is a very centralized economy. So what was new about this? Yeah, first of all, I would like to clarify on this point of China being a centralized economy. China is politically very centralized, but economically quite decentralized. So the central government usually sets very broad priorities and guidelines, but local governments always fill in the details. So that means that China actually has a lot of variation about how policies are implemented, very different across regions. And in fact, according to a paper by Hanming Fang, Ming Li, and Guanli Lu, 80% of China's industrial policy comes from the local level. Even though after I would say 2013 and 2014, why I'm saying there's a more systematic change is that there's more like a Chinese central influence on the local policy priorities. But I think in general, a lot of people will be equating government intervention as industrial policy. But as I said, you need to have some sort of a targeting, you need to have some very concrete government backing on that. If we take those two measures, in fact, before the mid-2000s, China didn't really have very active and systematic industrial policies even though state intervention was always there. But between roughly 2006 and 2014, there was some ad hoc supporting strategic emerging industries. So the government started to have a list of the industries they wanted to support. But what is really going on and why I'm calling this as a qualitative change is around 2014, there is more of a government becoming an investor. In the sense that, you know, the government not only started to have more systematic measures like "Made in China 2025," try to upgrade the manufacturing industry and also having more like robotics, semiconductors, all these advanced manufacturing industries competing with other countries. But also there's more of a rise of government-guided funds. Meaning that the government would allocate part of the public funds and behave like a VC even though it's not a private entity. So they started to invest in startups and also trying to cultivate these strategic sectors. In fact, I would say industrial policy does have this like phenomenon of policy loop like a feedback. Essentially countries sometimes imitate each other, including I guess some of the practices. Oh, one thing I forgot to mention is that after 2020, there's actually another turn of China's industrial policy, which is because of more of a credible threat from the US export control and restricting China's access to these core technologies. Therefore they started to shift the industrial policy towards more of self-reliance and national security. So they feel that we definitely need to build an indigenous industry rather than just importing American or Western technology. So that's another very meaningful turn. On that, I'm going to quote from your article again, Liz, at the risk of slightly repeating what you just said, but I'm going to quote verbatim: "external pressure is unlikely to curb China's industrial policy. If anything, export controls, technology restrictions, and geopolitical tensions have strengthened Beijing's resolve to support strategic industries and accelerate self-reliance." So what are we concretely talking about here? It's chips and the AI race. Is that the main thing when we talking about export restrictions? Yeah. Something I found to be interesting because industrial policy always has mixed results. And people like us try to figure out what works and what doesn't work. But sometimes it's not the industrial policy per se what worked; it is whether you have access to foreign technology that would actually influence the effectiveness of a domestic industrial policy. I think one example is the chip industry that you're talking about and also that's what I wrote. Which is, in fact, the Chinese government always wanted to build a very strong chip industry and poured a lot of money into this. But it also created a lot of fraud in the past. For example, in the mid-2000s, there was a Chinese scientist who claimed that they had a domestically developed chip called Hanxin. It got a lot of government support, but it turned out that they just bought some Motorola chip and used a sandpaper to scratch the Moto from it and then they just claimed it to be their own. And when this was discovered, it was a huge blow to China's industrial policy, just suggesting how wasteful it can be. However, export control actually helped somehow. I mean, definitely it created a lot of disruptions to Chinese tech development, which I all agree. But in the long run, especially in hindsight, I think you can see that because the Chinese government and Chinese firms see now it is a credible threat that they are no longer getting access to foreign chips. So they have stronger incentives to buy domestic alternatives. And the Chinese government even mandated some of the tech companies to purchase domestic chips. So that in itself created a market for the domestic chips. Because before that, there was no such a market even though there had been some government support but it wasn't very successful. So I would say it's a combination of government support but at the same time sometimes external pressure can have unintended consequences to incentivize countries to really have domestic substitution for foreign technology. Export restrictions the other way around then; there are fears that China has too dominant a position over certain critical minerals that are critical for manufacturing. Is China exerting its influence there and restricting those exports or are we just worried that it could do that from one moment to the next? That question was framed, Robin, as X or Y. I think it's both. I think that Beijing is now trying to use its leverage whenever and however it can. And the reason for this is something that you mentioned earlier and Liz affirmed in her reaction. And it's all about self-sufficiency and sovereignty now. And I think that countries, if we look at the most significant economies in the world, there's a push now in virtually every one of them to be thinking about ways to find sovereignty and self-sufficiency, whether it's digital sovereignty in Europe or whether it's the elements that Liz has just highlighted in China. We're all watching developments here in the US these days. And the key question is how rapidly will the scope of government intervention move in order to try to support this self-sufficiency. And let me repeat that that goal is non-economic in the end and it's inefficient and it carries with it significant cost. And not only that, but if we look at major economies mobilizing public resources in order to support their interventions, Liz mentioned that I think probably the most aggressive area these days is in subsidy support of industrial policy. The bottom line is that they're piling on the debt that they've already put together. We see significant debt overhang in a number of economies worldwide. So the question is: how much longer can this game be played and how effective will it be? Yes, I fully agree with Erik. One thing I want to add is that China's rare earth industry is another example about similarly to the chip industry that I mentioned, how export control and China's exercise of export control would lead to other countries trying to build their domestic capacity to circumvent China's export control. So we do see that there are some other countries trying to do this. But one thing I also want to mention is that manufacturing sometimes moves or mining, they move not just because of labor quality or labor cost; they also move with pollution. So the reason why China is good at the rare earth industry is not just because China has a huge stock of it, a huge reserve of it. Some other countries also have some reserves. I think part of this is because rare earth refinement and also extraction is a highly polluting industry. So I actually had a conversation with someone who specialized on this and I was asking the question about whether the US would build some of the capacity in this area and she was saying that at least right now because of the highly polluting nature of this industry, probably the US is going to rely on some of the alliances and allies to actually do this. So I think this kind of islandized based trade blocks, probably especially around these critical minerals, probably are on the rise as well. Forging better alliances with countries if the product you're relying on is restricted from somewhere. You can either try and make it yourself or you can go to somewhere else and that may well happen for things like minerals and mining. We've seen how important China has become in terms of investment in Africa, for example. Is this resurgence of industrial policy creating also a new era of international relations? Yes, it is. And it could go one of two ways. We could see it moderated in the end. We could see wise government interventions in support of broader social policy goals. Or we could see the kind of phenomenon of neo-mercantilism that the Financial Times writer Martin Wolf has highlighted post-Davos. He presented a number of articles that I thought were really compelling suggesting that unless we get it right, this whole government intervention competition could get out of control. And as I've suggested, I think it carries costs, but it has as you note, Robin, geopolitical consequences that we need to take very seriously. There still is the World Trade Organization, which is meant to set rules, which as you've said are under pressure, but on things like tariffs and on things like subsidies and non-tariff barriers to try to provide somewhere where countries can diplomatically supposedly work out their differences. Are we going to see a change to the WTO? Does it have to be reinvented? Where do countries go now to resolve their differences if it's not literally on a battlefield like I suppose it is in the Strait of Hormuz? Where's the future for that kind of multinational cooperation which in theory should be a good thing for everyone? It's impossible to know. We are watching with concern the attempts now to revivify developments in the WTO. But if you look at the UN specialized agencies or even the UN itself, or the Bretton Woods group, there's a constant pressure in terms of evidence of dysfunctionality that needs to be addressed. So the operative question as far as I'm concerned is: how do you modernize? How do you create structures that reflect the current circumstances? Do you just kind of pile on or do you find wholesale new institutions? And we've seen this, Robin, not only in the UN system but in the G20 and the G7 and all kinds of other intergovernmental mechanisms that have had their ups and downs, let's face it, with respect to their effectiveness. There's a crying need right now for us to find ways to put together an international structure that reflects the world that not only exists in front of us now but where it's going to be; where the puck is, we have to skate to where the puck is going to be in the future. I fully agree with Erik, especially I think what he was saying is that international relations has fundamentally been changed from one that's more like a rules-based, now unfortunately to something that's more power-based. And I think with regard to the WTO, there have been some structural issues, especially the dispute resolution system, the appellate body right now having some issues with filling in the judges. I think partly because the US is unhappy with the WTO and required some changes. I think indeed some of the reforms are yet to be seen in the WTO structure and the future is uncertain. But on the other hand, I'm kind of still an optimistic person, even though I know being an optimistic person in this period means that I'm always wrong. But I think things tend to go in cycles, right? If you look at the history, in terms of political polarization, in terms of tariffs, we used to have very high tariffs and then tit for tat ones and then there is multilateral negotiation that really negotiated down the tariffs. So I just feel like perhaps in the future, especially when the costs are high enough, even though I hope the costs wouldn't be that high, maybe there will be some incentives for countries to sit down and really figure out a multilateral solution to solve the current issues. So does that mean that regional groupings of countries will take on more importance? Because if it's a question now of the stronger will get away with what they can, so the big economies will push the others around, the only way for smaller economies is to band together in some ways. I think we're already seeing that. Is that something that's going to happen in the future? I would say right now geo-distance does matter much more than before. Geo-distance basically means the two countries' diplomatic relations, their friendship, for example, whether they are in the same alliance. Because we used to say distance matters for trade, but now geo-distance matters. So indeed a lot of this kind of rule-setting can be set within a block of countries that's friendlier to each other. However, to be honest, one thing to be optimistic again going to this question about being optimistic, is that according to the transportation data, for example from DHL, they track these shipments and mobility of persons and commodities across border, you still don't see a very clear sign of regionalization of trade. In other words, I think in terms of the rules, perhaps negotiations will be based in the regions, but still different regions are still connected with trade. So I think that's something that brings some hope. Robin, I think we need to revise our thinking now about the traditional way that we've addressed trade blocks and trade organizations and regional groupings. I think in the future it's all going to be a lot more virtual. And we're not going to have the same kind of way where we memorialize our trade agreement in 20 detailed clauses etc. etc. It will all be fluid; my sense is to reflect the current circumstances. Because you know we've been talking about a lot of kind of macro factors, but I can tell you because I work day by day with the business community, that business is quite concerned about the uncertainties and the volatilities implicit with many of these issues that we've been talking about. Including in some cases industrial policy, some benefit, others don't, etc. But the upshot is that business knows that things change immediately and you put together kind of quick alliances and you need to be remarkably agile and remarkably resilient in terms of planning. Yeah, well that was going to be my next question. I mean what advice do you have for businesses in this because they can't sit back and wait for the smoke to clear. You know these are companies that are doing business around the world right now. What advice do you give them? Yeah, I think that companies that are sitting and waiting right now are probably asking for trouble long range. As I say, we work with a lot of great companies that are proactive and basing their thinking on strategic foresight. And from my point of view, they need to be thinking about a world in which they can no longer rely on stable geopolitics; that's a thing of the past. They can no longer bank on the continuation of globalization; I think Liz you know that DHL work is so important, but nevertheless we know that there's turbulence now and there are disruptions in the trade space. They can't rely on the rules-based order, Robin, we talked about that. And then government policies that in the old days used to promote free markets and reduction of cross-border barriers have really changed with this whole industrial policy reality now. And then the last point, and this is one that I know that resonates in Europe, is that the conditions defining business and stability are really quite ambiguous now. You know with hybrid warfare, for example, in Europe, it's tough to navigate effectively between peace and war. And I think that that's from the standpoint of our CEOs who are engaged in our work, that we need to be thinking about that in the future. I'm proud to say that Kearney is doing good work now on an industrial policy effort with the World Economic Forum. And I know that there's great interest in this from the standpoint of business precisely because we know that things are really changing so rapidly that we have to respond. So there's no wait and see. It's take a position, aggressively use strategic foresight to think through competitive futures, and get your organization aligned for a turbulent outcome. So I would give like two tips for companies probably in this era of industrial policy. One is look closely and the other is look upstream. So what do I mean by look closely? So I think if your sector is targeted by the industrial policy, you should really pay attention to the policy details. I'm saying this is because right now people know that governments are really passionate about industrial policy. However, sometimes a lot of industrial policy are also performative rather than being substantive. I did some research actually about China's local government industrial policy variations with great co-authors like Junting Chen, Jerry Xing, and Xiangmeng Liu. And we actually looked at Chinese industrial policy about EV chargers in different local areas. You definitely can see that the level of details are quite different; like some of the industrial policies they do this probably to please the central leaders, and in democracies we can see that the politicians would announce huge amount of government spending just to win popular votes, right? So there are different motivations for really promoting certain sectors, but that's not always real commitment to of funding. So I would say like companies should really look at that, whether there's enforcement detail, whether there's follow-through and etc. The second advice is that if you're outside of these sectors that are targeted by the industrial policy, but you're interested in moving in or if you're an investor, you need to be cautious. I think the reason why some of the industries are targeted is because commercially they probably are not making money or it's very difficult to figure out the technology, such as chips or advanced manufacturing. So in other words, there is a high failure rate if not very high failure rate. And the history basically tells us that industrial policy does not always work. So I think a safer strategy for people who are outside of those sectors is probably instead of competing directly in the crowded policy-driven sectors, focus on supplying the inputs and infrastructures that these sectors will rely on. For example in the chip industry you focus on the minings, the raw materials that would be used by the chip industry or data centers. Tips, proper tips for investing and for running businesses there. You heard it on Radio Davos. Thanks for that. We don't have much time left, I'm just wondering, it really strikes me the way you're both talking here that we're in a new era for a lot of things. It's a new era of industrial policy, and it's a new era of geopolitics. We can mark 1990 for the fall of the Berlin Wall and you can mark certain economic crises like 2008. I wonder if we'll find out that this year or somewhere around this time the world has entered a new era. Have you had time to think about that kind of historically? I was wondering, are we in a post-COVID world? We mentioned COVID right earlier on and that made so many changes in our lives. Are we in a post-post-Cold War world? Because we were post-Cold War for a couple of decades. Have you coined a phrase or have you seen anyone else kind of define the era we're in now? In a nutshell, no, is how I would respond to that, Robin. I haven't seen anybody kind of capture where we are right now. If we pull back the stick from industrial policy, I think we stand at a moment of remarkable geopolitical reformulation right now. We've talked about the broader global economy and the changes that are happening. Technology is just reshaping us by the day. And then on top of that, I believe that business is coming up with remarkable and interesting new approaches to a lot of these issues. And drawing inspiration from Liz's optimism, I would like to make a point about I'm really hopeful that some of the incredible innovation that we're seeing now in technology and elsewhere will spill over to the ways that we deal with these big global international issues. And here's a case in point that zooms in on our topic, industrial policy. I was really interested to see a report come out of OpenAI on industrial policy, a recent report. And you know that they would lay a marker with this report, and we're all watching AI every single day these days. That they would lay a marker in such a critical area of industrial policy as a dimension of the AI process and the AI phenomenon, to me is meaningful. And there was also an amazing report that came out of the World Bank in terms of industrial policy and its effect on development. And so I think both these are great examples of the kind of fresh thinking that we need here and the opportunity that we have to kind of reshape our future. Yeah, I agree with Erik because I think right now the world is very different from the previous Cold War. Even though some people would say this is Cold War number two due to there's some sort of a great power dynamics right now between US and China. But I think the structure of global trade is entirely different, right? Because in the previous Cold War, the US and the Soviet Union were basically economically separate largely. Whereas right now between the US and China, there's still lots of business that is running and also strengthening the ties; even with the talk of decoupling, the two economies are still pretty much connected. So I think that means there have been a lot of opportunities. But I do think one thing I would like to say is that shocks sometimes bring good opportunities, just in following Erik's point. Because I think we right now do not have a very good understanding about COVID. We have some understanding but not full understanding. I think in relation to industrial policy, one thing is that of course COVID exposed the real vulnerabilities in global supply chains. That's why governments really doubled down on industrial policy. But on the other hand, it really created opportunities for domestic substitution and for latecomers in certain areas. Just to give you an example. I'm actually working on a project with Jong Choi and also Mike Chen on China's industrial robot sector. This is a sector that is now exporting to the rising manufacturing hubs in Southeast Asia, also to Mexico. And China actually had a very interesting transition from a net importer of industrial robots to a net exporter. So I think this is a very important industry. This is also an industry that supposedly is helped by the Made in China 2025 which attracted foreign players to really localize in China and facilitate technology diffusion. However, before COVID, domestic Chinese robot makers still struggled to gain market shares because even Chinese companies would not like to buy from them. They wanted to buy from leading producers from Switzerland, US, Japan, and Germany. So COVID really changed this because of the supply chain disruptions. A lot of these foreign companies couldn't deliver on time, so downstream manufacturers, for example like BYD, the EV maker, started to turn to domestic robotics producers. And they surprisingly found out that these firms were good enough automation. They're not at the technology frontier, but they're reliable and more importantly much cheaper. So that's why domestic companies started to buy from these domestic makers. So I do think that this example tells us that sometimes shocks or even fragmentation can create opportunities that businesses can really take on. It's been a fascinating discussion. We're definitely in a new era, whatever we're going to call it. My thanks very much to Lizhi Liu. Thanks very much from Georgetown University. Thanks to Erik Peterson from Kearney. Radio Davos is available wherever you get podcasts and at wef.ch/podcasts. We'll be back next week. But for now, thanks to you for listening and goodbye.

President Trump Participates in a Health Care Affordability Event

The White House(3.1M) ·67:08 ·41.7K views · 2026-04-23
Donald Trump (President of the United States)
President Trump announced that Regeneron has become the 17th major pharmaceutical company to adopt "Most Favored Nation" pricing, ensuring Americans pay the lowest global prices for drugs, while also revealing a $27 billion domestic manufacturing inv...
TL;DR President Trump announced that Regeneron has become the 17th major pharmaceutical company to adopt "Most Favored Nation" pricing, ensuring Americans pay the lowest global prices for drugs, while also revealing a $27 billion domestic manufacturing investment and the...

TL;DR

President Trump announced that Regeneron has become the 17th major pharmaceutical company to adopt "Most Favored Nation" pricing, ensuring Americans pay the lowest global prices for drugs, while also revealing a $27 billion domestic manufacturing investment and the rescheduling of medical marijuana to Schedule 3.

Prescription Drug Affordability & "Most Favored Nation" (MFN) Pricing

  • Expansion of MFN Program: Regeneron has joined 16 other global pharmaceutical companies in a "Most Favored Nation" agreement, which now covers 80% of the branded drug market.
      • The policy mandates that these companies sell drugs to American patients at the lowest prices available anywhere in the world.
      • Savings Examples:
          • Cholesterol/Heart Disease (Praluent): Reduced from $537 to $225.
          • Weight Loss Medications: Prices dropped from $1,350/month to as low as $199/month.
          • COPD Medication: Reduced from $458 to $50.
  • Trump-RX.gov Platform: A new transparency website (trump-rx.gov) has been launched to allow consumers to find these discounted rates.
      • Administrator Dr. Mehmet Oz reported the site has already served over 10 million unique visitors and serves as a "transparency site" to ensure correct pricing.
  • Profit Rebalancing: Trump noted that while pharmaceutical companies previously made 75% of their profits from the U.S. (despite the U.S. having only 4% of the population), MFN pricing forces other wealthy nations to pay their fair share, reducing the burden on American consumers.

Medical Miracles & Breakthrough Innovation

  • Gene Therapy for Deafness: The FDA has approved Otophemy, a Regeneron gene therapy that cures a rare form of genetic deafness.
      • Free Access: Regeneron CEO Len Schleifer and CSO George Yancopoulos announced they will provide the treatment for free to eligible children in the U.S. for an initial period.
      • Case Study: The event featured two-year-old Travis Smith, who was born 100% deaf and can now hear following the treatment.
  • R&D Commitment: Regeneron committed to investing $27 billion in U.S.-based research, development, and manufacturing.
      • Innovation Protection: Dr. Yancopoulos emphasized the importance of patent protection and "disruptive innovation" to stay ahead of global competitors like China.

Marijuana Rescheduling & Federal Policy Shifts

  • Schedule 3 Reclassification: HHS and the Department of Justice have moved to reclassify medical marijuana and FDA-approved CBD products from Schedule 1 to Schedule 3.
      • Research Expansion: The move is intended to allow doctors and scientists to study the efficacy and drug interactions of medical marijuana, which is currently used by one in ten seniors.
      • Veterans and Seniors: Trump noted this change is particularly important for military veterans and those suffering from chronic conditions who currently "fly blind" without federal research guidance.

Economic Strategy & Domestic Reshoring

  • Tariff Incentives: Secretary of Commerce Howard Lutnick reported that $448 billion in pharmaceutical manufacturing is "reshing" to America.
      • Companies like Eli Lilly and Pfizer are building major domestic plants to avoid tariffs and capitalize on U.S. manufacturing incentives.
  • Infrastructure Efficiency Case Study: Trump detailed his administration's repair of the Lincoln Memorial Reflecting Pool.
      • Cost/Time Savings: The previous administration's estimate was $301 million over 3.5 years; the Trump administration is completing the project for less than $2 million in approximately one week by using a specialized sealant instead of full demolition.
  • Semiconductor Dominance: Secretary Lutnick stated the U.S. is on track to hold nearly 50% of the chip market, with $1 trillion in "fabs" (factories) being built domestically through deals with companies like TSMC and Micron.

National Security & Personnel Updates

  • Iran Conflict: President Trump stated the U.S. has "decimated" the Iranian military and economy through a 100% effective blockade and strategic strikes.
      • The goal remains a permanent deal that ensures Iran never acquires a nuclear weapon.
  • Navy Secretary Departure: Trump addressed the firing of the Navy Secretary, attributing it to personality conflicts and disagreements regarding the pace of "hard-charging" new ship-building initiatives.

***

Notable Quotes

"We have 4.2% of the world's population. We take 13% of the pharmaceutical drugs... and we provide 75% of the profits to the pharmaceutical industry. This is a rip-off that has irked [the President] for 20 years." — Robert F. Kennedy Jr., Secretary
"Other wealthy nations have been getting a free ride on American innovation. Just like defense, they've been getting a free ride in health. Now President Trump is putting policies in place to force these nations to pay their fair share." — Dr. Len Schleifer, Regeneron CEO
"It's hard to beat free. Free is the best price, and that's the starting price with the President oftentimes." — Dr. Mehmet Oz, CMS Administrator
"Instead of spending $301 million [on the reflecting pool] we're spending less than $2 million... I think it's a great business story because there are many things like that that I see." — President Donald Trump
"Are you all set?" "All set, sir." "Well, thank you very much. Today I'm thrilled to announce that one of the most respected pharmaceutical companies anywhere in the world—frankly, I know it very well—is Regeneron. It's agreed to offer their prescription medications at heavily discounted, most favored nation prices. In other words, the numbers will come down at levels that nobody's ever seen before. The whole most favored nation thing that we've been able to do with Bobby and Maz and everybody behind me, Marty, has been incredible. It's the biggest price reduction in drugs in history. By itself, we should win the midterms, but it doesn't work that way. Unfortunately, you know it doesn't work that way. People forget too quickly, but we'll have the biggest—it's the biggest cut in drug prices in the history of our country by many percentage points. With this announcement, 17 of the world's largest pharmaceutical companies, representing 80% of the branded drug market, have now agreed to sell their drugs to American patients at the lowest prices anywhere in the world. We're going to have the lowest prices in the world. This will result in the largest drop in prescription drug prices in the history of the United States of America. I mean, that should be front-page news, but it won't be. It'll be back on page 19. But the people get it. That's why we're in the Oval Office, I guess. I want to thank Regeneron's CEO—a friend of mine for a long time, good guy, great guy—CEO Len Schleifer, and Chief Scientific Officer George Yancopoulos, who are two fantastic people in the medical world, very respected. We're also joined by Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr. Bobby, thank you. We have Secretary Howard Lutnick, Administrator of the Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services Dr. Mehmet Oz, who is doing a fantastic job—they all are—FDA Commissioner Marty Makary, and Chief Counselor of Health and Human Services Chris Klump. Thanks, Chris. Great job you're doing, Chris, wherever you may be. Oh, I see. For decades, Americans have been forced to pay the highest prices in the world for prescription drugs, by far. Prices were many, many times what the rest of the world was paying because we had a president that didn't know what they were doing, or they weren't able to get other countries to agree to it. I was able to do that because I used tariffs. I said if you don't agree to it, we're going to put tariffs on your country, and they said we would be honored to do it, sir, after telling everybody no, including me. But they agreed to do it. The United States has just 4% of the world's population and consumes only 13% of all prescription drugs, yet pharmaceutical companies have been making 75% of their profits from American customers. So think of that. With 4% of the population and doing only 13% of all prescription drugs, the companies made 75%. I don't know, maybe hopefully they continue because actually it's not as bad for them as it sounds. In fact, many of their stocks are up because actually when the price goes up on the one end, they make up for the price coming down in the United States. So it really worked out pretty good. To address this chronic unfairness, I signed an executive order instructing my administration to do everything in our power to slash prescription drug prices for Americans while getting other countries to pay more. In many cases, much more. Their other prices doubled and sometimes they tripled, and ours would go down by 60, 70, 80 percent, even more. Today we're building on our unprecedented success with Regeneron committing to offer all new drugs moving forward at most favored nation prices. They'll make their current drugs available to Medicaid and MFN—that's most favored nation. Additionally, they'll also offer their current drug Praluent, which is to lower cholesterol and fight heart disease, to all consumers at dramatically lower cost. It'll come down from $537 to $225 and be available at trump-rx.gov. I didn't put the name; Bobby did and Oz did. It's true, actually. Remember I came in and I said, 'What's this?' But they said it'll be better, it'll sell better. 'Would you help us?' So I now have my name on medication, but it's doing great. I hear it's setting records, actually. I was watching the other day, Mark Cuban of all people said it's amazing what Trump has done with this. I can't believe when I heard him say that; I was sort of in a state of shock, but others have said it too. This whole thing has turned out to be hot. So that's trump-rx.gov. The company will also invest $27 billion to build up research and development and pharmaceutical manufacturing here in America, which is a big thank you, very much. Today I'm also announcing that the FDA has just approved a new drug from Regeneron called Otophemy, a gene therapy curing a rare disease that causes deafness. People are totally deaf and it's amazing; I've seen some work on it. It's actually hard to believe. We're going to talk about it in a minute. Normally cures for rare diseases can cost millions of dollars and really the results aren't that good, but in this case I'm pleased to say that Regeneron will be giving this away for free for a period of time. For free. And here with us is two-year-old Travis Smith. He was born deaf. Was he totally deaf? Pretty much, right? 100%. This is 100%. He was 100% deaf, but he took this miracle cure and now he can hear his mom, Sierra, say, 'I love you.' And Sierra, could you maybe say a few words? That's really incredible. "It's absolutely incredible. Regeneron works miracles. Yeah, he didn't know his name, he couldn't hear me tell him how much I loved him, and now with Regeneron and this amazing surgery he can listen to music and he loves it. He loves to dance and he loves instruments and I'm so proud of him. He's been so brave through all of this. And yeah, now he can hear and he has a bright future ahead of him and I'm so thankful to everybody on the Regeneron team." "Well, that's incredible. I mean, even the fake news has to be impressed with this. They'll find a way to make it a bad story. 'He doesn't hear perfectly,' but he actually does, it's about 1% off. They'll figure a way—how are you going to do it, you've got to explain. No, they're genius, they are genius. I respect them a lot. But I just want to thank you, Sierra. That's incredible for you and your family. He's a beautiful person too; that's a beautiful boy. Thank you for having us. Thank you. Thank you. Thank you. His life is a happier life? 'Oh yeah, he is so full of energy and love now and he was such a serious baby.' So you see such a difference? 'Yeah, he was such a serious baby.' And before that, it was very tough for him? 'Oh for sure, you can't tell me what he wants or what he wants to eat or what he needs and now he'll be able to do that.' Wow. It's life-changing. That's a great job they've done. I want to thank Regeneron and other companies for doing their part to bring down the cost of healthcare for all Americans under our most favored nation agreement. We've secured gigantic discounts with price differences from 4 to 5 and even 600%. 600%! One of the most popular weight loss drugs has gone from $1,350 a month to as low as $199 a month. So that's a price that's incredible. It used to sell in London for $87 and they raised it to $199, which is a big raise from 87 to 199. But that's been going on; our country has been being ripped off for 30 years. How long would you say, Doug? Like 30, 40 years. For years with drugs and drug prices. So they are going up, we are going way down. And because the world is a bigger place than we are—believe it or not, hard to believe that, but they are—we go down much more than they go up, so they don't go up as much as people would have thought. So they are going to 199 and we're going down, all the way down, think of that. We're going down from $1,350 a month to $199. Nobody would believe it. And this is true with all—I mean this is the weight loss drug, but this is true with all drugs. Sometimes even more of a difference than that, sometimes a little bit less, but sometimes substantially more than even that difference, and you see what that is. So we cut the per-cycle cost of IVF drugs by thousands of dollars and we reduced the price of a major COPD medication from $458 to $50. What? That must be a typo. Is that a typo? From $458 to $50. If your boy was here he'd go, 'Wow, we're in.' I guess he got bored. I think he got bored. He wants to go into bigger and better things, but there's nothing bigger and better than this. They'll all be available right now at trump-rx.gov. So it's, you know, that's why it's doing well. It's not doing well for other reasons; it's doing well, think of that—that's some discount. But we have others, and these are all top companies and top medications, and I think in the end we're going to have every company involved. We're almost there right now, but we're going to have every company involved. trump-rx has already received more than 15 million visitors, and every American should log on to check for massive discounts before they go to the pharmacy. Setting a record; nobody's ever seen anything like this. I've actually had many companies—I shouldn't tell you this Bobby, but there've been many companies said, 'Sir, we'd like to buy it from you if you could,' what, trump-rx.com. I said, 'It's not for sale. It's not me, it's the government.' It's the government owns it; I don't own it. But they're all impressed, these pharmaceutical companies. They said they've never seen anything like this. So it's fantastic. Now I'd like to ask Secretary Kennedy to say a few words and then we'll have Dr. Oz, the team at Regeneron and Sierra. And Sierra, I'm so happy for you. And Chris—Chris Klump has been unbelievable, a real star. You don't know his name as much as some of the others but he's a real star of the group. So Bobby, please. Thank you." "Thank you, Mr. President. I was reminded when the president was speaking of a conversation that I had yesterday with one of the Democratic senators who was questioning me during a hearing, and she was ridiculing President Trump for his math. She was saying it's mathematically impossible to have a drug drop by 600% cost, which he had claimed. And I said, 'Well, if the drug was $100 and it raised the price to $600, that would be a 600% rise. But if it drops from 600 to 100, that's a 600% savings.' And the president used that mathematical device to illustrate the magnitude of the theft that has been happening against our country and our people, as he said. We have 4.2% of the world's population. We take 13% of the pharmaceutical drugs. We spend 80% of the biotechnology research in our country and we provide 75% of the profits to the pharmaceutical industry. This is a rip-off that has irked him for 20 years. During his first term, he held the line on drug prices for the first time in history. He came back on this term and he said we're going to lower them this time no matter what. There's people in this room who are part of the White House staff who said, 'We can't do that.' Oz and I said we probably can't do that, but he harassed us; we were beleaguered. It was like Fort Apache. Dr. Oz would not even answer his phone because he's calling all night long with the president on the line saying, 'Get this done.' And we brought in this amazing superstar Chris Klump, and he negotiated these agreements that people said were impossible. I want to thank him for his leadership, Chris, and I want to thank George and Len who are the co-founders of this company, of Regeneron. Their generosity, their idealism, their love of this country and their compassion for these children who are injured. Thank you." "Thank you, Bobby. Amazing. It is amazing. And you're right, I took a lot of heat to say 500, 600, 700. But we also say sometimes 50%, 60%—different kind of calculation. 70, 80 and 90%, and people understand that better. But there's two ways of calculating it, but either way it doesn't make any difference whether it's 60, 70 or 80%. Nobody's ever heard of anything, but it's also 500, 600, 700 depending on the way you want to look at it, the way you word the calculation. It's either way. But Bobby, you're doing a fantastic job. We wanted somebody out of the box and we got him, right? I would say we got him. And Bobby, he's a great guy actually. So Oz, how about saying a few words, please." "So this has been an unprecedented process. The president insisted we deal with one critical number you should all remember, which is that one in three Americans, when they go to the drugstore after they've seen a doctor, will often leave empty-handed because they cannot afford the medications they're picking up. So affordability was the main word the president would use in many of those phone calls; he wanted it to be done fairly. We created a partnership with the pharmaceutical industry that was remarkable in that it pushed 17 massive companies into the fold to try to do deals. Again, unprecedented, that would allow us to claim that they're giving the most favored nation pricing to this country, to the American people. The president would not take no for an answer. We got 16 companies in but not the 17th. How many times do you think we spoke about the 17th company? Too much. 17. He said 17; he wanted it to be 17. Whatever math you want to make, 16 is not 17. There's a reason that the 17th company was delayed—it is why they're here today—it is that beautiful young boy you saw earlier. When you can save a child's hearing... And Mr. President, we talked about a lot of numbers. Secretary Kennedy quoted 100—you know, 100%, 600%. It's hard to beat free. Free is the best price, and that's the starting price with the president oftentimes, and that's what this wonderful company has agreed to. I want to applaud trump-rx.gov, a remarkable site created by George Ebby and Ed Korstein, who have done a wonderful job with zero glitches serving more than 10 million individual unique Americans so far. And Mr. President, it's the fastest website I've ever seen. People love it because it works well. There's all kinds of additional elements being added to it which we'll be talking about. But this website, as the president said, must be checked. It must be checked if you're going to buy a medication to make sure your price is the right price. It's a transparency site that is more rigorous than anything that I have seen. And Mr. President, your name is attached to it and of course that brings a tremendous amount of attention to it. We want it to be perfect, and the team who did that has accomplished that exactly. I want to applaud Chris because again, I can't thank him enough, but it was also John Brooks, Imma Hernandez, I see Beth back there, Theo, Rudel—a bunch of people who have done a tremendous amount of work. And what I keep hearing from the pharmaceutical industry—maybe my colleagues can address in your comments—they never expected to have a counterpart as qualified in the federal government. If you're thinking of serving your nation, this is the right time to do it. This is the right administration to serve you because the president makes it easy to do the right thing. Gentlemen." "Thank you. Thank you, Mr. President. Secretary Kennedy, Secretary Lutnick, Commissioner Makary, Administrator Oz, and I guess superstar Klump. Thanks for having us here on behalf of all Regeneron. For those of you who don't know me, I'm Dr. Len Schleifer, president and CEO of Regeneron, and with me is Dr. George Yancopoulos, president and chief scientific officer of Regeneron. And together—well, I should pause, I just realized something. I'm a president, he's a president, he's a president and all three of us grew up in Queens, New York. So there's something maybe that was going on back then that was good. I'm not sure about now. So anyway, together we founded—George and I founded Regeneron and built it over the past four decades. Today we are here; we were not pushed to be here, we are happy to be here because it marks an important step to lower drug prices. We've been arguing for more than a decade that other wealthy nations have been getting a free ride on American innovation. Just like the defense, but they've been getting a free ride in health defense. And now President Trump is putting policies in place to force these nations to pay their fair share, which will in turn lower prices for Americans. It's a good deal. But we have to remember this is just the beginning. The most important battle we all face is disease itself. We have to recognize how hard this fight is. For most major diseases that you or your loved one might suffer from, like Alzheimer's disease to cancers to genetic diseases, we are very far away from a cure. And we have to understand that discovering cures is perhaps the hardest scientific challenge humans undertake. Much harder than getting to the moon—not that we don't like that—but much harder than even getting to Mars or making the next generation of cell phones or electric cars. Hard, like staring into the unknown for years, sometimes decades, with no guarantee that anything you are doing will ever make a difference for somebody like Travis. Regeneron is a great example both of the difficulty of the challenge but also of the potential reward. We spent 25 years—25 years and billions of dollars, not millions, billions of dollars—before we produced our first important medicine or even earned our first profit. Maybe we should have been fired, but we stuck with it. Our relentless commitment to disruptive innovation ultimately turned us into one of the most productive biotechnology companies in history. We have produced, thanks to George and his team, new medicines at the rate of about one important medicine a year for the last 15 years, including truly miraculous advances impacting millions of lives. And we have two people here who are living proof. Mr. President, when you were ill with COVID, it was Regeneron's monoclonal cocktail that you affectionately dubbed 'the Regeneron.' We love it, and that helped save your life. That was invented by George right here and his team. George." "Well, in addition to the president, we had a few minutes ago Sierra's beautiful boy Travis, who was born without the ability to hear. As you heard from Sierra. And the same company and the same dedicated team of scientists that helped save the president's life also delivered a first-of-its-kind gene therapy known as Otophemy. So Travis can now hear his mother as she tells him that she loves him. Hard to think of a greater gift. And just like you, Mr. President, who made our antibody treatment for COVID free for all Americans and thus saved many lives, we have made the decision—the unprecedented corporate commitment—to provide this first-of-its-kind gene therapy for hearing loss free to all the children in the United States who can benefit from it. And we do this, why? To hopefully highlight the power of disruptive biotech innovation and all the good that it can bring to all of our lives and our health. But we have to remember, despite the miraculous advances like Otophemy and like the Regeneron COVID treatment, we also have to highlight how far we have yet to go to address the many diseases that lack effective treatments. And the cures will not be coming unless we not only celebrate the advances of the biopharma industry to date, but we all work together—private sector together with the government—to not only maintain America's position as world leaders in biotech innovation, but to actually bump up innovation and become even more disruptive. Especially in a world where China is increasing investment and heavily subsidizing its biotech industry and reducing regulatory hurdles so as to try to displace America's leading position. President Trump's efforts to make drugs more affordable, as well as new FDA programs such as the Commissioner's Voucher Program that bring safe and effective new medicines like Otophemy to patients more quickly—these are important steps. One additional important way that I've talked to the administration about to maintain our leading position in the world is to create catalysts and accelerants for our industry and for the healthcare system, such as developing a national healthcare database linked to molecular data, including human sequence data, where Regeneron once again is a world leader. Without such big data sets, powerful new technologies such as AI, despite its potential, will not be as useful as it could be in the quest to develop new medicines and improve healthcare. The biopharma industry of America has and will continue to deliver miraculous breakthroughs. You, Mr. President, and young Travis are living testaments of that. But many more lives need to be helped and saved. We need to attain a new level of disruptive innovation. We have to all work together to meet this challenge. Why? Because lives are at stake. Let me briefly say something. I'm married to a lawyer, my son is a lawyer, but I'm going to talk about the Constitution and I'm not an expert. But I know our Constitution lays out a vision on how America would out-innovate, out-produce, out-thrive every other nation on Earth. And in it you will find a description of a system built on incentive and reward, a system that says take the risk, do the work, and you will share in what you create. That is why they enshrined patent protection directly in the Constitution, not as an afterthought but as a founding principle. As Abraham Lincoln said, another great president, 'Patents add the fuel of interest to the fire of genius.' We must keep that fire burning; what we did here today will do that. Little Travis can now hear for the first time and his potential is endless. Let's make sure he grows up in a country that never stops reaching for the next miracle. Thank you, Mr. President." "Well thank you, great. Great to be with you again. Chris, could you say a few words please?" "Thanks, Mr. President. It's not lost on me that this year is America 250, and it's powerful, I think, to pause and remember that 250 years ago a revolutionary free republic was born, and with it the greatest innovation engine in the history of humanity. And we stand here today, 250 years later, getting to witness yet another groundbreaking innovation that doesn't just treat but cures deafness in children. And because of this president and his policies around most favored nation, it will enable families who are struggling and suffering with this affliction to have access to that groundbreaking therapy for free. Now, as the president mentioned, we've negotiated the 17th of 17 agreements. This represents 86% of the branded pharmaceutical drug market in the United States.
