When you're streaming data, if you've got a stale context, you're going to get hallucinations and all other kinds of problems. How do you know that some part of your data is going stale? We weren't sure and we really struggled with that problem, but it turns out the answer was staring us right in the face. How do you know that this strawberry isn't fresh? Or when it's not fresh? It's when it starts getting spots. It has anomalies. The same is true of data. You can detect when your data and your context is starting to go stale by the rate of anomalies. That means you can already start using all the tools that are in AWS to detect the anomalies and therefore detect when your data is going stale. You can filter noise, you can flag drift, and you can get that cleaner context. For us, without all of that, our pipelines would have been working off outdated information, undermining trust in every output. So when you're watching for anomalies, you don't need to actually taste the strawberry to know that it's gone bad—just look for the spots. Now, a second question for you, another surprising one, another thing that surprised us: would you rather have a 10x larger context window or would you take 10x the token speed? Two years ago, I would have answered this question very differently from how I would answer it today. Today there is absolutely no question for me: it's all about token speed, inference speed. Because our models are no longer just answering monolithic questions. We don't need more tokens in our windows. What we need is our models to run faster. Because now with hundreds or thousands of agents interacting with one another, it's not a marathon anymore, it's a relay race. And how do you improve your relay race? You speed up how fast you can hand off from one to another. So we took our systems, once we realized that we had that epiphany, and we rebuilt everything that we had for inference speed. And this is another place where AWS really came to our advantage. We were able to build a global mesh that went across 16 AWS regions with edge infrastructure. We mapped out all of the inference bottlenecks and in the end we actually achieved a 10x—not a 10 percent, a 10x—improvement in our total inference speed, which is how we got so much more power out of our models. The third lesson for us was: you might have heard the AWS slogan "two is better than none," well it's not always true that two is better than none and that's the case in governance. We started off with two, or maybe far more than two, ways of governing our AI. In the end we discovered what's really important is that you have one set of guardrails, one set of rules that govern everything. In the same way that you only want one captain on your boat, you want one set of rules governing all that you do. If you put all that together, those three lessons just to recap are: use anomaly detection, as that's the best way to figure out if your data has gone stale; if you're going to speed up anything, speed up inference, that's all you really care about; and then third, just get one set of rules. It's a lot of work but that's really what's going to make the difference. We might at Lilly be 149.9 years old but I promise you we don't act like we're a day over 150. We actually really do think of ourselves as more of a tech company. And I'm looking out here recognizing a few folks, I know for many companies here you feel the same way. You're actually like a tech company inside of something bigger in life sciences. This is the most exciting time in my career. AI brings opportunities to apply technology at an entirely new scale than we've ever been able to do before. We're at hundreds of agents today going to thousands of agents from none three years ago. So it's not inconceivable that in another three years we might be talking about millions of agents interacting with each other, replicating so much of the work that we do in our labs or in the rest of the world. But in the end, this is not all about creating agents for the sake of creating agents. This is all about us as humans. We're doing all of this to make lives better for people around the world. Thank you. Thank you Diogo, that was fantastic. It's been so inspiring to be on the journey with Diogo and his leadership team these past several years as they've built everything that Diogo just shared with you. I would like to just drill down on a couple of the points that Diogo made and tie them to what I was sharing earlier. Building for production from day one—that's the Cortex system that Diogo just described, the pro code and low code options. But then also his latter point about having one enterprise-wide platform. You need both of those things and fortunately, we have a solution for you. The second one is becoming a builder. So those low code and no code options are being used widely across Lilly and that is the reason why they've been able to innovate at the pace that Diogo described. Now, we also just heard about some challenges related to data and the need to structure your data for inference and to make sure your data stays fresh. I'd like to spend a minute on another dimension of data because getting your data ready for agents is both important and, frankly, it's hard. It's harder than almost all of us realized initially. Why? This is how your data is currently structured at most organizations. This structure of data is designed for an end user who is a human being. There should be no surprises to anybody in the room about this—if you're remotely involved in technology, this is the standard stack for data, governance, and then the actual end user applications and UX. What's the problem here? Well, I'm going to show you the problem. This is an example of a recent agent interaction where the end user, the human, asks the question: "Which HCPs in Dr. Harper's referral network are under-targeted relative to their prescribing potential?" A very natural, normal question in a commercial organization at a biopharma. The agent could not begin to answer this question. And if it did try to answer the question, it would have given a wrong answer. Why? Number one: there is no data source anywhere in the CRM for the agent to figure out what a referral network even is. How do you define under-targeted? Relative to what? How do you define prescribing potential? Across all three of these in one prompt, the agent is going to get stuck. And the reason is agents need context. We walk around with an enormous amount of what we consider to be common sense or common knowledge. It is neither. And so what you need to do is to get your common knowledge and common sense somehow explicated in a manner that the agents are going to be able to figure out how to disambiguate the question and go to the right source and interpret it the right way. We have a technical track later today in the tech building track that explains how we do this. But in general, it's called the semantic hub. And what we do with the semantic hub is we take the industry and company specific ontologies, taxonomies, business glossaries, and metadata and we put them into various types of data stores. It might be a knowledge graph, it might be a SQL database, sometimes it's in a data catalog. The details don't matter for now, but they matter a lot; you can learn about it both in that tech builder track and the J&J track that's in the commercial breakout. But what matters is if you do that work, then the agent is able to answer a question like that. Now this text is a little small so I'm going to share it with you. This is a real example that once we implemented that semantic hub, the agent was able to not only find the referrers in the referral network, but was able to identify specifically how under-targeted they are and specifically what the recommended frequency of calls would be. So in the first case it's Dr. O'Connor, and the agent is recommending four times per year and it's actually forecasting the incremental revenue per quarter if that plan is adopted. It shows—which is always very important with AI—what was the basis, the rationale for this recommendation. Now, just to give you a quick sense—again, we'll go into this in these breakout sessions—of how the agent did this: what the agent had to do going through the semantic hub was figure out the following. If Dr. Harper sees a patient and within the next 30 days that patient is prescribed a drug by a different doctor, but it's for the same condition that we know the patient likely saw Dr. Harper for, well, that's how the agent can start to intuit what a referral relationship is. That's what it actually did in this case. So that's data. You need to build a semantic hub for your data. The second thing you need to do is to have a platform for building agents, deploying agents, and operating effectively at scale. Building the type of capability that Diogo just shared with you is a very heavy lift. But it's necessary in order to address all the challenges I spoke about earlier that cause pilots to stall. You have to take account of all of these factors. You have to have secure memory so you don't have confidential information or patient information sitting around in an agent beyond the time that it should be running. You need a runtime. Right now when you go and build an agent on your own, if you're not using a platform like I'm describing, somebody's not only got to figure out how the agent should work but you then actually got to instantiate it as a web service. You have to have observability, you have to have guardrails. This is an enormous amount of work and you need to do it all. Here's another thing you don't think about when you're just building a pilot: you think about the agents you've built. In reality, think about the world Diogo described. You have hundreds and hundreds of agents that all sorts of other teams are building, and not only is it across your enterprise but increasingly it's from your other vendors. So you're going to have your vendor's agents, your homegrown agents, and you're going to have agents that are coming in the future that you haven't even thought of yet. How are they all going to interoperate? What if they're using different protocols? What if they're using different frameworks? You're going to take all that on your own shoulders and assume that what you built in a pilot is going to work with all those agents? That's not going to work. You have to take this into consideration from day one and that's why we introduced Amazon Bedrock AgentCore. It takes care of all of those things. It is being widely adopted for that reason across the industry and, candidly, the big pharma customers in the room already know this; most of them are already adopting AgentCore. So why am I spending so much time on it? Because we have a lot of partners, meaning vendors in the room as well. And so if there's one thing I want you to take away from this talk if you are a supplier to mid-sized and large life sciences organizations, it is that many of them will be asking you to bring your agentic solutions to them built for AgentCore. That is how you are going to be able to scale and help them scale. So that's building, deploying, and operating agents at scale. I just want to spend one more minute on the build part though. Because it is important to become a builder as I've mentioned and it's important to meet each of your users where they are. That means you need different types of build tools. As Diogo said, at Lilly they have pro code and they have low code. There are three main tools that we have developed and are seeing widely adopted across the industry and, again, they're for different levels of complexity and different types of users: Strands Agents, Kiro, and Amazon Quick. We have lots of breakout sessions about each of these today. The key point I want to make is wherever, at whatever technical level somebody has—if they have no computer background, no computer science background, no coding, not even scripting—we have tools that allow them just with a consumer-grade user interface to define what their goal is and the AI will take it from there. The second point I really want to be clear about is this: the opportunities in AI apply across the value chain. And for many of the most common use cases, you don't need to be building most of the solution yourself; there is still going to be some building. But because we've been working now with all of you for over three years, we and you together have built a massive repository of agents that just work for the use cases that already have been proven to show a positive ROI. And so before you go off and say, "Hey I heard Dan say we got to go start building, we're going to go build," great—that's the right attitude. But first, also check with us: which agents do we already have that we can just give you so you can have a quick start? Oftentimes we can give you 60 to 70 percent of what you need for the most common use cases and this, again, is across all the different parts of the enterprise. And then you spend the 30 to 40 percent customizing it and hardening it for your environment, for your compliance, and for your end users and your data. Okay, so I'm going to now go through some examples of what we consider to be industry-leading use cases for application of AI across R&D, clinical, and commercial. And we're going to start with our work with Genentech, which those of you who have been with us at the symposium for the past two years will know that we have been collaborating with Genentech in a couple of areas. The two main areas—and frankly, these are the two main categories in research where we are seeing the greatest acceleration across all of our customer base—is number one in use cases involving search and analysis of knowledge and data that already exists. This is the class of products that we typically call co-scientist or research assistants. We at AWS, we call this the Research Agent. We've been collaborating with Genentech now for over two years on just how far we can pull research agents to drive change and faster time to insights for their researchers. And I recently sat down with Aviv Regev, the EVP of Research and Early Development at Genentech, to share a little bit about a check-in regarding the progress we've made together. 50 years ago, in 1976 when Genentech was founded, was a very special moment in science. There was a revolution going on at that time, and that revolution was molecular biology. Just like 50 years ago, there's another revolution that's happening right now and that's what's cool about exactly right now. And that revolution now is AI. We bring together the best science and technology in order to make better medicines for patients and do it faster. We have developed the research agent together and we have deployed it. And so it's in the hands of scientists, of small molecule medicinal chemists, of antibody engineers, of biologists. Synthesis planning—they do it three to five times faster. That means that they save maybe 30 to 60 hours a quarter. Or a query of compound—an advanced, complicated query when you have a complicated idea in your head—might take you 60 minutes. Instead it takes three minutes now, 95 percent faster. You are now at the speed of your own thoughts. And then my personal favorite is now people have 15 years of screening that was locked in all sorts of assays and databases and so on at their fingertips. It would've taken months. When something takes months of manual extraction, it's not that you waste months, it's that you never do it at all. And so all that golden information, all the hard work of many, many chemists across many, many programs every single year was just not yours. Even though it was right here on our campus, it was not in your hands at all. And now it is. So, it's not just that it saved them time and made them happier. Their chain of thought is better now. Their brilliance is spent on the most important thing now, and they can do things that they couldn't have done before. That's what research agents help us do. So I'm also really proud of our people for having been willing to kind of take this bet, which is uncomfortable and new and doesn't always actually work the first time we try it out or even the second time. And try, try again. That's a very Genentech thing that we do because, you know, our North Star is our patient and they need us to try very hard. It's bringing in the cloud expertise, the AI expertise, and the software engineering expertise of a partner like AWS. You put them together, you do great things. If everyone just tries to solve it on their own, they'll do something, but it's not going to be as big and it's not going to be as impactful and it won't deliver as much for patients. That's been a very exciting journey, and we've over the past two years pursued research agent engagements with many of our customers and we're increasingly seeing a positive impact. One of the reasons is the rate at which it's become easier to build the tools that the agent needs to call. So many of you have seen this diagram before as we've talked about this journey over the past two years. The way these systems are constructed is you have one supervisor agent; it builds a plan when you ask it a question and it's going to break that question down into a discrete series of steps. Each step is then going to result in calling a given sub-agent or tool. In the case of Genentech, we have tools specifically for their small molecule researchers and a different set of tools for their large molecule researchers, and some of them are common, like the ones you can see at the top. But here's the thing: even one year ago, building one of these tools took—well, it took many weeks. And what we're encountering now is we can now build these tools in what used to take weeks, we can do in five minutes. I'm going to show you a very quick example. So this is an example of us using the Kiro tool that I introduced a few minutes ago and building a tool that will access the OpenTargets consortium data. OpenTargets is a consortium of multiple both commercial and non-profit research organizations that are developing data to help researchers identify and prioritize the right targets. Historically their data's only been available via an API or via their web app. Many of our customers would like to have their agents be able to access these research agent type use cases. But as I said, that typically would take many weeks for us to build. In this case, we just gave it the instructions using the Strands SDK—sorry, using Kiro—and then Kiro went and built the agent, invoked the Strands SDK, which then deployed the agent into Amazon Bedrock AgentCore. In five minutes—this is about 90 seconds we have shortened it just for the sake of brevity here in this talk—but this literally in real time takes five minutes. Not only to build the agent but to deploy it into a production environment with all of the enterprise security, observability, and all the other policy capabilities that we all know are necessary. So it's really a wonderful example of what you can now empower your teams to do. So discovery and access to insights of knowledge that already exists is the first category of how AI is accelerating research. The second one is the creation of new knowledge. And the primary way that our customers of all sizes are using AI to develop new knowledge is through what's called lab-in-the-loop. In fact, every one of the companies that I referenced at the start of the talk this morning uses lab-in-the-loop; we've been engaged with many of these companies for years as the lab-in-the-loop architecture has evolved. There is no world in which we will actually test all possible combinations in all cell types under all the conditions because there aren't enough cells on the planet to do that. But there is possibly a world where we get to an AI model that actually encodes in itself having seen enough possibilities that we have not measured. And if we get to that world, then our ability to deliver medicines for patients will be radically transformed. And I think that makes it incredibly worthy to try. And that brought us to this strategy that we have for the last five, almost six years now, called Lab-in-the-loop. I don't have to make the prediction perfectly; I just have to make it better because I have a lab and in the lab I can test. So I can use the old experiments from the lab, but I can make new experiments in the lab. This is a way to find the answer even when you don't know the answer. And that's the way scientists operate. But really the most exciting part is it is now in the scientist's hands. It's no longer like something done by special people who do AI. This is a lab-in-the-loop, which means it's not just one suggestion from the AI and then everyone goes back to doing things the same way. It's after the synthesis, after the data comes back, it's another iteration and another iteration. AI is not alone. You need to feed it with data. The data needs to be accessible. It starts on an instrument. It somehow has to end up in the right place. And a lot of it is legacy data. And one day it'll be relevant and will be discovered—hence foundational data sets for foundation models. Every project gets better, but the key is all projects get better. There is also a substantial dependence on compute, and it's many aspects of compute. So obviously you need models and algorithms and so on, but you actually need computational powers, GPUs. This is one of the areas where we had a great partnership together, relying on super robust cloud infrastructure that really allows us to do amazing computational work. So we have, depending on which program, a hundred percent of our antibody programs have an AI component in. We have, for example, an antibody program where AI helped us identify what we call an immunogenicity risk, which is one of those things that can actually make a therapeutic not work once you put it inside a patient's body. And because the AI didn't just recognize the risk, it actually helped us solve it because it suggested how would you erase that problem from your molecule and still maintain all of its good properties. This is one of our fastest moving programs ever. It also reduced the risk of the program. It made it more likely to be successful. So lab-in-the-loop is an incredibly powerful new approach for drug research. That's the good news. The less good news is it's really hard to implement and operate at enormous scale. At most of our customers, there are components of lab-in-the-loop happening, but they're only components or it's only a small team. And so we've spent a lot of time exploring with customers what are the things that are holding you back from reaching the full potential with this technology. The answers we got really fall into three categories. One: just the technical complexity and the fact that, as a result, you have these precious teams of computational biologists that you hired to do science and they're spending a significant, sometimes a majority, of their time not doing science but instead writing scripts and building pipelines and fixing pipelines and testing new models. The second challenge is that small group is the only group that's actually able to do lab-in-the-loop, and most of the other biologists or chemists across the organization are still doing their job the way they used to be because they have not had access—it's not been democratized. The second set of challenges is what Aviv just referenced regarding the needle in the haystack problem. The fact that there are so many molecules you could design—there's more theoretical designs for an antibody molecule than there are atoms in the universe squared. So even if you are using AI to design lots of molecules, you're actually in some ways creating a bigger problem for yourself because you can't possibly test all of them. So you need decision science tools to help you narrow the search space. And then the third problem that we've heard from folks is there's a lot of integration challenges. The wet labs and the in-silico systems have not been designed to work together; it creates a lot of problems, both data problems and organizational problems. Unify and accelerate closed-loop AI drug discovery. INTRODUCING Amazon Bio Discovery. I'm very glad to announce we are introducing a new product today, and it is called Amazon Bio Discovery. It is designed to solve all three of these problems. Now I'm going to walk you through some of the many features and capabilities of Amazon Bio Discovery. The first is we have all of the models in one place: all the open source models, all the models you develop internally, models you develop through federated learning, and models you license from commercial vendors. And they're all benchmarked. So all that effort that you go through, that your teams go through every day—they read an article and they say, "Oh there's this brand new model, how are you going to find it, how are you going to get it, download it, do your testing"—all that's taken care of. You can just go to Amazon Bio Discovery, we will have the latest model there, we will have it benchmarked. And that will include models from the Chan Zuckerberg Biohub. So we're announcing this today: Chan Zuckerberg Biohub, it's the first large-scale scientific initiative building frontier AI with both frontier biology and frontier AI to solve disease. Their ESM2 model is available at launch today with Amazon Bio Discovery. We're adding the ESM Cambrian model in the near future and Biohub is committed to making future models available on Amazon Bio Discovery as soon as they are released publicly. So what this means is you no longer need to go to—we love Hugging Face, but you no longer need to go to Hugging Face, download the model, do all the work to get it set up in your system, and do all the testing. It's just going to be there. I mentioned benchmarking. Benchmarking is one of the most challenging and yet important activities customers always tell us about as a pain point for their AI biology systems. Because when a lab announces a new model and announces their test results, how do you make use of it? Our customers always say that doesn't help us that much, we need to bring it in-house. We need to do our own testing, and yet you're all reinventing the wheel. So why don't we do this for you? Why don't we create a set of standardized tests using a very large and very diverse antibody dataset? That's what we've done and we've already benchmarked all these models that I just showed you, and we are making the commitment that as their new versions come out, we will again benchmark them. We've also validated the tests that we've done in a partnership with a lab at Johns Hopkins University, and we will continue to have independent validation for these benchmarks. We have included, as I said, the ability to bring your own models and to bring commercial models. So we have partners like Profluent, a commercial model vendor, that you will also be able to see if you've licensed their model. That will be in that catalog, and then you will be able to fine-tune your own results. The lab-in-the-loop process: after you narrow the candidate list down and you send your most promising candidates to the wet lab, you get the test results back. What happens? Then you start the cycle again; you feed those test results into the model and you fine-tune the model before the next cycle of AI modeling. Every time you do that, we will be adjusting the Spearman score, which is the benchmark for your own model, so you can see: are my models getting better? In fact, are my models now better than the models that I was previously using from open source? Or how do they compare with the commercial models that I've licensed? We're also very excited about the progress that's happening in creating models through federated learning. This has been an area of tremendous theoretical potential for a long time, but it's only been in the last couple of years that, for most of our customers, federated learning has started to have a huge impact. Why is federated learning so important? It's because long-term, the most important thing is not a model architecture—typically model architecture advances and others catch up within a matter of months. What matters is the data. And there's just not enough training data, especially for the hardest problems like toxicity, but also for structure prediction. And so each of our customers has been experimenting more with federated learning. One of the startups is Rhino Federated Computing; they're in the startup showcase here today and they are the technology that Lilly Tune Lab uses—excellent technology built in AWS. We're also very excited about the series of federated learning models that Apheris has made possible. And in particular, the Apheris-powered AISB Network, which sets a new operational standard for industrial federation, hosted on AWS. So the AISB is a collection of nine and growing large pharmaceutical organizations who are collaborating to design models. In particular, the model that a subset of them—these five pharma—have developed and trained together is available to those five companies at the launch of Amazon Bio Discovery. So they can access it and use it in their pipelines as of today. The chart on the right here shows you why the power of federated learning is so significant. This is the OpenFold 3 structure prediction model, which I know many of you are familiar with. On their own, just using their own proprietary in-house data, on average these five companies were able to improve the model from having 34 percent to 39 percent reliable predictions for binding. But by collaborating, they got that up to 51 percent. So this is very exciting: both the power of the models and the ability to use these models easily in pipelines and integrate with the rest of your system, including with benchmarking. This is why you see this quote from Justin at Johnson & Johnson, an example of the overall enthusiasm for how federated learning is transforming research and how tools like Amazon Bio Discovery can really democratize that for more researchers. So we have all the models in one place benchmarked. The next thing is we have the ability to help the researcher figure out which model to use or which set of models to use in what sequence, depending on their goal. Are they starting de novo or are they starting with an existing seed and they just want to use guided evolution to evolve that design? Either way, we can take those benchmarks and the system—the agent—takes the benchmarks and guides the user as to which models to use in what order. Now oftentimes—this actually surprised us a little bit in all of our user interviews for this product—scientists would tell us, "Look, one problem you could really help me with is I think I've got a pretty good idea of where I should be targeting on the antigen the hotspot residue, but you know, I'm not always sure." So we put a science team behind that and built into the product another agent that helps figure out and confirm that you've got the right hotspot residue that you're targeting. It provides recommendations but also with the scientific rationale and references. So if you want to adjust the parameters, you can explore alternative approaches; it will go and find new hotspot recommendations and always give you the rationale for why it made that recommendation. And now I'm going to get to what I think is potentially the most significant benefit, again, based on user feedback about their challenges. And that's the following: when you use one of these AI systems, as I mentioned, it can generate an infinite number of ideas. It's very common for the first step of the process using RF Diffusion and models like that to generate 500,000 recommendations—or 100,000, or a million. So how do you take that if you can only test, you know, a thousand or 5,000 of those in your wet lab per week? How do you narrow down that list? So what we've built is a multi-parameter optimization—it's Pareto-based—that narrows that down by comparing all of the parameters against each other. This is a very hard problem because each one of those 500,000 is going to have its own score across the five or six or ten different parameters you're optimizing for, and the more you weight one of them, the more you're going to negatively impact others. So we've built this into the system to make sure that you are picking the right candidates, and it's not just a single cycle. You can run this and you can go from 500,000 down to say 300,000 and then run it again, look again, re-optimize, and keep going until you get to the number that you can afford to go test in the lab. And that's what's shown on this slide where you've gotten it down to the top 92 candidates. Now, the lab-in-the-loop has this word called "lab." And so the question is: how and when do you get the orders to the lab and how do you get them back? Now, long term we know that most of our customers would like to be using their existing in-house labs, and we support that as well. But in the short... and I'll come to that. Right now we're giving you a system you can use out of the box. And the reason you can use it out of the box is because we've integrated with leading CROs like Twist, Ginkgo Bioworks, and Aalpha Bio so that you can simply—once you've narrowed down that list—have an Amazon-like experience where you can see: if I go with this vendor, what is going to be the cost and what is going to be the turnaround time? And then you send it off to them, they send it back, and immediately you see it inside of—you get a notification—it's inside of your Amazon Bio Discovery system and then you can restart the cycle. I mentioned we're also able to integrate Amazon Bio Discovery with your in-house labs. I do not want to trivialize this. We have been working with many of the big pharma represented here today for several years on programs to get their existing labs into the cloud. This is a non-trivial effort because most of these systems were not designed to be cloud-based. We have made a lot of progress and agents have been a great ally for us in this regard. And so as a result, we've been able to develop a number of agents that not only automate getting your data from your instruments into the cloud, but also automate getting that data as part of the lab-in-the-loop process. So we're going to talk a lot more about this in a breakout session led by Sanofi this afternoon because they're really an example of what we think good looks like in terms of having data across both the in-silico side and the wet lab side that's starting to function as one integrated system across the enterprise. So that's this afternoon in the research track. Now, in that second video from—we saw with Aviv, Aviv talked about the importance of compute. And when you're talking about compute, you need to—you know, it immediately raises the question: where am I going to do my compute? And this is a hard question to answer, but I want you to park that for one second. The second question that you're going to have to be studying is: how am I going to automate my labs? It's one thing just to get the existing lab systems that you have—the existing data from the existing assays that you have—into the cloud. But if you really want to start operating at the throughput that lab-in-the-loop ends up driving, you're also going to have to automate, like in the video that Diogo showed of robotic labs. In both of these areas, AWS and NVIDIA are deeply collaborating. We are developing joint solutions on recommendations for how to scale your compute infrastructure, but also how to deploy robotics in a manner that will allow your own wet labs to scale and then integrate through the agents that I just talked about with the rest of your lab-in-the-loop system. There's a wonderful session today in the research track led by Lila Sciences where you can learn about their journey doing all of that. Now, the final dimension of Amazon Bio Discovery I'd like to surface for you is a problem you may not have already thought about. But it's one that we are very concerned about. It is coming faster than most folks expect, and it's going to be a nightmare if you don't plan for it now. And that is the following: Lab-in-the-loop systems generate an enormous amount of data. Think about everything that I just walked through. Every target that you decide to focus on, every hotspot residue, and every candidate you ever design in silico. All the processes you use to narrow down that list, the subset that you actually sent to the wet lab, and the test results you got back from the wet lab. All of this data is valuable. But it's only going to be of use if you store it in a manner where you can find it and interrogate it later. So we're also building that capability into Amazon Bio Discovery. However, the problem is even greater than I just described, because what I just described was the data problem and therefore the insights problem for one lab-in-the-loop system. Every big pharma that I talk with—and that's just about all of them—is on a journey to have multiple of these systems. They're probably going to have at least one for their small molecule team and one for their large molecule team. They might be part of a federated learning network like I just described. They might have multiple partnerships with commercial vendors like Chai and Latent and Nabla. Each of these systems is a silo. If you are not thinking right now about how you are going to manage all the data from this in an enterprise-wide manner, you are going to create brand new silos. So we are on a journey, as part of Amazon Bio Discovery, to make sure that does not happen. As I mentioned at launch, we already have the experiment registry that allows the data from the experiments that you're doing to be captured and analyzed. But we are also working on the roadmap for Amazon Discovery for an enterprise-wide system of record and system of analysis, so that you can draw insights from your work with one commercial model vendor that benefits your work with your own models. Or you may realize, "We shouldn't do that experiment; we've already done that experiment. We've already paid for that." So this is something that we think is extremely important long term, and the value of which will be increasingly obvious as we evolve on this journey together. Now, I'm delighted to announce that we built the system that I just described and then found a wonderful science partner in Memorial Sloan Kettering to help us validate that the antibody candidates designed by Amazon Bio Discovery actually work. To share more about that, I'd like to welcome Dr. Dash, VP of entrepreneurship and commercialization at MSK, to share more about that journey first hand. Thanks, Dan. I'm Yashodhara Dash. I'm the VP of entrepreneurship and commercialization at Memorial Sloan Kettering Cancer Center. My office supports the full cycle of innovation and commercialization at MSK, from translational research to strategic partnerships, venture creation, and commercialization. I'm here to share how MSK is partnering with AWS to bring AI into the hands of our researchers and what that means for our patients. MSK exists for one reason: to end cancer for life. We're one of the world's leading cancer centers and the oldest private cancer center in the U.S. This means over 135 research labs and over 1,800 active clinical trials. MSK researchers have contributed to more than 40 FDA drug approvals since 2019. We've created over 50 spinout companies from MSK-originated science. We have the ability to take innovation from the bench to the bedside—from lab discovery all the way through clinical trials into actual patient care. That's our competitive advantage and it's our responsibility. To achieve our mission, we recognize the need for a technology partner that could operate at our scale. We formalized a multi-year strategic partnership with AWS spanning five different workstreams. First, translational AI funding through our AI TDF program—this is gap funding for promising AI innovations. Second, startup innovation through our innovation hub accelerator. Third, enterprise AI tools, including our AI portal which now serves over 2,400 MSK staff. Fourth, data curation—so using LLMs to unlock our vast clinical data for research and drug discovery. And then AI drug discovery, which is the focus of what I'm talking about today. Within drug discovery, Amazon Bio Discovery is the application that we're exploring as a launch partner. It's a modular platform, as Dan described, for closed-loop AI-powered drug design. So let me tell you a little bit about the challenges that are facing our scientific researchers today. Picture a world-class MSK scientist with decades of years of studying a novel cancer target. They know the biology better than anyone and they're ready to design a drug, but the traditional pathway is not really built for academic labs. The theoretical design space is enormous. As some people have referred to already, the number of possible antibody sequences that an investigator would need to search through to design is more than the number of atoms in the observable universe squared. And this is why no human team can do this alone. The academic postdocs are focused on basic research and publications, not computational drug design. If they partner with an external biotech, MSK doesn't get the appropriate value for the discovery. Also, drug discovery and development, as everyone knows, can cost tens to hundreds of millions of dollars, and timelines can stretch over four years just to get to a lead candidate stage. So the investigator is really stuck: deep expertise, a clear target, but no practical path to drug design. And that's the gap that we wanted to close, not just for one lab but across our entire research enterprise. Now picture that same investigator with access to Amazon Bio Discovery. The same lab can now design antibody sequences in-house using biological foundation models. They can go from drug target to candidate molecules in months, not years. So think about all that that changes: months versus years from target hypothesis to wet-lab validated data; in-house versus outsourced, so our scientists can run experiments directly with no queue and no external contract. It is iterative versus one-shot—rapid design-test-learn cycles with integrated wet-lab workflows—and accessible versus gated, so any MSK lab can use this, not just those with dedicated computational teams. That's what democratizing access to AI drug design looks like to us in practice. We were really excited to take this platform for a spin for the first time. The Nai-Kong Cheung lab at MSK identified a promising new target for desmoplastic small round cell tumor, which is a rare and pretty aggressive pediatric cancer. They wanted to design nanobody therapeutics against this target, but they faced significant obstacles of the type that I described earlier. There was no experimental protein structure for the target and no publicly available therapeutic antibody database for it. Traditional lab methods would take years to design against this type of a target. So this was exactly the type of challenge where AI-guided design could make the biggest difference. So we brought it to Amazon Bio Discovery. And here's what we achieved together: the pipeline started with recipe creation and four structure prediction models to predict the target structure from sequence. Then hotspot identification, which Dan described a little earlier, found eight binding hotspots using seven bioinformatics tools. Bio Discovery generated around 300,000 nanobody candidates from scratch using de novo methods including RF Antibody, IGM and Ember. These were filtered to 100,000 candidates using Pareto multi-objective optimization, and 116 enriched candidates were then sent for wet-lab screening. The results were that 46 of those 116 were confirmed as binders, and the top candidate achieved sub-nanomolar binding affinity. The timeline from target to 100,000 de novo designs sent to the lab was weeks, against a target that no one had ever designed against before. The Cheung lab was our first co-development partner, but adoption is growing. Six MSK labs now have active Bio Discovery accounts with over 10 individual researchers working with it. Our investigators are accelerating discovery cycles, designing therapeutics in timelines that would have been unimaginable with traditional methods. Active use cases include de novo antibody/nanobody design, machine learning guided antibody evolution for radioisotope therapy, and antibody discovery for novel high-value targets. As Dr. Cheung has said, we're really glad to join forces with Amazon Bio Discovery to develop the next generation of antibodies that'll speed up the process to help patients worldwide. This is about putting powerful tools directly in the hands of scientists who are closest to the biology. What if breakthrough cancer treatments could be designed in months, not years? In the near term, we're validating AI-designed molecules, with the first antibodies undergoing wet-lab validation at MSK right now. In the medium term, AI-discovered antibodies will enter MSK's clinical pipeline—the same infrastructure that was behind the 40-plus FDA approvals in six years. Long term, we believe that MSK can be a blueprint for how academic medicine embeds AI across its research enterprise, training the next generation of researchers, launching companies, and setting new standards. Our mission hasn't changed. We still exist to end cancer for life. AI won't do that alone, but it will help us get there faster for more patients sooner. Thank you. That's been a very exciting journey, and we've over the past two years pursued research agent engagements with many of our customers. We're increasingly seeing a positive impact, and one of the reasons is the rate at which it's become easier to build the tools that the agent needs to call. Many of you have seen this diagram before as we've talked about this journey over the past two years. The way these systems are constructed is you have one supervisor agent. It builds a plan when you ask it a question, and it's going to break that question down into a discrete series of steps. Each step is then going to result in calling a given sub-agent or tool. In the case of Genentech, we have tools specifically for their small molecule researchers and a different set of tools for their large molecule researchers, and some of them are common like the ones you can see at the top. But here's the thing: even one year ago, building one of these tools took many weeks. What we're encountering now is we can now build these tools in what used to take weeks—we can do in five minutes. I'm going to show you a very quick example. This is an example of us using the Kiro tool that I introduced a few minutes ago and building a tool that will access the OpenTargets consortium data. OpenTargets is a consortium of multiple both commercial and non-profit research organizations that are developing data to help researchers identify and prioritize the right targets. Historically, their data's only been available via an API or via their web app. Many of our customers would like to have their agents be able to access these in research agent type use cases. But as I said, that typically would take many weeks for us to build. In this case, we just gave it the instructions using Kiro, and then Kiro went and built the agent, invoked the Strands SDK, which then deployed the agent into Amazon Bedrock AgentCore. In five minutes—and we have shortened it just for the sake of brevity here—but this literally in real time takes five minutes. Not only to build the agent but to deploy it into a production environment with all of the enterprise security, observability, and all the other policy capabilities that we all know are necessary. So it's really a wonderful example of what you can now empower your teams to do. Lab-in-the-loop is an incredibly powerful new approach for drug research. That's the good news. The less good news is it's really hard to implement and operate at enormous scale. At most of our customers, there are components of lab-in-the-loop happening, but they're only components or it's only a small team. So we've spent a lot of time exploring with customers what are the things that are holding you back from reaching the full potential with this technology. The answers we got really fall into three categories. One: just the technical complexity and the fact that as a result, you have these precious teams of computational biologists that you hired to do science. They're spending a significant—sometimes a majority—of their time not doing science but instead writing scripts and building pipelines and fixing pipelines and testing new models. The second challenge is that small group is the only group that's actually able to do lab-in-the-loop, and most of the other biologists or chemists across the organization are still doing their job the way they used to. They have not had access; it's not been democratized. The second set of challenges is what Aviv just referenced regarding the needle in the haystack problem. The fact that there are so many molecules you could design—there's more theoretical designs for an antibody molecule than there are atoms in the universe squared. So even if you are using AI to design lots of molecules, you're actually in some ways creating a bigger problem for yourself because you can't possibly test all of them. You need decision science tools to help you narrow the search space. And then the third problem that we've heard from folks is there's a lot of integration challenges. The wet labs and the in-silico systems have not been designed to work together. It creates a lot of problems, both data problems and organizational problems. Unify and accelerate closed-loop AI drug discovery. INTRODUCING Amazon Bio Discovery. I'm very glad to announce we are introducing a new product today, and it is called Amazon Bio Discovery. It is designed to solve all three of these problems. Now I'm going to walk you through some of the many features and capabilities of Amazon Bio Discovery. The first is we have all of the models in one place. All the open source models, all the models you develop internally, models you develop through federated learning, and models you license from commercial vendors. And they're all benchmarked. So all that effort that your teams go through every day—they read an article and they say, "Oh there's this brand new model"—all that's taken care of. How are you going to find it, how are you going to get it, download it, do your testing? You can just go to Amazon Bio Discovery. We will have the latest model there, and we will have it benchmarked. And that will include models from the Chan Zuckerberg Biohub. So we're announcing this today: Chan Zuckerberg Biohub. It's the first large-scale scientific initiative building frontier AI with both frontier biology and frontier AI to solve disease. Their ESM2 model is available at launch today with Amazon Bio Discovery. We're adding the ESM Cambrian model in the near future and Biohub is committed to making future models available on Amazon Bio Discovery as soon as they are released publicly. What this means is you no longer need to go to Hugging Face—we love Hugging Face—but you no longer need to go to Hugging Face, download the model, do all the work to get it set up in your system, and do all the testing. It's just going to be there. I mentioned benchmarking. Benchmarking is one of the most challenging and yet important activities customers always tell us about as a pain point for their AI biology systems. Because when a lab announces a new model and announces their test results, how do you make use of it? Our customers always say that doesn't help us that much; we need to bring it in-house. We need to do our own testing, and yet you're all reinventing the wheel. So why don't we do this for you? Why don't we create a set of standardized tests using a very large and very diverse antibody dataset? That's what we've done, and we've already benchmarked all these models that I just showed you, and we are making the commitment that as their new versions come out, we will again benchmark them. We've also validated the tests that we've done in a partnership with a lab at Johns Hopkins University. We will continue to have independent validation for these benchmarks. We have included, as I said, the ability to bring your own models and to bring commercial models. So we have partners like Profluent, a commercial model vendor, that you will also be able to see. If you've licensed their model, that will be in that catalog, and then you will be able to fine-tune your own results. The lab-in-the-loop process, after you narrow the candidate list down and you send your most promising candidates to the wet lab, you get the test results back. What happens? Then you start the cycle again. You feed those test results into the model and you fine-tune the model before the next cycle of AI modeling. So every time you do that, we will be adjusting the Spearman score, which is the benchmark for your own model so you can see: are my models getting better? In fact, are my models now better than the models that I was previously using from open source? Or how do they compare with the commercial models that I've licensed? We're also very excited about the progress that's happening in creating models through federated learning. This has been an area of tremendous theoretical potential for a long time, but it's only been in the last couple of years that for most of our customers, federated learning has started to have a huge impact. Why is federated learning so important? It's because long term, the most important thing is not a model architecture—typically model architecture advances and others catch up within a matter of months. What matters is the data. And there's just not enough training data, especially for the hardest problems like toxicity but also for structure prediction. So each of our customers has been experimenting more with federated learning. One of the startups is Rhino Federated Computing; they're in the startup showcase here today and they are the technology that Lilly Tune Lab uses. It's an excellent technology built in AWS. We're also very excited about the series of federated learning models that Apheris has made possible. And in particular, the Apheris-powered AISB Network, which sets a new operational standard for industrial federation hosted on AWS. The AISB is a collection of nine and growing large pharmaceutical organizations who are collaborating to design models. In particular, the one that a subset of these five pharma have developed and trained together is available to those five companies at the launch of Amazon Bio Discovery. They can access it and use it in their pipelines as of today, and the chart on the right here shows you why the power of federated learning is so significant. This is the OpenFold 3 structure prediction model. On their own, just using their own proprietary in-house data, on average these five companies were able to improve the model from having 34 percent to 39 percent of reliable predictions for binding. But by collaborating, they got that up to 51 percent. So this is very exciting—both the power of the models and the ability to use these models easily in pipelines and integrate with the rest of your system, including with benchmarking. This is why you see this quote from Justin at Johnson & Johnson, an example of the overall enthusiasm for how federated learning is transforming research and how tools like Amazon Bio Discovery can really democratize that for more researchers. So we have all the models in one place benchmarked. The next thing is we have the ability to help the researcher figure out which model to use or which set of models to use in what sequence, depending on their goal. Are they starting de novo, or are they starting with an existing seed and they just want to use guided evolution to evolve that design? Either way, we can take those benchmarks and the agent takes the benchmarks and guides the user as to which models to use in what order. Now oftentimes—this actually surprised us a little bit in all of our user interviews—scientists would tell us, "Look, one problem you could really help me with is I think I've got a pretty good idea of where I should be targeting on the antigen the hotspot residue, but you know I'm not always sure." So we put a science team behind that and built into the product another agent that helps figure out and confirm that you've got the right hotspot residue that you're targeting. It provides recommendations but also with the scientific rationale and references. So if you want to adjust the parameters, you can explore alternative approaches. It will go and find new hotspot recommendations and always give you the rationale for why it made that recommendation. And now I'm going to get to what I think is potentially the most significant benefit again based on user feedback. When you use one of these AI systems, as I mentioned, it can generate an infinite number of ideas. It's very common for the first step of the process using RF Diffusion and models like that to generate 500,000 recommendations. Or 100,000, or a million. So how do you take that if you can only test, say, a thousand or 5,000 of those in your wet lab per week? How do you narrow down that list? So what we've built is a multi-parameter optimization—it's Pareto-based—that narrows that down by comparing all of the parameters against each other. This is a very hard problem because each one of those 500,000 is going to have its own score across the five or six or ten different parameters you're optimizing for, and the more you weight one of them, the more you're going to negatively impact others. So we've built this into the system to make sure that you are picking the right candidates, and it's not just a single cycle. You can run this and you can go from 500,000 down to say 300,000 and then run it again, look again, re-optimize, and keep going until you get to the number that you can afford to test in the lab. And that's what's shown on this slide where you've gotten it down to the top 92 candidates. Now, the lab-in-the-loop has this word called "lab." And so the question is: how and when do you get the orders to the lab and how do you get them back? Long term, we know that most of our customers would like to be using their existing in-house labs and we support that as well. But right now, we're giving you a system you can use out of the box. The reason you can use it out of the box is because we've integrated with leading CROs like Twist, Ginkgo Bioworks and Aalpha Bio. You can simply, once you've narrowed down that list, have an Amazon-like experience. You can see: "If I go with this vendor, what is going to be the cost, and what is going to be the turnaround time?" Then you send it off to them, they send it back, and immediately you see a notification inside of your Amazon Bio Discovery system. Then you can restart the cycle. I mentioned we're also able to integrate Amazon Bio Discovery with your in-house labs. I do not want to trivialize this. We have been working with many of the big pharma represented here today for several years on programs to get their existing labs into the cloud. This is a non-trivial effort because most of these systems were not designed to be cloud-based. We have made a lot of progress and agents have been a great ally for us in this regard. And so as a result, we've been able to develop a number of agents that not only automate getting your data from your instruments into the cloud but also automate getting that data as part of the lab-in-the-loop process. We're going to talk a lot more about this in a breakout session led by Sanofi this afternoon, because they're really an example of what we think good looks like in terms of having data across both the in-silico side and the wet lab side that's starting to function as one integrated system. Now, in that second video we saw with Aviv, Aviv talked about the importance of compute. And when you're talking about compute, it immediately raises the question: where am I going to do my compute? This is a hard question to answer, but I want you to park that for one second. The second question that you're going to have to be studying is: how am I going to automate my labs? It's one thing just to get the existing lab systems that you have and the data from the existing assays into the cloud. But if you really want to start operating at the throughput that lab-in-the-loop drives, you're also going to have to automate. In both of these areas, AWS and NVIDIA are deeply collaborating. We are developing joint solutions on recommendations for how to scale your compute infrastructure, but also how to deploy robotics in a manner that will allow your own wet labs to scale and integrate through the agents that I just talked about. Now, the final dimension of Amazon Bio Discovery I'd like to surface for you is a problem you may not have already thought about. But it's one that we are very concerned about. It is coming faster than most folks expect, and it's going to be a nightmare if you don't plan for it now. And that is the following: Lab-in-the-loop systems generate an enormous amount of data. Think about everything that I just walked through. Every target that you decide to focus on, every hotspot residue, and every candidate you ever design in silico. Think of all the process you use to narrow down that list, the subset that you actually sent to the wet lab, and the test results you got back from the wet lab. All of this data is valuable. But it's only going to be of use if you store it in a manner where you can find it and interrogate it later. So we're also building that capability into Amazon Bio Discovery. However, the problem is even greater than I just described, because what I just described was the data problem and therefore the insights problem for one lab-in-the-loop system. Every big pharma that I talk with—and that's just about all of them—is on a journey to have multiple of these systems. They're probably going to have at least one for their small molecule team and one for their large molecule team. They might be part of a federated learning network. They might have multiple partnerships with commercial vendors like Chai and Latent and Nabla. Each of these systems is a silo. If you are not thinking right now about how you are going to manage all the data from this in an enterprise-wide manner, you are going to create brand new silos. So we are on a journey as part of Amazon Bio Discovery to make sure that does not happen. As I mentioned at launch, we already have the experiment registry that allows the data from the experiments that you're doing to be captured and analyzed. But we are also working on the roadmap for Amazon Discovery for an enterprise-wide system of record and system of analysis, so that you can draw insights from your work with one commercial model vendor that benefits your work with your own models. Or you may realize, "We shouldn't do that experiment; we've already done that experiment. We've already paid for that." So this is something that we think is extremely important long term, and the value of which will be increasingly obvious as we evolve on this journey together. Now, I'm delighted to announce that we built the system that I just described and then found a wonderful science partner in Memorial Sloan Kettering to help us validate that the antibody candidates designed by Amazon Bio Discovery actually work. To share more about that, I'd like to welcome Dr. Dash, VP of entrepreneurship and commercialization at MSK, to share more about that journey first hand. Well, after an inspiring note, I'm now going to share something that's a little bit more prosaic. I have been asked to inform you that because I talk too much and we're therefore running late, we're going to start the breakout sessions at a slightly later time. I would like to take a moment and thank our event sponsors. This is a massive effort today for those of you who have been at our symposium for many years. We are now in year eight. It started out as about 200 people in Boston and it's now well over 2,000 people in New York. So we would not have gotten here without our sponsors this year and in prior years. They're all here in the exhibit hall; please spend time with them and share with them your appreciation. We also have, as I've mentioned a couple of times, a curated assembly of many of the most exciting startups in lots of different areas, from research to clinical and even in commercial. So please also go and visit the startup showcase. Now I'd like to finish today with basically where we started. What was the question that I asked at the start of the talk today? I asked the question: Are we seeing impact, or are we not seeing impact? I'd like to go back to our work with MSK and just have Dr. Cheung share with you the impact that they are seeing from the work that we're doing together. Because at the end of the day, as multiple speakers have said: Why are we all here? We're all here for the patient. Thank you. In the drug development field, patients come here with a clock. They have only a few months or maybe a few years to live. We're trying to beat that clock. We spent 20 years when we were using the first generation antibody just to prove that it worked, and then we spent another 13 years to get it into the human form before we get FDA approved. This is the problem—is that this linear path of one step at a time has been very inefficient. We're glad to be able to join forces with Amazon Bio Discovery to really optimize and speed up that process. We're able to have the tissues from patients to study them and then go back to Amazon Bio Discovery and say, "Make the antibody." We don't have to make the same mistake over and over again. I think that's a powerful future.

WATCH LIVE: Trump delivers remarks on health care agenda

PBS NewsHour(5.2M) ·67:21 ·6.4K views · 2026-04-23
Donald Trump (President of the United States)
> "The United States is just 4% of the world's population and consumes only 13% of all prescription drugs, yet pharmaceutical companies have been making 75% of their profits from American customers.
TL;DR: President Trump announced a landmark "Most Favored Nation" pricing agreement with 17 major pharmaceutical companies to slash U.S. drug prices to the lowest in the world, while also confirming the rescheduling of medical marijuana to Schedule III and highlighting the...

TL;DR: President Trump announced a landmark "Most Favored Nation" pricing agreement with 17 major pharmaceutical companies to slash U.S. drug prices to the lowest in the world, while also confirming the rescheduling of medical marijuana to Schedule III and highlighting the reshoring of American manufacturing via tariff policy.

Healthcare Reform and Drug Pricing

  • Most Favored Nation (MFN) Pricing: The administration has secured agreements with 17 of the world’s largest pharmaceutical companies, representing 86% of the branded drug market.
      • Global Parity: Under these deals, companies agree to sell drugs to American patients at the lowest prices offered anywhere in the world.
      • Tariff Leverage: President Trump attributed the success of these negotiations to the use of tariffs, forcing other countries to pay more while U.S. prices drop by 60% to 90%.
  • TrumpRX.gov: A new transparency and discount website that has already seen over 15 million visitors.
      • Price Reductions: Specific examples cited include a cholesterol medication (Praluent) dropping from $537 to $225, weight loss drugs dropping from $1,350 to $199, and COPD medication falling from $458 to $50.
  • Regeneron Partnership: CEO Len Schleifer and CSO George Yancopoulos announced a $27 billion investment in U.S. research and manufacturing.
      • Hearing Restoration: The FDA approved Otarmani, a gene therapy that cures certain types of deafness in children. Regeneron has committed to providing this therapy for free for an initial period.

Regulatory and Economic Shifts

  • Marijuana Rescheduling: HHS Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr. and staff confirmed that medical marijuana and FDA-approved CBD products are being moved to Schedule III.
      • Research Focus: The move is intended to facilitate clinical research to guide doctors and patients on drug interactions and efficacy, particularly for seniors.
  • Reshoring Manufacturing: Secretary of Commerce Howard Lutnick reported that $448 billion in drug manufacturing is returning to the U.S.
      • Tariff Strategy: Companies like Eli Lilly and Pfizer are building domestic plants to avoid substantial pharmaceutical tariffs.
      • Semiconductors: The administration expects $1 trillion in semiconductor "fab" investment (including TSMC and Micron), aiming for the U.S. to control 50% of the chip market.

Infrastructure and Government Efficiency

  • Lincoln Memorial Reflecting Pool: President Trump highlighted a private-sector approach to federal repairs.
      • Cost Savings: Bypassed a $301 million, 3.5-year government bid in favor of a $1.5 million, one-week renovation using an industrial-grade "American Flag Blue" pool surface.
  • White House Renovations: Replacement of 1940s-era slate with high-grade, "million-year life" charcoal granite paths.
  • Federal Reserve Criticism: The President criticized the $4 billion renovation of the Federal Reserve building, labeling it a waste of taxpayer money compared to his own real estate benchmarks.

Foreign Policy and National Security

  • Iran Conflict: The President asserted that 75-80% of military targets in Iran have been hit, effectively decimating their navy and air force.
      • Nuclear Non-Proliferation: Reiterated a hardline stance that Iran will never be allowed to possess a nuclear weapon.
      • Economic Blockade: Confirmed the Strait of Hormuz remains closed under U.S. control to prevent Iran from generating oil revenue until a "great deal" is reached.
  • UK Relations: Mentioned a landmark agreement with the United Kingdom to increase their spending on branded pharmaceuticals for the first time in 26 years, easing the burden on U.S. consumers.
"The United States is just 4% of the world's population and consumes only 13% of all prescription drugs, yet pharmaceutical companies have been making 75% of their profits from American customers. We are ending that chronic unfairness today."

— Donald J. Trump

"If the drug was $100 and it raised the price to $600, that would be a 600% rise. If it drops from 600 to 100, that's a 600% savings. The President used that mathematical device to illustrate the magnitude of the theft that has been happening against our country."

— Robert F. Kennedy Jr.

"We've been arguing for more than a decade that other wealthy nations have been getting a free ride on American innovation. Now policies are in place to force these nations to pay their fair share, which will in turn lower prices for Americans."

— Dr. Len Schleifer

"One in three Americans, when they go to the drugstore... will often leave empty-handed because they cannot afford the medications. Affordability was the main word the President would use in every phone call."

— Dr. Mehmet Oz

One of the most respected pharmaceutical companies anywhere in the world, frankly, I know it very well, is Regeneron. And it's agreed to offer their prescription medications at heavily discounted "most favored nation" prices. In other words, their numbers will come down at levels that nobody's ever seen before. The whole most favored nation thing that we've been able to do with Bobby and Oz and everybody behind me, Marty, has been incredible. It's the biggest price reduction in drugs in history. By itself, we should win the midterms, but it doesn't work that way, unfortunately. You know, it doesn't work that way. People forget too quickly. But we'll have the biggest—it's the biggest cut in drug prices in the history of our country by many percentage points. With this announcement, 17 of the world's largest pharmaceutical companies, representing 86% of the branded drug market, have now agreed to sell their drugs to American patients at the lowest prices anywhere in the world. We are going to have the lowest prices in the world. This will result in the largest drop in prescription drug prices in the history of the United States of America. I mean, that should be front-page news, but it won't be. It'll be back on page 19. But the people get it. That's why we're in the Oval Office, I guess. I want to thank Regeneron CEO, a friend of mine for a long time, good guy, great guy, CEO Len Schleifer and Chief Scientific Officer George Yancopoulos, who are two fantastic people in the medical world, very respected. We're also joined by Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr. Bobby, thank you. Secretary Howard Lutnick. Administrator of the Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services, Dr. Mehmet Oz, who's doing a fantastic job—they all are. FDA Commissioner Marty Makary and Chief Counselor of Health and Human Services Chris Klump. Thanks, Chris. Great job you're doing, Chris, wherever you may be. Oh, he's here. Great guy. For decades, Americans have been forced to pay the highest prices in the world for prescription drugs by far. Prices were many, many times what the rest of the world was paying because we had a president that didn't know what they were doing, or they weren't able to get other countries to agree to it. I was able to do that because I used tariffs. I said, "If you don't agree to it, we're going to put tariffs on your country," and they said, "We would be honored to do it, sir," after telling everybody no, including me. But they agreed to do it. The United States is just 4% of the world's population and consumes only 13% of all prescription drugs, yet pharmaceutical companies have been making 75% of their profits from American customers. So think of that. With 4% of the population and doing only 13% of all prescription drugs, the companies make 75%... made. I don't know, maybe hopefully they continue because actually, it's not as bad for them as it sounds. In fact, many of their stocks are up because actually, when the price goes up on the one end, they make up for the price coming down in the United States, so it really worked out pretty well. To address this chronic unfairness, I signed an executive order instructing my administration to do everything in our power to slash prescription drug prices for Americans while getting other countries to pay more—in many cases much more. But their other prices doubled and sometimes they tripled, and ours would go down by 60, 70, 80%, even more. Today, we're building on our unprecedented success with Regeneron committing to offer all new drugs moving forward at most favored nations prices. They'll make their current drugs available to Medicaid and MFN—that's most favored nation. Additionally, they'll also offer their current drug Praluent, which is to lower cholesterol and fight heart disease, to all consumers at a dramatically lower cost. It'll come down from $537 to $225 and be available at TrumpRX.gov. I didn't put the name; Bobby did and Oz did, one of them. It's true actually. Remember, I came in and I said, "What's this?" But they said it'll be better, it'll sell better, "Would you help us?" So, I now have my name on medication, but it's doing great. I hear it's setting records actually. I was watching the other day and Mark Cuban, of all people, said it's amazing what Trump has done with this. I can't believe when I heard him say that. I was sort of in a state of shock, but others have said it too. This whole thing, it's turned out to be hot. So that's TrumpRX.gov. The company will also invest $27 billion to build up research and development and pharmaceutical manufacturing here in America, which is a big "thank you very much." Today, I'm also announcing that the FDA has just approved a new drug from Regeneron called Otarmani, a gene therapy curing a rare disease that causes deafness. Now, people are totally deaf and it's amazing. I've seen some work on it; it's actually hard to believe. We're going to talk about it in a minute. Normally, cures for rare diseases can cost millions of dollars and really the results aren't that good, but in this case, I'm pleased to say that Regeneron will be giving this away for free for a period of time. For free. And here with us is 2-year-old Travis Smith. He was born deaf. Was he totally deaf? Pretty much, right? It's absolutely incredible. Regeneron works miracles. Yeah, he didn't know his name. He couldn't hear me tell him how much I love him. And now with Regeneron and this amazing surgery, he can listen to music and he loves it, and he loves to dance and he loves instruments. I'm so proud of him and he's been so brave through all of this. And, yeah, now he can hear and he has a bright future ahead of him, and I'm so thankful to everybody on the Regeneron team. Oh, that's incredible. I mean, even the fake news has to be impressed with this. They'll find a way to make it a bad story. He doesn't hear perfectly, but he actually does; it's about 1% off. They'll figure a way—how are you going to do it? You got to explain. No, they're genius. They are genius. I respect them a lot. But I just want to thank you, Sierra. That's incredible. Beautiful family. He's a beautiful person, too. That's a beautiful boy. Thank you. Hi. Thank you. Is life a happier life? "Oh, yeah. He is so full of energy and love now, and he was such a serious baby." So you see such a difference? "Yeah, he was such a serious baby and before that, it was very tough for him." Oh, for sure. "You can't tell me what he wants or what he wants to eat or what he needs, and now he'll be able to do that." Wow. "It's life-changing." That's a great job they've done. I want to thank Regeneron and other companies for doing their part to bring down the cost of healthcare for all Americans. Under our most favored nations agreement, we've secured gigantic discounts with price differences from 4 to 5 and even 600%. 600 percent! One of the most popular weight loss drugs has gone from $1,350 a month to as low as $199 a month. So that's a price that's incredible. So it used to sell in London for $87 and they raised it to 199, which is a big raise from 87 to 199. But that's been going on. Our country's been being ripped off for 30 years. How long would you say, Doc? Like 30, 40 years? For years. With drugs and drug prices, they're going up, we're going way down. And because the world is a bigger place than we are—believe it or not, it's hard to believe that, but they are—we go down much more than they go up. So they don't go up as much as people would have thought. So they're going to 199 and we're going down all the way down. Think of that. We're going down from $1,350 a month to $199. Nobody would believe it and this is true with all—I mean, this is the weight loss drug—but this is true with all drugs. Sometimes even more of a difference than that, sometimes a little bit less, but sometimes substantially more than even that difference.
