So we cut the per cycle cost of IVF drugs by thousands of dollars, and we reduced the price of the major COPD medication from $458 to 50 dollars. What? That must be a typo. Is that a typo? From $458 to $50. If your boy was here, he'd go "wow." He laughed. He got bored. I think he got a little bored. He wants to go onto bigger and better things, but there's nothing bigger and better than this. They'll all be available right now at TrumpRX.gov. So it's, you know, that's why it's doing well. It's not doing well for other reasons; it's doing well. Think of that, that's some discount. But we have others, and these are all top companies and top medications, and I think in the end we're going to have every company involved. We're almost there right now, but we're going to have every company involved. So TrumpRX has already received more than 15 million visitors, and every American should log on to check for massive discounts before they go to the pharmacy. It's setting a record; nobody's ever seen anything like this. I've actually had many companies—I shouldn't tell you this, Bobby—but there have been many companies say, "Sir, we'd like to buy from you... TrumpRX.com." I said, "It's not for sale. It's not me, it's the government." The government owns it; I don't own it. But they're all impressed, these pharmaceutical companies. They said they've never seen anything like this. So it's fantastic. Now I'd like to ask Secretary Kennedy to say a few words, and then we'll have Dr. Oz, the team at Regeneron, and Sierra. And Sierra, I'm so happy for you. And Chris—Chris Klump has been unbelievable, real star. You don't know his name as much as some of the others, but he's a real star of the group. So Bobby, please. Thank you. Thank you, Mr. President. I was reminded when the president was speaking of a conversation that I had yesterday with one of the Democratic senators who was questioning me during a hearing. She was ridiculing President Trump for his math and she was saying it's mathematically impossible to have a drug drop by 600% cost, which he had claimed. And I said, "Well, if the drug was $100 and it raised the price to $600, that would be a 600% rise. If it drops from 600 to 100, that's a 600% savings." And the president used that mathematical device to illustrate the magnitude of the theft that has been happening against our country and our people. As he said, we're 4.2% of the world's population, we take 13% of the pharmaceutical drugs, we spend 80% of the biotechnology research in our country, and we provide 75% of the profits to the pharmaceutical industry. This is a rip-off that has irked him for 20 years. During his first term, he held the line on drug prices for the first time in history. He came back on this term and he said we're going to lower them this term no matter what. There are people in this room who are part of the White House staff who said, "We can't do that." Oz and I said we probably can't do that, but he harassed us. We were beleaguered. It was like Fort Apache. Dr. Oz would not answer his phone because he was calling all night long with the president on the line saying, "Get this done." And we brought in this amazing superstar Chris Klump, and he negotiated these agreements that people said were impossible. I want to thank him for his leadership, Chris, and I want to thank George and Len who are the co-founders of this company of Regeneron. Their generosity, their idealism, their love of this country, and their compassion for these children like Travis. Thank you. Thank you, Bobby. Amazing. It is amazing, and you're right, I took a lot of heat. They say 500, 600, 700. But we also say sometimes 50%, 60%—different kind of calculation. 70, 80, and 90%, and people understand that better. But there are two ways of calculating it, but either way, it doesn't make any difference. Whether it's 60, 70, or 80%, nobody's ever heard of it. But it's also 500, 600, 700 depending on the way you want to look at it, the way you word the calculation. It's either way. But, Bobby, you're doing a fantastic job. We wanted somebody out of the box and we got him, right? I would say we got him and Bobby, he's the great guy actually. So, Oz, how about saying a few words, please? So this has been an unprecedented process. The president insisted we deal with one critical number you should all remember, which is that one in three Americans, when they go to the drugstore after they've seen a doctor, will often leave empty-handed because they cannot afford the medications they're picking up. So affordability was the main word the president would use in many of those phone calls. He wanted it to be done fairly. We created a partnership with the pharmaceutical industry that was remarkable, and that it pushed 17 massive companies into the fold to try to do deals. Again, unprecedented. That would allow us to claim that they're giving the most favored nations pricing to this country, to the American people. The president would not take no for an answer. We got 16 companies in, but not the 17th. How many times do you think we spoke about the 17th company? Too much. 17. He said 17; he wanted it to be 17, whatever math you want to make. 16 is not 17. There's a reason that the 17th company was delayed—it's why they're here today. It's that beautiful young boy you saw earlier. When you can save a child's hearing. And Mr. President, we talked about a lot of numbers. Secretary Kennedy quoted 100, you know, 100%, 600%. It's hard to beat free. Free is the best price, and that's the starting price with the president oftentimes, and that's what this wonderful company's agreed to. I want to applaud TrumpRX.gov, a remarkable site created by Joe Gebbia and Ed Korstine. They have done a wonderful job with zero glitches, serving more than 10 million individual unique Americans so far. And Mr. President, it's the fastest website I've ever seen. People love it because it works well; there's all kinds of additional elements being added to it which we'll be talking about. But this website, as the president said, must be checked—must be checked if you're going to buy a medication to make sure your price is the right price. It's a transparency site that is more rigorous than anything that I have seen and Mr. President, your name is attached to it, and of course, that brings a tremendous amount of attention to it. We wanted it to be perfect and the team who did that has accomplished that exactly. I want to applaud Chris because you cannot thank him enough, but it was also John Brooks, Imba Hernandez, I see Beth back there, Theo, Rudgel, a bunch of people who have done a tremendous amount of work. And what I keep hearing from the pharmaceutical industry—maybe my colleagues can address it in your comments—they never expected to have a counterpart as qualified in the federal government. If you're thinking of serving your nation, this is the right time to do it. This is the right administration to serve him because the president makes it easy to do the right thing. Gentlemen? Go ahead please. Thank you. Mr. President, Secretary Kennedy, Secretary Lutnick, Commissioner Makary, Administrator Oz, and I guess superstar Klump. Yes. Thanks for having us here on behalf of all of Regeneron. For those of you who don't know me, I'm Dr. Len Schleifer, President and CEO of Regeneron. With me is Dr. George Yancopoulos, President and Chief Scientific Officer of Regeneron, and together—well, I should pause there, I just realized something. I'm a president, he's a president, he's a president, and all three of us grew up in Queens, New York. That's right. So there's something maybe that was going on back then that was good. I'm not sure about now. So anyway, together George and I founded Regeneron and have built it over the past four decades. Today we are here—we were not pushed to be here, we are happy to be here because it marks an important step to lower drug prices. We've been arguing for more than a decade that other wealthy nations have been getting a free ride on American innovation. Just like in defense, but they've been getting in health defense, a free ride. And now President Trump is putting policies in place to force these nations to pay their fair share, which will in turn lower prices for Americans. It's a good deal. But we have to remember this is just the beginning. The most important battle we all face is disease itself. We have to recognize how hard this fight is. For most major diseases that you or your loved one might suffer from, like Alzheimer's disease to cancers, to genetic diseases, we are very far away from a cure. We have to understand that discovering cures is perhaps the hardest scientific challenge humans undertake. Much harder than getting to the moon—not that we don't like that—but much harder than even getting to Mars or making the next generation of cell phones or electric cars. Hard, like staring into the unknown for years, sometimes decades, with no guarantee that anything you're doing will ever make a difference for somebody like Travis. Regeneron is a great example of both the difficulty of the challenge but also of the potential reward. We spent 25 years—25 years and billions of dollars, not millions, billions of dollars—before we produced our first important medicine or even earned our first profit. Maybe we would have been, should have been, or might have been fired, but we stuck with it. Our relentless commitment to disruptive innovation ultimately turned us into one of the most productive biotech companies in history. We have produced, thanks to George and his team, new medicines at the rate of about one important medicine a year for the last 15 years, including truly miraculous advances impacting millions of lives. And we have two people here who are living proof. Mr. President, when you were ill with COVID, it was Regeneron's monoclonal cocktail that you affectionately dubbed "the Regeneron." We love it. And that helped save your life. That was invented by George right here and his team. George? Well, in addition to the president, we had a few minutes ago Sierra's beautiful boy Travis who was born without the ability to hear, as you heard from Sierra. And the same company and the same dedicated team of scientists that helped save the president's life also delivered a first-of-its-kind gene therapy known as Otarmani. So Travis can now hear his mother as she told you, tell him that she loves him. Hard to think of a greater gift. And just like you, Mr. President, who made our antibody treatment for COVID free for all Americans and thus saved many lives, we have made the decision, the unprecedented corporate commitment, to provide this first-of-its-kind gene therapy for hearing loss free to all the children in the United States who can benefit from it. And we do this, why? To hopefully highlight the power of disruptive biotech innovation and all the good that it can bring to all of our lives and our health. But we have to remember, despite the miraculous advances like Otarmani and like the Regeneron COVID treatment, we also have to highlight how far we have yet to go to address the many diseases that lack effective treatments. And the cures will not be coming unless we not only celebrate the advances of the biopharma industry today, but we all work together—the private sector together with the government—to not only maintain America's position as a world leader in biotech innovation, but to actually bump up innovation and become even more disruptive. Especially in a world where China is increasing investment and heavily subsidizing its biotech industry and reducing regulatory hurdles so as to try to displace America's leading position. President Trump's efforts to make drugs more affordable, as well as new FDA programs such as the Commissioner's voucher program that bring safe and effective new medicines like Otarmani to patients more quickly—these are important steps. One additional important way that I've been talking to the administration about to maintain our leading position in the world is to create catalysts and accelerants for our industry and for the healthcare system, such as developing a national healthcare database linked to molecular data including human sequence data where Regeneron once again is a world leader. Without such big datasets, powerful new technologies such as AI, despite its potential, will not be as useful as it could be in the quest to develop new medicines and improve healthcare. The biopharma industry of America has and will continue to deliver miraculous breakthroughs. You, Mr. President, and young Travis are living testaments of that, but many more lives need to be helped and saved. We need to attain a new level of disruptive innovation. We have to all work together to meet this challenge. Why? Because lives are at stake. Let me briefly say something. I'm married to a lawyer; my son is a lawyer. But I'm going to talk about the Constitution, and I'm not an expert. But I know our Constitution lays out a vision on how America would out-innovate, out-produce, and out-thrive every other nation on earth. And in it, you will find a description of a system built on incentive and reward. A system that says: take the risk, do the work, and you will share in what you create. That is why they enshrined patent protection directly in the Constitution, not as an afterthought but as a founding principle. As Abraham Lincoln said—another great president—patents add the fuel of interest to the fire of genius. We must keep that fire burning. What we did here today will do that. Little Travis can now hear for the first time, and his potential is endless. Let's make sure he grows up in a country that never stops reaching for the next miracle. Thank you, Mr. President. No, thank you, George. Great to be with you again. Chris, could you say a few words, please? Thanks, Mr. President. It's not lost on me that this year is America 250, and it's powerful, I think, to pause and remember that 250 years ago, a revolutionary free republic was born and with it the greatest innovation engine in the history of humanity. And we stand here today 250 years later getting to witness yet another groundbreaking innovation that doesn't just treat but cures deafness in children. And because of this president and his policies around most favored nation, it will enable families who are struggling and suffering with this affliction to have access to that groundbreaking therapy for free. Now, as the president mentioned, we've negotiated the 17th of 17 agreements. This represents 86% of the branded pharmaceutical drug market in the United States, but anyone who knows us knows that we're not done. That's a milestone; it's not the finish line. And so we have three core objectives that are next up. Number one, and this is already underway: we are negotiating with the many hundreds of biotechnology and pharmaceutical companies representing that other 14% that are building the cures for tomorrow. To all those who are listening, I hope that we have proven that we are credible and reliable and trustworthy partners. We want to sit with you, we will listen to you, we will take the time to understand your businesses—not only as we negotiate on behalf of every American family for affordability, but also to ensure that you're best positioned to create the cures for tomorrow. Second, we continue our work on negotiating international trade agreements. 59 days after we stood in this office and President Trump, standing with Albert Bourla from Pfizer, announced the first of the 17 MFN deals, together with our colleagues from the Department of Commerce under Secretary Lutnick's leadership and from the US Trade Representative's office under the leadership of Ambassador Greer, we negotiated a landmark agreement with the government of the United Kingdom. In it, they agreed to increase significantly the amount that they spend on branded pharmaceuticals for the first time in 26 years. That work continues with other countries. And third, we will continue our work with our partners in Congress to codify the principles that underpin these most favored nation deals. This will ensure that these savings for American families and this innovation that is unleashed at the same time persists for decades to come. So that the one in three or four families that Doctor Oz mentioned, standing at a pharmaceutical counter or pharmacy counter right now wrestling with a decision that no family should have to make of whether or not they can pick up their prescription because they can't afford it, will no longer be the case. And at the same time, we know that there are roughly 20,000 known diseases in the world, and we have a treatment or a cure for fewer than a quarter of those. And so this president and his policies are not only safeguarding affordability for American families, but are ensuring that we continue to bet on American exceptionalism. We bet on innovation, we bet on the principles that have taken us to this place in 250 years, for the next 250 years. Thank you, Mr. President. No, thank you, Chris, great job you do. Maybe you want to talk about rescheduling while we're here? Just say what you're doing and the progress that you've made. Yes. Well, Mr. President, today is another "promise made, promise kept" day. On December 18th, you signed an executive order on increasing medical marijuana and cannabidiol research, and today we delivered on that promise. The acting Attorney General this morning signed an order that moves into Schedule III medical marijuana products that are FDA-approved or that are state-licensed. This is a giant move forward to implement your promise, and it's actually a campaign promise that you made. You said we were going to move into Schedule III to increase the research on these medical products, and I just wanted everyone to know that one, you make promises, you keep them, and two, what does Schedule III mean for state-licensed medical marijuana products. We will begin implementation right away of this order. It does not legalize marijuana; it makes it easy for the researchers to actually study and understand medical marijuana, especially in populations that are using it. We know that one in 10 seniors right now used medical marijuana in the past year, and they report that it really helps them. But we don't have the science or the understanding to guide the patients and the doctors, and so both patients and doctors are flying blind without knowing how they interact with other medications because the research has been difficult to do. And so your promise today says those state-licensed medical marijuana products are now in Schedule III, and we expect the research to significantly increase so that we can understand how to guide patients and doctors in this effort. And there will be additional actions that will come this summer to do an expedited hearing for the Department of Justice for all of marijuana to move into Schedule III. And the military is very happy about it, and a lot of people suffering from big problems, which this seems to be the best answer. They're very happy about it, so the rescheduling is starting and that's a big thing, rescheduling. They kept saying, "What about the rescheduling?" And so great job, I appreciate it. I want to thank Mr. and Mrs. Howard Kessler. They're one of the most successful men in the world actually, and he had some medical difficulties and he came upon this by accident in a way. He said well, he had to go through a lot of different medications and he said this was the one that was much better than anything else. And so he experienced it; he didn't benefit by it other than from the standpoint that he lives a much better life now. So hopefully you don't need it, but if you do need it I hear it's the best of all the alternatives. But I want to thank Howard Kessler and Michelle Kessler; they really pushed this very hard. These are two people, very straight arrows; they're not into marijuana, they're not into anything, but he found this answer and he said he's doing it for other people. And so Howard and Michelle, probably they're watching or they'll be hearing about it, but they worked very hard to get this done. And it's an honor and you've done fantastically, you all have, and I appreciate it. And a man who's doing an excellent job is Howard Lutnick; he's in a little different sphere, he's not in the medical sphere, but he knows what it all means, and from Commerce, he's the Secretary of Commerce. Howard, would you say a few words, please? Sure. So in partnership with Bobby Kennedy and Mehmet Oz and the whole team, when they do most favored nations, we also ask the company to reshore and build their drugs here in America. Too many of our drugs, key drugs for our health, are being made overseas, and in a conflict, we can't rely upon them. And I am honored to say that our partners from Regeneron have agreed to reshore their production of pharmaceutical drugs, and this is exactly from your pharmaceutical tariffs. That's the point. So that means 448 billion dollars of drug manufacturing is coming to America because of your tariffs, because of your pharmaceutical strategy. And we are proud to invite Regeneron to do this giant build in America, and all the rest of the pharmaceutical companies—they've committed to build in America because of your tariff policy and your strategy. And because of the tariffs we have, it's been amazing actually. We have Eli Lilly and Pfizer and all of the companies. We had, I guess, seven of them here one time, which was interesting to see, but they actually got along with each other pretty well. These stood right behind me—the biggest companies in the world—and they're all building. Eli Lilly's building, I think, five major plants, which without tariffs they would have built none. I said, "How many would you build without them?" None. They were all making these products in other countries—a lot of other countries actually, not just China, but a lot of other countries. And I think by the time I leave office, they'll be mostly operating these. They have an incentive to do them quickly. Big incentive, like the tariffs go up if they don't. And remember, if they do them here, they don't have any tariffs to pay. It's an incredible incentive, and if they don't do them here, they have a very substantial tariff, so they get penalized. And it's bringing countries—not only drug companies but car companies. AI is a different thing altogether but even there. But the AI is—we're leading China in AI, we're leading everybody in AI, and we're leading now in car companies. They're coming from Canada, they're coming from Mexico, Germany, Japan. They're pouring into our country because of very smart—should have been done long ago by other presidents. We were the car capital of the world 50 or 60 years ago and then they just started taking them away. We were the chip capital of the world and now, you know, Intel, and now they're coming back. All the chip companies are coming back. You might say a word about that, Howard, real fast, because what we're doing with chips is incredible. And all we're doing is saying at the end of a year and a half, two years, if you don't have your chip company, you're going to pay a tremendous tariff to put chips into this country. And they are building—I think we'll have close to 50% of the chip market very soon. Right now, we had nothing; we had virtually nothing. And they're coming in from Taiwan—they're coming in mostly from Taiwan because that's where chips are made—but mostly from South Korea, Taiwan, various other countries, but they're coming back to America. We're bringing them all back. It's a revolution. Nobody's ever seen what we've done; there's never been anything like what we've done. We have 18 trillion dollars being invested—that's for 11 months—and the last administration had less than 1 trillion in four years. We have in 11 months over 18 trillion dollars. Howard, you want to talk just for a second about the chip market because it's explosive? "Sure. So you've got semiconductors, you've got leading-edge logic, you've got high bandwidth memory, and you have foundational chips, and America had a couple of points—3 or 4% market share—and we are, of course, the biggest demand." And the President gave the order: let's get these factories, these fabs, built in America. And our expectation is we will have a 1 trillion dollars of these fabs. These are factories; this is not the hyperscalers buying chips, this is literally the making of the chips, right? We had Micron, 200 billion dollars; TSMC, 165 billion; the list goes on and on. Negotiated a great deal with Taiwan: 500 billion dollars coming to America. We'll have 1 trillion dollars of semiconductors—that's part of his 18 trillion—but it's foundational for us to take care of ourselves. Well, thank you very much. Now we'll go off subject for a minute, but I think it's very interesting and I hope you go over and take a look because it's happening right now as we speak. But first, if you look outside, we had flooring outside—we had slate and it was coming to pieces. It's been there since the early 1940s and it's a path to the Oval Office, it's a path to the West Wing, and it was terrible. It was in broken, bad shape. The whole White House has been in bad shape. It's right now in better shape than it is, I think, before the day they built it. This is better right now, but we're putting magnificent new granite. It's called charcoal; it's the black granite against the white—beautiful white walls. We've stripped all of the paints off; we had 200 years of paint and we've redone it. And it's beautiful, as you can see the building. But you could also see the columns and everything else, but we're replacing and they're working right outside. If the press wants to go and take a look at what they do—really pros—you see the large slabs of black granite. Granite is rated, this particular granite, over a million-year life. As an example, concrete gets rated for about 200 years if you get very high grade. This is over—it's rated over. Can you imagine nature produces something better than you can make? So it's very interesting; it's a beautiful job they're doing and it's highly polished granite at the areas you don't walk on. It's called flame-finished in the areas that you do walk on; there's no slip. So it's a beautiful job and it's going up nicely. And if you want to take a look outside the window, so that's replacing a stone that was here from the early 1940s, a little before that. And you'll see the ramp, too. We're keeping the ramp—that was for FDR. They needed a ramp, some people would know, and in his honor, we're leaving it. I wanted to leave it; it means something. The other thing that we're doing that's taking place right now is the Lincoln Memorial. It has a beautiful reflecting pond, or lake—they call it a pool, lake, and pond, every day is different—but the word "reflecting" is a good term. It was built in 1922 and it was built out of granite and various stones on the bottom. And they never looked great because that's not really meant to be a stone that's underwater actually that much of a period of time. It's about a foot and a half to 2 feet deep, you all know it well. That's where Martin Luther King gave his great speech and he had a million people, and I had the same exact crowd, maybe a little bit more. But they said I had 25,000 people. On July 4th, I have pictures of Martin Luther King's crowd, my crowd's the exact same, everything. But it was 70 years' difference. The exact same crowd, but I actually had more people. But that's okay, they gave him a million people and they said I had 25,000 people. So. But these are the things that you get. We had on July 4th, a few years ago, first term. So you have this reflecting lake pond—it's incredible—but it was in terrible condition, you all know it. A friend of mine came from Germany to see me and he said, "Oh, we went over, it's so sad." "What's sad?" This is a number of months ago. He said, "It's filthy dirty, it's—the water's disgusting looking. It's not representative of the country." And I said, "Well, I'm gonna have to go take a look," because you know, Secret Service doesn't let you just walk around too freely actually. And I went over there with Secret Service in tow and I said, "Isn't that a shame? It's terrible." And so they had bids from the Biden administration, Sleepy Joe, and they had bids for years. They were going to do something to fix it but they never got it done. And it's so important for our country, everybody knows this. It covers the Washington Monument, Lincoln Memorial—it's in between. It's 2,200 feet; that's taller than any building in the world if you lay it down on its side. Think of it: over 2,000 feet long. So that would be many swimming pools, many, many, many stacked up together. But it would be the equivalent of the tallest building in the world plus, laid down on its side. So it's very big. It's very wide actually; it looks narrow because it's so long. And so the pricing was coming in over the years, but it came in sort of recently that it was going to cost 301 million dollars to fix it. It was going to take three and a half years, so they had to take all the granite out—which is there again, 1922—and it was leaking like a sieve. You couldn't keep water, couldn't keep anything; granite's not the right stone for that. Right stone for that, but not for the bottom. And I said, "You know, I have an idea." It's 301 million, it was going to take three to three and a half years. So remember those numbers: 301 million—this is a business study—and it was going to take three to three and a half years. And over the years, as a developer, I have probably built more than 100 swimming pools, different buildings I built. And I have some really good pool builders; I also had some really bad ones, but we took care of them. They didn't last too long, Howard. But I've had some really good ones, some very talented, very talented people. They're great people. I have such great respect for contractors that are good and such disdain for contractors that are bad. They charge you more money and they give you a bad job, but we don't accept it. But over the years, I've had three or four really good ones and I took the best of the three—I had all three go, but I ended up taking the best—and I said, "Would you take a look at this? It's 2,200 feet long." He said, "What do you want me to look at, sir?" I said, "A little swimming pool. Think of it as a swimming pool." I said, "Oh, how big is it?" I said, "2,200 feet by 167 feet wide." He said, "That's the biggest swimming pool... I've been doing this for a lot of years, sir, I've never heard of a pool like that." I said, "Well, you'll see. It's a reflecting pool and very famous. Very famous, very big signal and very important for Washington, DC." Where we, by the way, have almost no crime. I have to say that's maybe more important than talking about this. We have crime down from a tremendously high—one of the worst in the country—we have almost no crime. People are walking around, restaurants are opening all over the place, the place is bustling. But that makes this even more important. So instead of being a dirty, disgusting place where garbage was in it and everything else—and I want to give Doug Burgum credit because he was involved. The Secretary of the Interior, he was involved right from the beginning. I called him, I said, "Doug, I'm getting a lot of complaints about the condition of this, what do you think?" And he went over, he studied it, and he came back with some ideas. But I said, "You know, Doug, I have a guy who's unbelievable at doing swimming pools up the road. We have a club, we have an Olympic-size swimming pool. He did it 20 years ago and it's perfect to this day." So I asked them, I said, "I have an idea. I'm going to send my contractor over and take a look." He looked at it, he called me up, he said, "Sir, we can do something on it." He said, "It's perfect actually... It's really decaying and it's a terrible condition, but if you would, I'd like to work two weeks on cleaning it up." Which is, if you see right here, you'd like to like to repoint and fix some of the joints. It won't matter because we're putting a substance over the top, but it's nice to have a nice surface. And he said it'll take me two weeks, and if you don't mind, then I'd like to pour the latest and greatest filament or material, which is essentially a pool surface but it's industrial grade pool. And he said, "What color would you like, sir?" I said, "Well, what about turquoise like in the Bahamas?" He said, "Well, this is Washington, sir. We can give you turquoise, but why don't you try like we have a color—it's called American Flag Blue." I said, "That's the color I like." He talked me into it very easily, so he said, "That's the color I like." So he came in, and remember: three and a half years, you have to take all the granite out, then you have to put all brand new granite in, take years to do it, over 300 million. Our job will take one week and it will cost about a million and a half dollars. And people said, "Wow." And here's the only difference, because somebody would say well, the difference is... No, the difference is this is much better. This will last 30, 40, 50 years, and if it didn't, you do it again quickly if you want. So it's being done now, and I think you'd find it really amazing to go take a look. So here it is, you see the trucks. He's got scrapers and, you know, all sorts of equipment to make the stone as good as possible without removing it. So we're using the existing surface so we don't have to spend millions of dollars in demolition. And he said much better to leave the stone than it is what's underneath—they're afraid to look. And so you see, it's beautiful, clean, it's ready to be taken care of. Here's a sign that we put, and so Doug Burgum and myself are involved in that. But when you take a look at the the surface, we have it in good shape and they just started today putting down the material. It's a machine; it lays it very evenly. Beautiful. Goes from one side to the other. It'll take about three days and you're going to end up with a beautiful, beautiful reflecting pool the way it's supposed to be—much better than it ever was, actually. It'll stay clean; we even have robots that go in it and they they ride around the bottom and they clean it like your swimming pool. But these are a much higher level; this is industrial grade and it's really beautiful. So you're going to have a beautiful pool and you'll have it for July 4th—long before July 4th. Instead of taking three, three and a half years, we're taking a week. And instead of spending 301 million dollars, we're spending less than 2 million dollars. And I think it's a great business story because there are many things like that that I see—I mean, not maybe to that extent—but there are many things like that that I say. I often talk about the pen. This pen's better than the one that they used to give away for 2,000. I won't go into the long story about that because a couple of people said, "He kept talking about the pen." No, I gave you the whole history of it, actually. Many people found it interesting and some people thought I was doing too much of a "weave." But actually, it was just with this pen. For 3 dollars, 5 dollars, 2 dollars, whatever the hell it costs, it is better than the one for 1,000 to 2,000 dollars that they used to hand away. So that's the short version of it, and I like the short version better, but the long version is this one. So this is an amazing thing that's happening, and this is a big, big beautification. In addition to that, the sidewalk areas around it—which were done in granite, similar granite to that, actually not as nice as we have here to be honest with you. But you know, the people that did it weren't in the world of real estate like I am. But they have good granite circling it, and we're going to repoint it—meaning we're going to caulk it up and repoint it, make it good—and we're going to also sandblast it. It'll be like brand new. So we're going to have, in another couple of weeks, we're going to have the most beautiful reflecting pool between the Washington Monument and the Lincoln Memorial that you've ever seen. And I hope the media can go over, maybe watch them do it, because I think it's very exciting. And so, fraction of the cost and a fraction of the time, and you get a better product than you could ever get the other way. And it'll be beautiful and, as he said, it will be American Flag Blue. Any questions, please? "Yes, sir. Mr. President, could you talk a little bit about why your Navy Secretary was fired yesterday, sir?" He's a wonderful guy. I just put out a statement about him. Very—he's a very good man. I love—I really liked him. But he had some conflict with, not necessarily Pete, but with some other... He's a hard charger, and he had some conflicts with some other people, mostly as to building and buying new ships. I'm very aggressive in the new ship building and somehow he just didn't get along with them. He's an excellent guy; I think he would have gotten along great with me. I didn't really deal with him too much, but he's a—you know, I consider him to have done a very good job. I put out a nice statement about him. You gotta get along, especially in the military you gotta get along, you know. And some people liked him, some people didn't, and that's usually the truth about everything. But I found him to be a very good man and I liked him a lot. "President, on the war—on the war with Iran—how long are you willing to wait until you get a unified response?" Well, don't rush me, Jeff. You know, guys like you, you want to say "oh." So we're in Vietnam for like 18 years, we're in Iraq for many, many years. We're in for all of the—I don't like to say World War II because that was a biggie—but we were four and a half, almost five years in World War II. We were in the Korean War for seven years. I've been doing this for six weeks and we're—their military is totally defeated. They're outside of the little wise-guy ships—I call them the wise-guy ships, the little boats that they have running around with guns in them. We'll take them out too when we see them. But their Navy is gone, their Air Force is gone, their anti-aircraft is gone. All of their anti-aircraft machinery's gone. Maybe they loaded up a little bit during the two-week hiatus, but we'll knock that out in about one day if they did. We've done an amazing job. Their leaders are gone, you know. Their leaders are all gone. Part of the problem is—that's why I can't really answer your question—they have all new leadership and they're fighting like cats and dogs for who's going to control, because we've created a real mess for them. But they've created a mess for the world over the last 47 years. They've killed a lot of our people. When you see a soldier—a young person or now an older person, but a person without legs or without arms or with a face that got blown to pieces—most likely it was from Iran. That was Soleimani, who I killed. Soleimani loved the roadside bomb. He's an evil genius; he was a brilliant general. Probably we wouldn't be as far advanced had I not taken him out. That was the beginning of it all. And then I ended the Obama horror show. The nuclear transaction that he made was horror. Gave him a road to the—you call it—I mean, the deal that he did, the Iran nuclear deal, was so bad. It gave him a road to a nuclear weapon. And I will tell you, I deal with them: you cannot give Iran a nuclear weapon. This is all about a nuclear weapon. They cannot have the nuclear bomb, and they're not going to have the nuclear bomb. So we've taken out their military. We've hit about 75% of our targets. We stopped a little earlier because they wanted to have some peace, and we have a blockade that's 100% effective. And they're getting no business and, as you know, they're not doing well economically. Financially, they're not doing any business because of the blockade. They want to make a deal. We have been speaking to them, but they don't even know who's leading the country. They're in turmoil; they're in turmoil. So we thought we'd give them a little chance to get some of their turmoil resolved. But you know, and I hope the fake news people like you—I hope the fake news are going to be able to write about it accurately. Because when you say, "Oh, they're fighting very well," they're not fighting well. They were—they've been obliterated, Jeff. Obliterated. 159 ships were in their Navy. You know how many ships are at the bottom of the sea, Jeff? 159. So you know, and if you read the New York Times—the Failing New York Times, subscriptions are way down, as you know—that's because people don't believe the stuff anymore. If you watch CNN, you'd think that they're doing well in the war. They're not doing well; they're getting absolutely decimated. Now, with all of that being said, we'll see what happens. They have a lot of—we have no pressure. It's only guys like you with a question like that about, "What's your time?" We've been doing it for five and a half weeks. "Sir, but it has been eight weeks that the US now has been involved with Iran. You had initially said it would be four to six weeks and it would be over." Well, I thought that, but I also took a little break. I gave them a break and remember this: I want to make the best deal I could make. I could make a deal right now. Do you know that if I left right now, we had a tremendous success? It would take them 20 years to rebuild, but I don't want to do that. I want to have it everlasting. I want to have it where they never have a chance to get a—I mean, the way you asked that question, yeah, I did say it. I thought it would take four to six weeks and I was right, because at the end of six weeks, at the end of four weeks, their military was decimated. But now what I'm doing, I don't know, I can't tell you that. I don't want to put that kind of a timetable on it, but it'll go pretty quickly and we'll have the strait opened up. Now, right now, we have it closed. We have total control of the strait and the fact that it's closed. You know, they would have opened it up three days ago. They came to us and they said, "We will agree to open the strait," and all my people are happy. Everyone was happy except me. I said, "Wait a minute, if we open the strait, that means they're going to make 500 million dollars a day." I don't want them to make 500 million dollars a day until they settle this thing. So I'm the one that kept it closed. We have total control of it, and it'll open when they make a deal or something else happens—that's very possible. "What do you say to the American people who question how much longer this will take? Obviously, you know that they are having high—" You are such a disgrace! Do you hear what I just said? Vietnam—how many years was Vietnam? "I understand, sir, but you're not capturing six-week deadlines—" How many years was Vietnam? Well, I did my—I took the country out militarily in the first four weeks. I took it out militarily. Now, all we're doing is sitting back and seeing what deal they make. And if they don't want to make a deal, then I'll finish it up militarily with the other 25% of the targets. We've hit 78% of the targets that we've wanted to hit. We've knocked out their manufacturing, we've knocked out their missile production, we've knocked out their drone production. We've knocked out everything. In some cases, when I say knocked out 70, 80, 90%, it's amazing what we've done. So, I've done that within that period of time that I mentioned, but I don't want to rush myself. You know, because every story is, "Oh, Trump is under time pressure." I'm not. No, no. You know who's under time pressure? They are, because if they don't get their oil moving, their whole oil infrastructure's going to explode. You know what that means? Because they have no place to store it. And because they have no place to store it, if they have to stop it, something happens that only Len can explain. Something happens underground that essentially renders it in very poor shape, and you never recover fully. You can recover 50, 60%, but you can never have it like it is right now. And they have a matter of days before that event takes place. So, I'm not under any pressure whatsoever. We've never had so much ammunition. Our ships are loaded; I call them locked and loaded. They're locked and loaded. They're ready to go. We have much higher quality equipment than we did when we first started the war, or the military operation—whatever you want to call it. And they're coming to us. The problem they have is they are very disorganized right now. Well now, yesterday—and I was very pleased with this—eight young women were going to be executed yesterday afternoon. And I asked them—call it a favor or call it just a moral request—that they not be executed. And they came back with an answer that they won't be executed. They're going to release—it was protesting—eight beautiful young women, very young women. And their pictures were in the paper, and it's been a story for a little while. I saw that and I said, "Let's see if we can save them." And it was very nice what happened. So they're not going to be—what they're doing is, as you know, they're releasing four of them very shortly, and they're going to keep four of them in jail for a period of one month and release them. So they won't be executed, yes. "So if you're asking for more time to sort out negotiations—" I'm not asking you for more time! I'm not asking for them. No, it's no more time. "I'm just—if you need more time, does that mean Americans should anticipate spending more on gasoline for the foreseeable future?" For a little while. And you know what they get for that? Iran without a nuclear weapon that's going to try to blow up one of our cities or blow up the entire Middle East. You want to see what shock would be? And I have to be honest: the stock market is at an all-time high right now. I thought it would have been down 20, 25% when we hit—Wait a minute, can I finish my question, wise guy? Stock market's at an all-time high right now. It hit an all-time high yesterday, the day before, and it's staying there. I projected—and I'm pretty good at this—that the stock market would drop maybe 20%, 25%. And I understood that and I said, "Hey, it's a bad thing, but I have to do what's right for the country, even the world." Because you can't have them—you cannot let them have a nuclear weapon. So the stock market, unlike what a lot of people were predicting—I thought they weren't necessarily wrong—the stock market today hit an all-time high. It hit an all-time high yesterday, the day before, and it's staying there. I thought oil would go up to maybe 200 dollars a barrel, and oil is a very different number than anyone thought. In fact, this country is much lower because we have all the oil we can use. We're actually—ships are coming from all over the world to Texas, Louisiana, and Alaska. They're coming from all over the world to get oil from the United States, you know, while this is closed, the Hormuz strait. So if you look at what I said, I guess it was right. Because I said I'd have it in four to six weeks, and in four weeks we have totally defeated their military. So right now, I don't want to rush it because you guys are, you know, trying to make us look as bad as possible. I don't want to rush it. I want to take my time. We have plenty of time, and I want to get a great deal. I want to get a deal where our nation and the world is safe from lunatics with nuclear weapons. "Sir, would you use a nuclear weapon against Iran? You posted on Truth Social a few weeks ago that you wouldn't..." No, no. We don't need it. Why do I need it? Why would a stupid question like that be asked? Why would I use a nuclear weapon when we've totally, in a very conventional way, decimated them without it? No, I wouldn't use it. A nuclear weapon should never be allowed to be used by anybody. "Prince Harry today has said that he would like to see you do more to end the war in Ukraine. Do you think it's appropriate for a Royal to make those comments ahead of the visit on Monday?" Prince Harry? "Yes, sir." How's he doing? How's his wife? Please give them my regards, okay? No, no, I don't know. It's Prince—I think I know one thing: Prince Harry is not speaking for the UK, that's for sure. I think I'm speaking for the UK more than Prince Harry, but I appreciate his advice very much. Great advice. I look forward to the dinner we're having. King Charles is coming; he's a friend of mine. We're really looking forward to it. We've spoken and it's—we're going to have a great time. I tell you, if I had that ballroom built, it would be full. I wish we had more seats. You know, they've wanted a new ballroom for 150 years; now they're getting the best in the world. We're going to have the best in the world. But you know, we have a room that's not big enough to handle what would be a big crowd. But we're going to have very great people that love the UK. I love the UK. I think they made a big mistake on energy; you should open up the North Sea in Aberdeen. You should open it up. And the other thing is, they've made a big mistake on immigration. "Sir, on TrumpRX, what do you hope to learn from Janine Pirro's investigation into the Federal Reserve's renovation that you did not learn when you visited the site yourself last month?" Well look, you know, the Federal Reserve building is a small building. They're going to be in there by the time it opens, in my opinion, for 4 billion dollars. I built a hotel down the road for 201 million that I believe is bigger than the Federal Reserve building. 201 million! The ballroom—I'm going to have the ballroom for 3 to 400 million, depending on finishes, like marbles to quality—you know, quality finishes. We'll go top of the line, but that's a much bigger project. When you look at the fact that they've taken this beautiful building and destroyed it—they've taken down the beautiful ceilings with eagles, they've taken down the walls that are a foot and a half thick masonry. You couldn't hear from office to office. They ripped it all down. They ripped it down because Kevin Warsh, who's terrific, said the saddest thing is they ripped down the nicest building in Washington for a boardroom, and all they needed was a boardroom. They ripped it down—a boardroom building! It was beautiful. They ripped it down, probably because it cost so much to fix it. So, I would have done that job for 25 million and had money left over. It's going to cost more than—in my opinion, it's not going to open for a long time. You know, Kevin may not be able to have an office. I'm going to have to get him an office. Can you give him an office? "Yes." Bobby, do you have some space over there, please? "Yes, we have a lot of space." We're going to have Kevin sitting right next to you, Bobby, but it may not open for a long time. I looked at it the other day. It's a see-through—see-through, you know what that means? The walls haven't even been up yet. This is after years and years of construction, and somehow you have to find out what went wrong that a small building—I could have done it for 25 million and had money left over—it would have been beautiful. That a small building can cost 4 billion dollars? It may never open. It may never open, and we have to get to that. On top of that, he's been terrible on interest rates because he should have lowered interest rates. That's why I call him Jerome Too Late. "Too Late"—it's his nickname. Jerome Too Late Powell. He likes me a lot. Thank you very much! Thank you.

LIVE | US Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick Testifies on President Trump's 2027 Budget Request

Mint(1.4M) ·61:50 ·159 views · 2026-04-23
Howard Lutnick (Commerce Secretary)
Secretary Howard Lutnick presented a FY 2027 budget focused on aggressive export controls, semiconductor reshoring, and agency modernization, while facing intense criticism from committee members over proposed cuts to domestic development programs, tariff impacts, and personal ethics.
TL;DR Secretary Howard Lutnick presented a FY 2027 budget focused on aggressive export controls, semiconductor reshoring, and agency modernization, while facing intense criticism from committee members over proposed cuts to domestic development programs, tariff impacts, and...

TL;DR

Secretary Howard Lutnick presented a FY 2027 budget focused on aggressive export controls, semiconductor reshoring, and agency modernization, while facing intense criticism from committee members over proposed cuts to domestic development programs, tariff impacts, and personal ethics.

Economic Priorities and Industrial Strategy

  • Semiconductor and Energy Investments: Secretary Lutnick highlighted record-setting capital investments spurred by the administration’s policies.
      • Key Projects: Micron ($200 billion), TSMC ($165 billion), and Texas Instruments ($60 billion) investments in domestic manufacturing.
      • Energy Deals: Recent announcements include $550 billion in trade deals with Japan and Taiwan, including natural gas facilities in Ohio and Pennsylvania and small modular reactors in Tennessee.
  • Trade Deficit and Tariffs: The Secretary claimed the administration has achieved the lowest trade deficit in 16 years (down to $3.4 trillion in exports).
      • Job Growth Dispute: Ranking Member Rosa DeLauro challenged this, noting the U.S. lost over 88,000 manufacturing jobs between Feb 2025 and Feb 2026, and that tariffs cost American families roughly $1,700 each in 2025.
      • Lutnick's Defense: Argued that while factory production takes years to start, current policies have created hundreds of thousands of construction jobs.

Security, Enforcement, and Modernization

  • Bureau of Industry and Security (BIS): The budget requests a $215 million increase to nearly double the bureau’s appropriation.
      • Countering China: Funds would hire hundreds of special agents to prevent the theft of U.S. technological leadership and enforce export controls on high-end chips and software.
      • Revenue Generation: Lutnick noted that BIS agents generate 3x to 10x their cost in revenue through fines levied against companies violating export rules.
  • NOAA and Infrastructure:
      • Modernization: The administration is moving NOAA’s IT from hardwired systems to a distributed cloud model to improve forecasting.
      • Weather Data: The budget includes $70 million for next-generation satellites and shifts toward purchasing commercial satellite data rather than relying solely on government-owned hardware.
  • Broadband Savings: Lutnick reported saving $21 billion in the BEAD (Broadband Equity Access and Deployment) program by eliminating "one-size-fits-all" fiber requirements in favor of technology-neutral solutions (satellite, fixed wireless, etc.).

Proposed Program Eliminations and Agency Cuts

  • Domestic Program Cuts: The FY 2027 request proposes eliminating several long-standing programs, drawing sharp bipartisan scrutiny.
      • Economic Development Administration (EDA): The budget seeks to zero out EDA grants. Lutnick argued the department would focus on efficiently spending already-appropriated funds before the end of the year.
      • Manufacturing Extension Partnership (MEP): Proposed for elimination based on what Lutnick described as a "sickening" Inspector General report detailing overpayment and inefficiency.
      • Minority Business Development Agency (MBDA): Slated for elimination; Lutnick cited an audit claiming 92% of its awards were improperly made.
  • Oversight and Accountability: Rep. Grace Meng and Rep. DeLauro criticized the $10 million cut to the Office of the Inspector General and the department’s failure to submit legally required spend plans on time.

Ethical Concerns and Targeted Interrogations

  • Jeffrey Epstein Connections: Rep. Meng and Rep. Madeleine Dean questioned the Secretary regarding disclosures that he visited Epstein’s private island in 2012, four years after Epstein’s sex offense conviction.
      • Lutnick’s Response: He stated he has "nothing to hide" and has voluntarily agreed to a separate House hearing in two weeks to address the topic exclusively.
  • "Gold Card" Visa Program: The Secretary confirmed the establishment of a program where individuals can gain residency by paying $1 million into a Commerce gift account. He noted one approval has been made thus far with hundreds in the queue, subject to rigorous DHS vetting.
  • Firearm Export Controls: Rep. Andrew Clyde thanked the Secretary for rescinding Biden-era interim rules on firearm exports, which Clyde argued had unfairly burdened American manufacturers.

***

Notable Quotes

"These agents are the intellectual law enforcement agents of the U.S. government. They have badges and guns, but they work... to make sure those who are trying to steal our best [technology] can't get away with it." — Secretary Howard Lutnick
"Minor changes in our trade deficit are cold comfort to anyone who has to pay attention to the price of groceries, although I understand there are not many people in the president's cabinet who fall into this category." — Rep. Rosa DeLauro
"The manufacturing is going to start in two or three years when these factories come online, but construction... is bringing this back." — Secretary Howard Lutnick
"The Secretary's dishonesty calls into question the truthfulness of other statements he has made to Congress on the record. The American people deserve honesty from those with the privilege of holding public office." — Rep. Grace Meng
That would make things worse. The fiscal year 2027 budget request would hurt American jobs, small businesses, and economically distressed communities by completely eliminating Economic Development Administration grants and gutting the ITA's efforts to expand US exports. The administration is once again proposing to zero out the Manufacturing Extension Partnership program, which helps small and medium-sized US manufacturers create and preserve US manufacturing jobs. It also is again proposing to defund the Minority Business Development Agency after attempting to illegally shut it down. And the administration is proposing to rescind all of the remaining funding for the digital equity program, which Congress appropriated on a bipartisan basis to help veterans, seniors, rural communities, and people with disabilities afford and make full use of high-speed internet. The American economy and hardworking Americans are getting squeezed. Yet at the same time, Secretary Lutnick's own family has greatly benefited from many of the deals that have been arranged by the Commerce Department over the past year. According to a New York Times investigation in November, never in modern US history has the office of the Secretary of Commerce intersected so broadly and deeply with the financial interests of the commerce secretary's own family. Furthermore, the Commerce Department has been plagued by mismanagement over the past year, beginning with the damage done by Elon Musk's DOGE. Last year, Secretary Lutnick, you told this subcommittee that no weather service meteorologists were fired, when in fact many meteorologists were fired. We learned in a hearing before this subcommittee last month that the department and the National Weather Service still have not backfilled all of the National Weather Service's frontline staff positions that were so needlessly lost last year. The department's responsiveness, or lack thereof, to this committee has also been concerning. More than 10 months after our subcommittee submitted questions for the record following the Secretary's last appearance, we still have received no answers. The department also failed to submit its fiscal year 2026 spend plan in accordance with the legal deadline, as all of the spend plans were four or more weeks late. We only this week received the spend plans for the department's largest bureau, NOAA, and for the Minority Business Development Agency, which were legally due to our appropriations committee a month and a half ago. The department's fiscal year 2027 budget justification documents were also late in being publicly released. All of these delays are completely unacceptable and obstruct our committee's ability to conduct oversight and to make informed decisions about the federal budget on behalf of the American people. Finally, I was disturbed to learn from disclosures in the Epstein files that Secretary Lutnick had personal and business interactions with Jeffrey Epstein for years after Epstein was registered as a sex offender. Secretary Lutnick previously claimed that he cut ties with Mr. Epstein in 2005, but the Epstein files show the Secretary took a trip to Epstein's private island in 2012, four years after Epstein's conviction for solicitation of prostitution from a minor. The Secretary's dishonesty calls into question the truthfulness of other statements he has made to Congress on the record. The American people deserve honesty from those with the privilege of holding public office. And I will say once again that the survivors of Jeffrey Epstein's horrific crimes deserve truth, accountability, and justice. I look forward to the Secretary's testimony and to answers to the many questions that my colleagues and I have. Thank you, and I yield back. Thank you, Miss Meng. I'd like now to recognize the ranking member of the full committee, Miss DeLauro. Thank you very much, Mr. Chairman and ranking member Meng, for convening this hearing today. And thank you Secretary Lutnick for being here and listening to your testimony and having a good back and forth and give and take and questioning. From where I'm sitting, it is difficult to view the Trump administration's trade policy as anything short of disastrous. The president ignited a global economic crisis by imposing significant taxes on American consumers without any thought to whom they were taxing or why they were imposing these taxes in the first place. In case my colleagues have forgotten, in April of last year the president announced he was imposing a 10% tariff on almost every country in the world, including a group of volcanic islands that are populated not by people, but by penguins. This would be amusing if not for the fact that this lazy and haphazard application of tariffs has had such a harmful effect on American consumers. Data from the Yale Budget Lab found that the president's trade war increased the cost of food for American families by $1,700 throughout 2025. Minor changes in our trade deficit are cold comfort to anyone who has to pay attention to the price of groceries, although I understand there are not many people in the president's cabinet who fall into this category. If the purpose of implementing these tariffs and price hikes on consumers was to promote US job growth, it failed. The US economy created less than 600,000 jobs in 2025. In 2024, before the president took office and put these tariffs in place, that number was 2 million. When all was said and done, at the end of 2025 unemployment was up and the economy had the worst year for job growth since 2020, the last year of President Trump's prior term. Earlier this year we learned that the tariffs the president chose to charge American consumers were not only ill-advised but also illegal, according to the Supreme Court. And now, after charging higher prices because of these illegal tariffs, American companies are trying to get their money back from the government. American consumers on the other hand, working families who are struggling to keep up with the rising cost of living, they get no relief. I've introduced legislation, the Tariff Relief for Consumers Act, to ensure that refund dollars provide relief for consumers and small businesses and do not simply line the pockets of large corporations and other importers. The American people are fed up with this administration's gross mismanagement of the economy. A poll released earlier this week, an Associated Press poll, found that 70% of Americans disapprove of the president's handling of the economy while only 30% approve. Mr. Secretary, I appreciate that you are here today to tout what you believe the administration's accomplishments on trade and the economy are. But seven in ten Americans are not buying what this administration is selling. Given the increasingly dire economic outlook, I would have thought that the Trump administration would look to shore up its domestic economic programs, to find ways to pull us out of this hole that they have been digging. Unfortunately, the president's budget request does just the opposite. The administration is proposing to eliminate all economic development administration grants, and claw back another $63 million in grant funding that was already approved in this body on a bipartisan basis. The EDA helps create jobs, especially in rural areas. From 2018 to 2024, the EDA created or retained more than 727,000 jobs and spurred more than $80 billion in private investment. I know I do not need to sell you on the economic development administration, Mr. Secretary, given that during your confirmation hearing you expressed support for the program and said that you intended to maintain it. The president's budget also proposes eliminating the manufacturing extension partnership program, which is designed to help small and medium-sized American manufacturers grow, creating more jobs and stimulating local economies. I thought the whole point of the trade war was to support American manufacturing. It has obviously failed in that respect, so why eliminate a long-standing program that actually helps solve the problem? It does not make any sense. You've eliminated the minority business development agency and again in your confirmation testimony you assured us that you did not support dismantling this program. Currently there is a permanent injunction blocking the implementation, but still the administration proposes the elimination of the minority business development agency. You cut funds for the international trade administration. The administration proposes clawing back a total of more than $2.3 billion from funding under the commerce department in addition to the EDA. The administration is proposing to rescind funding that was passed—I want to repeat, we passed as an appropriations committee: Democrats, Republicans, House and Senate. We appropriated this money. It was part of the bipartisan infrastructure law, as well as clawing back money and rescinding funding for operations, research, and facilities at NOAA. Lastly, the president's request proposes cutting the budget for the office of the inspector general at the commerce department by $10 million, more than a fifth of their current funding. We have seen how officials in this administration have exploited the public trust for private gain. It is deeply concerning to see that the internal oversight office charged with preventing waste, fraud, and abuse is being defunded under this budget. The American people should have faith that government officials are working in their interest and are not just out to make a quick buck for themselves. I look forward to hearing your testimony and again the answers to our questions. Thank you very much for being here, Mr. Secretary. I thank the Chairman and I yield back. Thank you, Miss DeLauro. I would now like to recognize our witness, Secretary Lutnick, for an opening statement. Without objection, your written statement will be entered into the record. Mr. Secretary, we're delighted to have you with us. This is your official congressional oversight subcommittee. We want to be helpful to you in your duties, and we want to know some answers that we all have. So, Mr. Secretary, thank you for being here. The floor is yours. Thank you, Mr. Chairman. And thank you for having me here. Thank you, Representative Meng, and the members of the subcommittee. I appreciate you having me here today and welcome, welcome. Over the past 15 months, President Trump has proven that the American economy is the strongest and most resilient in the world. When businesses look for the place to expand manufacturing, they drive innovation and they hire more workers, they are decisively choosing the United States. Micron, for example, $200 billion investment in memory semiconductor manufacturing in Idaho, upstate New York and Virginia. TSMC's $165 billion advanced manufacturing investment in America's semiconductor logic sector. Texas Instruments $60 billion investment for seven US semiconductor fabs across three manufacturing mega sites in Texas and Utah are just a few examples of the Trump administration's commitment to driving semiconductor investment and bringing jobs back to the United States. I could spend our entire time together listing the record-setting commitments company after company and industry after industry have made to build in America. Trillions of dollars of capital investment are pouring into America led by President Trump and the United States Department of Commerce. I'm proud of the leadership role the Department of Commerce has played in providing the support and encouragement these businesses need to revive American manufacturing and we have only just begun. Over the past few months alone, the Department of Commerce has announced multiple $15 billion plus record-setting critical energy investments across this country. From Pennsylvania and Georgia to Texas and Tennessee, they will accelerate economic growth, ensure US energy dominance, bolster critical supply chains and strengthen US national and economic security. In addition to the massive historic trade agreements with Korea and Taiwan, the impressive $550 billion trade deal with Japan has announced six projects in the past six months, which include $40 billion for small modular reactors in Tennessee and Alabama, a $33 billion 9.2 gigawatt natural gas-fired facility in Ohio, a $17 billion natural gas-fired power generation hub in southwest Pennsylvania, and $16 billion for natural gas in east Texas. That goes on top of $2 billion for a deepwater crude oil export terminal in the Gulf of America and $600 million for a critical high-pressure, high-temperature synthetic diamond grit facility in Georgia. Just last month, Secretary Wright and I were in southern Ohio to break ground on that 9.2 gigawatt natural gas facility. This project will develop the largest natural gas facility in history. It'll lower electricity costs across the region, create thousands and thousands of American jobs, strengthen our national security, and help the US meet the energy needs of our future. Beyond these historic investments, the Department of Commerce continues to work around the clock to implement President Trump's America First trade policy. Under Section 232, the Commerce Department is taking action on steel, aluminum, copper, pharmaceuticals, autos, semiconductors, and investigating other sectors to protect, reshore, and revive manufacturing. These actions have led to multi-billion dollar commitments to reshore steel and aluminum manufacturing and restore American excellence in critical industries. In the first year of his second term, President Trump has dramatically reduced the trade deficit, lowering imports and increasing exports. Exports went to $3.4 trillion, which is a 6% increase from 2024, and in 2025 the trade deficit was the lowest we've seen in 16 years. I'm proud of the role I played in negotiating our most important trade agreement, including the largest deal ever done, which was our trade deal and investment deal with the European Union. BIS is working hand-in-hand with the Department of Health and Human Services and we've arranged historic agreements to lower drug prices for American consumers, negotiating most favored nation deals and ensuring Americans pay the lowest prices for innovative medicines. As a result of these deals, additionally we have secured over $400 billion in commitments to reshore pharmaceuticals and manufacturing in America, those key pharmaceuticals. The budget includes a $215 million increase to BIS above the 2026 enacted level, which support the hiring of hundreds of new special agent law enforcement officials to address the theft of American technological leadership by bad actors. These officers are critical to protecting and defending America's innovation. In ITA we are driving record foreign direct investment, cracking down on harmful foreign trade practices, and supporting our 1.3 million American jobs with export and investment deals. In 2025, ITA helped Boeing, Wabtec and other major American companies secure a record $244 billion in contracts with foreign public buyers. This exceeds the 17 billion that the Biden administration did in its first year. The budget includes a $10 million increase to the 2026 enacted level for trade enforcement in ITA and $100 million for the United States investment accelerator. The budget slashes unnecessary wasteful spending in NIST and other groups and eliminates the manufacturing extension partnership, which I'm sure we will talk about. At the USPTO we are rapidly modernizing and leveraging technology to cut classification prep dramatically. We want to get rid of the massive backlog that we had and do a much better job. And finally we have launched a new era of modernization of NOAA, where we are in the process of transferring the agency's IT system from a decades-old hardwired technology to the modern distributed cloud. This update will improve our forecasting capabilities, transform the way our forecasters do their jobs, and will make Americans safer. The budget also includes a $135 million increase to the 2026 enacted level for NOAA shipbuilding and our improvement of unmanned systems programs. For far too long our nation was mired in status quo, leaving American consumers and producers alike with unfair, poorly implemented trade deals, outsourced jobs, and paltry investments that failed to meet our country's potential. President Trump is committed to ushering in a golden age of domestic commerce and bringing jobs back to the United States and that's exactly what he has done in his first 15 months of this administration. I look forward to working with you as the department continues to spur economic growth and secure unprecedented prosperity for the American people. Thank you, Mr. Chairman, for having me here today. Thank you, Mr. Secretary. We will now proceed under the five-minute rule for questions. I will begin by recognizing myself. Mr. Secretary, the request for the Bureau of Industry and Security, which is instrumental to the nation's export control system, seeks to nearly double the size of the bureau's annual appropriation. How would this historic investment help counter China? Thank you, Mr. Chairman. BIS is the frontline in our key defense from China trying to get our best chips and our best technology, which we have export controls and we don't allow them to get. But if we don't have the law enforcement officials and we can't stop them, then they'll find their way around our rules. And so we want to significantly grow our law enforcement special agents at BIS. And their returns are remarkable. I know on the budget we talk about expense, but these agents bring between three and 10 times their revenue annually in fines. Not only do they stop these companies, but then we fine these companies and make way, way more revenue for the United States of America. And but that budget is two sides, right? The revenue is not part of my budget, just the expense. But I want you to know these agents are massively economically and intellectually important to the United States. On this topic, how is the department using its export control authority to prevent sensitive US technologies from ending up in the hands of our adversaries? We use at BIS our export control to make sure that our, the countries of concern and countries that are our adversaries cannot get our best technology. We don't want them to get our best chips, we don't want them to get our best software. We want to make sure that we who are the innovator in the world remain far in the lead. And so this group is the frontline that holds those back and we continuously discuss across this administration each and every product. We work together as an administration to make sure that those countries are not getting our best technology and innovations. How is BIS partnering with other law enforcement agencies? We work extremely well with Customs and Border Patrol, right? That is a key place. DHS is very close with us, the FBI is very close with us, the DOJ. We just had a huge criminal action where a large company was trying to ship billions of dollars of our best chips to China and they were arrested together with DOJ. So we work very closely with law enforcement. These are the intellectual law enforcement agents of the US government. They're in BIS; they have badges and guns but they work closely with other law enforcement to make sure those who are trying to steal our best can't get away with it. Let me talk with you briefly about the important work of EDA in rural communities like mine. The assistance to coal communities supports areas negatively impacted by changes in the coal economy. I've seen the devastation that a local economic downturn can cause to a small community and the positive impact that economic development programs have in the aftermath to support recovery and foster economic growth. Sir. I think the coal program can and is very important. And we can help these communities. You know, the Trump administration just announced a deal in Pennsylvania to open two very large coal mines. We are very pro-clean beautiful coal as you know. The EDA has significant appropriations and I will make sure to get that money out the door efficiently, effectively and with deep consideration. If you appropriate it, I will make sure and our department will make sure we do it as smart and efficiently as possible. Prior administrations sat on these. That is not this department. This department will have the appropriated money that you have given us for EDA out the door by the end of this year. NTIA under your leadership was able to refocus the broadband equity access and deployment program on maximizing taxpayer value. Kentucky was allocated $1.1 billion under the program and had plans approved for about $400 million to expand broadband access. When will NTIA provide Kentucky guidance on eligible uses for the remaining 700 million? For the Bead program, Congress had allocated $42 billion when I walked in the door. And you had a set of rules on who to cover for all, each state and their and their teams were amazing. The working with the states was amazing and working with Kentucky was very impressive; your team did a great job. What but they were only going to give it to fiber and spend all $42 billion in a one-size-fits-all model which made no sense. You had some people who were $150,000 fiber run to a house that was worth $100,000. It was crazy town. So we rebid it without any technological limitations. If it's satellite, if it's you know fixed wireless, if it's fiber, whatever's best. And we executed the plan for $21 billion exactly according to the statute. Half the money. So we now have a high-class problem. We have executed what you wanted to do exactly right for $21 billion and we have 21 billion remaining. So here's what we did.
