We went on a listening tour, we had over a thousand people give us hundreds and hundreds of comments. We're sorting through those hundreds of comments and we're going to come out with a plan for the high-class problem of having absolutely executed the model that you gave us to go out and now do new and exciting things with that money in the model of the statute that you've given us. Thank you, sir. Miss Meng. Thank you, Mr. Chairman. And first before I begin my question, I just want to say that it's great to hear that you Mr. Secretary and the Trump administration is continuing the great investments that were made into our domestic chips manufacturing started by the Biden administration, Biden-Harris administration. Whether it's TSMC, Micron, Texas Instruments, billions were invested during the Biden administration and I was grateful as a New Yorker to see the investments in Micron in my home state and your home state of New York. And we were so thrilled to welcome President Joe Biden to announce them during his administration. But I do want to start Mr. Secretary, I mentioned in my opening statement that we had learned in recent months that contrary to your earlier statement in which you suggested that you had cut off all contact with Epstein in 05, you and your family had in fact had lunch with Mr. Epstein on a boat during a trip to his private island in 2012 and exchanged business emails with him as recently as 2018. This was years after his 2008 conviction for soliciting prostitution from a minor. I just wanted to ask why did you publicly claim following your nomination that you were never in a room with him after 2005? So I voluntarily agreed in less than two weeks to sit with your colleagues in the house and answer any and all questions on this topic. I have nothing to hide and I am glad to answer their questions. Whatever questions you have on this topic, I will be glad to answer. It's in less than two weeks and we've set aside the time to do that and I will answer all the questions. So whatever questions you have, if you would give them to your colleagues, I will answer them. But today I am here to testify about the budget. I've set aside voluntarily the time to answer those questions in less than two weeks, and as you correctly have said, I have nothing to hide. No, I appreciate that and we'll definitely make sure that that message and offer is emphasized. When Senator Van Hollen in February asked if you would be willing to share any records that you have, you told him that you would surely talk about that, you had nothing to hide. You know what if anything have you done since that hearing to ensure that you are sharing all of your records, and yeah. I've set aside in less than two weeks the time that your house colleagues want to have me answer these questions. I am here to testify about the budget but I have set aside voluntarily the time to answer these questions. And as you correctly have said, I have nothing to hide. Okay and I just you know why I'm asking is because our American public deserves honesty and accountability from those of us who have the privilege of holding public office. This is a budget hearing but it's also about our American taxpayers' money. The survivors of Epstein and his associates' horrific crimes deserve so much better from this government. I do want to use the rest of my time to ask about the gold card visa program. President Trump directed you in coordination with the Secretary of State and Homeland to establish a gold card visa program. As you know people who are able to pay at least $1 million into commerce's gift account are eligible for permanent legal residence and potentially citizenship. I wanted to ask how many of these applications have been approved and how many gifts have been received by commerce? The process was recently resolved with DHS who runs the program. And they do a $15,000 the most serious vetting and analysis of any potential applicant in the history of government. Usually it was $600. These pay $15,000 for an extraordinary vet. So they have approved recently one person and there are hundreds in the queue that they are going through the process. But this is a new program and they've just set it up and they wanted to make sure they did it perfectly and so we've worked through that. But it's a DHS program done with a rigorous, rigorous vetting. Okay. And do you know how the department would intend to spend the billions of dollars in the gift account? That will be determined by the administration and its terms are for the betterment of the United States of America. It needs to be for commerce and the betterment of the United States of America. Okay. Thank you Mr. Chairman, I yield back. Thank you Miss Meng. Mr. Alford. Well thank you Mr. Chairman, thank you ranking member Meng and our ranking member at our full committee, good to see you. And thank you Secretary for being here today. You know we depend on the Department of Commerce to play a central role in supporting American competitiveness through weather forecasting, standard development, trade enforcement and economic development in communities all over our great nation. The fiscal year 27 proposal reflects the administration's effort to streamline operations to prioritize core missions and to make sure that taxpayer resources are focused where they can have the greatest impact. With proposed budget adjustments across NOAA, NIST, EDA and ITA, I look forward to hearing how the department will continue delivering strong reliable services while advancing productivity and modernization. I want to talk a little bit about weather. I think last time you were here we had a very robust conversation about NOAA. I'm glad to say that our operation in Pleasant Hill, Missouri is still operating with humans, and thank you for that. We have this tornado season in Missouri and we had a EF1 hit not too far from my home last week in Belton, Missouri; it did minimal damage. The next few days could become an outbreak for tornadoes in the Kansas City area; Sunday and Monday is not looking good. The FY 27 budget proposal includes $70 million in procurement funding for the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration including investments in next-generation weather satellites. How will these planned upgrades translate into benefits for my constituents? The National Weather Service had been sort of had walls around itself, right? We had satellites we use the data we had for satellites, we'd launch balloons we use the data that we captured from balloons, we had our own tracking aircraft. We are now going to buy data; that 70 million allows us to buy data available on commercial satellites. Take in more data, give up none, but add data, right? Have unmanned aircraft that can go into the hurricane and stay there without the risk of loss of life, right? Think of how much better that is, they can live inside the hurricane. And you know how horrifically scary tornadoes are and the ability to warn early on tornadoes is absolutely difficult. But I you know the people at the National Weather Service have been the most impressive. They are the most dedicated, most loyal and they work their tails off and you see when when it becomes risky you've never seen people pour in to try to help. And that's why we need to have an automated model so that the people in the next town, right, those forecasters can get the same data and assist and we can have the whole set of meteorologists and hydrologists help. And that's what I'm going to leave this department with, the most modern National Weather Service ever that will help protect and defend all of us because we rely on it and it's really important to you and really important to me. I appreciate that Mr. Secretary. I'm also proud to support the University of Missouri's Research Reactor. The most powerful university-based reactor in the country. It is a critical national supplier, as you know, of life-saving medical isotopes that treat cancer patients each and every year. We're trying to raise the money now for a second reactor. It is a game changer in medicine. How will commerce continue to support university-linked research facilities such as reactor operations? Our university system is one of the great assets of our country and our tech hubs and our biology work, medical work, these are extraordinary opportunities. And we are focused on making sure that the universities work together in consortium with business to create the best outcomes, right? That's we are very, very focused on outcomes and that's what matters the most to commerce is that we want to make sure if you appropriate money that we're going to get the benefit of the bargain for the American people and be great stewards of the money you give us. Mr. Secretary, thank you for your service and with that I yield back chair. Ranking member DeLauro. Mr. Secretary, during the campaign the president made a lot of promises about trade policy. And I quote, he would put more money into our pockets and create millions and millions of new manufacturing jobs. I mentioned earlier in my opening remarks I think hardworking families are not really feeling this reality. The president's tariffs have exacerbated the cost of living crisis that people are experiencing today. I know the president says it's not an affordability crisis, that's a hoax, but talk to working Americans every day; they are living paycheck to paycheck, it's tough. And American families now have paid more than $1700 each in tariff costs last year. And so things are now worse because of the president's war of choice with Iran and we see the price of gasoline going up. The president made promises also about manufacturing and the US trade deficit in manufactured goods was $62 billion higher in 2025 compared to 2024. Not exactly the manufacturing renaissance that was promised. So let me ask you secretary, and I asked Ambassador Greer the same question last week and let me ask this of you: do you know how many manufacturing jobs the United States lost per day last year, the first year of President Trump's second term? I don't know that statistic one way or the other. Okay, well let me just say this is according to the Bureau of Labor Statistics data. US manufacturing lost more than 100 jobs per day from February 2025 to February 2026. That's more than 88,000 jobs in total. It's not a trade policy that delivers for working people. Do you not calculate the number of jobs lost and what do we think about that? You know, this is really pretty extraordinary when we were supposed to be producing all of these manufacturing jobs. I would just say that the other thing was the Bureau of Labor Statistics, according to them on net there was an increase of over 500,000 manufacturing jobs under the Biden administration. And I don't really want to have to talk about the Biden administration, but you all have been here for well over a year and we now need to measure your performance against standards that you set. There is a decline in manufacturing jobs that we've seen in the first year. So let me ask you what your response to that is and to the decline of manufacturing jobs. The tariff and trade policy of President Trump and this administration is to bring manufacturing back to America. But as Representative Meng points out, when we were up in Micron, we broke ground on a $100 billion plant that's going to have 40,000 construction jobs. TSMC in Arizona, again 40,000 construction jobs. I broke ground in Ohio, 25,000 construction jobs. So the manufacturing is going to start in two or three years when these factories come online, but construction is bringing this back. $400 billion in pharmaceuticals coming in because of our tariff policy, semiconductors, we will have a trillion dollars of semiconductor announced investments during President Trump's administration, a trillion dollars of semiconductor manufacturing again and again. Let me reclaim my time for a moment Mr. Secretary because I know, but you can't even tell us how many jobs we've lost. So I don't know about what we've seen in the future. But you just as a good segue for me what you're doing. Your budget suggests that this committee what in your budget request you abandon the programs that do the work of trying to promote American manufacturing. Just look at how you zero out the manufacturing extension partnership, which provides small businesses with tools, training, and resources to grow, innovate, and remain competitive. You also eliminate the economic development administration grants supporting workforce development, innovation, and entrepreneurship. And let me also say it's worth noting that in your confirmation hearing you opposed cuts to EDA and it's now going to be eliminated. You gut the international trade administration, the global markets program. How do you propose that small businesses survive and thrive if you're not going to give them the tools that they need to be able to perform, to support development, to support workforce development? It's just incomprehensible. You mentioned these very large projects that you're doing but you're cutting all of these efforts off to deal with small businesses and their ability to be able to succeed and to grow manufacturing. Comment? What's the matter? Miss DeLauro, thank you. The time—I just wanted to ask whether you wanted me to respond in bulk or... Why don't you try to answer it as the Chairman will, why don't you try to answer the question Mr. Secretary. Time of the gentlelady has expired. No answers to the questions. Mr. Clyde. Thank you very much Mr. Chairman. Secretary Lutnick thank you for appearing before our committee today. I want to thank you for the Bureau of Industry and Security's decision to rescind the Biden-era interim final rule on firearm export controls. That rule placed an undue burden on American firearm manufacturers and shut them out of overseas markets. Rescinding it was the right call. And so I appreciate the administration's action on this and want to say thank you. And also the example that you gave of the Bead, the broadband equity access and deployment program savings of $21 billion—did I get that right, 21 billion dollars? Okay, 21 billion dollars. That is incredible and that is a great testament to someone who comes in and says you know I think we need some competition in this and we need to be able to look at it and say one size doesn't fit all. All right, and so I think this highlights the president's constitutional authority of impoundment. Here you have done something with 50% of the money and you've got 50% of the money saved and therefore I think that should be able to either go back to the treasury or simply be unspent because in the end it ends up being borrowed money. So with that I thank you. Now I want to talk a little bit about the Bead program. Blue Ridge Mountain EMC in my district is a member-owned cooperative or electric cooperative that was excited to receive a $3.8 million Bead award towards their $13.1 million project to connect 3,700 hard-to-reach households in northeast Georgia with broadband. But on January 12th of this year, the National Telecom and Information Administration quietly added new language to the Bead general terms and conditions requiring cooperatives to comply with FCC pole attachment rules across their entire service footprint, not just the Bead-funded areas, but for the entire service footprint. And this is a serious problem for a few reasons and I'll go through that. First, Congress explicitly exempted electric cooperatives from FCC pole attachment rules under Section 224 of the Communications Act. And as I understand it, the NTIA has no mandate to undo that exemption. Second and most critical, Blue Ridge Mountain EMC is regulated by the Tennessee Valley Authority, which governs pole attachment rates for all 153 TVA-served utilities using a standardized valley-wide methodology. And so as I see it, NTIA is demanding that this cooperative violate its obligations to its own congressionally authorized federal regulator in order to accept a federal grant from a different agency. Blue Ridge Mountain EMC has concluded that the compliance burden may already outweigh the benefit and may be forced to walk away from the grant entirely and I don't want to see that happen at all. All right, so are you familiar with this kind of issue, with this issue? That's the benefit of this job. I'm familiar with all of these issues. Okay great, fantastic. I just want to let you know... So I looked into this. The TVA is exempt from these rules and Blue Ridge Mountain EMC is exempt and they can stick to the policy and the process that you and I would agree is the best for your area. Okay, so thank you for that commitment. I was going to ask you for that and you beat me to the punch. So thank you very much, we'll take that as gospel. So thank you sir. I was encouraged to see your budget request reduces the Department of Commerce's footprint by 1.8 billion, a 16.5% decrease from the FY 26 enacted level. And your proposal rightly eliminates duplicative programs within the Economic Development Administration and the Minority Business Development Agency and curtails billions in federal funding dollars that the Biden-Harris administration funneled into radical climate initiatives research and grant programs. So Mr. Secretary, you propose eliminating the Economic Development Administration and the Minority Business Development Agency. Can you describe how both of these agencies, the EDA and the MBDA, were wasting taxpayer dollars on earmarks for political pet projects and subsidies for leftist ideologies and the reasoning then behind that savings? So for example the MBDA had a very, very rough IG report. And I have that audit here. And it is brutal. It actually literally says that over 92% of the awards should never have been made, that the economic analysis was wrong and flawed, and so it was a mess. So we sought to make it zero because it was a mess. If it was effective, great. But if, you know, and these other programs—the problem like the MEP, right? The idea is a good idea. But you should read the inspector general's report. It'll make you sick to your stomach. They overpay the people. This was a setup in 1988 to do advanced manufacturing. And what happened is it just crept and crept and crept to where the money was just not being used effectively. So whatever appropriations you give to me, what I will promise you is I will do our darndest to use it incredibly effectively for the benefit of the bargain of the American people. But if the current program is broken, like MBDA, right, I'm obviously going to suggest to you zero it out, because it's broken. I'm not saying the ideas are wrong, I'm saying if the execution is wrong, I've got to come here and tell you it's broken and you can feel good to zero it out. If you want to come up with a new program and talk about it, let's do that together, but let's not do it wrong. Well thank you, I think that's wise counsel and I appreciate that. Mr. Chairman, with that I yield back. Gentleman yields back. Mr. Morelle. Thank you. I would note Mr. Chair that gave Mr. Clyde a minute and a half additional time and and the ranking member was cut off and wouldn't answer the wouldn't listen to the answer by the Secretary. But anyway, Mr. Secretary thank you for being here. I often quote as my friend reminds me Mark Twain, but I'll quote you. You said the US economy is the strongest and most resilient in the world. I think if Mr. Twain were here he would say given this administration it needs to be. Last year you testified before this subcommittee when I shared my concerns about I have business leaders in Rochester, New York because you know Republicans aren't the only people who talk to business leaders and people making investment about tariff policies that have thrown them into a constant state of uncertainty. And the chaos remains the only predictable thing in this administration. The past year there have been dozens of changes to tariff rates, target countries, products covered, specific exemptions and a complete overhaul of the tariff authorities being used. When businesses don't have predictability—and I learned a long time ago when I'd meet with business leaders they'd say predictability is the most important thing. You'd do almost anything you want, I mean they wouldn't like it, but at least if it's predictable. There is no predictability. And the market conditions are changing literally week to week. The ranking member mentioned 34 billion dollar drop in factory construction investments in the last year, 89,000 manufacturing jobs lost. I and I appreciate the continuity between the Biden and Trump administrations for Micron, which is up in my region and others. I'm proud of that as Americans we should be. But it's hard to argue when 30% of Americans approve of the administration's handling of the economy. 89,000 manufacturing jobs lost and chaos. I don't know what to tell business leaders, so if you don't mind just answering that question because I think this uncertainty is—and look these are people's lives, families' lives, and from day to day I hear from leaders say I don't know what to do. You would tell business leaders to build in America, build in America. We have advanced manufacturing let's build in America. $400 billion of pharmaceuticals coming in because of our tariff policy. Semiconductors, we will have a trillion dollars of semiconductor announced investments during President Trump's administration, a trillion dollars of semiconductor manufacturing again and again. Let me just for a moment let's just hone in on that. So I represent Rochester, New York, dozens of small and medium-sized optics manufacturers, one of the great manufacturing optics and research centers in the United States. These companies create everything from eyeglasses to metrology machines. They have lenses and hypersonic missiles; almost everything that has a microelectronic chip has a lens. One I talked to just a few weeks ago, they're trying to onshore more of their production. To do that they have to buy a multi-million dollar machine because it's not made in the United States; they have to buy it from allies. It's a $10 million purchase. The tariff alone from the country that they're bringing it in from under a Section 232 tariff is 10%, adds a million dollars to the upfront cost. They're thinking about not doing it. This is reclaiming a million dollars on these. So respectfully, building in the United States is great. I talk to a company that has manufacturing in Rochester and manufacturing in Berlin. Because of Canadian imports that are going to get taxed, they said to me it is less expensive for us, more profitable for us, to build and extend that manufacturing line in Berlin than it is in the United States. So I look, I'd love to tell people to build. We all want all of us, Democrats, Republicans, I don't care who you are, to build more manufacturing jobs in the United States. But you're losing them. And yes, you can cite an investment here or there, but the problem is the playing field is uneven, it's chaotic, respectfully it's reckless. And look, you—I appreciate you have to be the cheerleader for it, and not just you, every commerce secretary is a cheerleader for every president. I get that. But at some point you gotta advise the president this is reckless, it's hurting Americans. Even the notion you're talking about auto manufacturers, the president's talking about Chinese auto manufacturers coming to the United States. They're going to compete with American auto manufacturers, but that's what we're talking about. And you're talking about China and by the way their electric vehicles... No, no, they're not allowed to bring Chinese cars here, absolutely not. Well the president's talking about allowing Chinese manufacturers to build and sell into American markets. It was announced last week. He's talking about it. No. Okay well you should talk to him about what he says then. I'll yield back Mr. Chair. Gentleman yields back. Mr. Shreve. Thank you Mr. Chairman and thank you to our witness Mr. Secretary. Happy to be here. Yes this is my first day at subcommittee; this was my first week at committee. But I am—thank you, thank you, thank you. I'm also a fairly new representative, I'm in my first term and I come from the middle of Indiana. I represent central Indiana, eastern Indiana, and as a consequence in part of that as I came into service I came to realize how victimized so many of my constituents—sort of trusting Hoosiers from the heartland—had been victimized by scams, many of which emanate from foreign scam centers. This is not a mission that I had coming into service but one which I came to realize how much and to what degree so many of my colleagues up here represent constituents who are being bilked out of billions of dollars in scams. And I think particularly so with some of our older constituents, those from maybe more by default trusting parts of the country like mine and our chairman, maybe perhaps than your neck of the woods where people may be less likely to answer an unknown call. But in the vein of the all too common text and email scams, phishing scams, that are collectively stealing from our citizens, I want to ask you what our Department of Commerce is doing to push stronger authentication standards for foreign digital traffic before those calls and texts reach our citizens. I think that's a really interesting point to try to have the origin of the call be made clear to the recipient. So if—because especially with what's coming with AI where they someone could, you know have my voice, right, and use my voice to call my family. It would be nice to know that it's from some crazy location in Asia. So I think this is excellent and if you'd like to work together with my team I think let's go explore how to change that. I think that's a really extraordinary idea and very valuable to America. All right, I would indeed like to play that forward and it relates in part to the conversation on export controls of these high-end chips as as the chairman noted. Your budget requests a historic increase of $215 million and the addition of enforcement agents. And AI is at the root of some of these challenges. Certainly the fairly small scams, the text message scam, the speed cam scams, toll road scams. But the real sophisticated ones, some of which in my own district bilked a constituent out of $600,000 in property, the pig butchering scams are enabled by the AI technology. These high-end chips can over time increasingly dupe our citizens. Can you just add a little color to how you aim to use the additional funding in this budget increase on the enforcement for BIS enforcement of those high-end chips that are making their way all too often to South Central Asia? I serve on, well have served up to this point on foreign affairs in the South Central Asia subcommittee and that's how I really came to realize how focused these foreign scam centers were on that part of the world. We need law enforcement agents, right? The budget increase is for law enforcement agents and the economics of those law enforcement agents while we're here talking about the budget expense, the revenues that these agents call in by fining these companies far far exceed the cost. So if this was a business conversation, it would be a slam dunk if there ever was one, right? Because I think these increases will go between five and 10 times the money of each agent we add. But because this is government where we have expense in one conversation and revenue's a whole another thing, you know as Commerce Secretary I really want those agents. They will protect us, they will protect Americans, and they will pay for themselves multiple multiple times. Thank you. Mr. Chairman, I timely yield back. Congratulations to the gentleman. Miss Dean. Thank you Chairman Rogers, Ranking Member Meng, Ranking Member DeLauro. Good afternoon, Secretary Lutnick. Last Tuesday I had the chance to attend a breakfast for RAINN, the Rape, Abuse & Incest National Network. It was their congressional day of action. Survivors came to me, told me their horrific stories, and the survivors I spoke with had one message. They're seeking accountability and justice. After speaking with the survivors I again went to the Department of Justice to review the unredacted Epstein files to seek accountability and justice for the victims, the more than 1,000 victims and survivors. And today, Secretary Lutnick, I have some questions for you about what I saw. Let's start with your relationship with sex trafficker Jeffrey Epstein. In October, you told the New York Post with colorful narration that the last time you saw Jeffrey Epstein was in 2005 when you took a tour of his house, which happened to be right next door to yours. You claim you and your wife were so grossed out by this disgusting person you swore you would never go back. But when the Epstein files came out it turned out that that was a lie. You were forced to admit that you had visited Epstein's private island in 2012, four years after Epstein pled guilty to soliciting a minor for prostitution and was labeled a sex offender. You brought along your wife, your kids, and your nannies to the predator's island. Incredibly poor judgment. But that even wasn't the whole truth. It turns out that five days after visiting his island you...

Revolut CEO Nik Storonsky on Building a Fintech Giant | The David Rubenstein Show

David Rubenstein(308.0K) ·24:06 ·17.4K views · 2026-04-23
Nik Storonsky (CEO, Revolut) David Rubenstein (Host, The David Rubenstein Show)
Founded by former trader Nik Storonsky, Revolut has grown from a niche currency exchange app into a $75 billion global "super-app" aiming to become the world’s first truly global digital bank with a target of 100 million daily active users.
TL;DR Founded by former trader Nik Storonsky, Revolut has grown from a niche currency exchange app into a $75 billion global "super-app" aiming to become the world’s first truly global digital bank with a target of 100 million daily active users. Product Ecosystem: Revolut...

TL;DR

Founded by former trader Nik Storonsky, Revolut has grown from a niche currency exchange app into a $75 billion global "super-app" aiming to become the world’s first truly global digital bank with a target of 100 million daily active users.

The Growth and Scale of the Revolut "Super-App"

  • Product Ecosystem: Revolut functions as an all-in-one financial hub, offering retail banking, wealth management, stock and crypto trading, insurance, and lifestyle services like eSIMs and hotel bookings.
      • Market Penetration: The platform currently serves 75 million customers. In Europe, one in five adults uses Revolut; in Ireland, penetration is as high as 80%.
      • Revenue Model: While initially built on offering interbank exchange rates with no fees, the company earns revenue through merchant fees (2-3% per transaction) and a suite of premium banking and business services.
  • Global Expansion: Currently operating in 40 countries, the company aims to reach 100 markets by 2030.
      • US and Asia Strategy: Storonsky is actively pursuing banking licenses in the United States, Mexico, and several Asian markets, including plans for China.
      • US License Timeline: While official targets are four months, Storonsky anticipates the process could take up to 12 months, noting that a new administration and existing licenses in the UK and Mexico may streamline the process.

Origins and Professional Philosophy

  • Founding Context: Storonsky, a former trader at Lehman Brothers and Credit Suisse, founded the company in 2013 after becoming frustrated by the high fees associated with international spending.
      • The Lehman Experience: Storonsky was on the trading floor when Lehman Brothers collapsed in 2008, an experience that fueled his confidence in his ability to navigate volatile markets.
      • Bootstrapping: He launched the product in 2015 using approximately $500,000 of his own savings and did not spend money on marketing for the first five to seven years, relying entirely on word-of-mouth.
  • IPO Outlook: Storonsky expects the company to be ready for an IPO in roughly two years. He views going public primarily as a way to increase institutional "trust," which is vital for a global bank.

Quantum Light: A Scientific Approach to Venture Capital

  • VC Disruption: Storonsky founded Quantum Light, a $250 million venture arm, out of frustration with the "crowd thinking" and lack of logic in traditional venture capital.
  • Algorithmic Investing: The fund uses machine learning models and millions of data points to identify Series B and C startups, rather than relying on human judgment.
      • Performance: The model-driven approach reportedly produces 30-40% annual returns, placing it in the top 5% of global funds.
      • Value Add: Beyond capital, Storonsky provides startups with operational knowledge and access to Revolut's massive distribution network.

Corporate Culture and Technological Future

  • Talent Density: Storonsky prioritizes a "Dream Team" culture, preferring small groups of high-IQ, ambitious individuals over large teams of average performers.
      • Hiring Criteria: The company evaluates candidates based on three pillars: High IQ/logic, character (hunger and ambition), and specific technical skills.
  • The Role of AI: Rather than fearing AI, Storonsky views Large Language Models (LLMs) as a new "interface" layer that allows customers to interact more seamlessly with the bank's complex backend logic.
  • The Decline of Legacy Banks: Storonsky predicts significant consolidation in the banking sector over the next 10-20 years, with "digital champions" eventually displacing legacy institutions that rely on physical infrastructure.
"My initial idea was to build a product, a simple app with a card attached, that allows you to spend money anywhere in the world at interbank rate—saving effectively $50 to $70 on every $1,000 that you spend." — Nik Storonsky
"We prefer to work in a small team of extremely high intelligence people rather than in large teams of average people." — Nik Storonsky
"For the bank it’s super important to have trust. And public companies are trusted more compared to private companies. That’s the reason [to go public]." — Nik Storonsky
This is my kitchen table and also my filing system. Over much of the past three decades, I've been an investor. The highest calling of mankind, I've often thought, was private equity. And then I started interviewing. While I watched your interview shows, I know how to do some interviews. I've learned in doing my interviews how leaders make it to the top. I asked him how much he wanted. He said 250. I said fine, didn't negotiate with him and did no due diligence. I have something I'd like to sell. How they stay there. You don't feel inadequate now because being only the second wealthiest man in the world, is that right? Bloomberg presents The David Rubenstein Show. Peer to peer conversations. In 2015, Nik Storonsky started a small company designed to help people with their foreign currency exchanges. Today, that company is Revolut. It is the most valuable pre-IPO company in all of Europe. I had a chance recently in Washington, DC, to sit down with Nik Storonsky to talk about his amazing story, how he grew a very small company into such a giant financial behemoth. Let's talk about Revolut because it's a company that's based in UK, focused more on Europe than the United States, and we'll get to that in a moment. But let's talk about what it does for the American audience, for example. What is Revolut? What does it actually do? Well, Revolut is a digital bank. So we provide global banking services for people in more than 40 countries. So everything is packed into one simple app. So you can do your banking, you can do wealth management, stock trading, CFDs, bonds, crypto. You can borrow money. And you also have lifestyle services. For example, you can get yourself eSIM. You can, you know, book a lounge, you can book a hotel and so on. So everything is in one app for your financial services. Did you have a partner to start it with? I actually started alone. So I built the product, I hired a team. Then one of my first CTOs that I hired, so I later made him, I think it was 2017, 2018, I made him co-founder as well. It was easier for him to kind of, you know, recruit engineers if he had the title co-founder. So you start the company and the mission you had was to enable people to do what that they couldn't do. What was your purpose in starting the company? So I was a trader back then. And then I'm very quick in calculating numbers in my head. And then every time when I went abroad or when I sent money, I could instantly calculate, okay, I lost here $10, $50 and so on. My initial idea was to build a product, a simple app with a card attached, that allows you to spend money anywhere in the world at interbank rate. Saving effectively $50 to $70 on every $1,000 that you spend. In other words, you weren't trying to convert people's currency, you were letting them have a card that would enable them to buy something in whatever currency they wanted? Yeah, so it's both, right? You can pre-exchange currency or you can buy directly from your existing currency. Now normally, throughout Europe, when you go to Europe, you see everywhere currency exchange places, and they always charge a fee. I don't remember what it is, 6%, 7%. So you decided to start a currency exchange business without charging a fee? Well, you can call it currency exchange business, yes, at interbank rate without charging a fee. Correct. You didn't go out and build a physical bank. You did it all online. Is that right? Exactly, yeah. So how did you get people to know about this service? Did you start advertising online? Funny thing, we didn't spend any single marketing for the first five to seven years of being in operation. So it was all word of mouth. So I remember when I finally launched the product, it was summer 2015. And I physically, you know, was selling my product to friends, family, even people I don't know. Okay, so if I go to London today and I say here's $10 and I want to exchange it on a regular place, I would get back pounds minus about a 6, 7%, 8% fee. Correct. So if I went on your service, what would I get back? Would I get back everything with virtually no fee? Exactly, yeah. Okay, then you make the money in effect on the float, because you're getting paid by somebody and then you kind of can invest that money and then you don't have to pay somebody for a while? Is that how you make the money? So every time when you spend with the card, merchant effectively pays you, pays 2 to 3%, yeah. Okay. But that's not a customer paying, but rather the business itself. But I want to be clear, that was the initial product in 2015. Now, especially for US customers, we are a fully kind of licensed digital bank, providing all kind of services. So you have a European banking license now, is that right? So we have a European banking license; we're regulated by the European Central Bank. We have a UK banking license regulated by the PRA. And then we also have multiple other licenses. For example, Mexico, Colombia, Peru and so on. And you're now applying for a US banking license? Correct, yes. How long does it take to get a US banking license? Right now, it should take I would say up to 12 months. But again, it depends. The official target is four months, but then there are multiple steps involved. It can be longer. Are you confident that you can get a banking license in the United States or you think you'll get it? I think it's obviously much easier for us given the new administration, plus that we have so many other banking licenses, plus we have a banking license in UK now. So for us it became much easier compared to two years ago. Do you have a credit card business as well? Yeah, we have credit cards in several markets, yes. And you have credit cards and debit cards? We have debit cards in every single market and then credit cards, I think, in five or six markets. So in Europe, we have 20% penetration. So one out of five adults in Europe, they use Revolut cards. And then in other markets, we have much higher penetration. For example, in Ireland, we have four out of five adults using Revolut. So younger people seem to like things that are called cryptocurrency. Are you involved in cryptocurrency? Do you trade cryptocurrencies or make markets for people? So as a business, we have a pretty large cryptocurrency business. I think we are the largest, we have the largest crypto business in Europe with our volumes. Personally, I don't speculate crypto, but I use it sometimes, you know, for transfers. So in the United States, what do you hope to be able to do with your banking license if you get it? What is the market gap that you think you can fill that maybe others aren't doing? So basically I mentioned already our retail business. It's one simple app where you can do everything what you need for your financial life. And then second direction, we also provide Revolut Business accounts for businesses. And again, it's end-to-end services that you need to run your business. So payments, banking, credit will come as well, wealth management, or treasury, we call it treasury. And then acquiring as well. For example, right now if I'm an American company, I'll need to set up say Stripe for acquiring, then JP Morgan for my business banking, then, you know, something else for payouts, something else for payroll. So we provide all these services in one product. Well, if you conquer the US the way you've conquered the UK and Europe, is China next or you're not going to focus on that? Asia we're definitely focusing. So we are spending a lot of time studying Asia. And then as I said, we applied for multiple banking licenses in Asia as well. And then some of them we got already. And then China is on the list as well, you know, one day I hope we can set up our business there. Now some people have written that your bank, Revolut, is worth $45 billion. Have you ever thought of taking your organization public? Well, the way I think about it, we kind of will be ready probably in two years' time. But then again, it depends on how good the market is. Since you don't have an IPO, how do people who work for you make some money? Do you make a market for people to sell their shares internally? Yeah, so we do secondaries every one or two years when people can sell a certain portion of their stocks to external investors. So you've done secondary sales for your employees. So employees can then get some liquidity. Do you expect before you go public you'll do any more of those or you're just going to wait till you go public before you liquefy any more stock? Yeah, I think so. We might do secondaries as well before we do the IPO because we do it every one or two years, so we might do secondaries as well. All right. Well, if anybody wants to sell their shares to me, let me know. And have any investment bankers told you why they're the best to do the IPO for you? Yeah, obviously every single one is the best. Every one is the best. You're not in a rush to do it. You don't need the money. So we are a bank, and then for the bank it's super important to have trust. And then public companies are trusted more compared to private companies. So that's the reason. What are you most proud of having done so far and what do you most enjoy? Well, it's basically designing something, designing a solution to solve a problem and then seeing it being realized and then using it. That's what I enjoy when I see some elegant solution. In September 2025, Revolut announced plans to enter how many markets by 2030? Revolut announced plans to enter more than 30 new markets by 2030 as part of its global expansion strategy. Targeting regions including Latin America, APAC, Africa and the Middle East. Many people think that companies like yours which are often called fintech companies, financial technology companies, are changing the world dramatically, but they could be changed dramatically. So, for example, artificial intelligence is coming along. Are you worried about artificial intelligence or do you use artificial intelligence to help grow your company? Well, it's an additional tool that we obviously use. And I think with LLM chatbots, it's another surface on top of the infrastructure that we build, because banking is not a simple app, right? Under the app you have a lot of backend, a lot of systems, you know, a lot of regulatory systems and so on. And then the app is effectively an interface to all this backend logic. And then LLM can be something on top of the app or directly interacting with our backend logic. So call it another interface, and that's what we are building as well and allowing people to interact with all our products through the chatbot. Let's talk about how you came to start the company. So you aren't, doesn't sound like you're a native of England from your accent. Originally from Russia. So I came to UK first in 2006 for the internship. Then in 2007 I came for another internship and afterwards I stayed to be employed by Lehman Brothers as a trader. And at what age did you leave Russia? 20 years old. And then as soon as I finished my university I moved to London to work full-time for Lehman Brothers. And were your parents educated, were they math specialists, or did they have computer training or not? So my father, he is a physicist by training. My mother, she is a teacher. I assume you were a pretty good math student or physics student or something because you have technology background, I assume. Yeah, I was always trained in math, physics as well. All right, so you go to England and did you have a job lined up before you got there? So Lehman Brothers came to my university and then they tried to recruit some people for the internship. So they recruited me for the internship, I came there in summer 2006. And then they invited me for a second internship in spring 2007, and they also gave me a job offer for full-time when I graduate from my university. So when I graduated in July, I think it was July 2007, I came to London to work full-time for Lehman Brothers. Didn't Lehman go under at some point? 2008, yeah, so I was in job for—were you there then? Yeah, I was there. I was one of probably 200 people on the trading floor when it all happened. I still remember it; there was I think Sunday evening announcement. And then I was wondering, okay, do I need to come Monday morning to my kind of, you know, job? So I came and then we had all these 200 traders locked at one trading floor, everything all the systems were locked, and we were all playing the same game. I think it was a game shooting helicopters or something, all all 200 people because there was nothing to do. So did you think capitalism wasn't such a great system at that point when you go to Lehman Brothers and they go bankrupt and you're out of work? Yeah, I was full of confidence, right, about myself. I was never afraid to lose the job because I knew I can always, you know, find a new one. So did you have a hard time finding another job? No, I got kind of job offers from Nomura and then from Credit Suisse as well. All right, and so where did you go? I went to Credit Suisse. You went to Credit Suisse. They've had their problems too, eventually. Did you leave Credit Suisse before they had their problems? Yeah, of course, I left Credit Suisse 2013. So basically as soon as I got my British passport I resigned, and then when I came to UK it was never kind of an objective to stay there more than two years. I also wanted to start my own business, but then you know, I stayed one year, then the second year, then the third year. And then after year six when I got my passport, I decided to resign and then start my own business. Okay, so you started your business in 2015? So started working in 2013 and I launched it 2015. Did you have any capital to help you start the company? I had my own savings, yes. Right, so you put in some of your own money, but you presumably weren't a multi-billionaire, so you didn't put in that much money, right? Well I mean back then I put probably about what half a million dollars overall. But obviously I also didn't work for two years myself, so if you don't count my salary I didn't have any salary. So to build something back then was much cheaper compared to nowadays. You have a venture capital arm, I think it's called Quantum Light? So it's a separate business; I set it up think it was three years ago. As an entrepreneur I've done a lot of fundraising rounds with VCs. And then I think at a certain point of time I was very pissed off with the crowd thinking mentality, right? So I have cases where term sheets were withdrawn, signed term sheets. I had cases where the round was marked down because S&P was down. So I had quite quite a lot of negative experience with VCs. And then as a founder I also wanted to invest my own money and I thought I can create something better in terms of investment. I think there has to be a scientific approach rather than human judgment. And then we build effectively models that invest in startups series B based on millions and millions of data points on startups. So they are trained machine learning models that invest. Plus on top of it I'm able to share with these startups my operational knowledge as a founder of Revolut. Plus we help startups to distribute their product through Revolut or to sell into Revolut. Overall it's a much better value proposition to founders compared to VC. But what would you call what you're doing, angel investing or round A or? We we do series B. We we started we we just launched a fund for series C as well. But did you raise money for it? I assume people gave you all the money you wanted, but did you just use your own money and your friends' money or did you have to go out and raise money? First fund was $250 million. So I put around 25% my own money and then the rest was external LPs. And what types of rates of return do you think you can get in that fund? So basically when I had this idea, when we build the model and then we started back testing the model, it produces effectively 40 to 50% year-on-year returns, long-term returns. So when we started investing based on the model, I think now we are call it top 4, top 5% of the funds in the world in terms of returns. So it's usually 30 to 40% returns. So do you ever use your credit card and it gets turned down? In other words, you go to use a credit card at a restaurant or somewhere and they say your credit's denied, has that ever happened to you? Initially, yes. Yeah, but it was more than 8-9 years ago when the service was wasn't that stable. Now we just don't have declines. But now when that happened to you, did you tell people who you were? Or you didn't say, look, I'm the founder of the company and you can't—? Just no, no, I was just calling engineers and then trying to find out what happened, why why why it's declined. How many customers does Revolut have now? So we have 75 million customers. And how big do you think you can take it? Do you think you can take it to 100 million customers or more? Yeah, we can definitely take it to 100 million customers. Our internal target is 100 100 million daily active customers. And then 100 million daily active customers will transfer about 300 million monthly actives. It is said that companies, if they're really going to survive and prosper in for over many period years of time, they have to have a good culture. What is the culture you're trying to build in your company? So we have these five cultural values pretty well defined, starting with never settle. Never settle is about being ambitious and setting up big goals. Secondly it's about dream team. So we prefer to work in a small team of extremely high intelligence people rather than in a large teams of average people. Third one is get things done. It's very important to be kind of, you know, getting things done oriented. Then we have a cultural principle called deliver wow. It's about almost perfection in everything what we do in terms of product. What new worlds do you want to conquer? You've built a big company. Well, we're still scratching the surface in terms of what is possible in in building the first truly global bank. Because now we are in 40 countries, we want to be in 100 countries. If you look at the future of the financial service world, do you think that there's a future for large banks to keep having these big buildings or do you think ultimately 5 or 10 or 15 years from now everybody's going to be online with everything? In 5-10 years' time we'll see more and more consolidation and I think we'll see more and more certain digital champions in regions taking more and more banking shares in these regions. So maybe it will take 10 years, maybe it will take 15 years, 20 years, but I do think that we'll see less and less legacy banks. Or less and less legacy banks will make so much money. So are you enjoying doing all this? Do you enjoy running the bank, running the venture fund and doing interviews with people like me or you're having a good time or are you not that happy? I mean certainly I like certain things; I like, certain things I I don't like. So I love building products. I love kind of solving problems. I don't like delays which can be quite often in regulatory business, because certain things can be outside of kind of, you know, your decision making, your speed. What are the skill sets that you think are required to build a company like this and do you look for those same skill sets in the people you hire? Well, you definitely do not need to be good at trading at all, right? For me when I started it, I knew nothing about banks because I was a trader, investment bank and it's very different organization compared to a retail bank. And then a payments company as well. So I learned everything from scratch. Skills-wise what you need to have. I think you definitely need to have, you know, ambitions and hunger plus as a character. Plus, you know, a lot of energy because it's pretty painful, you know, in a lot of ways with all the problems. Plus, you always need to have very strong logic, logical thinking, abstract thinking. You should be good with product as well, like, you know, ability to build something nice and sleek. We are just looking for ultimately three things. First is high IQ or brains and then we have certain ways how to understand whether a person is smart or not, whether they're logical or not. That's number one. Number two is character, how ambitious they are, how hungry they are. And third is skills, right? If we hire a designer, they need to be good in design and so on. What new worlds do you want to conquer? You've built a big company, eventually you might take it public, you got a great venture capital firm. What do you want to do next? Well, the majority of time I dedicate still to Revolut. We're still scratching the surface in terms of what is possible in in building the first truly global bank. Are you worried that somebody who's 10 years younger than you is going to come along and with a better mousetrap and beat what you're doing or you're not worried about that so much? I mean really, I think there are windows of opportunity in the world. And then when there are certain tools, certain opportunities and then usually what happens, 10-20 people jump on these opportunities in this particular country to do this particular product and then people compete. I think the opportunity for fintech I think in the majority of countries is gone now because okay, there are already players and then legacy banks, they can kind of, you know, catch up as well with products. So it's very hard to build anything new in fintech, or at least I don't see opportunity. But nonetheless, I mean, maybe I don't see anything, maybe there is you know, someone smart who can come up with a better product. So most of the bankers I know have hair that's like mine, it's shorter, yours is a little bit longer. Is that your trademark or if you want to get a banking license in the United States you think you need to cut your hair or not? I think I definitely need to wear a suit. Yeah, that's what I've done. Do you have friends from high school that that call you up and say remember me, I was your friend in high school? Sometimes, yeah, friends of friends. But it's not that hard to get a hold of you? No, no, it's easy. It's easy. Okay, well next time I'm in London I will call you up and see how how easy it is to get a hold of you. Of course. Make sure you tell me your number so I record it, otherwise I don't pick up unknown numbers. You're now probably the richest person in UK, or certainly one of them. What are you going to do with this? You're going to build art galleries or museums or give it away to social causes? What do you want to do with all this wealth? You can't spend $25 billion in your lifetime probably. Well, I'm just very focused on business, continue to make the best product and then continue to expand. And then all this wealth is in the company, right? So I do not really plan to sell the company and then spend all this money. So I just continue to work on, you know, what's necessary to build the first global bank. Because now we are in 40 countries, we want to be in 100 countries.

Tim Cook’s Apple CEO era + John Ternus’s Apple future | Engadget Podcast

Engadget(1.4M) ·87:07 ·1.2K views · 2026-04-23
Tim Cook (CEO, Apple) John Ternus (SVP of Hardware Engineering, Apple)
Tim Cook is stepping down as Apple CEO on September 1st to become Executive Chairman, handing the reins to hardware lead John Ternus in a shift that moves the company from a logistics-focused leadership style back toward a product-driven "visionary" approach.
TL;DR Tim Cook is stepping down as Apple CEO on September 1st to become Executive Chairman, handing the reins to hardware lead John Ternus in a shift that moves the company from a logistics-focused leadership style back toward a product-driven "visionary" approach. Executive...

TL;DR

Tim Cook is stepping down as Apple CEO on September 1st to become Executive Chairman, handing the reins to hardware lead John Ternus in a shift that moves the company from a logistics-focused leadership style back toward a product-driven "visionary" approach.

The Apple CEO Transition: From Logistics to Product

  • Executive Leadership Change: Tim Cook will transition to Executive Chairman of the Board, while John Ternus, currently the SVP of Hardware Engineering, will take over as CEO on September 1st.
      • The "Product Guy" Shift: Unlike Cook, who was a Chief Operations Officer known for logistics and supply chain efficiency, Ternus is a designer and engineer who led the redesigns of the MacBook and Apple Silicon integration.
      • Leadership Style: Reporting suggests Ternus is a more "decisive" leader compared to Cook’s consensus-based approach, drawing parallels to Steve Jobs’ management style.
  • The "MacBook Neo" Soft Launch: The hosts noted that Ternus was the primary face of the recent MacBook Neo launch, handling key interviews and presentations—a role typically reserved for Cook during major product debuts.
  • The Road Ahead: Ternus will take over just as Apple enters "iPhone season," with rumors swirling about a potential 20th-anniversary redesign and a foldable iPhone in development.

Tim Cook’s Legacy and the "Services" Era

  • Operational Prowess: Cook is credited with turning Apple into a trillion-dollar powerhouse by streamlining inventory and mastering global logistics.
  • The Rise of Services: Under Cook, Services (Apple TV+, Apple Music, iCloud) became the company’s second-largest revenue driver behind the iPhone, creating a "capitalist loop" that locks users into the hardware ecosystem.
  • Political Navigation: The briefing touched on Cook's "stable" but controversial leadership, specifically his ability to navigate the Trump administration to avoid tariffs—a role he will continue to play as Executive Chairman when engaging with global policymakers.

Palantir and the "Hard Power" Manifesto

  • Alex Karp’s "Manifesto": Palantir CEO Alex Karp released a 1,000-word post summarizing his book, *The Technological Republic*, which the hosts described as "deciding to post fash [fascism] on main."
      • Hard Power Requirements: Karp argued that free societies require "hard power" built on software rather than just "soaring rhetoric."
      • Universal Service: The manifesto called for a move away from an all-volunteer military toward universal national service to ensure "everyone shares in the risk."
  • Silicon Valley Obligations: Karp claims that the engineering elite have an "affirmative obligation" to participate in national defense.

Surveillance Tech and Workplace Monitoring

  • Smart Glasses for ICE: Reports indicate Homeland Security is interested in developing smart glasses for ICE agents to record video and collect biometric data of migrants and protesters.
  • Meta Employee Tracking: Meta admitted to training AI models by tracking the keystrokes, mouse movements, and clicks of its own employees.
      • Data Harvesting: Hosts suggested this is a sign that tech companies are "running out of data" and are resortng to harvesting the digital lives of their workforce to build "AI agents."

Gaming and PC Market Trends

  • "RAMageddon" Panic Buying: Global PC shipments grew 3.2% year-over-year, largely driven by consumers "preemptive buying" to avoid expected price increases in memory and RAM.
  • Xbox Game Pass Restructuring: Microsoft has lowered the price of Game Pass Ultimate to $23 (down from $30) following consumer backlash over previous price hikes.
      • Call of Duty Exclusion: New *Call of Duty* titles will notably not be included in the standard tiers, potentially leading to a future "Call of Duty tier" for an additional fee.
"Tim Cook was the logistics guy... John Ternus is a product guy. John Ternus is a designer. He initially started at Apple doing the studio display, but went on to do AirPods, Apple Watch, and eventually iPhone." — Devindra Hardawar, Engadget Senior Editor
"Cook was the kind of CEO who would announce something and then get out of the way and let the people who know about it talk about it... it democratized the keynote a bit." — Nate Ingraham, Engadget Deputy Editor
"National service should be a universal duty. We should as a society seriously consider moving away from an all-volunteer force... Service guarantees citizenship. Would you like to know more?" — Alex Karp, Palantir CEO (quoted by Devindra Hardawar)
"If we're building agents to help people complete everyday tasks using computers, our models need real examples of how people actually use them. We're launching an internal tool that will capture these kinds of inputs... to help us train our models." — Meta Spokesperson (quoted by Devindra Hardawar)

Good morning, can't complain. All is well here outside of Boston, marathon week. You're not sick like me, Nate. I'm not sick like you. Oh man, oh man. Also, podcast producer Ben Ellman is here. Hey Ben. I'm getting all the shots that I should have gotten in November now, so fingers crossed. Wow, fingers crossed. Better late than never. I, I definitely, I did not have time to get my flu shot last year and maybe I'm feeling it. Maybe some things hit harder than they should have. Get your shots folks, just whenever you can. Anyway, we're going to be talking about the big news this week which is that Tim Cook announced that he is stepping down from the CEO role at Apple on September 1st. He's going to be replaced by John Ternus. And John Ternus is the head of hardware engineering right now, basically the head product guy, the head hardware guy. Very interesting choice there. So we'll be talking about all of that. As always folks, if you're enjoying the show please be sure to subscribe to us on iTunes or your podcatcher of choice. Leave us a review, leave us a review on iTunes and drop us an email at podcast@engadget.com. And we'll live stream Thursday mornings typically around 9:00 a.m. Eastern on our YouTube channel when we can, whatever it's possible. If you know, things get in the way sometimes, but I do like doing it and I like seeing the people on the chat, so join us for that. All right. So yeah, Tim Cook officially stepping down as Apple CEO. This is something that's been rumored since last fall. And since last fall, I believe it was the Financial Times who reported it first. John Ternus was like up there as a potential contender. And it's, it's kind of an interesting choice like on the face of it. We've always known Tim Cook as the logistics guy. He was, he was previously to being the CEO, and also taking over as CEO a couple of times before Steve Jobs passed away, like he came in during sick leave, he was the COO of Apple, Chief Operations Officer. He was the guy dealing with logistics. He was the guy who I think famously, like shortly after he started, he shut down a lot of factories. He shut down a lot of like extra inventory they kept around and streamlined Apple's operations so that they were shipping products basically more directly to consumers, saving a lot of money in the long run. He's the logistics guy. Not exactly a creative product guy in the way that Jobs was, you know, championed. John Ternus is a product guy. John Ternus is a designer. He initially started at Apple doing, I believe, the studio monitor, the studio display, but went on to do AirPods, Apple Watch, and eventually iPhone. And he became SVP mid-pandemic, I believe it was like 2021. So he became the top, top hardware guy. And something I noticed is that at the MacBook Neo launch a couple of months ago, it was all John Ternus. It was on all John Ternus all the time. He was the one who introduced the MacBook Neo, he was on stage, he was doing interviews for Good Morning America. And typically that's Tim Cook. Typically Tim Cook is the face of the company for a big new product. This time it was all John Ternus and I think it really worked out well for him. I've got a lot more about this, about what the Neo kind of represents, but Nate, what do you think of this move first of all? Yeah, it feels like they sort of soft-launched Ternus, right, to just like see how, how it'd go. Seems like people responded pretty well to him. He, he has a pretty solid, you know, stage slash presentation... Hey Nate, I have to do some producing now. Can you move your mic a little bit further over to one side of your mouth because we're catching those P-pops. Oh yep, yep. How's this? Better? MacBook Pro. Alright. Yep, there we go. Nice, thank you. Anyway, yeah, I think that they kind of soft-launched Ternus with the Neo launch and a little bit over the last couple of years. Like, he's been on their, their videos more. He's a pretty solid presenter. I think he's a good like base for the company. And I think it's interesting, like you said, obviously going to a guy who's more focused on product. But yeah, as far as Cook goes, yeah, I think you could see that he didn't try to be Steve Jobs pretty much right off the bat, both in terms of like holding court for 90 minutes about new products. He was more the kind of CEO who would announce something and then get out of the way and let the people who know about it talk about it. Like take the Apple Watch, that was the first big new product launched under his direction. You know, obviously he was the guy who came out, he was wearing it, he introduced it, he talked about it, and then he stepped aside and let the people who did the engineering and the software be the ones who really told us how it would work, which is totally different than what Steve Jobs did. So I'd say that like democratized the keynote a bit, which is like just a, a tonal shift. And I expect that will continue. I don't think Ternus is going to be the kind of like singular, like, I don't think Apple is that company anymore. They don't need to have like a singular vision guy. They are big enough and have enough diverse, different parts that like it makes sense to, to show those, those people off and, you know, get the next Ternus in the spotlight, right? On top of the fact that it's just logistics, you know, you don't have one person up there who probably needs to take time out of their day running the company to practice the keynote and everything. Now you're doing your little intros and then they have the drone fly somewhere else on Apple's campus and you go to a completely different person. It just makes sense in a logistical, in a Tim Cook sort of way. Logistical yes, but the thing about, if you guys remember the Steve Jobs keynotes, it was the magic, right? It was the magic of his aura, him as a presenter. Watching him show off these products. Like, his cult of personality was Apple. So that him practicing to do the keynote and everything, that's the job to sell the dream of Apple. And we did kind of lose that a little bit, and probably for, for the better, right? Like, Jobs was famously also a giant asshole, not the nicest person in the world, but he lived and breathed his company and thought deeply about what his products could do and what they meant. There's some reporting from Mark Gurman at Bloomberg this week that Ternus is a more decisive decision maker than Tim Cook was. Like, Tim Cook apparently was more of a consensus guy, like if it came down to a product decision he would like ask around, see what people thought, kind of gather the consensus. Interesting parallel. Ternus is more decisive in the guy who will just like make a decision. And that was something Jobs was famously known for. And there's some stuff you can read into that, like what that could mean for the future. Could it lead to maybe Apple taking some slightly bigger risks or at least some interesting choices when it comes to products? And I think the Neo is certainly a good representative of that, right? Because first of all they've done some amazing stuff while Ternus was the head of hardware. He was the guy basically in charge of all the MacBook redesigns around Apple Silicon. I mean, I know he's not the, the chip guy, but like he was part of the product team that made that a thing. Yeah. I mean, he was leading essentially the product team that made that. Johny Srouji who is like the chip guy and the guy really responsible for Apple Silicon, he has a new role at the company as well. So he's still going to be sticking around. I think there were some worries that he would leave and that would be a bad thing because Apple Silicon has been so, I don't know, so good for them. But yeah, Ternus led the redesign of these Macs. I don't think we've had any complaints, right? Like, they fixed the problems with the MacBooks, they turned the Mac Mini into something that's genuinely desirable and useful and I think a really good deal as far as computing goes. The Mac Studio has become like this tremendous thing. And they're retiring the Mac Pro because the Mac Studio itself is so good. But the Mac Neo is interesting because it's a risky thing for Apple, right? It's the cheapest computer they've ever made. It's also both trying to make an affordable device that feels premium and has compromises. Has only 8 gigabytes of RAM. Has a cell phone processor in it. And yet, because of those choices, because of those design choices and the fact that Apple has such a strong handle on its own software, like that marriage of hardware and software, they can produce something like the Neo that no PC maker can, right? Any $600, any $800 Windows laptop is a terrible experience honestly, you really have to hit the $1000 mark. And the Neo is like "Hey, Apple can be cheap and still be good." And maybe this is a vision of the future, maybe a more open, accessible Apple. To me, that's a good sign for Ternus at least. Yeah, the Neo, I mean we talked about this a month ago, obviously represents a complete change in direction for Apple as they just never bothered to compete at a less than premium price point. And I feel like the old adage, you know, I think Joanna Stern coined this one, which was when she was reviewing laptops last decade she would say "oh for $200 more you can get a MacBook Air" with the implication that you spend a little more and you get a better experience. And now that's flipped because you can spend less and get a better experience than, you know, a comparable Windows PC. I don't know that Ternus is going to like push that mentality across their product lineup, but I also don't think they have to because most, you know, the iPad already has one that starts at 349, the Apple Watch SE continues to be a really good value for people who want a more basic experience. Obviously the iPhone is a little pricier but, you know, I think in this economy getting the, you know, 17E for less than it's at now isn't really feasible. So they've got the, the entry level pretty well covered. And it's not that it's cheap, but you're going to get a lot even for something that's more affordable. Yeah, and they don't always need to be the entry level, it's more like doing something different, right? Doing something that Apple really hasn't done before. I think that's kind of an interesting choice. I'm also thinking of like things people are looking at now. A lot of parents are interested in tracking where their kids are. A lot of people are using the Apple Watch for that. I could foresee something that's a little more child-oriented, like an Apple Watch that is maybe a little smaller and a little more kid-friendly and maybe a little more durable, but you can use that as part of your Apple Watch family plan and have a sense of like where everybody in your family is. When we talked about Apple turning 50 a couple of weeks ago, the idea of Apple as a personal computing company I think really hits home and like the MacBook Neo, that's a truly personal computer. That is a $600 device that does most of what people need. For students, it's a $500 device. I don't think that's the way Microsoft is thinking now. I don't think that's the way most other even PC companies think now. They're trying to like, they think in terms of specs, they think less in terms of user experience. I'm just interested, I'm interested in like what a product guy could do now because, you know, Tim Cook had some hit devices, right? The AirPods end up being hit, the Apple Watch was not initially a hit but became kind of a big deal over time. And then there's the Vision Pro which was like the big swing for a lot of people and that didn't really go anywhere, but I also don't, I don't know if that's like indicative of bad leadership, that just seems like they tried and not everything's going to work, right? I mean, even when it was announced we were like, is this meant to go anywhere? This is priced at being a dev kit more than anything. It's a dev kit. Yeah, and I think, you know, they kind of had to... it's one of those situations where they, they had to put their foot in the water and see. And I think all the companies doing that have figured out that there still isn't that like compelling killer app for such hardware, so it's, it's going to continue to be a niche thing. But you know, I'm sure there's stuff that they're learning about the way people use it that they can bring to future products as well. So yeah, it's very much like... it reminds me about how they called, and this is a completely different product lineup but I think Jobs called the Apple TV hardware a hobby for like a decade, right? I guess he wasn't around that long, but you know what I mean. He called it a hobby for years and I think Cook even did after Jobs passed away. He never understood the Apple TV I think, or at least entertainment as a thing. Yep. Yeah, I mean that's actually something we didn't talk about with Cook is that he look, kind of led the big push into entertainment in a, in a broader sense services, right? Like Apple TV+ has been a big deal, Apple Music is the second biggest streaming service I believe behind Spotify obviously, you know, and then you've got stuff like Fitness Plus, there's like the whole like, you know, iCloud or Apple One subscription, like all these ways. I've seen a chart where it shows the services growing in the last decade plus and it's just crazy how that's the second biggest part of the company now behind the iPhone. Yeah, I mean those are recurring fees and they also lock in your customers. It's like the perfect, the perfect bit of a capitalist loop, right? Like you're paying something over and over again also you don't want to leave if you actually like the service. It also makes you stay within the hardware and within the family and maybe buy more. So yeah, perfect, perfect storm there. Apple TV+ is not the best streaming service but they have put a lot of money into it, they have produced some really good shows and I don't think it's an abject failure compared to like something like Peacock, you know, which just felt like a desperate move from NBC. So let me prognosticate a little bit. There is a possibility that things move around in the entertainment industry in the next like three to five years, especially given the Paramount acquisition of WB and everything which includes HBO, there could be an opening for an HBO sometime soon because what if HBO doesn't act like HBO or the HBO that we knew? What do you mean? I'm saying that Apple TV+ could slide into that niche. If it's allowed to. If it's continued to be watered in the same way that it was watered under Tim Cook. It could be. It's just HBO has that brand. Like that's the thing. Whatever happens with the, the Warner Discovery sale and everything like it's, it's going to be a huge mess. But I was, I was always thinking like it would be really interesting if Apple was one of those people considering, you know, WB. I always felt like Netflix, we talked about this, Netflix would have been the better choice and we'll see kind of where things go from here. Of bad options. Of bad options. Sure. Yeah, but we're all doing... I would, I would have much rather seen Netflix take it over. Apple has been actually pretty conservative, like they've spent a lot of money but they have not gone like, I don't know, as hardcore as Netflix or other folks have in terms of like the amount of stuff they're producing. I kind of like that though, like I prefer a, the slower cadence, like it's easier to keep track of, like it doesn't need to be flooded with stuff because you know there's only so many hours in the day, right?
