And so you kind of want this graduated approach and to make sure that you are moving down that pipeline as you can bring in additional safeguards in order to make sure that you can maximize the benefits and mitigate the risks. And so we've really taken a deliberate approach. I think our team has been working incredibly hard to think through the cyber implications of this model. We also believe in iterative deployment, that's part of this really bringing the models as they continuously get better. And we believe in democratic access, that we believe that ultimately the goal of creating this technology is to empower people, to ensure that it does benefit all of humanity, and so we are constantly trying to solve for how do we safely and responsibly bring this technology to bear in the world in a broad way. Right. And I think suffice it to say that your team hasn't been fans of the way that Anthropic's deployed Mythos. This is a quote from Sam, "It's clearly incredible marketing to say we have built a bomb, we're about to drop it on your head, we will sell you a bomb shelter for 100 million to run across all your stuff, but only if we pick you as a customer." Let me talk through the other case and then get your response. The other case would be there are—you can't account for everything and there are clearly going to be some vulnerabilities that will only be found by people or entities deploying this and looking for them. So maybe it makes sense to start with a trusted group of testers before you deploy it broadly. What do you think? Well, I believe the correct answer here is subtle, and I think it is rooted in the technical specifics of what you have in front of you and many, many factors, right? You need to think about how are the models progressing, right? Not just your own capabilities but others in the ecosystem. You need to think about what kind of benefit do you get from having a small group that has access and are able to have high leverage by being able to find and produce patches, but then how do you actually coordinate the disclosure of those across an industry? And so there's a lot of factors that go into it. And I think that the true answer is like if either extreme is not quite right, there are tools that can be applied to a specific situation. And I think that this is not the first time we've had to think about this problem, it's not the last time we will have to think about it. But one thing to note is that we have had our model in the hands of defenders for some time, that we've been building up our trusted access program, that the model that we're releasing is actually not cyber permissive, right? That it actually has a number of safeguards built into it, and that you can then have a gap between what you're privately sharing, testing, those kinds of things. And so I think my short answer is like there's definitely these different schools of thought in terms of values of is the value that you want to get these models into people's hands and empower them or is the value that you kind of want them to be centralized and controlled and that you don't want them in people's hands? That is something that is a maybe underlying tension in some of these debates. But I think that the tactics flow from the details and that they can be informed by these values, but either extreme reflexively I don't think will yield the best outcome for the world. Okay, I want to ask you about agents back to agents, if we could. These agents work the best if you sort of let them have a high degree of autonomy. I mean, sort of makes sense. So I'm just kind of curious to hear your perspective as we get more agents that can do more things and access more files and work across programs, what is the proper amount of trust to put into agents right now? So, I think that right now, actually agents tend to be quite reliable, and even things like prompt injections, I think that there's still holes there, but that we're patching them, that the models are becoming much more resilient. But I also think that the flip side is that as these models are given increasing responsibility and access to more important context, that you need to have some answer for just like if you have employees—you know, if you have a team of five employees, they're all kind of trustworthy, fine. But if you have 500,000 of the same employees, somehow those numbers, just like the law of large numbers, mean that you start to worry about okay, how do I have good governance and oversight? And so this is something where as we're investing in these capabilities and making the super app more accessible not just to coders but to any person doing work with a computer, we're also investing in governance and oversight. And you can see this very concretely in Workspace agents, which we released recently. So that's within your enterprise you can now define agents. So you get a hosted Codex harness in the cloud, you can hook up tools, you can hook it up to your Slack and it's doing work. It's like awesome. A lot of people use it. It's been very cool to see how sort of viral it goes within an organization when you use someone else's agent you're like wait, I could build one of these too, and you can just fork it and do your own thing. And then that's an opportunity to have great governance in that you can see that's baked into the product where your IT organization can see all the agents that have been created, that for an agent you can see the conversations it's had, and that you can think about exactly what the guardrails are around it. So I think that the short answer is like you want to ramp the responsibility and trust with the agent and the diversity of things that agents are doing together with security, safety, observability, oversight. And if you're not doing those hand in hand, then I think that's a little bit out of balance and I think it's important to think about both sides. Yeah, basically go ahead but be careful. But you really lean in, right? I think it's like as you scale, like you can prototype, and that it's just the nature of scale that starts to bring in the—do you still have the ability to oversee what's going on? So you need to kind of make sure at each step do you feel like you're calibrated, do you understand what your teams are up to. Greg, let's end with this. You've called this a compute-powered economy. What does that mean? Well, I think we are heading to a world where the more compute is poured into a problem, the faster that problem will be solved. And that the ceiling of problem that can be solved depends on how much compute is available. And you think about things like drug discovery, right? Being able to solve complex diseases. Like those are—solving complex diseases like Alzheimer's is kind of outside of humanity's reach right now. We've never really done it. But imagine a world where you can take a gigawatt data center and have it just think about how to solve Alzheimer's for a month, for a year, however long it takes. And it may not be literally just cerebrally solving this problem, but it may have to consult with world experts, maybe it has to suggest experiments that get run in a wet lab. But if you can actually solve such a problem, that would be such a transformatively positive thing for humanity. And I think we are heading to a world where that is how important problems get solved and that is how tasks in your daily life can also be solved. Whether it's having an agent that knows you, that has your personal context, that is trustworthy, that you can ask for advice on health and you get back trustworthy information, and that's just a thing that's a smartphone that's in your pocket, right? You can just talk to and it'll be out there doing things. It proactively knows what are your goals, what are your interests and how it can help you. And I think that big and small, compute is going to be the resource that shows how much compute can be used to help people, to do work on behalf of people. And I think we're heading to that world and it's one that we're all building collectively. Yeah, and that I think would explain the massive investments that you've led making these big infrastructure bets. Still not enough. We're going to feel the scarcity. We're going to feel it. We're feeling it already. You can sense it right now on people who are trying to use these agents and simply cannot, you know, hitting the rate limits. So we're working on behalf of our customers, on behalf of everyone who wants to use these agents to ensure that there is enough and I don't think we're going to get there. We're going to do our best, but I think that we are heading into a world of compute scarcity and again I think this is something where we can all contribute to trying to help there just be more availability of this in the world. Greg, busy day. Always appreciate your time. Always great to speak with you. Thanks again for coming on. Likewise. Great chatting, Alex.

Mythos, Muse, and the Opportunity Cost of Compute | Stratechery by Ben Thompson

Stratechery(13.9K) ·18:01 ·1.1K views · 2026-04-23
Ben Thompson (Author, Stratechery)
The era of "zero marginal costs" that defined the 2010s is ending as AI creates massive capital intensity and compute constraints, forcing tech giants to prioritize internal workloads over external customers based on opportunity costs rather than traditional supply/demand.
TL;DR The era of "zero marginal costs" that defined the 2010s is ending as AI creates massive capital intensity and compute constraints, forcing tech giants to prioritize internal workloads over external customers based on opportunity costs rather than traditional supply/demand....

TL;DR

The era of "zero marginal costs" that defined the 2010s is ending as AI creates massive capital intensity and compute constraints, forcing tech giants to prioritize internal workloads over external customers based on opportunity costs rather than traditional supply/demand.

The Shift from Marginal Cost to Opportunity Cost

  • The End of Aggregation Theory? Analyst Doug O'Laughlin argues that reasoning models (like o1) break the fundamental internet-era assumption that marginal costs are zero.
      • Capital Intensity: Unlike the 2010s, where scaling users was nearly free, AI requires massive, ongoing investments in compute and electricity.
  • Defining the Cost Structure: While digital outputs have no "raw material" costs, the massive fixed cost of AI chips creates a new "price floor" for technology services.
  • The Opportunity Cost Dilemma: For hyperscalers, the primary constraint is no longer just money, but where to point limited GPUs.
      • Microsoft’s Strategy: CFO Amy Hood noted that Microsoft missed Azure growth expectations because it prioritized allocating GPUs to its own products (M365 Copilot, GitHub Copilot) and internal R&D over external Azure customers.
      • Internal vs. External: Companies must now choose between serving enterprise clients, supporting strategic partners (like OpenAI/Anthropic), or powering their own consumer apps.

Anthropic’s Mythos and the Distillation Threat

  • Project Glasswing: Anthropic announced "Mythos," a frontier model with advanced coding and cybersecurity capabilities, but limited its release citing safety concerns.
  • Strategic Scarcity: Beyond safety, Anthropic is facing severe compute shortages. Restricting Mythos allows them to:
      • Protect Performance: Avoid "dumbing down" models due to over-capacity.
      • Maintain Pricing Power: Ensure they can charge high premiums to cover the rising costs of leasing compute from neoclouds.
  • The Distillation Problem: Anthropic identified "industrial-scale campaigns" by Chinese labs (DeepSeek, Moonshot, MiniMax) using Claude's outputs to train their own models.
      • Protecting the Moat: By limiting access to Mythos, Anthropic aims to prevent competitors from "distilling" their most advanced capabilities into cheaper open-source models.

Meta’s Unique Consumer Positioning

  • Muse Spark: Meta’s new natively multimodal reasoning model marks a ground-up overhaul of their AI efforts under "Meta Superintelligence Labs."
  • No Cloud Conflict: Unlike Microsoft, Google, or Amazon, Meta does not have a cloud/enterprise business.
      • Pure Consumer Focus: Serving the consumer market comes with zero opportunity cost for Meta; every GPU spent on AI for Instagram or WhatsApp directly supports their existing advertising-based monetization.
  • Open Source as a Weapon: Thompson argues Meta should open-source its frontier models (like Muse) to erode the pricing power of OpenAI and Anthropic.
      • By making frontier-level intelligence a commodity, Meta forces competitors to spend more on compute to stay ahead, potentially driving them out of the consumer market.

Supply vs. Demand in the AI Era

  • The Infrastructure Bet: OpenAI is betting that owning the most compute (supply) will be the winning advantage, informing its massive infrastructure build-out to stay ahead of Anthropic.
  • The Distribution Bet: Thompson remains skeptical that supply alone is dispositive. He argues that "owning demand" (Aggregation Theory) still holds:
      • Customer Ownership: The company with the most compelling product will win the most users and the revenue necessary to buy the compute they need.
      • Enterprise Pivot: The high cost of compute is forcing OpenAI and Anthropic to pivot toward high-margin enterprise software, potentially ceding the consumer space to Meta and Google.
"The cost that Microsoft is contending with here is not marginal cost, but rather opportunity cost. Compute spent in one area cannot be used in another area." — Ben Thompson
"I believe that there is no practical limit to the improvements of models other than economics, and I think that will be the real constraint in the future." — Doug O'Laughlin
"Sometimes I think it’s probably better to think about the Azure guidance that we give as an allocated capacity guide about what we can deliver in Azure revenue." — Amy Hood
Mythos, Muse, and the Opportunity Cost of Compute was published on Monday, April 13, 2026. In January 2025, Doug O'Laughlin at Fabricated Knowledge declared that o1 and reasoning models marked the end of Aggregation Theory. "I believe that there is no practical limit to the improvements of models other than economics, and I think that will be the real constraint in the future. It is reasonable that if we spend infinite dollars on a model, it will be improved. The problem is whether infinite dollars would make sense for a business. That is going to be the key question for 2025. How do the economics of AI make this work? One of the core assumptions about the internet has just been broken. Marginal costs now exist again, meaning that most hyperscalers will become increasingly capital-intensive." The era of Aggregation Theory is behind us, and AI is again making technology expensive. This relation of increased costs from increased consumption is anti-internet era thinking, and this will be the big problem that will be reckoned with this year. Hyperscalers’ business models are mainly underpinned by the marginal cost being zero. So, as long as you set up the infrastructure and fill an internet-scale product with users, you can make money. This era will soon be over, and the future will be much weirder and more compute-intensive. Looking back on the 2010s, we will probably consider them a naive time in the long arc of technology. One of our fundamental assumptions about this period is unraveling. This will be the single most significant change in the technology landscape going forward. Aggregation Theory was, if I may say so myself, the single best way to understand the 2010s, particularly consumer tech. It explained the dynamics undergirding Google and Facebook's dominance, as well as the App Store and Amazon’s e-commerce business. It was also a useful, albeit incomplete, framework to understand an entire host of consumer services like Uber, Airbnb, and Netflix. It’s worth pointing out, however, that some of the critical insights undergirding Aggregation Theory are much older and are embedded in the fundamental nature of tech itself. They are, as O’Laughlin notes, rooted in the concept of zero marginal costs. Marginal costs are how much it costs to make one more unit of a good. Consider a widget-making factory. You need land for the factory. You need machines for the factory. You need electricity to operate the machines. You need humans to operate the machines. You need the raw material for the widgets. Land and machines are clearly fixed costs. You have to have both to get started, and you are paying for both whether or not you make one more widget. Raw material, on the other hand, is clearly a marginal cost. If you make one more widget, you need one more widget’s worth of raw material. When it comes to physical goods, electricity and humans are also marginal costs. You need more or fewer of them depending on whether you make more or fewer widgets. Where marginal costs matter is that they provide a price floor. Companies will operate unprofitably because profit and loss is an accounting concept that incorporates depreciation, i.e. your fixed costs. For example, imagine that a company spent $1,000 on a factory to make widgets that have a marginal cost of $10. As long as the price of widgets is greater than $10, the company will make them, even if they don't earn enough money to cover their depreciation costs. They operate at a loss because at least they are still making a marginal profit on each widget. What the company may not do is invest in any more fixed costs, and eventually, they will probably go bankrupt from interest on the debt they likely used to finance those fixed costs. I explain all this precisely because it’s almost completely immaterial to tech. First, there generally are no raw material costs because the outputs are digital. Second, because there are no raw material costs and because the fixed costs are so large, electricity and humans are generally treated as fixed costs, not marginal costs. Of course, you will run your servers all the time and at full capacity because every scrap of additional revenue you can generate is worth it. AI very much fits in this paradigm. The output is digital, and while AI chips use a lot of electricity, the cost is a fraction of the cost of the chips themselves. This is to say that no one with AI chips is making marginal cost calculations in terms of utilizing them. They’re going to be used. Rather, the decision that matters is what they will be used for. Consider Microsoft. Last quarter, the company missed the street’s Azure growth expectations, not because there wasn't demand, but because the company decided to use its capacity for its own products. CFO Amy Hood said on the company’s earnings call: "Sometimes I think it’s probably better to think about the Azure guidance that we give as an allocated capacity guide about what we can deliver in Azure revenue. Because as we spend the capital and put GPUs specifically, it applies to CPUs, but GPUs more specifically, we’re really making long-term decisions. And the first thing we’re doing is solving for the increased usage in sales and the accelerating pace of M365 Copilot as well as GitHub Copilot, our first-party apps." "Then we make sure we’re investing in the long-term nature of R&D and product innovation. And much of the acceleration that I think you’ve seen from us in products over the past a bit... is coming because we are allocating GPUs and capacity to many of the talented AI people we’ve been hiring over the past years. Then, when you end up, is that you end up with the remainder going towards serving the Azure capacity that continues to grow in terms of demand." "And a way to think about it, because I think I get asked this question sometimes, is, you know, if I had taken the GPUs that just came online in Q1 and Q2 in terms of GPUs and allocated them all to Azure, the KPI would have been over 40. And I think the most important thing to realize is that this is about investing in all the layers of the stack that benefit customers. And I think that’s hopefully helpful in terms of thinking about capital growth. It shows in every piece." "It shows in revenue growth across the business and shows as OpEx growth as we invest in our people." The cost that Microsoft is contending with here is not marginal cost, but rather opportunity cost. Compute spent in one area cannot be used in another area. In the case of these earnings, Microsoft was admitting that they could have made their Azure number if they wanted to. However, they chose to prioritize their own workloads because, as CEO Satya Nadella noted later in the call, those have higher gross margin profiles and higher lifetime value. It’s opportunity cost, not marginal cost, that is the challenge facing hyperscalers. How much compute should go to customers and which ones? How much should be reserved for internal workloads? Microsoft needs to balance Azure, both for its enterprise customers and OpenAI, and its software business. Amazon needs to balance its e-commerce business, AWS, and its strategic investments in both Anthropic and OpenAI. Google has to balance GCP, its own strategic investment in Anthropic, and its consumer businesses. Last week, Anthropic released, I mean, announced Mythos, its most advanced model. And, in somewhat typical Anthropic fashion, it did so by focusing on its dangers. From the introductory post for Project Glasswing, the company’s initiative for leveraging Mythos to address security: "We formed Project Glasswing because of capabilities we observed in a new frontier model trained by Anthropic that we believe could reshape cybersecurity." "Claude Mythos Preview is a general-purpose, unreleased frontier model that reveals a stark fact: AI models have reached a level of coding capability where they can surpass all but the most skilled humans at finding and exploiting software vulnerabilities. Mythos Preview has already found thousands of high-severity vulnerabilities, including some in every major operating system and web browser. Given the rate of AI progress, it will not be long before such capabilities proliferate." "The fallout, for economies, public safety, and national security could be severe. Project Glasswing is an urgent attempt to put these capabilities to work for defensive purposes." In an update last week, I analogized Anthropic’s disaster porn as marketing tool approach to the boy who cried wolf. What’s important about that analogy is not just that the boy raised false alarms, but also that, in the end, the wolf did come. To that end, I wrote two weeks ago about the myriad of security issues that underpin all software, and my optimism that AI would solve these issues in the long run, even if it made things much worse in the short run. In other words, it’s actually not important whether or not Mythos represents a major security threat. If this model doesn’t, a future model will. To that end, I do support leveraging Mythos to proactively find and fix bugs before bad actors can find and exploit them. At the same time, it’s also worth noting that there are other reasons for Anthropic to not make Mythos widely available, such as limiting access to a finite number of companies with a high capacity and willingness to pay. The first are those opportunity costs. Anthropic is already short on compute, serving its current models. X was overrun with complaints and debates this weekend about Anthropic allegedly dumbing down Claude over the last month or so. Making Mythos more widely available, particularly to subscription plans that don’t pay per usage, would make the situation much worse. In other words, Anthropic isn’t facing a marginal cost problem, but an opportunity cost problem. Where to allocate its compute? Of course, this could become a margin problem. I suspect that Anthropic is going to overcome its conservatism in terms of compute by acquiring more compute from hyperscalers and neoclouds, and paying dearly for the privilege. The key to handling those costs will be to charge more for Claude going forward. That, by extension, means maintaining pricing power, which leads to a second benefit of not releasing Mythos broadly. Anthropic certainly faces competition from OpenAI. For both frontier labs, however, the real competition in the long run are open source models. Right now, those primarily come from China, and a key ingredient in fast-following frontier models is distillation. From Anthropic’s blog: "We have identified industrial-scale campaigns by three AI laboratories—DeepSeek, Moonshot, and MiniMax to illicitly extract Claude’s capabilities to improve their own models. These labs generated over 16 million exchanges with Claude through approximately 24,000 fraudulent accounts." "These labs use a technique called distillation, which involves training a less capable model on the outputs of a stronger one." Distillation is a widely used and legitimate training method. For example, frontier AI labs routinely distill their own models to create smaller, cheaper versions for their customers. But distillation can also be used for illicit purposes. Competitors can use it to acquire powerful capabilities from other labs in a fraction of the time, and at a fraction of the cost, that it would take to develop them independently. I absolutely believe this is a real problem, and wrote as much when DeepSeek R1 was released last year. I also think it’s in the interest of everyone other than the frontier labs to pretend that it isn’t. Open source models are not subject to the frontier labs’ markup or compute constraints, which is exactly why it benefits most companies to have them available, whether or not they are distilled. Of course, that doesn’t mean they are free to run. You still need to provide the compute. Notice, however, how that makes stopping distillation even more of a priority for the frontier labs. First, they want to protect their margins. Second, however, their biggest cost is opportunity cost: the customers they can’t serve because they don’t have enough compute. To the extent they can make compute less useful for their potential customers by stopping open-source models from distilling their models, they can acquire that compute for themselves at more favorable rates. Mythos wasn’t the only new model announced last week. Meta announced the first fruit of their new frontier lab as well. From the company’s blog post: "Today, we’re excited to introduce Muse Spark, the first in the Muse family of models developed by Meta Superintelligence Labs. Muse Spark is a natively multimodal reasoning model with support for tool-use, visual chain of thought, and multi-agent orchestration." "Muse Spark is the first step on our scaling ladder and the first product of a ground-up overhaul of our AI efforts. To support further scaling, we are making strategic investments across the entire stack, from research and model training to infrastructure, including the Hyperion Data Center. Muse Spark offers competitive performance in multimodal perception, reasoning, health, and agentic tasks." "We continue to invest in areas with current performance gaps, such as long-horizon agentic systems and coding workflows." Muse Spark isn’t state-of-the-art, but it’s in the game, and overall a positive first impression from Meta Superintelligence Labs. What is most notable to me, however, is the extent to which the last nine months of AI have made clear that CEO Mark Zuckerberg made the right call. The trigger for O’Laughlin’s post was reasoning where models using more tokens led to better answers. Since then, agents have exponentially increased token demand, as they can use LLMs continuously without a human in the loop. This is a huge driver in sky-rocketing demand for Claude, as well as OpenAI’s Codex. Moreover, this use case is so potentially profitable that not only is Anthropic’s revenue sky-rocketing, but OpenAI is pivoting its focus to enterprise. Indeed, you can make the argument that one of OpenAI’s biggest challenges is the fact that it has such a popular consumer product in ChatGPT. I, with my Aggregation Theory lens, have long maintained that that userbase was a big advantage for OpenAI. That assumed that the company could effectively monetize it, which is why I’ve argued so vociferously for an advertising model. OpenAI has big projections for exactly that, but until that materializes, that big consumer base is a big opportunity cost in terms of OpenAI’s focus and compute. The company has made significant investments in more compute, but the temptation to allocate it to agentic use cases will be very large. This puts Meta in a unique position relative to everyone else in the industry. Unlike any of the hyperscalers or the frontier labs, Meta does not have an enterprise or cloud business to worry about. That means that serving the consumer market comes with no opportunity costs. Of course, those opportunity costs would be much smaller anyways, given that Meta already has an at-scale advertising business. In other words, Meta may actually face less competition in winning the consumer space than it might have seemed a few months ago, simply because that is their primary focus. And because they have their own model, they don’t need to worry about not having access to the frontier labs. Much of this analysis applies to Google, of course. This, by the same token, is why Meta should open-source Muse, just like they did Llama. The entities that will be the most hurt by widespread availability of a frontier model are other frontier labs, who will see their pricing power reduced and face increased competition for compute. This will leave the consumer market for Meta. So is "the era of Aggregation Theory...behind us"? On one hand, the insight that the way to create and maintain value will come from owning the customer is almost certainly going to continue to be the case. On the consumer side, owning customers leads to advertising, which provides the revenue to provide services to customers. On the enterprise side—which, I would note, has never been an arena where Aggregation Theory was meant to be applied—I think it’s likely that both Anthropic and OpenAI continue to move up the stack and deliver features that compete with software providers directly. This approach is also in line with not making leading-edge models publicly available. On the other hand, O’Laughlin’s observation that we are and will continue to be compute constrained is an important one. Companies will not be able to assume they can serve everyone, because serving one set of customers imposes the opportunity cost of not serving another. This won’t, at least in theory, last forever. At some point, AI will be good enough for enough use cases that there will be enough compute capacity to take advantage of the fact that there really aren’t meaningful marginal costs entailed in serving AI. That theoretical future, however, feels further away than ever. OpenAI is betting that this compute constraint will matter more than Anthropic’s current momentum with end-users. From Bloomberg: "OpenAI told investors this week that its early push to dramatically increase computing resources gives it a key advantage over Anthropic, at a moment when its longtime rival is gaining ground and mulling a potential public offering. The ChatGPT maker said it has outpaced Anthropic by rapidly and consistently adding computing capacity to support wider adoption of its software." "The ambitious infrastructure build-out, criticized by some as too costly, has enabled OpenAI to better keep pace with rising demand for AI products, the memo states." I’m less certain that this will be dispositive. When it comes to AI, distribution and transaction costs are still free, the two preconditions for aggregators, which means that the winners should be those with the most compelling products. Those products will win the most users, providing the money necessary to source the compute to serve them. Consider Anthropic’s deal to secure a meaningful portion of TPU supply, which given the capacity constraints at TSMC, is ultimately an example of taking supply from Google. I suspect that Anthropic can take more, including already built hyperscaler and neocloud capacity. Yes, that compute will be more expensive, but if demand is high enough, the necessary cash flow will be there. In other words, my bet is that owning demand will ultimately trump owning supply, suggesting that the underlying principles of Aggregation Theory live on. To put it another way, I think that OpenAI will need to win with better products, not just more compute. Then again, if more compute is the key to better products, then does supply matter most? Regardless, they’ll certainly be focused on delivering both to the enterprise customers who are driving Anthropic’s astonishing growth. The real cost may be the consumer market that they currently dominate, given that Meta has nothing to lose and everything to gain.

Fmr Goldman Sachs CEO: What Surviving The 2008 Financial Crisis Taught Me — Lloyd Blankfein

Anthony Scaramucci(252.0K) ·52:23 ·9.0K views · 2026-04-23
Lloyd Blankfein (Former CEO, Goldman Sachs) Anthony Scaramucci (Host)
Lloyd Blankfein’s career journey from Brooklyn public housing to the helm of Goldman Sachs underscores that elite leadership requires "streetwise" authenticity, the ability to pivot into "risk management mode" during uncertainty, and a commitment to ...
TL;DR Lloyd Blankfein’s career journey from Brooklyn public housing to the helm of Goldman Sachs underscores that elite leadership requires "streetwise" authenticity, the ability to pivot into "risk management mode" during uncertainty, and a commitment to radical transparency...

TL;DR

Lloyd Blankfein’s career journey from Brooklyn public housing to the helm of Goldman Sachs underscores that elite leadership requires "streetwise" authenticity, the ability to pivot into "risk management mode" during uncertainty, and a commitment to radical transparency with stakeholders during existential crises.

From the Brooklyn Projects to the Executive Floor

  • Socioeconomic Roots: Blankfein grew up in East New York public housing; his father was a night-shift mail sorter at the post office. This "outsider" upbringing instilled a permanent sense of being a non-establishmentarian.
  • The "Short" Perspective: Blankfein recounts feeling "short" for the first time at Harvard, a metaphor for the culture shock of moving from a working-class neighborhood to elite circles.
      • Competitive Edge: He argues that his background provided a level of empathy and self-criticism that protected him from the "swelling" egos common in high finance.
  • The "Back Door" Entry: Blankfein did not originally get hired by major Wall Street firms. He entered Goldman Sachs through the acquisition of J. Aron & Company, a commodity trading firm, where he rose through the trading ranks.

Management and the "Partnership" Culture

  • Ownership Mentality: Even after Goldman Sachs went public, Blankfein and his predecessors (Hank Paulson, David Solomon) fought to maintain a partnership culture.
      • Consensus Building: Unlike a standard corporate chain of command, a partnership requires "socializing" ideas and accepting pushback from co-owners to ensure organizational stability.
      • Information Flow: Employees in a partnership feel entitled to information across all "cylinders" of the firm, not just their own departments.
  • The "Alumni" Strategy: Goldman deliberately maintains close ties with former employees (like Scaramucci), viewing them as a lifelong extension of the firm’s brand and influence.
  • Growth vs. Swelling: Citing legendary leader John Weinberg, Blankfein distinguishes between people who "grow" through experience and those who merely "swell" with self-importance.

The Mechanics of Risk Management

  • Anticipation vs. Reaction: Blankfein differentiates between two distinct modes of trading:

1. Predictive Mode: Trying to guess what the market will do.

2. Risk Management Mode: Disregarding opinions on what *will* happen to focus exclusively on what *could* happen and how to mitigate those contingencies.

  • The 1994 Inflection Point: After a bond market crisis in 1994, Goldman transitioned from a "non-bureaucratic" firm to one with rigorous risk committees and surveillance to prevent "loopy" risk-taking.
  • Conservation of Risk: He views Wall Street as a necessary "responsible party" that manages the world's inherent risks (e.g., currency fluctuations for manufacturers like Ford) so other industries can focus on innovation.

Surviving the 2008 Financial Crisis

  • The Target of Vengeance: Because Bear Stearns, Merrill Lynch, and Lehman Brothers collapsed or were sold, Goldman Sachs became the primary target for public and political anger.
  • Legal and Personal Pressure: Blankfein describes the "torture" of a Justice Department referral and FBI investigation following a marathon Senate hearing.
      • Investigation: He was interviewed multiple times by the FBI and the Southern District office, facing the reality that a "34-year-old assistant attorney general" could potentially end his career or freedom.
  • Crisis Communication: During the height of the crisis, Blankfein used daily voicemails (deliberately choosing voice over email) to maintain transparency with his staff.
      • The Message: He urged employees to focus on their jobs to enhance the firm's reputation while promising to keep them fully informed of the firm's status.

Leadership and Resilience

  • Authenticity: Blankfein attributes his survival to being a "good communicator" and remaining authentic to his roots. He argues that being "streetwise" is a form of wisdom tempered by experience and time.
  • The Value of Adversity: Facing non-Hodgkin's lymphoma while CEO reinforced a fatalistic but focused mindset. He views his best professional year as 2008—not because the firm made money (it was flat), but because they survived an existential threat through superior hedging.
  • Historical Perspective: He advocates for reading history to build resilience, noting that "crises of the century" seem to occur every five to ten years.
"Everyone in poker over a lifetime of playing gets the same hands statistically, it's just who plays them better."

— Lloyd Blankfein

"I don't care what you think. I just want to know what's in the realm of the possible and what we're going to do if that happens."

— Lloyd Blankfein

"We are not going in through the garage. Man up."

— Laura Blankfein (to Lloyd during a protest at their building)

"In a public company, it's more of a chain of command... but at Goldman, everyone felt they were tied... the people own it."

— Lloyd Blankfein

The world was so vengeful at the financial crisis. They were looking for someone to set an example, and it wasn't going to be Bear Stearns and Lehman and Merrill; they didn't exist anymore. And it wasn't going to be the big banks; it was going to be Goldman. People, you know, went after us, which meant going after me. When I went through a long Senate hearing that lasted hours into midnight and I was giving interviews after that till in the morning after that and taking a car back to New York, that certainly wasn't fun. But again, I had the perspective; I knew at the end of the day we did stuff right. People were clamoring for something. I didn't feel super exposed in any fairness sense, but I was tortured. The Senate after that hearing, because they didn't get the satisfaction of showing that we were purposely trying to kill the market to make money—which of course we weren't—the committee chairman put out a referral to the Justice Department to investigate me for criminality. I didn't even know what the crime was. But the FBI, compliant as it is, did an investigation. I would get picked up and brought to the Southern District office and interviewed with a court stenographer; that happened like three or four times. And you know, your mind kind of says, "Whoa, some 34-year-old assistant attorney general could ask for a grand jury and do something." Well, welcome to Open Book. I am your host, Anthony Scaramucci. Joining me today is Lloyd Blankfein. He is the former chairman and CEO of Goldman Sachs from 2006 to 2018. And he's the author of a book that I think should be the business book of the year. I don't know if the FT would qualify for that because it is a memoir as well, but to me, it is a strategy manual for dealing with life. It's a rite of passage story, but also in here is how to manage a company going through a crisis. And, Lloyd, by the way, it took me back down memory lane. I left Goldman Sachs 30 years ago. Hard to believe, but I was there for the first seven years of my career. And you were very nice to me then, and you've been very nice to me now. So it's great to have you on. But again, I love this book. And it starts in East New York, Brooklyn. I want you to take us back there if you can, talk a little bit about the precocious nature of your childhood, but also you were growing up in public housing. So take us there because it's just an unbelievable, wonderful American story arc, Lloyd, of your career. Well, you know, I did grow up in public housing. My dad worked in the post office as a mail sorter. Actually, he had been—I remember earliest memories—bouts of unemployment with my dad and unhappiness around the table. He got a job at the post office, civil service. He took the night shift because it paid an extra 10% night differential. And so growing up, unfortunately, my dad worked nights. I think he had also, for part of the time, a part-time job during the day. So I really wish I had remembered more conversations with him than I do. But we grew up in that circumstance. But by the way, so did everybody around us. And so part of the genius of America is that probably 85% of the country thinks they're in the middle class, believes they're in the middle class for all practical purposes, given the minimum standard of living that we have. So I didn't know that we were that—I didn't know what there was necessarily to strive for, but I did know that I didn't like growing up in the projects, going to the high school that I went to in the neighborhood I lived in, and the neighborhood I traveled to. So I was very, very anxious to, from a very early age, leave and go to an out-of-town school. That was my big objective as a kid growing up. I have so many things that I've underlined in this book, and there are personalities in this book that I remember from the firm that stayed at the firm with you and helped you manage the firm. But the best line—I told my producers this—the best line in this book is on page 21. Lloyd Blankfein, it's the top of the page. And you write, and it's a very brief line, you write: "At Harvard, I was short." And I'm thinking you're writing about me, Lloyd Blankfein, but no. What did you mean by that sentence, "At Harvard, I was short"? You know, I hadn't gotten around much. Again, I went to school given my—you know, I didn't travel much, I didn't get out much. I was, you know, basically living in the projects with a lot of kids that were like me. And guess what? I think—I've never seen the sociological study, and I don't know whether it's the effects of malnutrition or just eating a lot of fatty foods or whatever—but I wasn't short where I was growing up. And then when I got to college, like the first day, I'm walking with some of the people from my floor. We're going to walk into the dining hall, and I looked to the left and I looked to the right and I felt like a valley between two mountains. And I said, "Boy, you guys are tall. You guys are tall." And they looked at me and they said, "No, we're not tall. You're short." And I looked around and I said, "You know, gosh, you're right." And it was the first time I thought of it. So I guess I didn't suffer from a bad ego at that point because I thought of myself bigger than I was. Well, see, I identify with you in so many ways in the book. I thought that we were well-to-do in a weird way, and then when I got to Tufts and I saw these kids riding around in BMWs, I'm like, "Okay, we—I'm short, Lloyd, but I'm also not rich." It was a combination of those two things. But we didn't have—we had, you know, I read your story too. You know, we didn't have, again, it's not the same, but it rhymes. You know, we're both—Italian household, Jewish household, striving, very working-class parents and, you know, somehow we made it, punched above our weight and made it to fancy schools. You and I both went to Harvard Law School. Who would believe that for either one of us at this point? No way. No, no one would have believed it, especially from my neighborhood or your neighborhood. And you know, we ended up in Goldman. You went the entrepreneur route, and I stuck it out in the climbing the greasy pole at Goldman Sachs. But you also write that Goldman was the right place for you. I appreciate that because one of my law school classmates—I don't know if you would remember Jeff Moslow—he was on the banking side. He was well-suited to be a Goldman Sachs partner and he took your career track. It was just for me, I always wanted to have my own business. That was one of my career dreams. And so we pursued the paths that we pursue and life takes us where it takes us. But there's something in the book I was dying to ask you about because threaded throughout this book, Lloyd, is you're coming into every room and the question is whether you are an establishment guy, Lloyd Blankfein, or are you a kid from East New York? And the question I was dying to ask you was how much of your career was actually using that tension as a competitive edge versus just surviving it and it being a rite of passage for you? Well, I gotta tell you, it's not optional. It's not as if it's a calculation. So it's all of my career. You are who you are. You get stamped. When I first started writing the book—and I started it and then I stopped for a while—I was 64 when I started it. And I realized I left home, I left East New York when I was 16 and I went to the hallowed halls of Harvard, which of course was culture shock. But the point was I did get away. But that 16 was exactly a quarter of my life and I was sort of ruminating on how you get stamped in your early life and you never shake it. You can lean towards it, you can lean against it, but you're never going to shake it. You are, you know, it's Popeye. That's a quote, that great philosopher Popeye: "I am what I am what I am, boop-boop." And so I'm always going to hear myself speak, I'm going to hear my Brooklyn accent, I'm going to try hard to say "rather" as opposed to "rather" or "sister" instead of "sister." And so I'm always conscious. So you're always both. At some point you just go with it and decide that all those—you're lucky for it because there are burdens to every position but there are advantages to every position. I think that that's what I always try to impress upon people as people grew through Goldman Sachs: think about who was in your position when you were striving and going up, and you become that person. Do you realize that you could put a name on it? Like the first time I realized I had a job that Bob Rubin had when I first got to the firm. Bob Rubin, who was then Treasury Secretary, was co-head of the firm for a while. And I said, "My god, when Bob Rubin walked into a room I used to shake." And now I'm that person. You have to realize how people look at you. And when you grow up like us, stepping out of yourself and seeing yourself in the room and being self-critical, by the way, is unavoidable. Very often it's really good, but sometimes it gets in your way too because you end up second-guessing yourself. You end up not living in the moment sometimes. You walk out of a room magnifying the things you would have, could have, should have done differently because you always grow up with that perspective on yourself. But in other ways, it's hugely helpful to be able to put yourself in other people's shoes. But sometimes you wish you could turn that on and off. And so the answer is I don't know whether I'm the kid from—I am the kid from Brooklyn and I was the CEO of a very establishment, very influential firm. And they got me for that job. I'm both. Thank you for tuning in to Open Book. And if you haven't already, please hit the subscribe button below so that you're the first to know when our new episodes drop each week. We got a lot more coming. And now back to the show. Yeah, but I mean, listen, I just let you know, I love the book so much that I bought 100 copies. Every one of my summer interns—we hire 20 people for the summer—will get a copy of the book. They'll have to discuss it with me. I gave it to every person at my firm Skybridge—almost said Goldman because thinking about Goldman—and I've given it to a lot of my friends to read. There's just so many touching stories in the book. I want to go to one that really moved me as well. Your mom made you rotate the rug so it wouldn't wear unevenly, right? So you know I identify with that because as an Italian in that community, Lloyd, we had rubber runners. You may have remembered those if you walked into an Italian apartment. My mother and my nana, they had plastic over the upholstery. Of course, we did too. And they had rubber runners going down the thing. How was it sitting on a summer's day? How was it coming in from outside when your house isn't air-conditioned and it's 97 degrees and you sit down on plastic? By the way, the sad thing is of course none of it even made any sense. When my parents finally moved away down to West Palm Beach, the furniture was like pristine. It was like still in the wrapper. But, you know, again we're talking about these things and it's not really the whole point of things, but I'm still that way. If somebody gives me a new sweater, I don't want to wear it until it's sat in my drawer for about eight years and is out of style because I just didn't want to get anything new dirty. So—but I not only identify with that, I think it also affected me in terms of my risk-taking on Wall Street. You know, I've done well, but I've been a pretty good risk avoider on certain things. I think it has to do with my upbringing. I guess my question to you about this is: what did that teach you about risk growing up like that, rotating the rug? You know, I always had—again, I don't know what it stems from—but I definitely saw the darker side of things, looking around corners. It's something that I wish I could turn on and off because sometimes it clouds what otherwise should be an unabashedly, unambivalently happy moment. I'll start to think what could go wrong, what could take it away. It helped you in 2008. We're going to get to 2008. Yeah, I mean, look, like anything else, things are positive and things have a dark side, taken too far. But I always, again, had a bit of a "what could go wrong with this?" And actually I found, look, in going to Goldman and coming up the trading side, I found something that really suited my ADD. I was very, very good at intense focus, but if you ask me to work on a project straight through for three days, I wasn't as good. For me, getting five hours worth of work done was usually working for two hours and procrastinating for three. I'd get five hours worth of work done because I was very intense in the two hours and very procrastinating and fidgety during the other three hours in order to make the other two hours work. But I did come from a place where I got used to looking around corners and seeing, and always thinking that low-probability risks are much higher probability than you think. And starting to think that, even as a trader, I always divided the trading job into two separate distinct parts. One is what do you think is going to happen? Trying to guess the future. And the other part of it was risk management, where we go at different times and say, "Okay, now we're in risk management mode. I don't care what you think is going to happen. I don't care that you're aligned up consistent with those views. I want to know anything you can think might happen and what you're doing to contingency plan for it." What are you doing to mitigate the consequence of it or try to avoid the bad consequence? What are you doing about something, no matter how if you could think and conceive that it could happen? Tell me now what you're going to do about it or what you're doing already to mitigate the consequences. And that's risk management. I remember going around a room at various financial crises that we've had. I said, "Okay, we're in risk management mode." And somebody will say, "Well, I think..." and I go, "I don't care what you think." And then that person would say, "Oh, if you don't care what I think, then tell me what you think." And I said, "I don't care what I think. I just want to know what's in the realm of the possible and what we're going to do if that happens." And if you do a good job at that, yeah, when things happen—as they did in the global financial crisis and in the dotcom bubble and in Long-Term Capital—when you do good contingency planning, you get off the mark so quickly when something adverse happens that people think you anticipated that. And no, you were just a very, very, very good reactor. So, again, two different modes. We were big risk-takers, we tried to get the market right, but sometimes when we didn't understand what was going on, we'd go into risk management mode and try to get closer to home and try to think of all the things that could affect our balance sheet. Our balance sheet was huge, so nothing could happen anywhere in the world that didn't affect us. And by the way, about 90% of the time they accused us of causing it anyway. Well, yeah, that's another part of the book. But I want to go to something that—and this could be hagiographic on my part—because I left Goldman with very good feelings about the firm. Some of the partners actually helped me, like Mark Winklemann and David George and others, get my firm started. When I launched it, I was actually a private banker for John Weinberg and I celebrated my birthday with him. I was born on January 6th. I used to go with Jack Shepard to the Four Seasons Grill Room. John had an office on the 10th floor. I used to, when he retired, go out to lunch with him like once a quarter. I just did it in appreciation after he long retired. Yeah, well—so I was his banker, so I went to see him regularly, specifically for his birthday. Me and Jack Shepard, his old roommate from Princeton who fought in the Second World War with him, we would sit in the Grill Room together. And he was the culture carrier extraordinaire, the legendary John Weinberg. And one of the things he said to me, Lloyd, which is so evident in this book, is that some people grow, others swell. Remember him saying that, Lloyd? He said it all the time. Lloyd, I'm going to say this sentence and you finish the sentence: "Trees don't..." Obviously grow—they don't grow to the sky. They don't grow to the sky. See? This was the stuff that you and I grew up with at the firm and it's imbued in your book. And I want to talk about the culture of the firm. You're embracing it from East New York. You're a non-establishmentarian, but there's something warm and embracing about this culture that you ride to the top job. So talk about that if you don't mind. Yeah, it was, in a way, because I didn't grow up in it. I kind of came in through the back door. I practiced law for a while and applied to Wall Street like everyone does after four or five years. You go get a different job when you're in the law firm because it's such a miserable life being a junior lawyer. I didn't get a job at any of the big firms, including Goldman. I got a job at J. Aron and Company, which was acquired by Goldman, which is how I got in through the back. That was a small commodity trading firm and I grew up through the then commodity division and started accruing more responsibilities in trading and sales as it went on. But because I was an outsider and because I always felt a little bit out of things—when I got to college other kids had gone to prep school, I went to a failed high school in East New York—I was always a little bit remote. I didn't dress the same or have the same life experience that other people had at that point. I didn't travel. I was always a little bit out of it. And when I got to a great institution that I could do well in, I just hugged it because for the first time I felt really grounded and had a great platform and was part of a great team. Now I can tell you, the culture of Goldman Sachs is very strong. One of the great achievements has been maintaining that culture that I started with when the firm was a partnership. We kept that partnership ownership culture more than a quarter of a century after the firm has gone public. That is a great achievement and it has consequence. Now what's the culture? It really is a partnership, an ownership culture. It's like the people you work with are your co—and technically they were, that's what a partnership is. You're not just looking at subordinates; you're looking at the ownership of the company. And as owners they feel entitled to information, to know what's going on in the whole firm, not just their cylinder. If you work at Amazon in the retail part of the firm, you're not really asking a lot of questions or getting a lot of information about the AWS part of the firm. But at Goldman, everyone felt they were tied; the compensation was tied to how the firm as a whole functioned. You got more information and had expectations that you'd be consulted on things. If you're running the firm, you have to socialize the things you want to do and you get pushback. You sometimes go five rounds with people and you may change your mind or you may slow it because you think the organization can't take it. In a public company, it's more of a chain of command, response, obedience—and the people don't own it. And so there's real consequences in terms of your feeling. If you're a junior person in the firm, you come away with a sense of ownership. And if you're running the firm, you have to respond to people as if you're there by the consent of the other owners. The firm kept that philosophy and had the discipline at the senior-most level—Hank, me, my successor David—to run the firm that way. It's a lot easier to say "this is the way we're going to do it." It's a lot harder when you feel the need to build consensus with people who are entitled to question you and second-guess you. But at the end of the day, you get a more stable firm. I think it's a difference like between the American military command structure and the Soviet. You don't just push a button; people in the field are expected to think, to have ownership, and to have rules of engagement that transcend other things that you may be told from time to time. I think that made for a stronger firm and it was certainly something that I appreciated. One of the cultural things of Goldman, which you're an example of, is the firm always stayed close to its alumni. You're out 30 years and if you're describing yourself to someone, at or near the top of your list is you're ex-Goldman. You're at the firm seven years, gone 30, and you still partially identify as Goldman. And the firm works at that. There'd be times when great people leave and you go, "Oh my god, my heart's breaking, this guy is leaving, I'm mad." You go into a room, count to a hundred, come back out and wish the person luck and say, "What can we do to help you?" Well, listen, I got a tremendous amount of help from my colleagues at Goldman, some former partners, current partners, etc. I always have a warm spot for Goldman. I would say this to you: when I left, my father who was a crane operator was very nervous for me. He was like, "You're leaving this big company and you're going to start a company based on this PowerPoint presentation?" I said, "Well, no, Dad, I learned a lot from that firm. I'm going to use a lot of their practices, which frankly we still use today." But I was going with the culture for another reason, Lloyd, because we're getting up there, you and me. Process matters, doesn't it? Because I do find that the people that stick to process do better than the people that are lone wolf operators. Am I wrong in saying that? Well, it's like anything else. You could find examples on both sides of the ledger. If you live in Silicon Valley, you'd rather apologize than get approval. You can see people have evolved a whole ethic and a management style by eschewing process. Now, that may work, and by the way, you hear about the success stories; you don't hear about the other 99 that crash and burn. But in a place like Goldman, which is very big and has a big balance sheet where if you make a mistake... This has come up a number of times where your big balance sheet is leveraged and you make wrong judgments, and you could do damage to the economic system and to the markets as a whole. And so we found out—and I found out in 1994 when the firm had very little process—that we were so proud of our non-bureaucracy, but I think we'd taken that pride to an excess. Let's get a little bit more regular surveillance on what we're doing. Let's have a different set of people look at what risk-takers are doing because you can get loopy and go off in the wrong direction and not get reeled back in. Let's have some more process. After the crisis of 1994—there's a crisis all the time—if you recall, you guys sold a piece of the business to the Kamei Trust. I was there when we went through that crisis and I was told—you know, I was in PCS at that time, so I was on a salary. Well, just to say what happened, that's when the Europeans raised interest rates by 6%—not six basis points—600 basis points in a few months. Everyone thought they weren't. When you lower interest rates, asset prices swell, and when you raise interest rates, they contract like physics, like relativity. Like Warren Buffett said, interest rates are the physical gravity of assets, right? That's what they are. And so it was a shock and we were not in great shape. You discovered the riskiness of what we had, which didn't feel that risky when things were going well, but when they were going poorly they suddenly felt a lot riskier. That was when we put in a regular risk committee that met and went over stuff, trying to walk the line between being an entrepreneurial risk-taking enterprise but having some surveillance that we could rely on to let us avoid that which an extra set of eyes could prevent. Well, listen, there's so much to talk about. Unfortunately, I have a 30-minute podcast. But I want to just say a few things about the book because I read it and then I went back and I've underlined a lot of it. I'd love to probably have lunch with you at some point and really talk about it. But the thing that I really got from the book was your self-awareness. You wrote about yourself in a very vulnerable way. Not just your upbringing, sir, but your time as the manager, the CEO going through a financial crisis at a time where you were the poster boy for some negativity in the press. And by the way, I have been there, Lloyd. I've had my face Xed out on the front of the New York Post as a member of the Trump survivors—you don't even want my getting my ass fired. Oh my god, I have a whole series—somebody made a whole billboard for me of my caricatures in the New York Post and the label of it was the Daily Tabloid. Right. Exactly. And I've had myself in a Bitcoin boat sinking from the New York Post; my head is seven times the size of my body. Well, think of all the jealous people who don't get as much attention as we did. Well, okay, yeah, you could say that. But there's something revealing about what you write at the time that it's happening. There's some pain in it though, isn't there, and there's some self-reflection and anxiety? Again this is a short talk, but the world was so vengeful at the financial crisis. They were looking for someone to set an example. It wasn't going to be Bear Stearns and Lehman and Merrill; they didn't exist anymore. And it wasn't going to be the big banks that lost $60 billion dollars; it was going to be Goldman that did relatively well or didn't lose money. Not because we knew what was going on but because we ran a much more hedged, sensible book. People went after us, which meant going after me and I knew what my role was. When I went through a long Senate hearing that lasted hours into midnight and I was giving interviews after that till in the morning after that and taking a car back to New York—that certainly wasn't fun. But again I had the perspective, I knew at the end of the day we did stuff right. People were clamoring for something. I didn't feel super exposed in any fairness sense, but I was tortured. The Senate after that hearing, because they didn't get the satisfaction of showing that we were purposely trying to kill the market to make money, the committee chairman put out a referral to the Justice Department to investigate me for criminality. I didn't even know what the crime was; potentially was it giving bad testimony? But the FBI did an investigation. I would get picked up and brought to the Southern District office and interviewed with a court stenographer; that happened like three or four times. Your mind kind of says, "Whoa, I know I'm fine," but some 34-year-old assistant attorney general could ask for a grand jury and do something. It didn't happen that way, but it certainly gets you to focus. Well, listen, I have a lot of empathy for it because I've been in those situations as a public figure. People probably wouldn't realize this, but in some ways I'm your doppelgänger. I took a different career path and did an entrepreneur's route from Harvard Law School and you did the corporate route. Yeah, but we're all in the financial markets. We knew what each other was doing. My point is that there's some vicariousness to your career that I really do enjoy and I think it came out in the book. I admire what you've done with your career but I also admire your thought process because it comes from your life experience. I want to go to that because in the middle of all this you've got to beat lymphoma. Some of my friends have had lymphoma; it's a tough disease. I don't know if you remember Mort Lacker, he was actually a very close friend. Yeah, there are different kinds. Like I had non-Hodgkin's. It was so funny because this goes back to my statement that I always had a fatalist streak. In this particular physical it was out of cycle because I was feeling off. For me to go to a doctor other than for my regularly scheduled was a big deal. I went back in and I got a chest X-ray which showed nothing. And I'm walking with friends and I said, "You guys are walking fast," and they said, "No, you're walking slow." At home I lived in a split-level house and when I walked up the stairs I was falling because I wasn't clearing the riser. Eventually I went back in, got a CAT scan, and that lit up like a Christmas tree. I had blood cancer—a million tumors in different places in my body. And I had to deal with that. When I started, that was kind of a 50/50 outcome. So, talk about risk management. You're lying in bed—I had my treatment every three weeks where I had 96 hours of chemo flowing in. And then the balance of the three weeks, the other 17 days, I was kind of recovering from the treatment. And you know, it worked out. But it does make you thoughtful. I didn't quit my job right after that. I stayed another three or more years after that, but it does—that's another thing in life which has benefits. Obviously you didn't want to volunteer for that, but you turn out to be better for the experience. Listen, the book reads like that. This book reads like a psychologically minded empathetic person that's been through a lot. One of the things I wrote in the back of your book that I wanted to ask you is: what's the actual skill of surviving at the top that people underestimate? I think communication. Showing who you are. Wrapped up in communication is authenticity—who you are, not pretending. I can be humble because I feel humble. I said, maybe if I were—if I had your head of hair and I was six foot four, it would be harder to be humble. But it got—you know, being short. I'm six foot four, but that's my width, Lloyd. That's the problem. It's not my height. You know, being a good communicator and certainly during the financial crisis and all the other crises I got on a voicemail every day. By the way, voicemail, not email, because I wanted people to hear my voice and know what was behind it. And I said, "People, guys, in these difficult times, we need you to do your jobs better than you've ever done before." "We don't need you wringing your hands staring at the TV screen worried about your share price. We need you doing your job so our reputation is enhanced during this difficult period. And in return, I'll keep you up to speed with what's going on in the firm." And every day I sent a voice. They aged well; I have the voicemails. I kept going on with my normal job. I traveled. The problems of the firm traveled with me if I was in France or in China. I kept communicating with people constantly. It turns out the quieter you are and when people don't know what's going on, that's when fear takes over. If you communicate and people are on board with you, it just made for a more resilient firm. I always liked resilience as an important character, because over your life cycle things are going to go wrong periodically. Everyone in poker over a lifetime of playing gets the same hands statistically; it's just who plays them better. I'd say that that's a very important thing. I'd say reading history teaches you resilience because you know we go through cycles. At the highs you have to bring people down to reality, but at the lows you also have upside-down bubbles where people get too negative and you know it's going to sort out. And then trying to convince people that their reputations are going to be made in these difficult times so they should act well because their colleagues are going to be very important 20 years from now. Even when you're a junior person, you're the cohort that's going to run the world and your reputation with these people is going to be formed and impossible to change because of what you do today. So, I'd say authenticity, openness, and a real conviction that you're not the smartest guy in the room. Especially at Goldman Sachs. To run Goldman Sachs, you have no prayer of being the best at anything. You're just a guy who's good enough to corral the people who are good to work for the platform and not kill each other. It's so well said. See, I would have said authenticity. You've fleshed it out more fully than me, but I really feel that I have survived most of my pitfalls and errors by just being authentic and getting up in the morning and keep going. That's something you had to do after the crisis. So, I have a part of the podcast where we take five words from the book and then we have a Rorschach test with the author. I picked out five words with my producer. But before I get there, someone told me over the weekend that one of your kids is getting married. I think you've got a kid that's just got engaged, so Mazel Tov. Yeah, my second son just got engaged happily. Okay, good. So Mazel Tov on that. But if you had a grandkid—and I'm talking 50 years from now—I already have four from my older kid. Okay, so there you go. I only have one producing well so far, but it's a gusher. The goal is always to get these kids off our balance sheets, although somehow I feel like my... No, no, no, no. I like them on my balance sheet where I can control—where I have some influence. Well, there you go. See, now you've spoken like an Italian grandmother, Lloyd Blankfein. We're all Mediterranean people. I don't think we're that different. Well, you got that right. But the grandkid—and I'm talking 50 years from now—if you said they had to read one chapter of this book, which one would you want it to be? Well, I'd want them to read the early chapter because I'd want them to know where I came from at some point when they're interested. My parents died something like 35 years ago and I was not deep into adulthood when they passed away. At the time of my young adulthood I was interested in myself. Now I wish they were around because I'd like to talk to them now more than I wanted to talk to them then. Of course, because that's what happens to us as we age. And that'll happen to my kids and grandkids too. And that was what I really thought of when I wrote the book. I incremented my way to a book by first saying, "You know, I'm going to write the story that I'd like my kids to read when I'm not in a position to tell it to them." Totally. I mean, that's why it reads so well and that's why the book is so compelling. We both know who Sidney Weinberg was, the father of John Weinberg. He was shining shoes for people in the 1920s and goes on to become the chairman of Goldman. So you have the quintessential American dream story, but you also have the pathos story in there where it's a very human story and those are the chapters that I would have picked. All right, so we're down to the five words, Lloyd. I'm going to say the word and you're going to give me like a two-sentence reaction. We talked a little bit about this already, but let's go to the first word: Wall Street. Well, I don't have a great connotation about it—when I think about finance, I think about being the invisible hand that matches people who have capital with the people who need capital. And that—but I've absorbed the pejorative of that. "Oh gosh, Wall Street's kind of menacing." On the other hand, when I think of what Wall Street does—I really do think it's hugely valuable because Wall Street creates the efficient markets that allow capital to find a good home, which is why the US leads in innovation and it's also ruthless in the way that mistakes get corrected quicker here. In China, you build a building or an airport in the wrong place and it'll stay there forever. Who the heck is going to say you made a mistake? But here, you build an airport in the wrong place, the bank repossesses it, you don't get paid, they pave it over and they build a Walmart. Next! And so that's why our economy does well. I think the crux of it, the fulcrum of the lever, is what Wall Street does. That's interesting. You know, I took it more personal. When I hear the word Wall Street, I think it was my yellow brick road, you know, it was my real aspirational opportunity. But you're right, it is ultimately the arterial flow of the capital and it can be ruthless in terms of the way it... It's the invisible hand that people talk about, the capture of the animal spirits. Frankly, I think it plays a big role in America being able to afford its greatness. I'm not going to say it's the source of greatness, but without the wealth creation, without the innovation, the jobs, the growth, really how great would we be? We agree, and it's very well connected in the mainstream more so than the average American thinks. Okay, the second word, Lloyd, I say the word risk, you say what? Well, you know, in a word association I should say "anxiety," but I think for me risk immediately follows "what do you want to do?" So in other words to me, risk in and of itself is not something to eschew. It's just: are you coping with it better than others? Are you getting compensated well for taking it? Are you helping people by allowing them to shed their risk? Ford wants to make cars; it doesn't want to speculate in aluminum or currency, but it has to get rid of those risks if it wants to really focus on making cars. We'll take on that risk and manage that risk. So to me, risk, there's almost like a law of conservation of risk. The world is a risky place. It's just a question of who is taking it on. Would we be able to ship things if there wasn't marine insurance? Would people be able to—how much extra savings would people have to have if they couldn't buy life insurance? In a way, we are in the insurance business because we take on other people's risk. And that I think is also a very important purpose aside from capital formation. We are the responsible party that can manage and take on other people's risk. Okay, I love it. The title of the book, which I love the title of this book, by the way, is "Streetwise." So I guess the question: how'd you come up with that title? Well, it was wisdom and street-y and kind of merging it. So it's a little bit of a play on Wall Street. I think it's smarter than street smart. If you're streetwise, you're smarter than just street smart. I love that. Well, wise has a kind of a knowing connotation that it's more of an embedded thing. I give you kudos for the title. I love the title. Maybe wisdom is smarts tempered by time and experience. All right, so I love the title. Okay, I'm going to say 2008 and you're going to say what? 2008, huge crisis, the best part of my career in a lot of ways because it was the time when you could rise to the occasion or you could have not, and I feel like I left it on the field. I did the best I could. If it had worked out badly, I wouldn't feel as sunny about it even if I had tried my best, but we got through it. That was the existential part of the crisis that we did better than anybody else and in fact we did well. And it was an inspiration to a lot of people in the firm for a long time that we managed that because we went into 2008 as the big risk-takers that we are. Yet we managed to get ourselves—we didn't recognize what would happen but we recognized that stuff was happening. And so we did what a good risk-taker will do when you don't really understand what's happening and you can't relate to it: we got closer to home and brought our risk down very aggressively. It was almost like a plane that's flying at 30,000 feet develops a pressure issue and does a crash dive down to 10,000 feet. That's kind of what we did. And so, looking back at it, I felt that that's the time where I made the biggest difference. The firm made no money—we were flat in that year—and it was the best year I had. You know, we made lots of record revenue years but I felt the best about a year that we were flat. I have to insert some of my editorial commentary here if you don't mind. When I wrote to you halfway through the book, I sent you an email and said that I'm loving your book. I said to myself, the best part of this book is over because to me it was your rite of passage story, your getting to Harvard and doing all the different things you did to get to Donovan Leisure, getting to Goldman Sachs and trying to find your way through J. Aron, your work ethic, the relationship you have with your wife, which was well rendered in the first couple of chapters. Yeah, she was tough. Tougher than me. I've only met your wife in the lobby once at like the Peninsula Hotel. No, once I was going into the building, there was a crowd protesting from the building. The driver said, "Should we go in through the garage and skip the front?" and I said, "Yes, let's do that." And she turned to me—we sit in the back of the car—and she said, "We are not going in through the garage. Man up." All right, all right, well there you go.
