I mentioned earlier in my opening remarks that I think hardworking families are not really feeling this reality. The President's tariffs have exacerbated the cost-of-living crisis that people are experiencing today. I know the President says it's not an affordability crisis, that it's a hoax, but talk to working Americans every day. They are living paycheck to paycheck; it's tough. American families now have paid more than $1,700 each in tariff costs last year. Things are now worse because of the President's war of choice with Iran, and we see the price of gasoline going up. The President made promises also about manufacturing, and the U.S. trade deficit in manufactured goods was $62 billion higher in 2025 compared to 2024. Not exactly the manufacturing renaissance that was promised. So let me ask you—and I asked Ambassador Greer the same question last week—do you know how many manufacturing jobs the United States lost per day last year, the first year of President Trump's second term? I don't know that statistic. Okay, well, let me just say this is according to the Bureau of Labor Statistics data. U.S. manufacturing lost more than 100 jobs per day from February 2025 to February 2026. That's more than 38,000 jobs in total. It's not a trade policy that delivers for working people. Do we not calculate the number of jobs lost, and what do we think about that? You know, this is really pretty extraordinary when we were supposed to be producing all of these manufacturing jobs. I will just say that, according to the Bureau of Labor Statistics, on net there was an increase of over 500,000 manufacturing jobs under the Biden administration. I don't really want to have to talk about the Biden administration, but you all have been here for well over a year and we now need to measure your performance against standards that you set. There is a decline in manufacturing jobs that we've seen in the first year. So, let me ask you: what is your response to that decline of manufacturing jobs? The tariff and trade policy of President Trump and this administration is to bring manufacturing back to America. But as Representative Meng points out, when we were up in Micron, we broke ground on a $100 billion plant that's going to have 40,000 construction jobs. TSMC in Arizona—again, 40,000 construction jobs. I broke ground in Ohio for 25,000 construction jobs. So, the manufacturing is going to start in two or three years when these factories come online, but construction is bringing this back. $400 billion in pharmaceuticals, $200 billion in autos—these are huge historic numbers. I don't mean to reclaim my time for a moment, Mr. Secretary, but you can't even tell us how many jobs we've lost. So, I don't know about what we're seeing in the future. But this is a good segue for me. Your budget suggests that you are abandoning the programs that do the work of trying to promote American manufacturing. You zero out the Manufacturing Extension Partnership, which provides small businesses with tools, training, and resources to grow, innovate, and remain competitive. You also eliminate the Economic Development Administration grants supporting workforce development, innovation, and entrepreneurship. It's worth noting that in your confirmation hearing you opposed cuts to EDA, and now it's going to be eliminated. You cut the International Trade Administration's global markets program. How do you propose that small businesses survive and thrive if you're not going to give them the tools that they need to be able to perform, to support development, and to support workforce development? It's just incomprehensible. You mentioned these very large projects that you're doing, but you're cutting all of these efforts off to deal with small businesses and their ability to be able to succeed and to grow manufacturing. Comment? What's the matter? The time of the gentlelady has expired. Mr. Clyde. No answers to the questions. Thank you very much, Mr. Chairman. Secretary Lutnick, thank you for appearing before our committee today. I want to thank you for the Bureau of Industry and Security's decision to rescind the Biden-era interim final rule on firearm export controls. That rule placed an undue burden on American firearm manufacturers and shut them out of overseas markets. Rescinding it was the right call. I appreciate the administration's action on this and want to say thank you. Also, regarding the example that you gave of the BEAD—the Broadband Equity Access and Deployment program—savings of $21 billion. Did I get that right? $21 billion? Okay, $21 billion. That is incredible and that is a great testament to someone who comes in and says, "I think we need some competition in this and we need to be able to look at it and say one size doesn't fit all." I think this highlights the President's constitutional authority of impoundment. Here you have done something with 50% of the money and you've got 50% of the money saved. Therefore, I think that should be able to either go back to the Treasury or simply be unspent, because in the end it ends up being borrowed money. So with that, I thank you. Now I want to talk a little bit about the BEAD program. Blue Ridge Mountain EMC in my district is a member-owned cooperative—an electric cooperative—that was excited to receive a $3.8 million BEAD award towards their $13.1 million project to connect 3,700 hard-to-reach households in northeast Georgia with broadband. But on January 12th of this year, the National Telecom and Information Administration quietly added new language to the BEAD general terms and conditions requiring cooperatives to comply with FCC pole attachment rules across their entire service footprint—not just the BEAD-funded areas, but for the entire service footprint. This is a serious problem for a few reasons. First, Congress explicitly exempted electric cooperatives from FCC pole attachment rules under Section 224 of the Communications Act, and as I understand it, the NTIA has no mandate to undo that exemption. Second, and most critically, Blue Ridge Mountain EMC is regulated by the Tennessee Valley Authority, which governs pole attachment rates for all 153 TVA-served utilities using a standardized valley-wide methodology. As I see it, NTIA is demanding that this cooperative violate its obligations to its own congressionally authorized federal regulator in order to accept a federal grant from a different agency. Blue Ridge Mountain EMC has concluded that the compliance burden may already outweigh the benefit and may be forced to walk away from the grant entirely, and I don't want to see that happen at all. Are you familiar with this issue? That's the benefit of this job. I'm familiar with all of these issues. Okay, great. Fantastic. I just want to let you know, I looked into this. The TVA is exempt from these rules and Blue Ridge Mountain EMC is exempt. They can stick to the policy and the process that you and I would agree is the best for your area. Okay, so thank you for that commitment. I was going to ask you for that and you beat me to the punch. Thank you very much; we'll take that as gospel. Thank you, sir. I was encouraged to see your budget request reduces the Department of Commerce's footprint by $1.8 billion—a 16.5% decrease from the FY 26 enacted level. Your proposal rightly eliminates duplicative programs within the Economic Development Administration and the Minority Business Development Agency, and curtails billions in federal funding dollars that the Biden-Harris administration funneled into radical climate initiatives, research, and grant programs. Mr. Secretary, you propose eliminating the Economic Development Administration and the Minority Business Development Agency. Can you just describe how both of these agencies, the EDA and the MBDA, were wasting taxpayer dollars on earmarks for political pet projects and subsidies for leftist ideologies, and the reasoning behind those savings? For example, the MBDA had a very, very rough IG report, and I have that audit here and it is brutal. It actually literally says that over 92% of the awards should never have been made, that the economic analysis was wrong and flawed, and so it was a mess. So we sought to make it zero because it was a mess. If it was effective, great, but if... you know. It's the same with these other programs. The problem, like with the MEP, right? The idea is a good idea, but you should read the Inspector General's report. It'll make you sick to your stomach. They overpay the people. This was set up in 1988 to do advanced manufacturing, and what happened is it just crept and crept and crept to where the money was just not being used effectively. Whatever appropriations you give to me, what I will promise you is I will do our darndest to use it incredibly effectively for the benefit of the bargain of the American people. But if the current program is broken, like the MBDA, I'm obviously going to suggest to you to zero it out. If you want to come up with a new program and talk about it, let's do that together, but let's not do it wrong. Well, thank you. I think that's wise counsel and I appreciate that, Mr. Chairman. With that, I yield back. The gentleman yields back. Mr. Morelle. Thank you. I would note, Mr. Chair, that you gave Mr. Clyde a minute and a half of additional time, and the ranking member was cut off and wouldn't be listened to by the Secretary. But anyway, Mr. Secretary, thank you for being here. I often quote, as my friend reminds me, Mark Twain, but I'll quote you. You said the U.S. economy is the strongest and most resilient in the world. I think if Mr. Twain were here, he would say that, given this administration, it needs to be. Last year, you testified before this subcommittee when I shared my concerns about business leaders in Rochester, New York—because, you know, Republicans aren't the only people who talk to business leaders and people making investments—about tariff policies that have thrown them into a constant state of uncertainty. The chaos remains the only predictable thing in this administration. Over the past year, there have been dozens of changes to tariff rates, target countries, products covered, specific exemptions, and a complete overhaul of the tariff authorities being used. When businesses don't have predictability—I learned a long time ago when I'd meet with business leaders they'd say predictability is the most important thing. You can do almost anything you want; I mean they wouldn't like it, but at least if it's predictable, they can plan. There is no predictability. Market conditions are changing literally week to week. The ranking member mentioned a $34 billion drop in factory construction investments in the last year, 89,000 manufacturing jobs lost, and chaos. I appreciate the continuity between the Biden and Trump administrations for Micron, which is up in my region, and others. I'm proud of that; as Americans, we should be. But it's hard to argue when only 30% of Americans approve of the administration's handling of the economy. 89,000 manufacturing jobs lost and chaos. I don't know what to tell business leaders. So, if you don't mind answering that question, because I think this uncertainty is—and look, this is important, these are people's lives and families' lives—from day to day, I hear from leaders saying, "I don't know what to do." You would tell business leaders to build in America. Build in America. We have advanced manufacturing; let's build in America. $400 billion of pharmaceuticals are coming in because of our tariff policy. Semiconductors? We will have a trillion dollars of semiconductor announced investments during President Trump's administration—a trillion dollars of semiconductor manufacturing, over and over again. Well, let me for a moment, let's just hone in on that. I represent Rochester, New York, home to dozens of small and medium-sized optics manufacturers, one of the great optics manufacturing and research centers in the United States. These companies create everything from eyeglasses to metrology machines; they have lenses in hypersonic missiles. Almost everything that has a microelectronic chip has a lens. One I talked to just a few weeks ago is trying to onshore more of their production. To do that, they have to buy a multi-million dollar machine. Because it's not made in the United States, they have to buy it from allies. It's a $10 million purchase. The tariff alone from the country that they're bringing it in from, under a Section 232 tariff, is 10%. That adds a million dollars to the upfront cost. They're thinking about not doing it. Reclaiming a million dollars on these... so, respectfully, building in the United States is great, but I talked to a company that has manufacturing in Rochester and manufacturing in Berlin. Because of Canadian imports that are going to get taxed, they said to me it is less expensive and more profitable for us to extend that manufacturing line in Berlin than it is in the United States. Look, I'd love to tell people to build. We all want—all of us, Democrats, Republicans, I don't care who you are—to build more manufacturing jobs in the United States. But you're losing them. And yes, you can cite an investment here or there, but the problem is the playing field is uneven and chaotic. Respectfully, it's reckless. Look, you have to be the cheerleader for it, and not just you; every Commerce Secretary is a cheerleader for every President. I get that. But at some point, you've got to advise the President that this is reckless and it's hurting Americans. Even the notion you're talking about auto manufacturers—the President's talking about Chinese auto manufacturers coming to the United States. They're going to compete with American auto manufacturers? That's what we're talking about. And you're talking about China and, by the way, they're electric vehicles. No, no, they're not allowed to bring Chinese cars here, absolutely not. Well, the President's talking about allowing Chinese manufacturers to build and sell into American markets. He announced last week he's talking about it. No. Okay, well, you should talk to him about what he says then. I yield back, Mr. Chair. The gentleman yields back. Now in his debut in the new Congress, the gentleman is recognized, Mr. Shreve. Thank you, Mr. Chairman, and thank you to our witness, Mr. Secretary. Happy to be here; yes, this is my first day at subcommittee and my first week at committee. But I am also a fairly new representative; I'm in my first term and I come from the middle of Indiana. I represent Central Indiana and Eastern Indiana. As a consequence, in part, of that as I came into service, I came to realize how victimized so many of my constituents—sort of trusting Hoosiers from the heartland—had been victimized by scams, many of which emanate from foreign scam centers. This is not a mission that I had coming into service, but one which I came to realize how much and to what degree so many of my colleagues up here represent constituents who are being bilked out of billions of dollars in scams. I think particularly so with some of our older constituents, those from maybe more, by default, trusting parts of the country like mine—and our Chairman maybe perhaps in your neck of the woods—where people may be less likely to answer an unknown call. But in the vein of the all-too-common text and email scams, phishing scams, that are collectively stealing from our citizens, I want to ask what our Department of Commerce is doing to push stronger authentication standards for foreign digital traffic before those calls and texts reach our citizens? I think that's a really interesting point, to try to have the origin of the call be made clear to the recipient. Especially with what's coming with AI, where someone could have my voice and use my voice to call my family, it would be nice to know that it's from some crazy location in far Asia. So, I think this is excellent, and if you'd like to work together with my team, let's go explore how to change that. I think that's a really extraordinary idea and very valuable to America. All right, I would indeed like to play that forward. It relates, in part, to the conversation on export controls of these high-end chips, as the Chairman noted. Your budget request includes a historic increase: $215 million and the addition of enforcement agents. AI is at the root of some of these challenges—certainly the fairly small scams, the text message scams, speed cam scams, and toll road scams. But the real sophisticated ones, some of which in my own district bilked a constituent out of $600,000 in property, the pig butchering scams, are enabled by the AI technology. These high-end chips can, over time, increasingly dupe our citizens. Can you just add a little color to how you aim to use the additional funding in this budget increase for BIS enforcement of those high-end chips that are making their way all too often to South Central Asia? I serve on—well, have served up to this point on—Foreign Affairs and the South Central Asia subcommittee, and that's how I really came to realize how focused these foreign scam centers were on that part of the world. We need law enforcement agents. The budget increases are for law enforcement agents, and the economics of those law enforcement agents—while we are here talking about the budget expense—the revenues that these agents call in by fining these companies far, far exceed the cost. So, if this was a business conversation, it would be a slam dunk if there ever was one, because I think these increases will go between five and ten times the money of each agent we add. But because this is government, where we have expense in one conversation and revenue is a whole other thing, as Commerce Secretary, I really want those agents. They will protect us, they will protect Americans, and they will pay for themselves multiple, multiple times. Thank you, Mr. Secretary, for your service, and with that I yield back, Chair. Ms. Dean. Thank you, Chairman Rogers, Ranking Member Meng, and Ranking Member DeLauro. Good afternoon, Secretary Lutnick. Last Tuesday, I had the chance to attend a breakfast for RAINN, the Rape, Abuse, Incest National Network. It was their congressional day of action. Survivors came to me and told me their horrific stories. The survivors I spoke with had one message: they are seeking accountability and justice. After speaking with the survivors, I again went to the Department of Justice to review the unredacted Epstein files to seek accountability and justice for the victims—the more than 1,000 victims and survivors. Today, Secretary Lutnick, I have some questions for you about what I saw. Let's start with your relationship with sex trafficker Jeffrey Epstein. In October, you told the New York Post, with colorful narration, that the last time you saw Jeffrey Epstein was in 2005 when you took a tour of his house, which happened to be right next door to yours. You claim you and your wife were so grossed out by this disgusting person that you swore you would never go back. But when the Epstein files came out, it turned out that was a lie. You were forced to admit that you had visited Epstein's private island in 2012, four years after Epstein pled guilty to soliciting a minor for prostitution and was labeled a sex offender. You brought along your wife, your kids, and your nannies to the predator's island. This was incredibly poor judgment. But that even wasn't the whole truth. It turns out that five days after visiting his island, you and Epstein signed a business deal together as co-investors in a digital advertising company, Adfin, and emails show you stayed in touch with Epstein about this deal as late as 2018. Secretary Lutnick, why did you lie to the New York Post about your relationship with Jeffrey Epstein? The House Oversight Committee and I have agreed that we will spend the time... Reclaiming my time, I do not accept that answer. We've heard that one. We are our own committee; we have our own reason to test your credibility and veracity. Please answer the question: why did you lie to the Post? I have voluntarily agreed to spend the time and talk about it. I reclaim the time. Let the record reflect you're dodging the question. The cover-up continues. You know what? In your conversations with the President, is the President concerned about your continued relationships that you had with Mr. Epstein as recently as 2018? I am not going to discuss conversations I have with the President. That is not something I'm going to do. If you remember in that interview, you said you would never be in the room with him socially, for business, or even philanthropy. President Trump has just fired three cabinet secretaries—three women: Kristi Noem, Pam Bondi, and Lori Chavez-DeRemer. If President Trump has even a shred of concern about accountability for Jeffrey Epstein's enablers, he would fire you, too. Beyond Adfin, what other financial ties do you have to Jeffrey Epstein? As I said, I'm going to answer all these... Beyond Adfin, what other financial ties? You're the Commerce Secretary; we have a right to ask this question and get it answered. Again, I'm not going to spend the budget day discussing something I've set aside time to spend on this topic with your House colleagues. I'm going to switch topics. Let me switch topics to a different and equally disturbing issue: how your family is profiting from your work as Commerce Secretary. Let's start with tariffs. You and the President illegally imposed the highest tariffs on the American people since the Great Depression, and your sons figured out how to profit from those high tariffs by buying up refund rights for pennies on the dollar, just as you were out there cheerleading the tariffs. As a result, federal taxpayers like my constituents may now owe your family tens or hundreds of millions of dollars. I wonder if President Trump is concerned about you and your family profiteering off the illegal tariffs. You know that that is false. Here is reporting I'd like to put into the record: "Howard Lutnick's old firm did not, in fact, profit from Supreme Court tariff ruling." That is just not true. It's your sons. It's your sons. The reporting is it's your sons. No, no. It's the old firm and my sons. It is not true. Mr. Chairman, reclaiming my time. I have asked many... Mr. Chairman, reclaiming about 20 seconds. I hope you recognize how fundamentally wrong and corrupt this is. I hope you take accountability and have the decency to resign before you are fired. It is not true. I yield. Time of the gentlelady has expired. Mr. Mrvan. Yes, Secretary Lutnick. Last week, at the Stanford World Economic Summit, you were quoted—and I want to thank you, it was a pro-union quote. You said, "The concept of taking an auto plant out of Ohio and Michigan and putting it in Mexico to break the union's back is nuts, and to break people is nuts." Is that something that you said? I don't know if I said it exactly that way, but the concept sounds right. The concept. Okay, and so you agreed that you want to protect American jobs and union jobs in that quote. Yes, I think automotive union jobs are excellent jobs for America. Okay. So, on Labor Day, the President of the United States put out an executive order which was the greatest collective bargaining and union-breaking executive order in the history of America. It took away collective bargaining rights from everyone in the Department of Commerce. Why the two mixed messages? Why take collective bargaining away from union members who work under you, and why now are you saying you're fighting for union jobs? Union jobs in the commercial side of the United States of America are fundamental. Taxpayer jobs in Article II, in the United States of America executive branch, should be run by the President of the United States elected by the people. Article II means national security. Okay, so you stand for union jobs because I need your help on this. British Petroleum in my district, which is an energy producer and the third largest oil refinery, has locked out the United Steel Workers—approximately 1,000 families, men and women—because of contract negotiations. My Republican governor, Governor Braun, came to the picket line. I agree with him in a bipartisan way that British Petroleum is putting our community at risk by having replacement workers who are untrained, creating environmental risks and safety risks for Hammond, East Chicago, and Whiting. My community is under threat. Will you join me in asking the President of the United States to tell British Petroleum—a foreign company and energy producer—to go back to the bargaining table so that those very valuable union jobs you were just theatrical about will be saved and families in my community will go back to work? I didn't mean to be theatrical. Don't laugh. There's nothing funny about... No, no, I said... Don't laugh at my situation about a thousand families out of work. That's not right. That's not fair. No, no, I said... I'm just putting things into perspective. I said your comment of "theatrical" I had not intended, but when we were talking about union jobs, I have been very clear. I am very supportive of great union jobs in America; I think they are excellent jobs for America. Will you make the commitment? I'm a fan of the steelworkers, I'm a fan of the teamsters, and everybody knows it. How about this: let's sit together, let's go through it together, and let's come up with the right move to get this company to treat our union workers fairly. I am on your side with that; let's work together to do it. So, the Secretary of Commerce, in your role, you will sit down with me to make sure British Petroleum goes back to the negotiation table so my families at Steelworkers go back to work? Seems like a fair request to me. Yes? That's a yes? Okay. So, thank you for that. That's progress. And I'm going to hold you to that, as I expect you to hold me accountable to things. Secondly, you had talked about Secretary Wright and yourself celebrating natural gas-powered investments for facilities in southern Pennsylvania and Ohio. In Northwest Indiana a week ago, on March 26th, Secretary Wright made an emergency order saying that our utility, NIPSCO, needed to reopen a coal-fired plant because of national security. I have the highest rates for electricity and energy in the country; my ratepayers in Northwest Indiana are getting crushed by those rates. It's $100 million to reopen a coal plant that NIPSCO privately chose to close in 2019. Explain the mixed messages to me. Why is natural gas good for Ohio and southern Pennsylvania, but my ratepayers have to pay $100 million because of an executive order? It's a tariff placed through MISO that the ratepayers have to pay. Tell me, why are my ratepayers—who have the highest energy bills in the country—paying for that, while you're celebrating natural gas power plants in Pennsylvania and Ohio? Just help me understand the differences. Reopening a coal-fired plant will employ lots of coal miners, which we just discussed. But why is natural gas good for Ohio and southern Pennsylvania, but my ratepayers have to pay $100 million because of an executive order? The ratepayers have to pay that. Tell me, why are my ratepayers—who have the highest energy bills in the country—paying for that, while you're celebrating natural gas power plants in Pennsylvania and Ohio? Opening a coal plant will lower the cost of energy in your district. I'm happy to sit down with you and go through it with you. I'm happy to do that; I love math. It's going to lower the cost. The math is it's a hundred million dollars to the ratepayers because they have to reopen something, while you're celebrating cutting ribbons for the same things in other parts of the country. I don't understand. The time of the gentleman has expired. With that, I yield back. I thank you for working with me on the British Petroleum issue. Mr. Ivey. Thank you, Mr. Chairman. Mr. Secretary, I've got some questions for you about the illegal tariff refunds that the administration is starting the process to make. The Trump administration collected about $166 billion or so in these illegal tariffs, and they were declared illegal by the Supreme Court. I think on Monday, CBP opened up a portal that would allow the importers of record and licensed customs brokers to file for refunds. I know there were some problems with how it got going forward and the like, but the biggest question I had was that it looks like only the first tier of entities that were interacting on the trade front are going to be eligible to get a refund. I asked the USTR head about this the other week. I'm trying to figure out if the Trump administration's going to do anything to help small businesses in the next tiers down who also ended up having to pay some of these tariffs as they were passed along. I'm especially thinking of everyday consumers, individuals, and customers who bought something at the store that cost more because the tariffs raised the price of it. Now, there's going to be a refund that's provided, but only to the frontline people who also benefited from charging the higher prices down the road. So, from one perspective, those corporations are going to get a windfall profit, but there doesn't seem to be anything in place to pass along a refund to the smaller businesses or the individual customers. Are you aware of anything that the Trump administration is doing to help those funds go back to the small businesses and the individual customers? Again, the process of setting tariffs I participate in, but Customs collects them. Customs is responsible for the portal and all these things. I'm just not involved in that process. Well, but hold up a second. I mean, I just saw the 60 Minutes interview—or I'm sorry, the Face the Nation interview—where you were talking about tariffs and you were praising the President's tariffs, the same tariffs that were declared illegal by the Supreme Court. In fact, you said that these tariffs are going to be so beneficial to the American people that there'd be a point where they wouldn't even have to pay individual income taxes anymore. Obviously, that's not going to be the case now. And by the way, at the time that the President learned of the Supreme Court's ruling, you were standing there in the Oval Office with him as he criticized the Supreme Court for the ruling. So it's clear that it's in your bandwidth as the Secretary of Commerce, and it's clear that the tariffs are highly relevant to your role as Secretary. If there was something the Trump administration was doing about refunding this, I'm sure you would know about it. So, is there anything that you're aware of that the Trump administration is doing to help the refunds go back to small businesses and individual customers? Again, as I said, there's a portal that has been put out, but it's by Customs and Border Patrol, and I'm not participating in that portal. Let's do it this way. Would you commit today to participate in setting up something with the administration, through the administration, to help make sure that small businesses who were not the frontline with respect to the payment of the brokers—and especially individual consumers—get a chance to get a refund? Well, that makes perfect sense. You're committing to do that? It makes perfect sense to me. All right, well we will definitely follow up with that. I appreciate your commitment on that front. And with that, I yield back. Gentleman yields back. That concludes today's hearing. I'd like to thank our witness, Secretary Lutnick, for his testimony and his presence with the committee. Without objection, members may have seven days to submit additional questions for the record. Thank you, Mr. Secretary, for being here today and answering questions. Subcommittee stands adjourned.

LIVE: Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick Testifies Before The House

Forbes Breaking News(5.5M) ·103:55 ·3.1K views · 2026-04-23
Howard Lutnick (US Commerce Secretary)
Secretary Howard Lutnick presented a $9.2 billion FY27 budget request for the Department of Commerce—a 16.5% decrease from current levels—focusing on "America First" trade policies and private-sector investment while facing intense criticism over pro...
TL;DR Secretary Howard Lutnick presented a $9.2 billion FY27 budget request for the Department of Commerce—a 16.5% decrease from current levels—focusing on "America First" trade policies and private-sector investment while facing intense criticism over program eliminations, late...

TL;DR

Secretary Howard Lutnick presented a $9.2 billion FY27 budget request for the Department of Commerce—a 16.5% decrease from current levels—focusing on "America First" trade policies and private-sector investment while facing intense criticism over program eliminations, late reporting, and personal ethical allegations.

FY27 Budget Priorities and Economic Strategy

  • Budget Overview: The request totals $9.2 billion, representing a $1.8 billion or 16.5% decrease from the current funding level.
  • Trade Deficit Reduction: Secretary Lutnick highlighted that in 2025, the trade deficit reached its lowest level in 16 years, with exports rising 6% to $3.4 trillion.
  • Enforcement and Security:
      • BIS Funding: The budget seeks a $215 million increase for the Bureau of Industry and Security (BIS) to hire hundreds of new special agents to combat the theft of U.S. technology.
      • China Strategy: The request prioritizes countering the "strong-arm trade tactics" of China and protecting national security.
  • Pharmaceutical Reshoring: Lutnick announced over $400 billion in commitments to reshore pharmaceutical manufacturing and new "most favored nation" deals with the Department of Health and Human Services to lower drug prices.

Major Capital Investments and Trade Deals

  • Semiconductor Growth: The Secretary cited massive private-sector commitments to build in the U.S., including Micron ($200 billion in ID, NY, and VA), TSMC ($165 billion), and Texas Instruments ($60 billion).
  • Japan Trade Deal: A $550 billion trade agreement with Japan has initiated six major projects, including:
      • Small Modular Reactors: $40 billion in Tennessee and Alabama.
      • Natural Gas Facilities: A $33 billion facility in Ohio and a $17 billion hub in Pennsylvania.
  • International Trade Administration (ITA) Successes: In 2025, the ITA helped U.S. companies like Boeing and Wabtec secure $244 billion in foreign public buyer contracts, significantly exceeding previous administration totals.

Program Eliminations and "Wasteful Spending"

  • Manufacturing and Equity Cuts: Ranking Member Grace Meng criticized the administration for proposing to "zero out" several programs, including:
      • Manufacturing Extension Partnership (MEP): A program designed to support small and medium-sized U.S. manufacturers.
      • Minority Business Development Agency (MBDA): The budget proposes to defund the agency entirely.
      • Digital Equity Program: The administration seeks to rescind all remaining funding intended to provide high-speed internet to veterans and rural communities.
  • Economic Development: The budget proposes the total elimination of Economic Development Administration (EDA) grants.

Oversight, Mismanagement, and Ethical Allegations

  • Responsiveness Issues: Both the Chairman and Ranking Member Meng criticized the department for "unacceptable" delays in providing oversight documentation.
      • Delayed Plans: Complete FY27 spend plans and budget justification documents were only received the week of the hearing, well past legal deadlines.
      • Unanswered Questions: More than 10 months after the Secretary’s last appearance, written questions for the record remain unanswered.
  • National Weather Service (NWS) Controversy: Meng alleged that Lutnick previously misled the subcommittee regarding firing NWS meteorologists through the "DOGE" initiative and noted that frontline staff positions remain unfilled.
  • Personal Ethical Challenges:
      • Family Financial Interests: Meng cited a New York Times investigation alleging an unprecedented intersection between Commerce Department deals and the financial interests of Lutnick’s family.
      • Epstein Allegations: Meng raised concerns regarding Lutnick’s disclosed interactions with Jeffrey Epstein, including a 2012 trip to Epstein’s private island, which she claimed contradicted previous statements made by the Secretary.

Notable Quotes

"When businesses look for the place to expand manufacturing, they drive innovation and they hire more workers, they are decisively choosing the United States." — Howard Lutnick, Secretary of Commerce
"Never in modern U.S. history has the office of the Secretary of Commerce intersected so broadly and deeply with the financial interests of the Commerce Secretary’s own family." — Grace Meng, Ranking Member
"The budget slashes unnecessary wasteful spending at NIST and other groups, and eliminates the manufacturing extension partnership... It also is again proposing to defund the minority business development agency." — Howard Lutnick, Secretary of Commerce
"Secretary Lutnick, these areas of communication and responsiveness should be a priority of yours." — Chairman (House CJS Subcommittee)
Good morning. The subcommittee will be in order. Without objection, the chair is authorized to declare recess at any time. I would like to welcome everyone to this hearing. I also want to welcome a new member to the Appropriations Committee and to CGS, Representative Jefferson Shreve. Welcome, sir. Thank you, Mr. Chairman. I know he will be a strong voice for Indiana on the committee. First one in the last—is it 40 years? 30. Yeah. And we look forward to working together. Likewise. Thank you to our subcommittee members and to Secretary Lutnick for joining us to discuss the Department of Commerce Fiscal Year 27 budget request. The request for the Department of Commerce totals $9.2 billion, a $1.8 billion or 16.5% decrease from the current level. The request reiterates and builds on the budget the President submitted to Congress last year. It seeks to reinforce and expand on efforts to counter the strong-arm trade tactics of China, and refocuses the department's trade activities on investments that create jobs in America and protect national security. The Department of Commerce has a wide-ranging portfolio. It must level an international trade landscape tilted against America, foster economic growth, conduct weather research, and produce statistics about our nation and economy. These responsibilities are bound together by the common goal of ensuring American prosperity. Its investments and resources are invaluable to rural communities like my district in Eastern Kentucky. From NOAA's weather forecasters who provide severe weather warnings when seconds count, to EDA's essential work to grow economic opportunity and the BEAD program's expansion of high-speed, reliable broadband to rural areas, it is opening opportunities and improving lives. The department's vital mission requires strong leadership and strong oversight. I would like to note that the committee only just this week received the department's complete Fiscal Year 27 spend plans and detailed budget request documents for Fiscal Year 27. Additionally, the department must ensure a response to written questions from subcommittee members. Secretary Lutnick, these areas of communication and responsiveness should be a priority of yours. With that said, I look forward to hearing how this budget request furthers our shared goals. Let me recognize now the ranking member of the subcommittee, Ms. Ming. Thank you, Mr. Chairman. I want to join you in welcoming Secretary Lutnick back to this subcommittee and also welcome our new member on the subcommittee, Mr. Shreve. At a time when American families are struggling with a high cost of gas, groceries, housing, and other essentials, the Trump administration is proposing a commerce budget that would make things worse. The Fiscal Year 27 budget request would hurt American jobs, small businesses, and economically distressed communities by completely eliminating Economic Development Administration grants and cutting the ITA's efforts to expand US exports. The administration is once again proposing to zero out the Manufacturing Extension Partnership program, which helps small and medium-sized US manufacturers create and preserve US manufacturing jobs. It also is again proposing to defund the Minority Business Development Agency after attempting to illegally shut it down. And the administration is proposing to rescind all of the remaining funding for the Digital Equity Program, which Congress appropriated on a bipartisan basis to help veterans, seniors, rural communities, and people with disabilities afford and make full use of high-speed internet. The American economy and hardworking Americans are getting squeezed. Yet, at the same time, Secretary Lutnick's own family has greatly benefited from many of the deals that have been arranged by the Commerce Department over the past year. According to a New York Times investigation in November, never in modern US history has the office of the Secretary of Commerce intersected so broadly and deeply with the financial interests of the Commerce Secretary's own family. Furthermore, the Commerce Department has been plagued by mismanagement over the past year, beginning with the damage done by Elon Musk's DOGE. Last year, Secretary Lutnick, you told this subcommittee that no Weather Service meteorologists were fired, when in fact, many meteorologists were fired. We learned in a hearing before this subcommittee last month that the department and the National Weather Service still have not backfilled all of the National Weather Service's frontline staff positions that were so needlessly lost last year. The department's responsiveness, or lack thereof, to this committee has also been concerning. More than 10 months after our subcommittee submitted questions for the record following the Secretary's last appearance, we still have received no answers. The department also failed to submit its Fiscal Year 26 spend plan in accordance with the legal deadline, as all of the spend plans were four or more weeks late. We only this week received the spend plans for the department's largest bureau, NOAA, and for the Minority Business Development Agency, which were legally due to our Appropriations Committee a month and a half ago. The department's Fiscal Year 27 budget justification documents were also late in being publicly released. All of these delays are completely unacceptable and obstruct our committee's ability to conduct oversight and to make informed decisions about the federal budget on behalf of the American people. Finally, I was disturbed to learn from disclosures in the Epstein files that Secretary Lutnick had personal and business interactions with Jeffrey Epstein for years after Epstein was registered as a sex offender. Secretary Lutnick previously claimed that he cut ties with Mr. Epstein in '05, but the Epstein files show the Secretary took a trip to Epstein's private island in 2012, four years after Epstein pled guilty to soliciting prostitution from a minor and was labeled a sex offender. The Secretary's dishonesty caused me to question the truthfulness of other statements he has made to Congress on the record. The American people deserve honesty from those with the privilege of holding public office. And I will say once again that the survivors of Jeffrey Epstein's horrific crimes deserve truth, accountability, and justice. I look forward to the Secretary's testimony and to answers to the many questions that my colleagues and I have. Thank you, and I yield back. Thank you, Ms. Ming. I would now like to recognize our witness, Secretary Lutnick, for an opening statement. Without objection, your written statement will be entered into the record. Mr. Secretary, we're delighted to have you with us. This is your official congressional oversight subcommittee. We want to be helpful to you in your duties, and we want to know some answers that we all have. So, Mr. Secretary, thank you for being here; the floor is yours. Thank you, Mr. Chairman, and thank you for having me here. Thank you, Representative Ming, and the members of the subcommittee. I appreciate you having me here today, and welcome and welcome. Over the past 15 months, President Trump has proven that the American economy is the strongest and most resilient in the world. When businesses look for the place to expand manufacturing, they drive innovation and they hire more workers; they are decisively choosing the United States. Micron, for example, $200 billion investment in memory semiconductor manufacturing in Idaho, Upstate New York, and Virginia. TSMC's $165 billion advanced manufacturing investment in America's semiconductor logic sector. Texas Instruments' $60 billion investment for seven US semiconductor fabs across three manufacturing megasites in Texas and Utah are just a few examples of the Trump administration's commitment to building America. Trillions of dollars of capital investment are pouring into America led by President Trump and the United States Department of Commerce. I could spend our entire time together listing the record-setting commitments company after company and industry after industry have made to build in America. I'm proud of the leadership role that the Department of Commerce has played in providing the support and encouragement these businesses need to revive American manufacturing, and we have only just begun. Over the fast past few months alone, the Department of Commerce has announced multiple $15 billion plus record-setting critical energy investments across this country, from Pennsylvania and Georgia to Texas and Tennessee. They will accelerate economic growth, ensure US energy dominance, bolster critical supply chains, and strengthen US national and economic security. In addition to the massive historic trade agreements with Korea and Taiwan, the impressive $550 billion trade deal with Japan has announced six projects in the past six months. These include $40 billion for small modular reactors in Tennessee and Alabama, a $33 billion, 9.2 gigawatt natural gas-fired facility in Ohio, a $17 billion natural gas-fired power generation hub in Southwest Pennsylvania, and $16 billion for natural gas in East Texas. That goes on top of $2 billion for a deep water crude oil export terminal in the Gulf of Mexico and $600 million for a critical high-pressure, high-temperature synthetic diamond grit facility in Georgia. Just last month, Secretary Wright and I were in Southern Ohio to break ground on that 9.2 gigawatt natural gas facility. This project will develop the largest natural gas facility in history. It will lower electricity costs across the region, create thousands and thousands of American jobs, strengthen our national security, and help the US meet the energy needs of our future. Beyond these historic investments, the Department of Commerce continues to work around the clock to implement President Trump's America First trade policy. Under Section 232, the Commerce Department is taking action on steel, aluminum, copper, pharmaceuticals, autos, semiconductors, and investigating other sectors to protect, reshore, and revive manufacturing. These actions have led to multibillion-dollar commitments to reshore steel and aluminum manufacturing and restore American excellence in critical industries. In the first year of his second term, President Trump has dramatically reduced the trade deficit, lowering imports and increasing exports. Exports went to $3.4 trillion, which is a 6% increase from 2024, and in 2025, the trade deficit was the lowest we've seen in 16 years. I'm proud of the role I played in negotiating our most important trade agreement, including the largest deal ever done, which was our trade deal and investment deal with the European Union. The IAS is working hand-in-hand with the Department of Health and Human Services, and we've arranged historic agreements to lower drug prices for American consumers. We are negotiating most-favored-nation deals and ensuring Americans pay the lowest prices for innovative medicines. As a result of these deals, additionally, we have secured over $400 billion in commitments to reshore pharmaceuticals and manufacturing in America—those key pharmaceuticals. The budget includes a $215 million increase to BIS above the 2026 enacted level, which supports the hiring of hundreds of new special agent law enforcement officials to address the theft of American technological leadership by bad actors. These officers are critical to protecting and defending America's innovation. In ITA, we are driving record foreign direct investment, cracking down on harmful foreign trade practices, and supporting our 1.3 million American jobs with export and investment deals. In 2025, ITA helped Boeing, Wabtec, and other major American companies secure a record $244 billion in contracts with foreign public buyers. This exceeds the $17 billion that the Biden administration did in its first year. The budget slashes unnecessary wasteful spending at NIST and other groups, and eliminates the Manufacturing Extension Partnership, which is designed to help small and medium-sized American manufacturers grow, create, and preserve US manufacturing jobs. It also is again proposing to defund the Minority Business Development Agency after attempting to illegally shut it down. And the administration is proposing to rescind all of the remaining funding for the Digital Equity Program, which Congress appropriated on a bipartisan basis to help veterans, seniors, rural communities, and people with disabilities afford and make full use of high-speed internet. The American economy and hardworking Americans are getting squeezed. Yet, at the same time, Secretary Lutnick's own family has greatly benefited from many of the deals that have been arranged by the Commerce Department over the past year. According to a New York Times investigation in November, never in modern US history has the office of the Secretary of Commerce intersected so broadly and deeply with the financial interests of the Commerce Secretary's own family. Furthermore, the Commerce Department has been plagued by mismanagement over the past year, beginning with the damage done by Elon Musk's DOGE. Last year, Secretary Lutnick, you told this subcommittee that no Weather Service meteorologists were fired, when in fact, many meteorologists were fired. We learned in a hearing before this subcommittee last month that the department and the National Weather Service still have not backfilled all of the National Weather Service's frontline staff positions that were so needlessly lost last year. The department's responsiveness, or lack thereof, to this committee has also been concerning. More than 10 months after our subcommittee submitted questions for the record following the Secretary's last appearance, we still have received no answers. The department also failed to submit its Fiscal Year 26 spend plan in accordance with the legal deadline, as all of the spend plans were four or more weeks late. And we only this week received the spend plans for the department's largest bureau, NOAA, and for the Minority Business Development Agency, which were legally due to our Appropriations Committee a month and a half ago. The department's Fiscal Year 27 budget justification documents were also late in being publicly released. All of these delays are completely unacceptable and obstruct our committee's ability to conduct oversight and to make informed decisions about the federal budget on behalf of the American people. Finally, I was disturbed to learn from disclosures in the Epstein files that Secretary Lutnick had personal and business interactions with Jeffrey Epstein for years after Epstein was registered as a sex offender. Secretary Lutnick's previous claim was that he cut ties with Mr. Epstein in '05, but the Epstein files show the Secretary took a trip to Epstein's private island in 2012, four years after Epstein pled guilty to soliciting prostitution from a minor and was labeled a sex offender. The Secretary's dishonesty caused me to question the truthfulness of other statements he has made to Congress on the record. The American people deserve honesty from those with the privilege of holding public office. And I will say once again that the survivors of Jeffrey Epstein's horrific crimes deserve truth, accountability, and justice. I look forward to the Secretary's testimony and to answers to the many questions that my colleagues and I have. Thank you, and I yield back. Thank you, Ms. Ming. I would now like to recognize our witness, Secretary Lutnick, for an opening statement. Without objection, your written statement will be entered into the record. Mr. Secretary, we're delighted to have you with us. This is your official congressional oversight subcommittee. We want to be helpful to you in your duties, and we want to know some answers that we all have. So, Mr. Secretary, thank you for being here. The floor is yours. Thank you, Mr. Chairman, and thank you for having me here. Thank you, Representative Ming, and the members of the subcommittee. I appreciate you having me here today, and welcome and welcome. Over the past 15 months, President Trump has proven that the American economy is the strongest and most resilient in the world. When businesses look for the place to expand manufacturing, they drive innovation and they hire more workers; they are decisively choosing the United States. Micron, for example, $200 billion investment in memory semiconductor manufacturing in Idaho, Upstate New York, and Virginia. TSMC's $165 billion advanced manufacturing investment in America's semiconductor logic sector. Texas Instruments' $60 billion investment for seven US semiconductor fabs across three manufacturing megasites in Texas and Utah are just a few examples of the Trump administration's commitment to building America. Trillions of dollars of capital investment are pouring into America led by President Trump and the United States Department of Commerce. I could spend our entire time together listing the record-setting commitments company after company and industry after industry have made to build in America. I'm proud of the leadership role that the Department of Commerce has played in providing the support and encouragement these businesses need to revive American manufacturing, and we have only just begun. Over the fast past few months alone, the Department of Commerce has announced multiple $15 billion plus record-setting critical energy investments across this country, from Pennsylvania and Georgia to Texas and Tennessee. They will accelerate economic growth, ensure US energy dominance, bolster critical supply chains, and strengthen US national and economic security. In addition to the massive historic trade agreements with Korea and Taiwan, the impressive $550 billion trade deal with Japan has announced six projects in the past six months. These include $40 billion for small modular reactors in Tennessee and Alabama, a $33 billion, 9.2 gigawatt natural gas-fired facility in Ohio, a $17 billion natural gas-fired power generation hub in Southwest Pennsylvania, and $16 billion for natural gas in East Texas. That goes on top of $2 billion for a deep water crude oil export terminal in the Gulf of Mexico and $600 million for a critical high-pressure, high-temperature synthetic diamond grit facility in Georgia. Just last month, Secretary Wright and I were in Southern Ohio to break ground on that 9.2 gigawatt natural gas facility. This project will develop the largest natural gas facility in history. It will lower electricity costs across the region, create thousands and thousands of American jobs, strengthen our national security, and help the US meet the energy needs of our future. Beyond these historic investments, the Department of Commerce continues to work around the clock to implement President Trump's America First trade policy. Under Section 232, the Commerce Department is taking action on steel, aluminum, copper, pharmaceuticals, autos, semiconductors, and investigating other sectors to protect, reshore, and revive manufacturing. These actions have led to multibillion-dollar commitments to reshore steel and aluminum manufacturing and restore American excellence in critical industries. In the first year of his second term, President Trump has dramatically reduced the trade deficit, lowering imports and increasing exports. Exports went to $3.4 trillion, which is a 6% increase from 2024, and in 2025, the trade deficit was the lowest we've seen in 16 years. I'm proud of the role I played in negotiating our most important trade agreement, including the largest deal ever done, which was our trade deal and investment deal with the European Union. The IAS is working hand-in-hand with the Department of Health and Human Services, and we've arranged historic agreements to lower drug prices for American consumers. We are negotiating most-favored-nation deals and ensuring Americans pay the lowest prices for innovative medicines. As a result of these deals, additionally, we have secured over $400 billion in commitments to reshore pharmaceuticals and manufacturing in America—those key pharmaceuticals. The budget includes a $215 million increase to BIS above the 2026 enacted level, which supports the hiring of hundreds of new special agent law enforcement officials to address the theft of American technological leadership by bad actors. These officers are critical to protecting and defending America's innovation. In ITA, we are driving record foreign direct investment, cracking down on harmful foreign trade practices, and supporting our 1.3 million American jobs with export and investment deals. In 2025, ITA helped Boeing, Wabtec, and other major American companies secure a record $244 billion in contracts with foreign public buyers. This exceeds the $17 billion that the Biden administration did in its first year. The budget slashes unnecessary wasteful spending at NIST and other groups, and eliminates the Manufacturing Extension Partnership, which is designed to help small and medium-sized American manufacturers grow, create, and preserve US manufacturing jobs. It also is again proposing to defund the Minority Business Development Agency after attempting to illegally shut it down. And the administration is proposing to rescind all of the remaining funding for the Digital Equity Program, which Congress appropriated on a bipartisan basis to help veterans, seniors, rural communities, and people with disabilities afford and make full use of high-speed internet. The American economy and hardworking Americans are getting squeezed. Yet, at the same time, Secretary Lutnick's own family has greatly benefited from many of the deals that have been arranged by the Commerce Department over the past year. According to a New York Times investigation in November, never in modern US history has the office of the Secretary of Commerce intersected so broadly and deeply with the financial interests of the Commerce Secretary's own family. Furthermore, the Commerce Department has been plagued by mismanagement over the past year, beginning with the damage done by Elon Musk's DOGE. Last year, Secretary Lutnick, you told this subcommittee that no Weather Service meteorologists were fired, when in fact, many meteorologists were fired. We learned in a hearing before this subcommittee last month that the department and the National Weather Service still have not backfilled all of the National Weather Service's frontline staff positions that were so needlessly lost last year. The department's responsiveness, or lack thereof, to this committee has also been concerning. More than 10 months after our subcommittee submitted questions for the record following the Secretary's last appearance, we still have received no answers. The department also failed to submit its fiscal year 26 spend plan in accordance with the legal deadline, as all of the spend plans were four or more weeks late. We only this week received the spend plans for the department's largest bureau, NOAA, and for the Minority Business Development Agency, which were legally due to our Appropriations Committee a month and a half ago. The department's fiscal year 27 budget justification documents were also late in being publicly released. All of these delays are completely unacceptable and obstruct our committee's ability to conduct oversight and to make informed decisions about the federal budget on behalf of the American people. Finally, I was disturbed to learn from disclosures in the Epstein files that Secretary Lutnick had personal and business interactions with Jeffrey Epstein for years after Epstein was registered as a sex offender. Secretary Lutnick's previous claim was that he cut ties with Mr. Epstein in '05, but the Epstein files show the Secretary took a trip to Epstein's private island in 2012, four years after Epstein pled guilty to soliciting prostitution from a minor and was labeled a sex offender. The Secretary's dishonesty caused me to question the truthfulness of other statements he has made to Congress on the record. The American people deserve honesty from those with the privilege of holding public office. And I will say once again that the survivors of Jeffrey Epstein's horrific crimes deserve truth, accountability, and justice. I look forward to the Secretary's testimony and to answers to the many questions that my colleagues and I have. Thank you, and I yield back. Thank you, Miss Ming. I would now like to recognize our witness, Secretary Lutnick, for an opening statement. Without objection, your written statement will be entered into the record. Mr. Secretary, we're delighted to have you with us. This is your official congressional oversight subcommittee. We want to be helpful to you in your duties, and we want to know some answers that we all have. So, Mr. Secretary, thank you for being here; the floor is yours. Thank you, Mr. Chairman, and thank you for having me here. Thank you, Representative Ming, and the members of the subcommittee. I appreciate you having me here today, and welcome and welcome. Over the past 15 months, President Trump has proven that the American economy is the strongest and most resilient in the world. When businesses look for the place to expand manufacturing, they drive innovation and they hire more workers; they are decisively choosing the United States. Micron, for example, has a $200 billion investment in memory semiconductor manufacturing in Idaho, Upstate New York, and Virginia. TSMC's $165 billion advanced manufacturing investment is in America's semiconductor logic sector. Texas Instruments' $60 billion investment for seven US semiconductor fabs across three manufacturing megasites in Texas and Utah are just a few examples of the Trump administration's commitment to building America. Trillions of dollars of capital investment are pouring into America led by President Trump and the United States Department of Commerce. I could spend our entire time together listing the record-setting commitments company after company and industry after industry have made to build in America. I'm proud of the leadership role that the Department of Commerce has played in providing the support and encouragement these businesses need to revive American manufacturing, and we have only just begun. They will accelerate economic growth, ensure US energy dominance, bolster critical supply chains, and strengthen US national and economic security. These include $40 billion for small modular reactors in Tennessee and Alabama, a $33 billion, 9.2 gigawatt natural-gas-fired facility in Ohio, and a $17 billion natural-gas-fired power generation hub in Southwest Pennsylvania. There is also $16 billion for natural gas in East Texas. That goes on top of $2 billion for a deep water crude oil export terminal in the Gulf of Mexico and $600 million for a critical high-pressure, high-temperature synthetic diamond grit facility in Georgia. Just last month, Secretary Wright and I were in Southern Ohio to break ground on that 9.2 gigawatt natural gas facility. This project will develop the largest natural gas facility in history. It will lower electricity costs across the region, create thousands and thousands of American jobs, strengthen our national security, and help the US meet the energy needs of our future. Beyond these historic investments, the Department of Commerce continues to work around the clock to implement President Trump's America First trade policy. Under section 232, the Commerce Department is taking action on steel, aluminum, copper, pharmaceuticals, autos, and semiconductors, and investigating other sectors to protect, reshore, and revive manufacturing. I'm proud of the role I played in negotiating our most important trade agreement, including the largest deal ever done, which was our trade deal and investment deal with the European Union. As a result of these deals, additionally, we have secured over $400 billion in commitments to reshore pharmaceuticals and manufacturing in America. The budget includes a $215 million increase to BIS above the 2026 enacted level, which supports the hiring of hundreds of new special agent law enforcement officials to address the theft of American technological leadership by bad actors. These officers are critical to protecting and defending America's innovation. In ITA, we are driving record foreign direct investment, cracking down on harmful foreign trade practices, and supporting our 1.3 million American jobs with export and investment deals. In 2025, ITA helped Boeing, Wabtec, and other major American companies secure a record $244 billion in contracts with foreign public buyers. This exceeds the $17 billion that the Biden administration did in its first year. The budget slashes unnecessary wasteful spending at NIST and other groups, and eliminates the manufacturing extension partnership, which is designed to help small and medium-sized American manufacturers grow, create, and preserve US manufacturing jobs. It also is again proposing to defund the Minority Business Development Agency after attempting to illegally shut it down. The administration is proposing to rescind all of the remaining funding for the digital equity program, which Congress appropriated on a bipartisan basis to help veterans, seniors, rural communities, and people with disabilities afford and make full use of high-speed internet. Yet, at the same time, Secretary Lutnick's own family has greatly benefited from many of the deals that have been arranged by the Commerce Department over the past year. According to a New York Times investigation in November, never in modern US history has the office of the Secretary of Commerce intersected so broadly and deeply with the financial interests of the Commerce Secretary's own family. Furthermore, the Commerce Department has been plagued by mismanagement over the past year, beginning with the damage done by Elon Musk's DOGE. Last year, Secretary Lutnick, you told this subcommittee that no weather service meteorologists were fired, when in fact, many meteorologists were fired. We learned in a hearing before this subcommittee last month that the department at the National Weather Service still has not backfilled all of the National Weather Service's frontline staff positions that were so needlessly lost last year. More than 10 months after our subcommittee submitted questions for the record following the Secretary's last appearance, we still have received no answers. The department's fiscal year 27 budget justification documents were also late in being publicly released. All of these delays are completely unacceptable and obstruct our committee's ability to conduct oversight and to make informed decisions about the federal budget on behalf of the American people. Finally, I was disturbed to learn from disclosures in the Epstein files that Secretary Lutnick had personal and business interactions with Jeffrey Epstein for years after Epstein was registered as a sex offender. Secretary Lutnick's previous claim was that he cut ties with Mr. Epstein in '05, but the Epstein files show the Secretary took a trip to Epstein's private island in 2012, four years after Epstein pled guilty to soliciting prostitution from a minor and was labeled a sex offender. The Secretary's dishonesty caused me to question the truthfulness of other statements he has made to Congress on the record. The American people deserve honesty from those with the privilege of holding public office. And I will say once again that the survivors of Jeffrey Epstein's horrific crimes deserve truth, accountability, and justice. I look forward to the Secretary's testimony and to answers to the many questions that my colleagues and I have. Thank you, and I yield back. Thank you, Miss Ming.
