In 2025, ITA helped Boeing, Wabtec, and other major American companies secure a record $244 billion in contracts with foreign public buyers. This exceeds the $17 billion that the Biden administration did in its first year. The budget slashes unnecessary wasteful spending at NIST and other groups, and eliminates the manufacturing extension partnership, which is designed to help small and medium-sized American manufacturers grow, create, and preserve US manufacturing jobs. It also is again proposing to defund the Minority Business Development Agency after attempting to illegally shut it down. The administration is proposing to rescind all of the remaining funding for the digital equity program, which Congress appropriated on a bipartisan basis to help veterans, seniors, rural communities, and people with disabilities afford and make full use of high-speed internet. The American economy and hardworking Americans are getting squeezed. Yet, at the same time, Secretary Lutnick's own family has greatly benefited from many of the deals that have been arranged by the Commerce Department over the past year. According to a New York Times investigation in November, never in modern US history has the office of the Secretary of Commerce intersected so broadly and deeply with the financial interests of the Commerce Secretary's own family. Furthermore, the Commerce Department has been plagued by mismanagement over the past year, beginning with the damage done by Elon Musk's DOGE. Last year, Secretary Lutnick, you told this subcommittee that no weather service meteorologists were fired, when in fact, many meteorologists were fired. We learned in a hearing before this subcommittee last month that the department at the National Weather Service still has not backfilled all of the National Weather Service's frontline staff positions that were so needlessly lost last year. The department's responsiveness, or lack thereof, to this committee has also been concerning. More than 10 months after our subcommittee submitted questions for the record following the Secretary's last appearance, we still have received no answers. The department also failed to submit its fiscal year 26 spend plan in accordance with the legal deadline as all of the spend plans were four or more weeks late. We only this week received the spend plans for the department's largest bureau, NOAA, and for the Minority Business Development Agency, which were legally due to our Appropriations Committee a month and a half ago. The department's fiscal year 27 budget justification documents were also late in being publicly released. All of these delays are completely unacceptable and obstruct our committee's ability to conduct oversight and to make informed decisions about the federal budget on behalf of the American people. Finally, I was disturbed to learn from disclosures in the Epstein files that Secretary Lutnick had personal and business interactions with Jeffrey Epstein for years after Epstein was registered as a sex offender. Secretary Lutnick's previous claim was that he cut ties with Mr. Epstein in '05, but the Epstein files show the Secretary took a trip to Epstein's private island in 2012, four years after Epstein pled guilty to soliciting prostitution from a minor and was labeled a sex offender. The Secretary's dishonesty caused me to question the truthfulness of other statements he has made to Congress on the record. The American people deserve honesty from those with the privilege of holding public office. And I will say once again that the survivors of Jeffrey Epstein's horrific crimes deserve truth, accountability, and justice. I look forward to the Secretary's testimony and to answers to the many questions that my colleagues and I have. Thank you, and I yield back. Thank you, Miss Ming. I would now like to recognize our witness, Secretary Lutnick, for an opening statement. Without objection, your written statement will be entered into the record. Mr. Secretary, we're delighted to have you with us. This is your official congressional oversight subcommittee. We want to be helpful to you in your duties, and we want to know some answers that we all have. So, Mr. Secretary, thank you for being here; the floor is yours. Thank you, Mr. Chairman, and thank you for having me here. Thank you, Representative Ming, and the members of the subcommittee. I appreciate you having me here today, and welcome and welcome. Over the past 15 months, President Trump has proven that the American economy is the strongest and most resilient in the world. When businesses look for the place to expand manufacturing, they drive innovation and they hire more workers; they are decisively choosing the United States. Micron, for example, has a $200 billion investment in memory semiconductor manufacturing in Idaho, Upstate New York, and Virginia. TSMC's $165 billion advanced manufacturing investment is in America's semiconductor logic sector. Texas Instruments' $60 billion investment for seven US semiconductor fabs across three manufacturing megasites in Texas and Utah are just a few examples of the Trump administration's commitment to building America. Trillions of dollars of capital investment are pouring into America led by President Trump and the United States Department of Commerce. I could spend our entire time together listing the record-setting commitments company after company and industry after industry have made to build in America. I'm proud of the leadership role that the Department of Commerce has played in providing the support and encouragement these businesses need to revive American manufacturing, and we have only just begun. Over the fast past few months alone, the Department of Commerce has announced multiple $15 billion-plus record-setting critical energy investments across this country, from Pennsylvania and Georgia to Texas and Tennessee. They will accelerate economic growth, ensure US energy dominance, bolster critical supply chains, and strengthen US national and economic security. In addition to the massive historic trade agreements with Korea and Taiwan, the impressive $550 billion trade deal with Japan has announced six projects in the past six months. These include $40 billion for small modular reactors in Tennessee and Alabama, a $33 billion, 9.2 gigawatt natural-gas-fired facility in Ohio, and a $17 billion natural-gas-fired power generation hub in Southwest Pennsylvania. There is also $16 billion for natural gas in East Texas. That goes on top of $2 billion for a deep water crude oil export terminal in the Gulf of Mexico and $600 million for a critical high-pressure, high-temperature synthetic diamond grit facility in Georgia. Just last month, Secretary Wright and I were in Southern Ohio to break ground on that 9.2 gigawatt natural gas facility. This project will develop the largest natural gas facility in history. It will lower electricity costs across the region, create thousands and thousands of American jobs, strengthen our national security, and help the US meet the energy needs of our future. Beyond these historic investments, the Department of Commerce continues to work around the clock to implement President Trump's America First trade policy. Under section 232, the Commerce Department is taking action on steel, aluminum, copper, pharmaceuticals, autos, and semiconductors, and investigating other sectors to protect, reshore, and revive manufacturing. These actions have led to multibillion-dollar commitments to reshore steel and aluminum manufacturing and restore American excellence in critical industries. In the first year of his second term, President Trump has dramatically reduced the trade deficit, lowering imports and increasing exports. Exports went to $3.4 trillion, which is a 6% increase from 2024, and in 2025, the trade deficit was the lowest we've seen in 16 years. I'm proud of the role I played in negotiating our most important trade agreement, including the largest deal ever done, which was our trade deal and investment deal with the European Union. The IAS is working hand-in-hand with the Department of Health and Human Services, and we've arranged historic agreements to lower drug prices for American consumers, negotiating most-favored-nation deals and ensuring Americans pay the lowest prices for innovative medicines. As a result of these deals, additionally, we have secured over $400 billion in commitments to reshore pharmaceuticals and manufacturing in America, those key pharmaceuticals. The budget includes a $215 million increase to BIS above the 2026 enacted level, which supports the hiring of hundreds of new special agent law enforcement officials to address the theft of American technological leadership by bad actors. These officers are critical to protecting and defending America's innovation. In ITA, we are driving record foreign direct investment, cracking down on harmful foreign trade practices, and supporting our 1.3 million American jobs with export and investment deals. In 2025, ITA helped Boeing, Wabtec, and other major American companies secure a record $244 billion in contracts with foreign public buyers. This exceeds the $17 billion that the Biden administration did in its first year. The budget slashes unnecessary wasteful spending at NIST and other groups, and eliminates the manufacturing extension partnership, which is designed to help small and medium-sized American manufacturers grow, create, and preserve US manufacturing jobs. It also is again proposing to defund the Minority Business Development Agency after attempting to illegally shut it down. The administration is proposing to rescind all of the remaining funding for the digital equity program, which Congress appropriated on a bipartisan basis to help veterans, seniors, rural communities, and people with disabilities afford and make full use of high-speed internet. The American economy and hardworking Americans are getting squeezed. Yet, at the same time, Secretary Lutnick's own family has greatly benefited from many of the deals that have been arranged by the Commerce Department over the past year. According to a New York Times investigation in November, never in modern US history has the office of the Secretary of Commerce intersected so broadly and deeply with the financial interests of the Commerce Secretary's own family. Furthermore, the Commerce Department has been plagued by mismanagement over the past year, beginning with the damage done by Elon Musk's DOGE. Last year, Secretary Lutnick, you told this subcommittee that no weather service meteorologists were fired, when in fact, many meteorologists were fired. We learned in a hearing before this subcommittee last month that the department at the National Weather Service still has not backfilled all of the National Weather Service's frontline staff positions that were so needlessly lost last year. The department's responsiveness, or lack thereof, to this committee has also been concerning. More than 10 months after our subcommittee submitted questions for the record following the Secretary's last appearance, we still have received no answers. The department also failed to submit its fiscal year 26 spend plan in accordance with the legal deadline as all of the spend plans were four or more weeks late. We only this week received the spend plans for the department's largest bureau, NOAA, and for the Minority Business Development Agency, which were legally due to our Appropriations Committee a month and a half ago. The department's fiscal year 27 budget justification documents were also late in being publicly released. All of these delays are completely unacceptable and obstruct our committee's ability to conduct oversight and to make informed decisions about the federal budget on behalf of the American people. Finally, I was disturbed to learn from disclosures in the Epstein files that Secretary Lutnick had personal and business interactions with Jeffrey Epstein for years after Epstein was registered as a sex offender. Secretary Lutnick's previous claim was that he cut ties with Mr. Epstein in '05, but the Epstein files show the Secretary took a trip to Epstein's private island in 2012, four years after Epstein pled guilty to soliciting prostitution from a minor and was labeled a sex offender. The Secretary's dishonesty caused me to question the truthfulness of other statements he has made to Congress on the record. The American people deserve honesty from those with the privilege of holding public office. And I will say once again that the survivors of Jeffrey Epstein's horrific crimes deserve truth, accountability, and justice. I look forward to the Secretary's testimony and to answers to the many questions that my colleagues and I have. Thank you, and I yield back. Thank you, Miss Ming. Mr. Secretary, we're delighted to have you with us. This is your official congressional oversight subcommittee. I would now like to recognize our witness, Secretary Lutnick, for an opening statement. Without objection, your written statement will be entered into the record. Mr. Secretary, we're delighted to have you with us. This is your official congressional oversight subcommittee. We want to be helpful to you in your duties, and we want to know some answers that we all have. So, Mr. Secretary, thank you for being here; the floor is yours. Thank you, Mr. Chairman, and thank you for having me here. Thank you, Representative Ming, and the members of the subcommittee. I appreciate you having me here today, and welcome and welcome. Over the past 15 months, President Trump has proven that the American economy is the strongest and most resilient in the world. When businesses look for the place to expand manufacturing, they drive innovation and they hire more workers; they are decisively choosing the United States. Micron, for example: a $200 billion investment in memory semiconductor manufacturing in Idaho, Upstate New York, and Virginia. TSMC's $165 billion advanced manufacturing investment in America's semiconductor logic sector. Texas Instruments' $60 billion investment for seven US semiconductor fabs across three manufacturing megasites in Texas and Utah are just a few examples of the Trump administration's commitment to building America. Trillions of dollars of capital investment are pouring into America led by President Trump and the United States Department of Commerce. I could spend our entire time together listing the record-setting commitments company after company and industry after industry have made to build in America. I'm proud of the leadership role that the Department of Commerce has played in providing the support and encouragement these businesses need to revive American manufacturing, and we have only just begun. Over the past few months alone, the Department of Commerce has announced multiple $15 billion-plus record-setting critical energy investments across this country, from Pennsylvania and Georgia to Texas and Tennessee. They will accelerate economic growth, ensure US energy dominance, bolster critical supply chains, and strengthen US national and economic security. In addition to the massive historic trade agreements with Korea and Taiwan, the impressive $550 billion trade deal with Japan has announced six projects in the past six months. These include $40 billion for small modular reactors in Tennessee and Alabama, a $33 billion, 9.2 gigawatt natural gas-fired facility in Ohio, a $17 billion natural gas-fired power generation hub in Southwest Pennsylvania, and $16 billion for natural gas in East Texas. That goes on top of $2 billion for a deep-water crude oil export terminal in the Gulf of Mexico and $600 million for a critical high-pressure, high-temperature synthetic diamond grit facility in Georgia. Just last month, Secretary Wright and I were in Southern Ohio to break ground on that 9.2 gigawatt natural gas facility. This project will develop the largest natural gas facility in history. It will lower electricity costs across the region, create thousands and thousands of American jobs, strengthen our national security, and help the US meet the energy needs of our future. Beyond these historic investments, the Department of Commerce continues to work around the clock to implement President Trump's America First trade policy. Under section 232, the Commerce Department is taking action on steel, aluminum, copper, pharmaceuticals, autos, semiconductors, and investigating other sectors to protect, reshore, and revive manufacturing. These actions have led to multibillion-dollar commitments to reshore steel and aluminum manufacturing and restore American excellence in critical industries. In the first year of his second term, President Trump has dramatically reduced the trade deficit, lowering imports and increasing exports. Exports went to $3.4 trillion, which is a 6% increase from 2024, and in 2025, the trade deficit was the lowest we've seen in 16 years. I'm proud of the role I played in negotiating our most important trade agreement, including the largest deal ever done, which was our trade deal and investment deal with the European Union. The IAS is working hand-in-hand with the Department of Health and Human Services, and we've arranged historic agreements to lower drug prices for American consumers, negotiating most-favored-nation deals and ensuring Americans pay the lowest prices for innovative medicines. As a result of these deals, additionally, we have secured over $400 billion in commitments to reshore pharmaceuticals and manufacturing in America—those key pharmaceuticals. The budget includes a $215 million increase to BIS above the 2026 enacted level, which supports the hiring of hundreds of new special agent law enforcement officials to address the theft of American technological leadership by bad actors. These officers are critical to protecting and defending America's innovation. In ITA, we are driving record foreign direct investment, cracking down on harmful foreign trade practices, and supporting our 1.3 million American jobs with export and investment deals. In 2025, ITA helped Boeing, Wabtec, and other major American companies secure a record $244 billion in contracts with foreign public buyers. This exceeds the $17 billion that the Biden administration did in its first year. The budget slashes unnecessary, wasteful spending at NIST and other groups, and eliminates the Manufacturing Extension Partnership, which is designed to help small and medium-sized American manufacturers grow, create, and preserve US manufacturing jobs. It also is again proposing to defund the Minority Business Development Agency after attempting to illegally shut it down. And the administration is proposing to rescind all of the remaining funding for the digital equity program, which Congress appropriated on a bipartisan basis to help veterans, seniors, rural communities, and people with disabilities afford and make full use of high-speed internet. The American economy and hardworking Americans are getting squeezed. Yet, at the same time, Secretary Lutnick's own family has greatly benefited from many of the deals that have been arranged by the Commerce Department over the past year. According to a New York Times investigation in November, never in modern US history has the office of the Secretary of Commerce intersected so broadly and deeply with the financial interests of the Commerce Secretary's own family. Furthermore, the Commerce Department has been plagued by mismanagement over the past year, beginning with the damage done by Elon Musk's DOGE. Last year, Secretary Lutnick, you told this subcommittee that no weather service meteorologists were fired, when in fact, many meteorologists were fired. We learned in a hearing before this subcommittee last month that the department and the National Weather Service still have not backfilled all of the National Weather Service's frontline staff positions that were so needlessly lost last year. The department's responsiveness, or lack thereof, to this committee has also been concerning. More than 10 months after our subcommittee submitted questions for the record following the Secretary's last appearance, we still have received no answers. The department also failed to submit its fiscal year '26 spend plan in accordance with the legal deadline, as all of the spend plans were four or more weeks late. And we only this week received the spend plans for the department's largest bureau, NOAA, and for the Minority Business Development Agency, which were legally due to our Appropriations Committee a month and a half ago. The department's fiscal year '27 budget justification documents were also late in being publicly released. All of these delays are completely unacceptable and obstruct our committee's ability to conduct oversight and to make informed decisions about the federal budget on behalf of the American people. Finally, I was disturbed to learn from disclosures in the Epstein files that Secretary Lutnick had personal and business interactions with Jeffrey Epstein for years after Epstein was registered as a sex offender. Secretary Lutnick's previous claim that he cut ties with Mr. Epstein in '05, but the Epstein files show the Secretary took a trip to Epstein's private island in 2012, four years after Epstein pled guilty to soliciting prostitution from a minor and was labeled a sex offender. The Secretary's dishonesty caused me to question the truthfulness of other statements he has made to Congress on the record. The American people deserve honesty from those with the privilege of holding public office. And I will say once again that the survivors of Jeffrey Epstein's horrific crimes deserve truth, accountability, and justice. I look forward to the Secretary's testimony and to answers to the many questions that my colleagues and I have. Thank you, and I yield back. Thank you, Miss Ming. I would now like to recognize our witness, Secretary Lutnick, for an opening statement. Without objection, your written statement will be entered into the record. Mr. Secretary, we're delighted to have you with us. This is your official congressional oversight subcommittee. We want to be helpful to you in your duties, and we want to know some answers that we all have. So, Mr. Secretary, thank you for being here. The floor is yours. Thank you, Mr. Chairman, and thank you for having me here. Thank you, Representative Ming, and the members of the subcommittee. I appreciate you having me here today, and welcome and welcome. Over the past 15 months, President Trump has proven that the American economy is the strongest and most resilient in the world. When businesses look for the place to expand manufacturing, they drive innovation and they hire more workers; they are decisively choosing the United States. Micron, for example, a $200 billion investment in memory semiconductor manufacturing in Idaho, Upstate New York, and Virginia. These include $40 billion for small modular reactors in Tennessee and Alabama, a $33 billion, 9.2 gigawatt natural gas-fired facility in Ohio, a $17 billion natural gas-fired power generation hub in Southwest Pennsylvania, and $16 billion for natural gas in East Texas. That goes on top of $2 billion for a deep water crude oil export terminal in the Gulf of Mexico and $600 million for a critical high-pressure, high-temperature synthetic diamond grit facility in Georgia. Just last month, Secretary Wright and I were in Southern Ohio to break ground on that 9.2 gigawatt natural gas facility. This project will develop the largest natural gas facility in history. It will lower electricity costs across the region, create thousands and thousands of American jobs, strengthen our national security, and help the US meet the energy needs of our future. Exports went to $3.4 trillion, which is a 6% increase from 2024, and in 2025, the trade deficit was the lowest we've seen in 16 years. I'm proud of the role I played in negotiating our most important trade agreement, including the largest deal ever done, which was our trade deal and investment deal with the European Union. The IAS is working hand-in-hand with the Department of Health and Human Services, and we've arranged historic agreements to lower drug prices for American consumers, negotiating most-favored-nation deals and ensuring Americans pay the lowest prices for innovative medicines. As a result of these deals, additionally, we have secured over $400 billion in commitments to reshore pharmaceuticals and manufacturing in America, those key pharmaceuticals. These officers are critical to protecting and defending America's innovation. In ITA, we are driving record foreign direct investment, cracking down on harmful foreign trade practices, and supporting our 1.3 million American jobs with export and investment deals. In 2025, ITA helped Boeing, Wabtec, and other major American companies secure a record $244 billion in contracts with foreign public buyers. This exceeds the $17 billion that the Biden administration did in its first year. It also is again proposing to defund the minority business development agency after attempting to illegally shut it down. And the administration is proposing to rescind all of the remaining funding for the digital equity program, which Congress appropriated on a bipartisan basis to help veterans, seniors, rural communities, and people with disabilities afford and make full use of high-speed internet. Yet, at the same time, Secretary Lutnick's own family has greatly benefited from many of the deals that have been arranged by the Commerce Department over the past year. According to a New York Times investigation in November, never in modern US history has the office of the Secretary of Commerce intersected so broadly and deeply with the financial interests of the Commerce Secretary's own family. Furthermore, the Commerce Department has been plagued by mismanagement over the past year, beginning with the damage done by Elon Musk's DOGE. Last year, Secretary Lutnick, you told this subcommittee that no weather service meteorologists were fired, when in fact, many meteorologists were fired. All of these delays are completely unacceptable and obstruct our committee's ability to conduct oversight and to make informed decisions about the federal budget on behalf of the American people. Finally, I was disturbed to learn from disclosures in the Epstein files that Secretary Lutnick had personal and business interactions with Jeffrey Epstein for years after Epstein was registered as a sex offender. Secretary Lutnick's previous claim that he cut ties with Mr. Epstein in '05, but the Epstein files show the Secretary took a trip to Epstein's private island in 2012, four years after Epstein pled guilty to soliciting prostitution from a minor and was labeled a sex offender. The Secretary's dishonesty caused me to question the truthfulness of other statements he has made to Congress on the record. The American people deserve honesty from those with the privilege of holding public office. And I will say once again that the survivors of Jeffrey Epstein's horrific crimes deserve truth, accountability, and justice. I look forward to the Secretary's testimony and to answers to the many questions that my colleagues and I have. Thank you, and I yield back. Thank you, Miss Ming. The IAS is working hand-in-hand with the Department of Health and Human Services, and we've arranged historic agreements to lower drug prices for American consumers. We are negotiating most favored nation deals and ensuring Americans pay the lowest prices for innovative medicines. As a result of these deals, additionally, we have secured over $400 billion in commitments to reshore pharmaceuticals and manufacturing in America—those key pharmaceuticals. The budget includes a $215 million increase to BIS above the 2026 enacted level, which supports the hiring of hundreds of new special agent law enforcement officials to address the theft of American technological leadership by bad actors. These officers are critical to protecting and defending America's innovation. In ITA, we are driving record foreign direct investment, cracking down on harmful foreign trade practices, and supporting our 1.3 million American jobs with export and investment deals. In 2025, ITA helped Boeing, Wabtec, and other major American companies secure a record $244 billion in contracts with foreign public buyers. This exceeds the $17 billion that the Biden administration did in its first year. The budget slashes unnecessary, wasteful spending at NIST and other groups, and eliminates the manufacturing extension partnership, which is designed to help small and medium-sized American manufacturers grow, create, and preserve US manufacturing jobs. It also is again proposing to defund the minority business development agency after attempting to illegally shut it down. And the administration is proposing to rescind all of the remaining funding for the digital equity program, which Congress appropriated on a bipartisan basis to help veterans, seniors, rural communities, and people with disabilities afford and make full use of high-speed internet. The American economy and hardworking Americans are getting squeezed. Yet, at the same time, Secretary Lutnick's own family has greatly benefited from many of the deals that have been arranged by the Commerce Department over the past year. According to a New York Times investigation in November, never in modern US history has the office of the Secretary of Commerce intersected so broadly and deeply with the financial interests of the Commerce Secretary's own family. Furthermore, the Commerce Department has been plagued by mismanagement over the past year, beginning with the damage done by Elon Musk's DOGE. Last year, Secretary Lutnick, you told this subcommittee that no weather service meteorologists were fired, when in fact, many meteorologists were fired. We learned in a hearing before this subcommittee last month that the department and the National Weather Service still have not backfilled all of the National Weather Service's frontline staff positions that were so needlessly lost last year. The department's responsiveness, or lack thereof, to this committee has also been concerning. More than 10 months after our subcommittee submitted questions for the record following the Secretary's last appearance, we still have received no answers. The department also failed to submit its fiscal year 2026 spend plan in accordance with the legal deadline. All of the spend plans were four or more weeks late, and we only this week received the spend plans for the department's largest bureau, NOAA, and for the minority business development agency. These were legally due to our Appropriations Committee a month and a half ago. The department's fiscal year 2027 budget justification documents were also late in being publicly released. All of these delays are completely unacceptable and obstruct our committee's ability to conduct oversight and to make informed decisions about the federal budget on behalf of the American people. Finally, I was disturbed to learn from disclosures in the Epstein files that Secretary Lutnick had personal and business interactions with Jeffrey Epstein for years after Epstein was registered as a sex offender. Secretary Lutnick previously claimed that he cut ties with Mr. Epstein in 2005, but the Epstein files show the Secretary took a trip to Epstein's private island in 2012. This was four years after Epstein pled guilty to soliciting prostitution from a minor and was labeled a sex offender. The Secretary's dishonesty caused me to question the truthfulness of other statements he has made to Congress on the record. The American people deserve honesty from those with the privilege of holding public office. And I will say once again that the survivors of Jeffrey Epstein's horrific crimes deserve truth, accountability, and justice. I look forward to the Secretary's testimony and to answers to the many questions that my colleagues and I have. Thank you, and I yield back. Thank you, Miss Ming. I would now like to recognize our witness, Secretary Lutnick, for an opening statement. Without objection, your written statement will be entered into the record. Mr. Secretary, we're delighted to have you with us. This is your official congressional oversight subcommittee. We want to be helpful to you in your duties, and we want to know some answers that we all have. So, Mr. Secretary, thank you for being here. The floor is yours. Thank you, Mr. Chairman, and thank you for having me here. Thank you, Representative Ming, and the members of the subcommittee. I appreciate you having me here today, and welcome and welcome. Over the past 15 months, President Trump has proven that the American economy is the strongest and most resilient in the world. When businesses look for the place to expand manufacturing, drive innovation, and hire more workers, they are decisively choosing the United States. Micron, for example, made a $200 billion investment in memory semiconductor manufacturing in Idaho, Upstate New York, and Virginia. TSMC's $165 billion advanced manufacturing investment in America's semiconductor logic sector is another example. Texas Instruments' $60 billion investment for seven US semiconductor fabs across three manufacturing megasites in Texas and Utah are just a few examples of the Trump administration's commitment to building America. Trillions of dollars of capital investment are pouring into America, led by President Trump and the United States Department of Commerce. I could spend our entire time together listing the record-setting commitments company after company and industry after industry have made to build in America. I'm proud of the leadership role that the Department of Commerce has played in providing the support and encouragement these businesses need to revive American manufacturing, and we have only just begun. Over the past few months alone, the Department of Commerce has announced multiple $15 billion plus record-setting critical energy investments across this country, from Pennsylvania and Georgia to Texas and Tennessee. They will accelerate economic growth, ensure US energy dominance, bolster critical supply chains, and strengthen US national and economic security. In addition to the massive historic trade agreements with Korea and Taiwan, the impressive $550 billion trade deal with Japan has announced six projects in the past six months. These include $40 billion for small modular reactors in Tennessee and Alabama, a $33 billion, 9.2 gigawatt natural gas fired facility in Ohio, and a $17 billion natural gas fired power generation hub in Southwest Pennsylvania. There is also $16 billion for natural gas in East Texas. That goes on top of $2 billion for a deep water crude oil export terminal in the Gulf of Mexico and $600 million for a critical high pressure, high temperature synthetic diamond grit facility in Georgia. Just last month, Secretary Wright and I were in Southern Ohio to break ground on that 9.2 gigawatt natural gas facility. This project will develop the largest natural gas facility in history. It will lower electricity costs across the region, create thousands and thousands of American jobs, strengthen our national security, and help the US meet the energy needs of our future. Beyond these historic investments, the Department of Commerce continues to work around the clock to implement President Trump's America First trade policy. Under Section 232, the Commerce Department is taking action on steel, aluminum, copper, pharmaceuticals, autos, and semiconductors, and investigating other sectors to protect, reshore, and revive manufacturing. These actions have led to multibillion dollar commitments to reshore steel and aluminum manufacturing and restore American excellence in critical industries. In the first year of his second term, President Trump has dramatically reduced the trade deficit, lowering imports and increasing exports. Exports went to $3.4 trillion, which is a 6% increase from 2024, and in 2025, the trade deficit was the lowest we've seen in 16 years. I'm proud of the role I played in negotiating our most important trade agreements, including the largest deal ever done, which was our trade deal and investment deal with the European Union. The IAS is working hand-in-hand with the Department of Health and Human Services, and we've arranged historic agreements to lower drug prices for American consumers. We are negotiating most favored nation deals and ensuring Americans pay the lowest prices for innovative medicines. As a result of these deals, additionally, we have secured over $400 billion in commitments to reshore pharmaceuticals and manufacturing in America—those key pharmaceuticals. The budget includes a $215 million increase to BIS above the 2026 enacted level, which supports the hiring of hundreds of new special agent law enforcement officials to address the theft of American technological leadership by bad actors. These officers are critical to protecting and defending America's innovation. In ITA, we are driving record foreign direct investment, cracking down on harmful foreign trade practices, and supporting our 1.3 million American jobs with export and investment deals. In 2025, ITA helped Boeing, Wabtec, and other major American companies secure a record $244 billion in contracts with foreign public buyers. This exceeds the $17 billion that the Biden administration did in its first year. The budget slashes unnecessary, wasteful spending at NIST and other groups, and eliminates the manufacturing extension partnership, which is designed to help small and medium-sized American manufacturers grow, create, and preserve US manufacturing jobs. It also is again proposing to defund the minority business development agency after attempting to illegally shut it down. And the administration is proposing to rescind all of the remaining funding for the digital equity program, which Congress appropriated on a bipartisan basis to help veterans, seniors, rural communities, and people with disabilities afford and make full use of high-speed internet. The American economy and hardworking Americans are getting squeezed. Yet, at the same time, Secretary Lutnick's own family has greatly benefited from many of the deals that have been arranged by the Commerce Department over the past year. According to a New York Times investigation in November, never in modern US history has the office of the Secretary of Commerce intersected so broadly and deeply with the financial interests of the Commerce Secretary's own family. Furthermore, the Commerce Department has been plagued by mismanagement over the past year, beginning with the damage done by Elon Musk's DOGE. Last year, Secretary Lutnick, you told this subcommittee that no weather service meteorologists were fired, when in fact, many meteorologists were fired. We learned in a hearing before this subcommittee last month that the department and the National Weather Service still have not backfilled all of the National Weather Service's frontline staff positions that were so needlessly lost last year. The department's responsiveness, or lack thereof, to this committee has also been concerning. More than 10 months after our subcommittee submitted questions for the record following the Secretary's last appearance, we still have received no answers. The department also failed to submit its fiscal year 2026 spend plan in accordance with the legal deadline. All of the spend plans were four or more weeks late, and we only this week received the spend plans for the department's largest bureau, NOAA, and for the minority business development agency. These were legally due to our Appropriations Committee a month and a half ago. The department's fiscal year 2027 budget justification documents were also late in being publicly released. All of these delays are completely unacceptable and obstruct our committee's ability to conduct oversight and to make informed decisions about the federal budget on behalf of the American people. Finally, I was disturbed to learn from disclosures in the Epstein files that Secretary Lutnick had personal and business interactions with Jeffrey Epstein for years after Epstein was registered as a sex offender. Secretary Lutnick previously claimed that he cut ties with Mr. Epstein in 2005, but the Epstein files show the Secretary took a trip to Epstein's private island in 2012. This was four years after Epstein pled guilty to soliciting prostitution from a minor and was labeled a sex offender. The Secretary's dishonesty caused me to question the truthfulness of other statements he has made to Congress on the record. The American people deserve honesty from those with the privilege of holding public office. And I will say once again that the survivors of Jeffrey Epstein's horrific crimes deserve truth, accountability, and justice. I look forward to the Secretary's testimony and to answers to the many questions that my colleagues and I have. Thank you, and I yield back. Thank you, Miss Ming. Mr. Secretary, we're delighted to have you with us. This is your official congressional oversight subcommittee. The Secretary's dishonesty caused me to question the truthfulness of other statements he has made to Congress on the record. The American people deserve honesty from those with the privilege of holding public office. And I will say once again that the survivors of Jeffrey Epstein's horrific crimes deserve truth, accountability, and justice. I look forward to the Secretary's testimony and to answers to the many questions that my colleagues and I have. Thank you, and I yield back. Thank you, Ms. Ming. I would now like to recognize our witness, Secretary Lutnick, for an opening statement. Without objection, your written statement will be entered into the record. Mr. Secretary, we're delighted to have you with us. This is your official congressional oversight subcommittee. We want to be helpful to you in your duties, and we want to know some answers that we all have. So, Mr. Secretary, thank you for being here; the floor is yours. Thank you, Mr. Chairman, and thank you for having me here. Thank you, Representative Ming, and the members of the subcommittee. I appreciate you having me here today, and welcome and welcome. Over the past 15 months, President Trump has proven that the American economy is the strongest and most resilient in the world. When businesses look for the place to expand manufacturing, they drive innovation and they hire more workers. They are decisively choosing the United States. Micron, for example, $200 billion investment in memory semiconductor manufacturing in Idaho, Upstate New York, and Virginia. TSMC's $165 billion advanced manufacturing investment in America's semiconductor logic sector. Texas Instruments' $60 billion investment for seven US semiconductor fabs across three manufacturing megasites in Texas and Utah are just a few examples of the Trump administration's commitment to building America. Trillions of dollars of capital investment are pouring into America led by President Trump and the United States Department of Commerce. I could spend our entire time together listing the record-setting commitments company after company and industry after industry have made to build in America. I'm proud of the leadership role that the Department of Commerce has played in providing the support and encouragement these businesses need to revive American manufacturing, and we have only just begun. Over the past few months alone, the Department of Commerce has announced multiple $15 billion plus record-setting critical energy investments across this country, from Pennsylvania and Georgia to Texas and Tennessee. They will accelerate economic growth, ensure US energy dominance, bolster critical supply chains, and strengthen US national and economic security. In addition to the massive historic trade agreements with Korea and Taiwan, the impressive $550 billion trade deal with Japan has announced six projects in the past six months. These include $40 billion for small modular reactors in Tennessee and Alabama, a $33 billion, 9.2 gigawatt natural gas fired facility in Ohio, a $17 billion natural gas fired power generation hub in Southwest Pennsylvania, and $16 billion for natural gas in East Texas. That goes on top of $2 billion for a deep water crude oil export terminal in the Gulf of Mexico and $600 million for a critical high pressure, high temperature synthetic diamond grit facility in Georgia. Just last month, Secretary Wright and I were in Southern Ohio to break ground on that 9.2 gigawatt natural gas facility. This project will develop the largest natural gas facility in history. It will lower electricity costs across the region, create thousands and thousands of American jobs, strengthen our national security, and help the US meet the energy needs of our future. Beyond these historic investments, the Department of Commerce continues to work around the clock to implement President Trump's America First trade policy. Under section 232, the Commerce Department is taking action on steel, aluminum, copper, pharmaceuticals, autos, and semiconductors, and investigating other sectors to protect, reshore, and revive manufacturing. These actions have led to multibillion-dollar commitments to reshore steel and aluminum manufacturing and restore American excellence in critical industries. In the first year of his second term, President Trump has dramatically reduced the trade deficit, lowering imports and increasing exports. Exports went to $3.4 trillion, which is a 6% increase from 2024, and in 2025, the trade deficit was the lowest we've seen in 16 years. I'm proud of the role I played in negotiating our most important trade agreements, including the largest deal ever done, which was our trade deal and investment deal with the European Union. The ITA is working hand-in-hand with the Department of Health and Human Services, and we've arranged historic agreements to lower drug prices for American consumers, negotiating most favored nation deals and ensuring Americans pay the lowest prices for innovative medicines. As a result of these deals, additionally, we have secured over $400 billion in commitments to reshore pharmaceuticals and manufacturing in America—those key pharmaceuticals. The budget includes a $215 million increase to BIS above the 2026 enacted level, which supports the hiring of hundreds of new special agent law enforcement officials to address the theft of American technological leadership by bad actors. These officers are critical to protecting and defending America's innovation. In ITA, we are driving record foreign direct investment, cracking down on harmful foreign trade practices, and supporting our 1.3 million American jobs with export and investment deals. In 2025, ITA helped Boeing, Wabtec, and other major American companies secure a record $244 billion in contracts with foreign public buyers. This exceeds the $17 billion that the Biden administration did in its first year. The budget slashes unnecessary wasteful spending at NIST and other groups and eliminates the Manufacturing Extension Partnership, which is designed to help small and medium-sized American manufacturers grow, create, and preserve US manufacturing jobs. It also is again proposing to defund the Minority Business Development Agency after attempting to illegally shut it down. The administration is proposing to rescind all of the remaining funding for the digital equity program, which Congress appropriated on a bipartisan basis to help veterans, seniors, rural communities, and people with disabilities afford and make full use of high-speed internet. The American economy and hardworking Americans are getting squeezed. Yet, at the same time, Secretary Lutnick's own family has greatly benefited from many of the deals that have been arranged by the Commerce Department over the past year. According to a New York Times investigation in November, never in modern US history has the office of the Secretary of Commerce intersected so broadly and deeply with the financial interests of the Commerce Secretary's own family. Furthermore, the Commerce Department has been plagued by mismanagement over the past year, beginning with the damage done by Elon Musk's DOGE. Last year, Secretary Lutnick, you told this subcommittee that no Weather Service meteorologists were fired, when in fact, many meteorologists were fired. We learned in a hearing before this subcommittee last month that the department at the National Weather Service still has not backfilled all of the National Weather Service's frontline staff positions that were so needlessly lost last year. The department's responsiveness, or lack thereof, to this committee has also been concerning. More than 10 months after our subcommittee submitted questions for the record following the Secretary's last appearance, we still have received no answers. The department also failed to submit its fiscal year 26 spend plan in accordance with the legal deadline, as all of the spend plans were four or more weeks late. We only this week received the spend plans for the department's largest bureau, NOAA, and for the Minority Business Development Agency, which were legally due to our Appropriations Committee a month and a half ago. The department's fiscal year 27 budget justification documents were also late in being publicly released. All of these delays are completely unacceptable and obstruct our committee's ability to conduct oversight and to make informed decisions about the federal budget on behalf of the American people. Finally, I was disturbed to learn from disclosures in the Epstein files that Secretary Lutnick had personal and business interactions with Jeffrey Epstein for years after Epstein was registered as a sex offender. Secretary Lutnick's previous claim was that he cut ties with Mr. Epstein in '05, but the Epstein files show the Secretary took a trip to Epstein's private island in 2012, four years after Epstein pled guilty to soliciting prostitution from a minor and was labeled a sex offender. The Secretary's dishonesty caused me to question the truthfulness of other statements he has made to Congress on the record. The American people deserve honesty from those with the privilege of holding public office. And I will say once again that the survivors of Jeffrey Epstein's horrific crimes deserve truth, accountability, and justice. I look forward to the Secretary's testimony and to answers to the many questions that my colleagues and I have. Thank you, and I yield back. Thank you, Ms. Ming. I would now like to recognize our witness, Secretary Lutnick, for an opening statement. Without objection, your written statement will be entered into the record. Mr. Secretary, we're delighted to have you with us. This is your official congressional oversight subcommittee. We want to be helpful to you in your duties, and we want to know some answers that we all have. So, Mr. Secretary, thank you for being here; the floor is yours. Thank you, Mr. Chairman, and thank you for having me here. Thank you, Representative Ming, and the members of the subcommittee. I appreciate you having me here today, and welcome and welcome. That goes on top of $2 billion for a deep water crude oil export terminal in the Gulf of Mexico and $600 million for a critical high pressure, high temperature synthetic diamond grit facility in Georgia. Secretary Lutnick's previous claim was that he cut ties with Mr. Epstein in '05, but the Epstein files show the Secretary took a trip to Epstein's private island in 2012, four years after Epstein pled guilty to soliciting prostitution from a minor and was labeled a sex offender. And I will say once again that the survivors of Jeffrey Epstein's horrific crimes deserve truth, accountability, and justice. Exports went to $3.4 trillion, which is a 6% increase from 2024, and in 2025, the trade deficit was the lowest we've seen in 16 years. I'm proud of the role I played in negotiating our most important trade agreement, including the largest deal ever done, which was our trade deal and investment deal with the European Union. The ITA is working hand-in-hand with the Department of Health and Human Services, and we've arranged historic agreements to lower drug prices for American consumers, negotiating most favored nation deals and ensuring Americans pay the lowest prices for innovative medicines. As a result of these deals, additionally, we have secured over $400 billion in commitments to reshore pharmaceuticals and manufacturing in America—those key pharmaceuticals. The budget includes a $215 million increase to BIS above the 2026 enacted level, which supports the hiring of hundreds of new special agent law enforcement officials to address the theft of American technological leadership by bad actors. These officers are critical to protecting and defending America's innovation. In ITA, we are driving record foreign direct investment, cracking down on harmful foreign trade practices and supporting our 1.3 million American jobs with export and investment deals. In 2025, ITA helped Boeing, Wabtec and other major American companies secure a record $244 billion in contracts with foreign public buyers. This exceeds the $17 billion that the Biden administration did in its first year. The budget slashes unnecessary wasteful spending at NIST and other groups, and eliminates the manufacturing extension partnership, which is designed to help small and medium-sized American manufacturers grow, create and preserve US manufacturing jobs. It also is again proposing to defund the minority business development agency, after attempting to illegally shut it down. And the administration is proposing to rescind all of the remaining funding for the digital equity program, which Congress appropriated on a bipartisan basis to help veterans, seniors, rural communities and people with disabilities afford and make full use of high-speed internet. The American economy and hardworking Americans are getting squeezed. Yet, at the same time, Secretary Lutnick's own family has greatly benefited from many of the deals that have been arranged by the Commerce Department over the past year. According to a New York Times investigation in November, never in modern US history has the office of the Secretary of Commerce intersected so broadly and deeply with the financial interests of the Commerce Secretary's own family. Furthermore, the Commerce Department has been plagued by mismanagement over the past year, beginning with the damage done by Elon Musk's DOGE. Last year, Secretary Lutnick, you told this subcommittee that no weather service meteorologists were fired, when in fact, many meteorologists were fired. We learned in a hearing before this subcommittee last month that the department and the National Weather Service still have not backfilled all of the National Weather Service's frontline staff positions that were so needlessly lost last year. The department's responsiveness, or lack thereof, to this committee has also been concerning. More than 10 months after our subcommittee submitted questions for the record following the Secretary's last appearance, we still have received no answers. The department also failed to submit its fiscal year 26 spend plan in accordance with the legal deadline, as all of the spend plans were four or more weeks late. We only this week received the spend plans for the department's largest bureau, NOAA, and for the minority business development agency, which were legally due to our Appropriations Committee a month and a half ago. The department's fiscal year 27 budget justification documents were also late in being publicly released. All of these delays are completely unacceptable and obstruct our committee's ability to conduct oversight and to make informed decisions about the federal budget on behalf of the American people. Finally, I was disturbed to learn from disclosures in the Epstein files that Secretary Lutnick had personal and business interactions with Jeffrey Epstein for years after Epstein was registered as a sex offender. Secretary Lutnick's previous claim was that he cut ties with Mr. Epstein in '05, but the Epstein files show the Secretary took a trip to Epstein's private island in 2012, four years after Epstein pled guilty to soliciting prostitution from a minor and was labeled a sex offender. The Secretary's dishonesty caused me to question the truthfulness of other statements he has made to Congress on the record. The American people deserve honesty from those with the privilege of holding public office. And I will say once again that the survivors of Jeffrey Epstein's horrific crimes deserve truth, accountability, and justice. I look forward to the Secretary's testimony and to answers to the many questions that my colleagues and I have. Thank you, and I yield back. Thank you, Miss Ming. I would now like to recognize our witness, Secretary Lutnick, for an opening statement. Without objection, your written statement will be entered into the record. Mr. Secretary, we're delighted to have you with us. This is your official congressional oversight subcommittee. We want to be helpful to you in your duties, and we want to know some answers that we all have. So, Mr. Secretary, thank you for being here. The floor is yours. Thank you, Mr. Chairman, and thank you for having me here. Thank you, Representative Ming, and the members of the subcommittee. I appreciate you having me here today, and welcome and welcome. Over the past 15 months, President Trump has proven that the American economy is the strongest and most resilient in the world. When businesses look for the place to expand manufacturing, they drive innovation and they hire more workers; they are decisively choosing the United States. Micron, for example, has a $200 billion investment in memory semiconductor manufacturing in Idaho, Upstate New York, and Virginia. TSMC's $165 billion advanced manufacturing investment is in America's semiconductor logic sector. Texas Instruments' $60 billion investment for seven US semiconductor fabs across three manufacturing megasites in Texas and Utah are just a few examples of the Trump administration's commitment to building America. Trillions of dollars of capital investment are pouring into America led by President Trump and the United States Department of Commerce. I could spend our entire time together listing the record-setting commitments company after company and industry after industry have made to build in America. I'm proud of the leadership role that the Department of Commerce has played in providing the support and encouragement these businesses need to revive American manufacturing, and we have only just begun. Over the past few months alone, the Department of Commerce has announced multiple $15 billion plus record-setting critical energy investments across this country, from Pennsylvania and Georgia to Texas and Tennessee. They will accelerate economic growth, ensure US energy dominance, bolster critical supply chains, and strengthen US national and economic security. In addition to the massive historic trade agreements with Korea and Taiwan, the impressive $550 billion trade deal with Japan has announced six projects in the past six months. These include $40 billion for small modular reactors in Tennessee and Alabama, a $33 billion, 9.2 gigawatt natural gas fired facility in Ohio, a $17 billion natural gas fired power generation hub in Southwest Pennsylvania, and $16 billion for natural gas in East Texas. That goes on top of $2 billion for a deep water crude oil export terminal in the Gulf of Mexico and $600 million for a critical high pressure, high temperature synthetic diamond grit facility in Georgia. Just last month, Secretary Wright and I were in Southern Ohio to break ground on that 9.2 gigawatt natural gas facility. This project will develop the largest natural gas facility in history. It will lower electricity costs across the region, create thousands and thousands of American jobs, strengthen our national security, and help the US meet the energy needs of our future. Beyond these historic investments, the Department of Commerce continues to work around the clock to implement President Trump's America First trade policy. Under section 232, the Commerce Department is taking action on steel, aluminum, copper, pharmaceuticals, autos, semiconductors, and investigating other sectors to protect, reshore, and revive manufacturing. These actions have led to multibillion dollar commitments to reshore steel and aluminum manufacturing and restore American excellence in critical industries. In the first year of his second term, President Trump has dramatically reduced the trade deficit, lowering imports and increasing exports. Exports went to $3.4 trillion, which is a 6% increase from 2024, and in 2025, the trade deficit was the lowest we've seen in 16 years. I'm proud of the role I played in negotiating our most important trade agreement, including the largest deal ever done, which was our trade deal and investment deal with the European Union. The ITA is working hand-in-hand with the Department of Health and Human Services, and we've arranged historic agreements to lower drug prices for American consumers, negotiating most favored nation deals and ensuring Americans pay the lowest prices for innovative medicines. As a result of these deals, additionally, we have secured over $400 billion in commitments to reshore pharmaceuticals and manufacturing in America—those key pharmaceuticals. The budget includes a $215 million increase to BIS above the 2026 enacted level, which supports the hiring of hundreds of new special agent law enforcement officials to address the theft of American technological leadership by bad actors. These officers are critical to protecting and defending America's innovation. In ITA, we are driving record foreign direct investment, cracking down on harmful foreign trade practices and supporting our 1.3 million American jobs with export and investment deals. In 2025, ITA helped Boeing, Wabtec and other major American companies secure a record $244 billion in contracts with foreign public buyers. This exceeds the $17 billion that the Biden administration did in its first year. The budget slashes unnecessary wasteful spending at NIST and other groups, and eliminates the manufacturing extension partnership, which is designed to help small and medium-sized American manufacturers grow, create and preserve US manufacturing jobs. It also is again proposing to defund the minority business development agency, after attempting to illegally shut it down. And the administration is proposing to rescind all of the remaining funding for the digital equity program, which Congress appropriated on a bipartisan basis to help veterans, seniors, rural communities and people with disabilities afford and make full use of high-speed internet. The American economy and hardworking Americans are getting squeezed. Yet, at the same time, Secretary Lutnick's own family has greatly benefited from many of the deals that have been arranged by the Commerce Department over the past year. According to a New York Times investigation in November, never in modern US history has the office of the Secretary of Commerce intersected so broadly and deeply with the financial interests of the Commerce Secretary's own family. Furthermore, the Commerce Department has been plagued by mismanagement over the past year, beginning with the damage done by Elon Musk's DOGE. Last year, Secretary Lutnick, you told this subcommittee that no weather service meteorologists were fired, when in fact, many meteorologists were fired. We learned in a hearing before this subcommittee last month that the department and the National Weather Service still have not backfilled all of the National Weather Service's frontline staff positions that were so needlessly lost last year. The department's responsiveness, or lack thereof, to this committee has also been concerning. More than 10 months after our subcommittee submitted questions for the record following the Secretary's last appearance, we still have received no answers. The department also failed to submit its fiscal year 26 spend plan in accordance with the legal deadline, as all of the spend plans were four or more weeks late. We only this week received the spend plans for the department's largest bureau, NOAA, and for the minority business development agency, which were legally due to our Appropriations Committee a month and a half ago. The department's fiscal year 27 budget justification documents were also late in being publicly released. All of these delays are completely unacceptable and obstruct our committee's ability to conduct oversight and to make informed decisions about the federal budget on behalf of the American people. Finally, I was disturbed to learn from disclosures in the Epstein files that Secretary Lutnick had personal and business interactions with Jeffrey Epstein for years after Epstein was registered as a sex offender. Secretary Lutnick's previous claim was that he cut ties with Mr. Epstein in '05, but the Epstein files show the Secretary took a trip to Epstein's private island in 2012, four years after Epstein pled guilty to soliciting prostitution from a minor and was labeled a sex offender. The Secretary's dishonesty caused me to question the truthfulness of other statements he has made to Congress on the record. The American people deserve honesty from those with the privilege of holding public office. And I will say once again that the survivors of Jeffrey Epstein's horrific crimes deserve truth, accountability, and justice. I look forward to the Secretary's testimony and to answers to the many questions that my colleagues and I have. Thank you, and I yield back. Thank you, Miss Ming. Mr. Secretary, we're delighted to have you with us. This is your official congressional oversight subcommittee.
