LIVE: Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick testifies on Capitol Hill

MS NOW(9.8M) ·104:51 ·36.3K views · 2026-04-23
Howard Lutnick (US Commerce Secretary)
> "These agents bring between three and 10 times their revenue annually in fines...
TL;DR: Secretary Howard Lutnick defended a "record-setting" influx of manufacturing and energy investments under the Trump administration while justifying significant budget cuts to "broken" legacy programs like the EDA and MBDA. The hearing was marked by sharp partisan divides...

TL;DR: Secretary Howard Lutnick defended a "record-setting" influx of manufacturing and energy investments under the Trump administration while justifying significant budget cuts to "broken" legacy programs like the EDA and MBDA. The hearing was marked by sharp partisan divides over manufacturing job losses, the impact of tariffs, and the Secretary’s past associations with Jeffrey Epstein.

Industrial Growth and National Security

  • Semiconductor Surge: Lutnick highlighted massive domestic investments, including a $200 billion commitment from Micron (ID, NY, VA), $165 billion from TSMC (AZ), and $60 billion from Texas Instruments (TX, UT).
      • He projected that total semiconductor manufacturing investments would reach $1 trillion during the current administration.
  • Bureau of Industry and Security (BIS) Expansion: The budget requests a $215 million increase to nearly double the size of the BIS law enforcement wing.
      • ROI Argument: Lutnick claimed special agents are "intellectual law enforcement" who pay for themselves, generating 3x to 10x their cost in revenue from fines against companies violating export controls.
      • China Countermeasures: The funding will support hundreds of new agents to prevent the theft of high-end chips and software by "countries of concern."
  • Energy Dominance: Lutnick emphasized $15 billion+ investments in critical energy sectors across PA, GA, TX, and TN, including the groundbreaking of a record-setting 9.2-gigawatt natural gas facility in southern Ohio.

Budget Efficiency and Program Eliminations

  • Broadband Savings (BEAD): Lutnick claimed the Department saved $21 billion of the $42 billion allocated for broadband by removing "fiber-only" mandates and allowing competition from satellite and fixed wireless providers.
  • Targeted Eliminations: The budget proposes zeroing out the Economic Development Administration (EDA), the Minority Business Development Agency (MBDA), and the Manufacturing Extension Partnership (MEP).
      • Justification: Lutnick cited "brutal" Inspector General reports, claiming 92% of MBDA awards were flawed and that MEP had suffered from decades of mission creep and overpayment.
  • NOAA Modernization: A requested $135 million increase will support shipbuilding and unmanned systems. The agency is also shifting its IT to a cloud-based system and beginning to purchase weather data from commercial satellites to improve forecasting.

Trade Policy and Economic Friction

  • Manufacturing Job Debate: Rep. Rosa DeLauro (D-CT) challenged the "manufacturing renaissance" narrative, citing BLS data showing a loss of 88,000 manufacturing jobs (100 per day) between early 2025 and 2026.
      • Lutnick countered that while factory production takes years to come online, the administration has created tens of thousands of construction jobs for these facilities.
  • Tariff Uncertainty: Rep. Joseph Morelle (D-NY) noted that business leaders are in a state of "constant uncertainty" due to fluctuating tariff rates, citing an optics company whose $10 million equipment purchase was stalled by a $1 million tariff.
  • Tariff Refunds: Rep. Glenn Ivey (D-MD) pressed Lutnick on the Supreme Court ruling declaring certain tariffs illegal. Lutnick committed to looking into a mechanism to ensure small businesses and consumers—not just large importers—receive refunds.
  • Gold Card Visa: Lutnick confirmed the launch of a new "Gold Card" program where individuals paying $1 million into a Commerce gift account can receive residency, subject to a $15,000 vetting process. One applicant has been approved to date.

Oversight and Ethical Challenges

  • Epstein Inquiries: Representatives Grace Meng (D-NY) and Madeleine Dean (D-PA) questioned Lutnick regarding his previous denials of contact with Jeffrey Epstein after 2005.
      • Evidence was cited regarding a 2012 visit to Epstein’s island and business communications through 2018.
      • Lutnick declined to answer specifics, stating he had voluntarily agreed to a separate session with the House Oversight Committee in two weeks to address the topic.
  • Conflict of Interest Allegations: Rep. Dean raised allegations that Lutnick’s family members were profiting from tariff refund rights. Lutnick vehemently denied these reports, calling them "false."
"These agents bring between three and 10 times their revenue annually in fines... they are the intellectual law enforcement agents of the U.S. government." — Secretary Howard Lutnick
"U.S. manufacturing lost more than 100 jobs per day from February 2025 to February 2026. That's more than 88,000 jobs in total. It's not a trade policy that delivers for working people." — Rep. Rosa DeLauro
"Congress had allocated $42 billion [for BEAD]... they were only going to give it to fiber... which made no sense. We rebid it without any technological limitations... and executed the plan for $21 billion." — Secretary Howard Lutnick
"The survivors of Epstein and his associates' horrific crimes deserve so much better from this government... our American public deserves honesty and accountability." — Rep. Grace Meng
Welcome as our witness, Secretary Lutnick, for an opening statement. Without objection, your written statement will be entered into the record. Now Mr. Secretary, we're delighted to have you with us. This is your official congressional oversight subcommittee. We want to be helpful to you in your duties, and we want to know some answers that we all have. So, Mr. Secretary, thank you for being here. The floor is yours. Thank you, Mr. Chairman. And thank you for having me here. Thank you, Representative Meng, and the members of the subcommittee. I appreciate you having me here today. And welcome. And welcome. Over the past 15 months, President Trump has proven that the American economy is the strongest and most resilient in the world. When businesses look for the place to expand manufacturing, they drive innovation and they hire more workers, and they are decisively choosing the United States. Micron, for example, has a $200 billion investment in memory semiconductor manufacturing in Idaho, upstate New York, and Virginia. There is TSMC's $165 billion advanced manufacturing investment in America's semiconductor logic sector. Texas Instruments has a $60 billion investment for seven U.S. semiconductor fabs across three manufacturing mega sites. Texas and Utah are just a few examples of the Trump administration's commitment to driving semiconductor investment and bringing jobs back to the United States. I could spend our entire time together listing the record-setting commitments company after company and industry after industry have made to build in America. Trillions of dollars of capital investment are pouring into America, led by President Trump and the United States Department of Commerce. I'm proud of the leadership role the Department of Commerce has played in providing the support and encouragement these businesses need to revive American manufacturing, and we have only just begun. Over the past few months alone, the Department of Commerce has announced multiple $15 billion plus record-setting critical energy investments across this country. From Pennsylvania and Georgia to Texas and Tennessee, they will accelerate economic growth, ensure U.S. energy dominance, bolster critical supply chains, and strengthen U.S. national and economic security. In addition to the massive historic trade agreements with Korea and Taiwan, the impressive $550 billion trade deal with Japan has announced six projects in the past six months. These include $40 billion for small modular reactors in Tennessee and Alabama, a $33 billion 9.2-gigawatt natural gas-fired facility in Ohio, and a $17 billion natural gas-fired power generation hub in southwest Pennsylvania. There is also $16 billion for natural gas in east Texas. That goes on top of $2 billion for a deep-water crude oil export terminal in the Gulf of America and $600 million for a critical high-pressure high-temperature synthetic diamond grit facility in Georgia. Just last month, Secretary Wright and I were in southern Ohio to break ground on that 9.2-gigawatt natural gas facility. This project will develop the largest natural gas facility in history. It'll lower electricity costs across the region, create thousands and thousands of American jobs, strengthen our national security, and help the U.S. meet the energy needs of our future. Beyond these historic investments, the Department of Commerce continues to work around the clock to implement President Trump's America First trade policy. Under Section 232, the Commerce Department has taken action on steel, aluminum, copper, pharmaceuticals, autos, and semiconductors, and is investigating other sectors to protect, reshore, and revive manufacturing. These actions have led to multi-billion dollar commitments to reshore steel and aluminum manufacturing and restore American excellence in critical industries. In the first year of his second term, President Trump has dramatically reduced the trade deficit, lowering imports and increasing exports. Exports went to $3.4 trillion, which is a 6% increase from 2024, and in 2025, the trade deficit was the lowest we've seen in 16 years. I'm proud of the role I played in negotiating our most important trade agreements, including the largest deal ever done, which was our trade and investment deal with the European Union. BIS is working hand-in-hand with the Department of Health and Human Services, and we've arranged historic agreements to lower drug prices for American consumers. We are negotiating most favored nation deals, and in turn, Americans pay the lowest prices for innovative medicines. As a result of these deals, additionally, we have secured over $400 billion in commitments to reshore pharmaceuticals and manufacturing in America for those key pharmaceuticals. The budget includes a $215 million increase to BIS above the 2026 enacted level, which supports the hiring of hundreds of new special agent law enforcement officials to address the theft of American technological leadership by bad actors. These officers are critical to protecting and defending America's innovation. In ITA, we are driving record foreign direct investment, cracking down on harmful foreign trade practices, and supporting our 1.3 million American jobs with export and investment deals. In 2025, ITA helped Boeing, Wabtec, and other major American companies secure a record $244 billion in contracts with foreign public buyers. This exceeds the $17 billion that the Biden administration did in its first year. The budget includes a $10 million increase to the 2026 enacted level for trade enforcement in ITA and $100 million for the United States Investment Accelerator. The budget slashes unnecessary wasteful spending in NIST and other groups and eliminates the manufacturing extension partnership, which I'm sure we will talk about. At the USPTO, we are rapidly modernizing and leveraging technology to cut classification prep dramatically. We want to get rid of the massive backlog that we had and do a much better job. And finally, we have launched a new era of modernization of NOAA, where we are in the process of transferring the agency's IT system from a decades-old hard-wired technology to the modern distributed cloud. This update will improve our forecasting capabilities, transform the way our forecasters do their jobs, and will make Americans safer. The budget also includes a $135 million increase to the 2026 enacted level for NOAA shipbuilding and our improvement of unmanned systems programs. For far too long, our nation was mired in the status quo, leaving American consumers and producers alike with unfair, poorly implemented trade deals, outsourced jobs, and paltry investments that failed to meet our country's potential. President Trump is committed to ushering in a golden age of domestic commerce and bringing jobs back to the United States, and that's exactly what he's done in his first 15 months of this administration. I look forward to working with you as the department continues to spur economic growth and secure unprecedented prosperity for the American people. Thank you, Mr. Chairman, for having me here today. Thank you, Mr. Secretary. We will now proceed under the five-minute rule for questions. I will begin by recognizing myself. Mr. Secretary, the request for the Bureau of Industry and Security, which is instrumental to the nation's export control system, seeks to nearly double the size of the Bureau's annual appropriation. How would this historic investment help counter China? Thank you, Mr. Chairman. BIS is the frontline in our key defense from China trying to get our best chips or our best technology, which we have export controls on and we don't allow them to get. But if we don't have the law enforcement officials and we can't stop them, then they'll find their way around our rules. And so we want to significantly grow our law enforcement special agents at BIS. Their returns are remarkable. I know on the budget we talk about expense, but these agents bring between three and 10 times their revenue annually in fines. Not only do they stop these companies, but then we fine these companies and make way, way more revenue for the United States of America. And, but that budget is two sides, right? The revenue is not part of my budget, just the expense. But I want you to know these agents are massively economically and intellectually important to the United States. On this topic, how is the department using its export control authority to prevent sensitive U.S. technologies from ending up in the hands of our adversaries? We use at BIS our export control to make sure that the countries of concern and countries that are our adversaries cannot get our best technology. We don't want them to get our best chips, we don't want them to get our best software, and we want to make sure that we, who are the innovator in the world, remain far in the lead. So this group is the frontline that holds those back, and we continuously discuss across this administration each and every product. We work together as an administration to make sure that those countries are not getting our best technology and innovations. How is BIS partnering with other law enforcement agencies? We work extremely well with Customs and Border Patrol, right? That is a key place. DHS is very close with us. The FBI is very close with us. The DOJ, we just had a huge criminal action where a large company was trying to ship billions of dollars of our best chips to China and they were arrested together with DOJ. So we work very closely with law enforcement. These are the intellectual law enforcement agents of the U.S. government. They're in BIS, they have badges and guns, but they work closely with other law enforcement to make sure those who are trying to steal our best can't get away with it. Let me talk with you briefly about the important work of EDA in rural communities like mine. The assistance to coal communities supports areas negatively impacted by changes in the coal economy. I've seen the devastation that a local economic downturn can cause to a small community and the positive impact that economic development programs have in the aftermath to support recovery and foster economic growth. Sir. I think the coal program can be and is very important, and we can help these communities. You know, the Trump administration just announced a deal in Pennsylvania to open two very large coal mines. We are very pro-clean, beautiful coal, as you know. The EDA has significant appropriations, and I will make sure to get that money out the door efficiently, effectively, and with deep consideration. If you appropriate it, I will make sure and our department will make sure we do it as smart and efficiently as possible. Prior administrations sat on these. That is not this department. This department will have the appropriated money that you have given us for EDA smartly, efficiently, and effectively out the door by the end of this year. NTIA, under your leadership, was able to refocus the Broadband Equity, Access, and Deployment program on maximizing taxpayer value. Kentucky was allocated $1.1 billion under the program and had plans approved for about $400 million to expand broadband access. When will NTIA provide Kentucky guidance on eligible uses for the remaining $700 million? For the BEAD program, Congress had allocated $42 billion when I walked in the door. And you had a set of rules on who to cover. All each state and their teams were amazing. The working with the states was amazing, and working with Kentucky was very impressive; your team did a great job. What happened was they were only going to give it to fiber and spend all $42 billion in a one-size-fits-all model, which made no sense. You had some people with a $150,000 fiber run to a house that was worth $100,000. It was crazy town. So we rebid it without any technological limitations. If it's satellite, if it's fixed wireless, if it's fiber, whatever's best. And we executed the plan for $21 billion exactly according to the statute. That is half the money. So we now have a high-class problem. We have executed what you wanted to do exactly right for $21 billion, and we have $21 billion remaining. So here's what we did. We went on a listening tour, and we had over a thousand people give us hundreds and hundreds of comments. We're sorting through those hundreds of comments and we're going to come out with a plan for the high-class problem of having absolutely executed the model that you gave us to go out and now do new and exciting things with that money in the model of the statute that you've given us. Thank you, sir. Ms. Meng. Thank you, Mr. Chairman. And first, before I begin my question, I just want to say that it's great to hear that you, Mr. Secretary, and the Trump administration is continuing the great investments that were made into our domestic chips manufacturing started by the Biden administration, Biden-Harris administration. Whether it's TSMC, Micron, or Texas Instruments, billions were invested during the Biden administration, and I was grateful as a New Yorker to see the investments in Micron in my home and your home state of New York. And we were so thrilled to welcome President Joe Biden to announce them during his administration. But I do want to start, Mr. Secretary, I mentioned in my opening statement that we had learned in recent months that contrary to your earlier statement in which you suggested that you had cut off all contact with Epstein in '05, you and your family had in fact had lunch with Mr. Epstein on a boat during a trip to his private island in 2012 and exchanged business emails with him as recently as 2018. This was years after his 2008 conviction for soliciting prostitution from a minor. I just wanted to ask, why did you publicly claim following your nomination that you were never in a room with him after 2005? So I have voluntarily agreed in less than two weeks to sit with your colleagues in the House and answer any and all questions on this topic. I have nothing to hide and I am glad to answer their questions. Whatever questions you have on this topic I will be glad to answer. It's in less than two weeks and we've set aside the time to do that, and I will answer all the questions. So whatever questions you have, if you would give them to the colleagues, I will answer them. But today I am here to testify about the budget. I've set aside voluntarily the time to answer those questions in less than two weeks. So today I am here to testify about the budget. No, I appreciate that, and we'll definitely make sure that that message and offer is emphasized. When Senator Van Hollen in February asked if you would be willing to share any records that you have, you told him that you would surely talk about that, you had nothing to hide. What, if anything, have you done since that hearing to ensure that you are sharing all of your records? I've set aside in less than two weeks the time that the House, your House colleagues, want to have me answer these questions. I am here to testify about the budget, but I have set aside voluntarily the time to answer these questions and, as you correctly have said, I have nothing to hide. Okay. And just, you know, why I'm asking is because our American public deserves honesty and accountability from those of us who have the privilege of holding public office. This is a budget hearing but it's also about our American taxpayers' money. The survivors of Epstein and his associates' horrific crimes deserve so much better from this government. I do want to use the rest of my time to ask about the gold card visa program. President Trump directed you, in coordination with the Secretary of State and Homeland Security, to establish a gold card visa program. As you know, people who are able to pay at least $1 million into the commerce's gift account are eligible for permanent legal residence and potentially citizenship. I wanted to ask how many of these applications have been approved and how many gifts have been received by commerce? The process was recently resolved with DHS, who runs the program, and they do a $15,000 vetting, which is the most serious vetting and analysis of any potential applicant in the history of government. Usually it was $600. These people pay $15,000 for an extraordinary vet. So they have approved recently one person and there are hundreds in the queue that they are going through the process, but this is a new program and they've just set it up and they wanted to make sure they did it perfectly, and so we've worked through that. But it's a DHS program done with a rigorous, rigorous vetting. Okay, and do you know how the department would intend to spend the billions of dollars in the gift account? That will be determined by the administration and its terms are for the betterment of the United States of America. It needs to be for commerce and the betterment of the United States of America. Okay. Thank you, Mr. Chairman. I yield back. Thank you, Ms. Meng. Mr. Alford. Well thank you, Mr. Chairman, thank you Ranking Member Meng and our Ranking Member of our full committee, good to see you. And thank you, Secretary, for being here today. You know, we depend on the Department of Commerce to play a central role in supporting American competitiveness through weather forecasting, standard development, trade enforcement, and economic development in communities all over our great nation. The fiscal year '27 proposal reflects the administration's effort to streamline operations, to prioritize core missions, and to make sure that taxpayer resources are focused where they can have the greatest impact. With proposed budget adjustments across NOAA, NIST, EDA, and ITA, I look forward to hearing how the department will continue delivering strong, reliable services while advancing productivity and modernization. I want to talk a little bit about weather. I think last time you were here we had a very robust conversation about NOAA. I'm glad to say that our operation in Pleasant Hill, Missouri is still operating with humans, and thank you for that. We have this tornado season in Missouri and we had a EF-1 hit not too far from my home last week in Belton, Missouri that did minimal damage. The next few days could become an outbreak for tornadoes in the Kansas City area. Sunday and Monday is not looking good. The FY '27 budget proposal includes $70 million in procurement funding for the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration, including investments in next-generation weather satellites. How will these planned upgrades translate into benefits for my constituents? The National Weather Service had been sort of had walls around itself, right? And we had satellites, we used the data we had from satellites, we'd launch balloons, we used the data that we captured from balloons, and we have our own tracking aircraft. We are now going to buy data; that $70 million allows us to buy data available on commercial satellites. So, we take in more data. We give up none, but add data, right? We have unmanned aircraft that can go into the hurricane and stay there without the risk of loss of life, right? Think of how much better that is; they can live inside the hurricane. And you know how horrifically scary tornadoes are, and the ability to warn early on tornadoes is absolutely difficult. But I, you know, the people at the National Weather Service have been the most impressive. They are the most dedicated, most loyal, and they work their tails off. And you see when it becomes risky, you've never seen people who pour in to try to help, and that's why we need to have an automated model so that the people in the next town, right? Those forecasters can get the same data and assist and we can have the whole set of meteorologists and hydrologists help. And that's what I'm going to leave this department with: the most modern National Weather Service ever that will help protect and defend all of us because we rely on it and it's really important to you and really important to me. I appreciate that, Mr. Secretary. I'm also proud to support the University of Missouri's research reactor, MURR, the most powerful university-based reactor in the country. It is a critical national supplier, as you know, of life-saving medical isotopes that treat cancer patients each and every year. We're trying to raise the money now for a second reactor. It is a game changer in medicine. How will commerce continue to support university-linked research facilities such as reactor operations? Our university system is one of the great assets of our country, and our tech hubs and our biology work, medical work, these are extraordinary opportunities. And we are focused on making sure that the universities work together in consortia with business to create the best outcomes, right? We are very, very focused on outcomes. And that's what matters the most to commerce is that we want to make sure if you, you know, appropriate money, that we're going to get the benefit of the bargain for the American people and be great stewards of the money you give us. Mr. Secretary, thank you for your service and with that I yield back, Chair. Gentleman yields. Ms. DeLauro. Mr. Secretary, during the campaign the president made a lot of promises about trade policy. And I quote, he would put more money into our pockets and create millions and millions of new manufacturing jobs. I mentioned earlier in my opening remarks, I think hardworking families are not really feeling this reality. The president's tariffs have exacerbated the cost of living crisis that people are experiencing today. I know that the president says it's not an affordability crisis, that's a hoax, but talk to working Americans every day; they are living paycheck to paycheck, and it's tough. And American families now have had to pay more than $1,700 each in tariff costs last year. And so things are now worse because of the president's war of choice with Iran and we see the price of gasoline going up. The president made promises also about manufacturing, and the U.S. trade deficit in manufactured goods was $62 billion higher in 2025 compared to 2024. Not exactly the manufacturing renaissance that was promised. So let me ask you, Secretary, and I asked Ambassador Greer the same question last week and let me ask this of you: do you know how many manufacturing jobs the United States lost per day last year, the first year of President Trump's second term? I don't know that statistic one way or the other. Okay, well let me just say, this is according to the Bureau of Labor Statistics data. U.S. manufacturing lost more than 100 jobs per day from February 2025 to February 2026. That's more than 88,000 jobs in total. It's not a trade policy that delivers for working people. Do we not calculate the number of jobs lost, and what do we think about that? You know, this is really pretty extraordinary when we were supposed to, you know, be producing all of these manufacturing jobs. I would just say that the other thing with the Bureau of Labor Statistics, according to them, on net there was an increase of over 500,000 manufacturing jobs under the Biden administration. And I don't really want to have to talk about the Biden administration, but you all have been here for well over a year and we now need to measure your performance against standards that you set. There is a decline in manufacturing jobs that we've seen in the first year. So let me ask you what your response to that is, to the decline in manufacturing jobs? The tariff and trade policy of President Trump and this administration is to bring manufacturing back to America. But as Representative Meng points out, when we were up in Micron, we broke ground on a $100 billion plant that's going to have 40,000 construction jobs. TSMC in Arizona, again, has 40,000 construction jobs. I broke ground in southern Ohio, 25,000 construction jobs. So the manufacturing is going to start in two or three years when these factories come online, but construction is bringing this back. There is 400 billion in pharmaceuticals coming in because of our tariff policy. For semiconductors, we will have a trillion dollars of semiconductor announced investments during President Trump's administration, a trillion dollars of semiconductor manufacturing, won again and again. I just reclaim my time for a moment, Mr. Secretary, because I know, but you can't even tell us how many jobs we've lost. So I don't know about what we're seeing in the future. But you've just given a good segue for me for what you're doing. Your budget suggests to this committee, in your budget request, you abandon the programs that do the work of trying to promote American manufacturing. Just look at the fact that you zero out the manufacturing extension partnership, which provides small businesses with tools, training, and resources to grow, innovate, and remain competitive. You also eliminate the Economic Development Administration grants supporting workforce development, innovation, and entrepreneurship. And let me also say, it's worth noting that in your confirmation hearing, you opposed cuts to EDA, and it's now going to be eliminated. You've got the International Trade Administration, the Global Markets program. How do you propose that small businesses survive and thrive if you're not going to give them the tools that they need to be able to perform, to support development, and to support workforce development? It's just incomprehensible. You mentioned these very large projects you're doing, but you're cutting all of these efforts off to deal with small businesses and their ability to be able to succeed and to grow manufacturing. Comment? Ms. DeLauro, thank you. The time... I just wanted to ask whether you wanted me to respond in the... Why don't you just try to answer it? As the chairman will, why don't you try to answer the question, Mr. Secretary? Time of the gentlelady has expired. No answers to the questions. Mr. Clyde. Thank you very much, Mr. Chairman. Secretary Lutnick, thank you for appearing before our committee today. I want to thank you for the Bureau of Industry and Security's decision to rescind the Biden-era interim final rule on firearm export controls. That rule placed an undue burden on American firearm manufacturers and shut them out of overseas markets. Rescinding it was the right call, and so I appreciate the administration's action on this and want to say thank you. And also, the example that you gave of the BEAD, the Broadband Equity, Access, and Deployment program savings of $21 billion—did I get that right, $21 billion? Okay. $21 billion. $21 billion. That is incredible and that is a great testament to someone who comes in and says, 'You know, I think we need some competition in this and we need to be able to look at it and say one size doesn't fit all.' All right? And so I think this highlights the president's constitutional authority of impoundment. Here you have done something with 50% of the money and you've got 50% of the money saved, and therefore I think that should be able to either go back to the treasury or simply be unspent because in the end it ends up being borrowed money. So with that, I thank you. Now I want to talk a little bit about the BEAD program. Blue Ridge Mountain EMC in my district is a member-owned electric cooperative. They were excited to receive a $3.8 million BEAD award toward their $13.1 million project to connect 3700 hard-to-reach households in northeast Georgia with broadband. But on January 12th of this year, the National Telecom and Information Administration quietly added new language to the BEAD general terms and conditions requiring cooperatives to comply with FCC pole attachment rules across their entire service footprint, not just the BEAD funded areas, but for the entire service footprint. This is a serious problem for a few reasons. And I'll go through that. First, Congress explicitly exempted electric cooperatives from FCC pole attachment rules under Section 224 of the Communications Act. And as I understand it, the NTIA has no mandate to undo that exemption. Second, and most critically, Blue Ridge Mountain EMC is regulated by the Tennessee Valley Authority, which governs pole attachment rates for all 153 TVA-served utilities using a standardized valley-wide methodology. And so as I see it, NTIA is demanding that this cooperative violate its obligations to its own congressionally authorized federal regulator in order to accept a federal grant from a different agency. Blue Ridge Mountain EMC has concluded that the compliance burden may already outweigh the benefit and may be forced to walk away from the grant entirely, and I don't want to see that happen at all. All right? So are you familiar with this issue? That's the benefit of this job; I'm familiar with all of these issues. I just want to let you know, so I looked into this, the TVA is exempt from these rules, and Blue Ridge Mountain EMC is exempt and they can stick to the policy and the process that you and I would agree is the best for your area. Okay, so thank you for that commitment. I was going to ask you for that and you beat me to the punch, so thank you very much. We'll take that as gospel. So thank you, sir. I was encouraged to see your budget request reduces the Department of Commerce's footprint by $1.8 billion, a 16.5% decrease from the FY 2026 enacted level. Your proposal rightly eliminates duplicative programs within the Economic Development Administration and the Minority Business Development Agency and curtails billions in federal funding dollars that the Biden-Harris administration funneled into radical climate initiatives, research, and grant programs. So Mr. Secretary, you propose eliminating the Economic Development Administration and the Minority Business Development Agency. Can you describe how both of these agencies, the EDA and the MBDA, were wasting taxpayer dollars on earmarks for political pet projects and subsidies for leftist ideologies and the reasoning behind that savings? So for example, the MBDA had a very, very rough IG report, and I have that audit here. And it is brutal. It actually literally says that over 92% of the awards should never have been made, that the economic analysis was wrong and flawed, and so it was a mess. So we sought to make it zero because it was a mess. If it was effective, great, but if, you know, and these other programs—the problem like the MEP, right? The idea is a good idea. But you should read the Inspector General's report; it'd make you sick to your stomach. They overpaid the people. This was a setup in 1988 to do advanced manufacturing and what happened is it just crept and crept to where the money was just not being used effectively. So whatever appropriations you give to me, what I will promise you is I will do our darnedest to use it incredibly effectively for the benefit of the bargain of the American people. But if the current program is broken like MBDA, right? I'm obviously going to suggest to you to zero it out, right? Because it's broken. I'm not saying the ideas are wrong; I'm saying if the execution is wrong, I've got to come here and tell you it's broken and you can feel good to zero it out. If you want to come up with a new program and talk about it, let's do that together, but let's not do it wrong. Well thank you. I think that's wise counsel, and I appreciate that, Mr. Chairman. With that I yield back. Gentleman yields back. Mr. Morelle. Thank you. I would note, Mr. Chair, that you gave Mr. Clyde a minute and a half additional time and the ranking member was cut off and wouldn't answer—wouldn't listen to the answer by the secretary. But anyway, Mr. Secretary, thank you for being here. I often quote, as my friend reminds me, Mark Twain, but I'll quote you. You said the U.S. economy is the strongest and most resilient in the world. I think if Mr. Twain were here he would say given this administration it needs to be. Last year you testified before this subcommittee when I shared my concerns about business leaders in Rochester, New York because, you know, Republicans aren't the only people who talk to business leaders and people making investments. I'm concerned about tariff policies that have thrown them into a constant state of uncertainty, and the chaos remains the only predictable thing in this administration. In the past year there have been dozens of changes to tariff rates, target countries, products covered, specific exemptions, and a complete overhaul of the tariff authorities being used. When businesses don't have predictability—and I learned a long time ago when I'd meet with business leaders they'd say predictability is the most important thing. You know, almost anything you want, I mean they wouldn't like it but at least if it's predictable. There is no predictability. And the market conditions are changing literally week to week. The ranking member mentioned a $34 billion drop in factory construction investments in the last year. There have been 89,000 manufacturing jobs lost. I appreciate the continuity between the Biden and Trump administrations for Micron, which is up in my region and others. I'm proud of that as Americans we should be. But it's hard to argue when only 30% of Americans approve of the administration's handling of the economy. 89,000 manufacturing jobs lost and chaos. I don't know what to tell business leaders, so if you don't mind just answering that question, because I think this uncertainty is—and look this is important, these are people's lives, families' lives, and from day to day I hear from leaders say I don't know what to do. You would tell business leaders to build in America, build in America. We have advanced manufacturing; let's build in America. There is 400 billion dollars of pharmaceuticals coming in because of our tariff policy. For semiconductors, we will have a trillion dollars of semiconductor announced investments during President Trump's administration, a trillion dollars of semiconductor manufacturing, won again and again. Well let me just for a moment let's just hone in on that. So I represent Rochester, New York, which has dozens of small and medium-sized optics manufacturers, one of the great optics manufacturing and research centers in the United States. These companies create everything from eyeglasses to metrology machines, they have lenses in hypersonic missiles, almost everything that has a microelectronic chip has a lens. I talked to one just a few weeks ago; they're trying to onshore more of their production. To do that they have to buy a multi-million dollar machine because it's not made in the United States; they have to buy it from allies. It's a $10 million purchase. The tariff alone from the country that they're bringing it in from under a Section 232 tariff is 10%, which adds a million dollars to the upfront cost. They're thinking about not doing that as a result. So respectfully, building in the United States is great, but I talk to a company that has manufacturing in Rochester and manufacturing in Berlin. Because of Canadian imports that are going to get taxed, they said to me it is less expensive for us, more profitable for us to build and extend that manufacturing line in Berlin than it is in the United States. So I look, I'd love to tell people to build it, we all want—all of us, Democrats, Republicans, I don't care who you are—to build more manufacturing jobs in the United States. But you're losing them. And yes, you can cite an investment here or there, but the problem is the playing field is uneven, it's chaotic, respectfully it's reckless, and look you—I appreciate you, you have to be the cheerleader for it, and not just you, every commerce secretary is a cheerleader for every president, I get that. But at some point you've gotta advise the president this is reckless, it's hurting Americans. Even the notion, you're talking about auto manufacturers, the president's talking about Chinese auto manufacturers coming to the United States. They're going to compete with American auto manufacturers, but that's what we're talking about, and you're talking about Chinese—and by the way, their electric vehicles... No, no, they're not allowed to bring Chinese cars here. Absolutely not. Well, well the president's talking about allowing Chinese manufacturers to build and sell into American markets. He just announced last week he's talking about it. No. Okay, well you should talk to him about what he says then. I yield back, Mr. Chair. Gentleman yields back. Now in his debut in the new congress, the gentleman is recognized, Mr. Shreve. Thank you, Mr. Chairman, and thank you to our witness, Mr. Secretary. Happy to be here. Yes, this is my first day at subcommittee, this was my first week at committee, but I am thank you, thank you, thank you. I'm also a fairly new representative, I'm in my first term, and I come from the middle of Indiana. I represent central Indiana and eastern Indiana, and as a consequence in part of that as I came into service, I came to realize how victimized so many of my constituents—trusting Hoosiers from the heartland—had been victimized by scams, many of which emanate from foreign scam centers. This is not a mission that I had coming into service, but one which I came to realize how much and to what degree so many of my colleagues up here represent constituents who are being bilked out of billions of dollars in scams. I think particularly so with some of our older constituents, those from maybe more by default trusting parts of the country like mine and our chairman—maybe perhaps more than your neck of the woods where people may be less likely to answer an unknown call. But in the vein of the all-too-common text and email scams, phishing scams that are collectively stealing from our citizens, I wanted to ask you what our Department of Commerce is doing to push stronger authentication standards for foreign digital traffic before those calls and texts reach our citizens? I think that's a really interesting point to try to have the origin of the call be made clear to the recipient. So if—because especially what's coming with AI where they—someone could, you know, have my voice, right? And use my voice to call my family, it would be nice to know that it's from some crazy location far in Asia. So I think this is an excellent idea and if you'd like to work together with my team, I think let's go explore how to change that. I think that's a really extraordinary idea and very valuable to America. All right, I would indeed like to play that forward. And it relates in part to the conversation on export controls of these high-end chips. As the chairman noted, your budget requests a historic increase of $215 million and the addition of enforcement agents. And AI is at the root of some of these challenges. Certainly the fairly small scams, the text message scam, speed cam scams, and toll road scams, but the real sophisticated ones, some of which in my own district bilked a constituent out of $600,000 in property. The pig butchering scams are enabled by the AI technology, these high-end chips that can over time and increasingly do harm our citizens. Can you just add a little color to how you aim to use the additional funding in this budget increase on the enforcement for BIS enforcement of those high-end chips that are making their way all too often to south-central Asia? I serve on—well have served up to this point on foreign affairs and the south-central Asia subcommittee and that's how I really came to realize how focused these foreign scam centers were on that part of the world. We need law enforcement agents, right? The budget increase is for law enforcement agents. And the economics of those law enforcement agents—while we are here talking about the budget expense, the revenues that these agents call in by fining these companies far, far exceed the cost. So if this was a business conversation, it would be a slam dunk if there ever was one, right? Because I think these increases will go between five and 10 times the money of each agent we add. But because this is government where we have expense in one conversation and revenue's a whole another thing, you know, as commerce secretary I really want those agents. They will protect us, they will protect Americans, and they will pay for themselves multiple, multiple times. Thank you, Mr. Chairman, I timely yield back. Congratulations to the gentleman. Ms. Dean. Thank you, Chairman Rogers, Ranking Member Meng, Ranking Member DeLauro. Good afternoon, Secretary Lutnick. Last Tuesday I had the chance to attend a breakfast for RAINN, the Rape, Abuse, Incest National Network. It was their congressional day of action. Survivors came to me and told me their horrific stories, and the survivors I spoke with had one message: they're seeking accountability and justice. After speaking with the survivors, I again went to the Department of Justice to review the unredacted Epstein files to seek accountability and justice for the victims, the more than 1,000 victims and survivors. And today, Secretary Lutnick, I have some questions for you about what I saw. Let's start with your relationship with sex trafficker Jeffrey Epstein. In October, you told the New York Post with colorful narration that the last time you saw Jeffrey Epstein was in 2005 when you took a tour of his house, which happened to be right next door to yours. You claim you and your wife were so grossed out by this disgusting person you swore you would never go back. But when the Epstein files came out, it turned out that was a lie. You were forced to admit that you had visited Epstein's private island in 2012, four years after Epstein pleaded guilty to soliciting a minor for prostitution and was labeled a sex offender. You brought along your wife, your kids, and your nannies to the predator's island. Incredibly poor judgment. But that even wasn't the whole truth. It turns out that five days after visiting his island, you and Epstein signed a business deal together as co-investors in a digital advertising company, Adfin. An email showed you stayed in touch with Epstein about this deal as late as 2018. Secretary Lutnick, why did you lie to the New York Post about your relationship with Jeffrey Epstein? The House Oversight Committee and I have agreed we will spend the time together... Oh, I did hear that. Please don't repeat your gracious offer to speak behind closed doors, not under oath, without the ability of this committee to question you. The American people want to know, why did you lie about your relationship with Epstein? I have voluntarily agreed to spend the time and talk about it... I reclaim the time. I do not accept that answer. We've heard that one. We are our own committee; we have our own reason to test your credibility and veracity. Please answer the question: why did you lie to the post? I have voluntarily agreed to spend the time... I reclaim the time. Let the record reflect you're dodging the question; the cover-up continues. You know what, in your conversations with the president, is the president concerned about your continued relationship that you had with Mr. Epstein as recently as 2018? I am not going to discuss conversations I have with the president; that is not something I'm going to do. If you remember in that interview you said you would never be in the room with him socially, for business, or even philanthropy. President Trump has just fired three cabinet secretaries, three women: Kristi Noem, Pam Bondi, and Lori Chavez-DeRemer. If President Trump has even a shred of concern about accountability for Jeffrey Epstein's enablers, he would fire you too. Beyond Adfin, what other financial ties do you have to Jeffrey Epstein? As I said, I'm going to answer all these... Beyond Adfin, what other financial ties? You're the commerce secretary; we have a right to ask this question and get it answered. Again, I'm not going to spend the budget day discussing something I've set aside time to spend on this topic with your House colleagues. I'm going to switch topics. Let me switch topics. A different and equally disturbing issue: how your family is profiting from your work as commerce secretary. Let's start with tariffs. You and the president illegally imposed the highest tariffs on the American people since the Great Depression. And your sons figured out how to profit from the high tariffs by buying up refund rights, pennies on the dollar, just as you are out there cheerleading the tariffs. As a result, federal taxpayers like my constituents may now owe your family tens of or hundreds of millions of dollars. I wonder if President Trump is concerned about you and your family profiteering off the illegal tariffs? You know that that is false. Here's reporting; I'd like to put it in the record. Howard Lutnick's old firm did not, in fact, profit from the Supreme Court tariff ruling. It is just not true. It's your sons. It's your sons. The reporting is... No, no, it's my old firm and my sons. It is not true. We know it's not true; it's not true. Mr. Chairman, reclaiming my time. I have asked many times... Mr. Chairman... reclaiming about 20 seconds. I hope you recognize how fundamentally wrong and corrupt this is. I hope you take accountability and have the decency to resign before you are fired. It's not true. I yield. Time of the gentlelady has expired. Mr. Mrvan. Yes, Secretary Lutnick. Last week at the Sanford World Economic Summit, you were quoted—which I want to thank you for, it was a pro-union quote—saying the concept of taking our auto in an auto plant out of Ohio and Michigan and putting it in Mexico to break the unions' back is nuts, and to break people is nuts. Is that something that you said? I don't know if I said it exactly that way, but the concept sounds right. The concept, okay. And so you agreed that you want to protect American jobs and union jobs in that quote? Yes, I think automotive union jobs are excellent jobs for America. So on Labor Day—on Labor Day—the President of the United States put out an executive order which was the greatest collective bargaining and union-breaking executive order in the history of America. It took away bargaining rights, collective bargaining rights from everyone in the Department of Commerce. Why the two mixed messages? Why take collective bargaining away from union members who work under you, and why now are you saying you're fighting for union jobs? Union jobs in the commercial side of the United States of America are fundamental, are fundamental. Taxpayer jobs, taxpayer jobs in Article 2 in the United States of America, in the executive branch, should be run by the President of United States elected by the people. Okay. So you stand for union jobs because I need your help on this. British Petroleum, which is in my district and is an energy producer with the third-largest oil refinery, has locked out the United Steelworkers—approximately 1,000 families, men and women—because of contract negotiations. My Republican governor came to the picket line, Governor Braun. I agree with him in a bipartisan way that British Petroleum is putting our community at risk by having replacement workers who are untrained. So there are environmental risks and also safety risks for Hammond, East Chicago, and Whiting; my community is under threat. Will you join me in asking the President of the United States to tell British Petroleum, a foreign company and energy producer, to go back to the bargaining table so that those very valuable union jobs you're just theatrical about will be saved and families in my community will go back to work? I didn't mean to be theatrical. Don't laugh; there's nothing funny about... No, no, I said... Don't laugh at my situation about 1,000 families out of work. No, no, no, that's not right and that's not fair. Okay, okay, I'm just putting things in perspective. I said your comment of theatrical, I had not intended. But when we're talking about union jobs, I have been very clear: I am very supportive of great union jobs in America. I think they are excellent jobs for America. I'm a fan of the Steelworkers, I'm a fan of the Teamsters, and everybody knows it. I would be glad—will you make the commitment, how about this—let's sit together, let's go through it together, and let's come up with the right move to get this company to treat our union workers fairly. I am on side with that. Let's work together to do that. So the Secretary of Commerce, as your act—as your role—you will sit down with me to make sure British Petroleum goes back to the negotiation table so my families at Steelworkers go back to work? Seems like a fair request to me, yes. That's a yes. Okay. So, thank you for that. That's progress, and I'm going to hold you to it, as I expect you to hold me accountable to things. Secondly, you had talked about Secretary Wright and yourself celebrating gas-powered investments for natural gas-powered facilities in southern Pennsylvania and Ohio. In northwest Indiana, a week ago on March 26th, Secretary Wright made an emergency order saying that our utility, NIPSCO, needed to—because of national security—reopen a coal-fired plant. I have the highest rates for electricity and energy in the country; my ratepayers in northwest Indiana are getting crushed by those rates. It's $100 million to reopen up a coal plant that NIPSCO privately, privately chose to close in 2019. Explain to me why the mixed messages? Why is natural gas good for Ohio and southern Pennsylvania, but why do my ratepayers, because of an executive order, have to pay $100 million? There's a tariff placed through MISO that has the ratepayers have to pay that. Tell me why are my ratepayers, who have NIPSCO, the highest energy bills in the country, why are they paying for that, but Pennsylvania and Ohio, you're celebrating natural gas power plants? Just help me understand the differences. Opening a coal plant will lower the cost of energy in your district. I'm happy to sit down with you and go through it with you. I'm happy to do that. I love math, I love math, and it's going to lower the cost. The math is it's $100 million to the ratepayers because they have to reopen something while you're celebrating cutting ribbons for the same things in other parts of the country. I don't understand. The time of the gentleman has expired. With that, Mr. Ivy. I yield back, and I thank you for working with me on the British Petroleum issue. Thank you, Mr. Chairman. Mr. Secretary, I've got some questions for you about the illegal tariffs refund that the administration's, I guess, starting in the process to make. So the Trump administration collected about $166 billion or so in these illegal tariffs, and they were declared illegal by the Supreme Court. I think on Monday CBP opened up a portal that would allow the importers of record and licensed customs brokers to file for refunds. I know there were some problems with how it got going forward and the like, but the biggest question I had was it looks like only the first tier of entities that were interacting on the trade front are going to be eligible to get a refund. I asked the USTR head about this the other week. I'm trying to figure out if the Trump administration's going to do anything to help small businesses in the next tiers down who also ended up having to pay some of these tariffs as they were passed along, and especially everyday consumers. Individuals and customers who bought something at the store paid more because the tariffs raised the price of it. Now there's going to be a refund that's provided, but only to the frontline people who also benefited from charging the higher prices down the road. So from one perspective, those corporations are going to get a windfall profit, but there doesn't seem to be anything in place to pass along a refund to the smaller businesses or the individual customers. Are you aware of anything that the Trump administration is doing to help those funds go back to the small businesses and the individual customers? So again, I am the Secretary of Commerce, and Customs and Border Patrol is under DHS and Secretary Mullen. So that's just, unfortunately, not that which I'm involved in. I'm not involved in it and I haven't participated in those discussions. Oh, well hold up a second. I just saw the Face the Nation interview where you were talking about tariffs and you were praising the president's tariffs, the same tariffs that were declared illegal by the Supreme Court. In fact, you said that these tariffs are going to be so beneficial to the American people that there'd be a point where they wouldn't even have to pay individual income taxes anymore. Obviously that's not going to be the case now. And by the way, at the time that the president learned of the Supreme Court's ruling, you were standing there in the Oval Office with him as he criticized the Supreme Court for the ruling. So it's clear that it's in your bandwidth as Secretary of Commerce and it's clear that the tariffs are highly relevant to your role as Secretary. Is there anything that you're aware of that the Trump administration is doing with respect to the refunds? You really don't? No, as I said, there's a portal that has been put out, but it's by Customs and Border Patrol and I am not participating in that portal. Let's do it this way. Would you commit today to participate in setting up something with the administration, through the administration, to help make sure that small businesses who were not the frontline with respect to the payment of the brokers, and especially individual consumers, get a chance to get a refund? Well, that makes perfect sense. You're committing to do that? That makes perfect sense to me. All right. Well, we will definitely follow up with that. I appreciate your commitment on that front, and with that I yield back. Gentleman yields back. That concludes today's hearing. I'd like to thank our witness, Secretary Lutnick, for his testimony and his presence with the committee. Without objection, members may have seven days to submit additional questions for the record. Thank you, Mr. Secretary, for being here today and answering questions. Subcommittee stands adjourned.
